File.Name: j315d_j309-007.jpg
Notes: 47389999691 This is looking close at a little Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) hanging on to a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. I don't believe the fly is a proper Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family because it doesn't have the long palps that those flies have - compare this photo for an example. I think it might be a Limoniid Crane Fly in the family Limoniidae, though this one doesn't have patterned wings like others I've been finding, see this photo. I've been finding a few of these gangly flies lately, but I still haven't seen any proper Large Crane Flies. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2019)
CommonNames: Crane Fly; Wild Sweetpea; Large Crane Fly; Limoniid Crane Fly
ScientificName: Tipulomorpha; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Tipulidae; Limoniidae
associatedTaxa: Tipulomorpha; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Tipulidae; Limoniidae

File.Name: j316a_j316-020.jpg
Notes:
46675435554 Here is a gorgeous female Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched for just a moment on a bare stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Their host plant is the succulent Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) which grows on the sandstone outcrops nearby, see this photo and this photo. This is the first one I've seen this year. It's interesting that the female butterflies are more colorful than the males of the species - compare this photo from last year. This reverses the usual pattern with ducks and most other butterflies that the males are more brightly colored than the females. This is only the second new butterfly I've managed to photograph this year that isn't overwintering from last year, the other one is here. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2019)

Today was just about a perfect almost-spring day! There was not much breeze, and it did get up into the low 70s. The NWS had the message that "there are no watches, warnings, or advisories at this time" for the first time in a while. There is still a forecast for "showers likely" next Wednesday - which happens to be the day of the vernal equinox and the official first day of spring.
CommonNames: Sonoran Blue; Coast Live Oak; Southern California Dudleya
ScientificName: Philotes sonorensis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae
associatedTaxa: Philotes sonorensis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae

File.Name: j316b_j316-002.jpg
Notes: 46675283584 I believe this is an actual Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae standing on a unopened leaf bud on a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I spend a lot of time photographing the galls that these wasps induce, but I rarely see the actual wasps - see my Gall Album with 650+ photos. I suppose this could also be a parasitic wasp about to lay its egg on a gall wasp larvae that's already in there, but I prefer to think I'm right! I have a few other photos that might be gall wasps here, here, and here. Hah - this tiny wasp would probably fit on the eye of the wasp in my [Previous] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2019)

File.Name: j316c_j316-030.jpg
Notes: 32456716017 Hah - this Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) is glowering at me from behind a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). A moment later it was gone. Maybe it was feeling grumpy because it's just becoming active on an almost-spring day after a long winter. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2019)
CommonNames: Western Paper Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j316d_j315-017.jpg
Notes: 32456603827 I believe this is a tiny Carpet Beetle (Dermestidae, Coleoptera) visiting a flower of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. If you look close you can see that it has pollen grains between its mandibles, but it was also munching on the greenish nectar disk at the base of the blue pistil. Maybe the beetle is species Anthrenus lepidus? The larvae of these or similar beetles are notorious for cleaning up dead animal parts or that old butterfly collection you forgot about. The adults are rather pretty and visit flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 15 March 2019)
ScientificName: Dermestidae; Coleoptera; Ceanothus cuneatus; Rhamnaceae; Anthrenus lepidus
associatedTaxa: Dermestidae; Coleoptera; Ceanothus cuneatus; Rhamnaceae; Anthrenus lepidus

File.Name: j317a_j317-001.jpg
Notes:
40442670653 This is a skeletonized oak leaf - in progress! I'm not sure what critter is doing the job, but the results are unmistakable. The leaf belongs to a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I know we have both fungi and caterpillars in our woods that skeletonize oak leaves, see this photo and this photo. Whatever is doing the damage in my new photo is working on a coarser scale, and I'm not sure it will even leave the bones. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2019)

Today was warm with temps in the upper-70s on the upstairs porch. The wind was mostly calm - with confusing gusts from both the south and north. It's expected to turn cooler now with "showers likely" on Wednesday - which happens to be the day of the vernal equinox and the official first day of spring. Nice!

File.Name: j317b_j317-014.jpg
Notes: 32465937527 I believe this is a winged ant (family Formicidae, Hymenoptera) perched for a moment on a small plant of succulent Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) on a sandstone outcrop. It's a reproductive form. I tried to get a photo showing its large abdomen, but the ant was crawling along too fast, and this is my only photo that's reasonably sharp. I don't know what kind of ant it is, but I wonder if it's a queen because of its size? This plant is the host of the Sonoran Blue butterflies I've been showing a couple of photos back, though I've never seen a caterpillar on (or inside?) one. They seem to be having a good year. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2019)

File.Name: j317c_j316-028.jpg
Notes: 46492676535 This is a nickle-size Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). This one has five-segment antennae unlike the four-segment antennae of the bug in this photo, hence the family name Penta-tomidae. There are many different kinds, but one possibility for its identity is the Consperse Stink Bug (Euschistus conspersus). It's curious that this one looks like the bug I found sitting in a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae), see this photo from last November. It seemed to be spending the winter in this seed pod, but along the way it changed color from brown to green, see this photo from February! Well, it either changed color or another one took its place. It finally disappeared a couple of weeks ago. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2019)
CommonNames: Stink Bug; Wild Blackberry; Consperse Stink Bug; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Penta-tomidae; Euschistus conspersus; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Penta-tomidae; Euschistus conspersus; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae

File.Name: j317d_j316-014.jpg
Notes: 46684548094 I can't resist one more shot of a gorgeous female Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched for just a moment on a bare stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - see my Previous] photo from yesterday for more info. This is the first one I've seen this year, and I hope it's not the last. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2019)

File.Name: j318a_j318-026.jpg
Notes:
47363609102 I found this little beetle (Coleoptera) working new flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in the chaparral near Lake Cachuma today. It seems to be munching the pistil of one of the flowers. I don't know what kind of beetle it is, though the long antennae and swollen femurs seem distinctive. Maybe Lepturinae or Cantharidae? Any help with identification will be appreciated. These shrubs are literally covered with small white flowers. You may not see many insects at first, but try knocking a flowering branch with your walking stick and catch the insects that fall in your hand. It's a memorable lesson with kids. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 March 2019)

I had to drive back into the Santa Ynez Valley today to finish what I started last week. so I stopped at my usual place near Lake Cachuma. It was another beautiful almost-spring day, maybe a few degrees cooler than yesterday but still in the 70s. There was a breeze from the south. Our chance of rain on Tuesday night and Wednesday stands at 80%.
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Ceanothus cuneatus; Rhamnaceae; Lepturinae; Cantharidae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Ceanothus cuneatus; Rhamnaceae; Lepturinae; Cantharidae

File.Name: j318b_j318-005.jpg
Notes: 33540546108 Here are pretty new flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. hoffmannii) in the Solanaceae plant family. If you look close back in the shadows, you can see that the stems and leaves are almost glabrous, with just a bit of "peach fuzz" on them. See this photo to compare with the species. This variety is not recognized in the Jepson manual except as a synonym for the species, but maybe it should be? It seems distinct and localized, and it is recognized in my old Munz manual. These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style, and pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers. The flowers usually point down, but these were growing high enough that I could get under them. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 March 2019)

File.Name: j318c_j318-007.jpg
Notes: 47416236951 I found these pretty blue flowers of native Tuberous Skullcap (Scutellaria tuberosa) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the chaparral near Lake Cachuma today. They are said to be a fire-follower plants, and this place did burn about 15 years ago. They are small plants, just 2 or 3 inches high, but very pretty. They look like an escaped garden ground cover plant, but they are native. These are plants I always forget about and then am surprised to find again. They don't seem to be very common, but they are easy to walk right past without noticing when hey are not flowering. I found another patch of them last year closer to home, see this photo, though those plants are hairier and more patterned than these plants near Lake Cachuma. I'll have to remember to check back on them. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 March 2019)

File.Name: j318d_j318-011.jpg
Notes: 40450662573 Here are tiny lollypop-like sporophytes coming from a genus Fossombronia liverwort. These plants have a ruffled thallus that looks like moss - but isn't. They are tiny, and I had to get on my belly to see that they actually had sporophytes. The whole scene of my photo is only about 1/2 inch across. You can also see a few different kinds of moss in this photo and a different liverwort in the lower-left. I found these tiny plants growing on bare ground in once-burned chaparral near Lake Cachuma, see this photo for more liverworts from this place. I like these secretive little plants that hide in plain sight! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 March 2019)

File.Name: j319a_j319-016.jpg
Notes:
32482277777 I found this somewhat tattered Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly working new flowers of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae). This butterfly looks pale, compare this photo of a fresher specimen that is almost black rather than brown. These are one of our usual winter butterflies, but they've been rather scarce this year, see this photo and this photo for the only two photos I've gotten. I believe this is the only butterfly of any kind that I've ever seen on these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2019)

It's been cloudy all day and quite breezy with wind from the south - our usual rain wind. It's finally started to rain as I've been writing these comments. It feels like a good storm coming, but the NWS forecast says it won't deliver much rain. We'll see.

File.Name: j319b_j319-034.jpg
Notes: 40458300613 Here is a little Timema insect (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) on still-not-open flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I first thought this one was a male because of it's small size, but it does seem to have "symmetric terminalia" which indicates it is female - see this photo for the details. There was another larger one nearby, but it got away from me. I've never found them on this plant before, but there was a native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) shrub nearby and it might have crossed over. The colors of these adorable little insects are highly variable. This is the first one I've seen this year, though it's still early. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects which are endemic to California and neighboring states. I have a bit more info in my comments on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2019)
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: j319c_j319-011.jpg
Notes: 47371116222 This Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has a non-native European Earwig (Forficula auricularia, Dermaptera) on a flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen, though this is larger than some that can fit into the throat of the flower - see this photo taken nearby. I think of earwigs as household pests, though I occasionally find them in the woods too - see this photo. Keep up the good work little spider! (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2019)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Forficula auricularia; Dermaptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Forficula auricularia; Dermaptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Mecaphesa

File.Name: j319d_j319-009.jpg
Notes: 47423910811 Here are the first new flowers and foliage of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Aceraceae plant family poking out of their buds on a winter-bare tree. It's a winter-deciduous tree that is common in Oregon but unusual in our mountains. They are particularly beautiful at this stage - and it's fitting since tomorrow is the vernal equinox and the first day of spring. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2019)

File.Name: j320a_j320-005.jpg
Notes:
46707991614 Here are new flowers on a shrub of native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family. The flowers look like buds, and maybe they'll open further like they did last year in May, see this photo. This is an uncommon plant in our mountains, and I only find it in a few shady places where the sun never shines. The foliage is holly-like and beautiful - but very thin, like paper, not what its looks like. These plants sometimes go years without flowering, but they might make up for it this year. The plant looks pretty bright in this photo, but it was actually raining fairly hard at the time. I reckon new flowers in the rain are an appropriate image for the official first day of spring. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2019)

We ended up with nearly an inch of rain from our storm today - about twice what was expected. It was raining too hard to go for a walk, so I went for a drive instead. It was curious that I could look out under the clouds and rain to see that it was sunny at the same time in Santa Barbara. Today is the vernal equinox and the first official day of spring. Happy equinox! The seasons change, life goes on . . .

File.Name: j320b_j320-001.jpg
Notes: 46515795895 Here are tiny "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) with decurrent gills that go down the stalk growing in sandy soil above our retaining wall - with my pen for scale. My pen has a 0.7 mm ball, so that smallest mushroom is about one millimeter across. These mushrooms might be Omphalina pyxidata or kin, though they don't seem to have the depressed cap that those typically have, compare this photo. I got this photo in light rain this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2019)

File.Name: j320c_j317-009.jpg
Notes: 40465107423 I believe this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider sitting on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - like the front porch of its web with a shelter behind. You can see the outline of a funnel-shaped opening around the spider. At one point I jiggled the web, and the spider disappeared in a flash. What's most interesting is that this was the web of Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider until a big wind damaged it last week, see this photo of the same web in other hands. I wonder how often this happens that one spider takes over the web of another? Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2019)
CommonNames: Funnel Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak; Sheet Web Weaver

File.Name: j320d_j317-035.jpg
Notes: 46707712564 This is the orb web of a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider stretched between dead plant stems above our retaining wall. You can see the spider in the center if you look close. It's perched between two parts of its stabilimentum, that thicker white webbing above and below. In time the spider will decorate this with the bodies of its prey, see this photo for a closer look. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2019)

File.Name: j321a_j321-004.jpg
Notes:
46715287734 California gold! I noticed this large Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushroom in the rain yesterday, and got the photo this morning. I'll give it another day or two to dry out before picking it, though in my experience these mushrooms aren't much affected by the rain. It's a large mushroom, the size of your open hand, with blunt ridges instead of proper gills. This one is pushing up a fairly large "mushrump" in the oak mulch. (I believe that's David Arora's felicitous word for the mound of leaves that forms when mushrooms push up from underneath. That's often all you see of the mushrooms that are developing!) According to my records, I've found Chanterelles as early as November and as late as May, with a peak in February. We dry-sautÈ them with garlic, I have a recipe with this photo. Yum! This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2019)

We had a few more showers today, bringing our storm total up to 1.13 inch - more than twice what was expected. It's still cloudy, and I wouldn't be surprised if we got another shower. Hah - we've now had 44 inches of rain since September, and the average annual rainfall in Portland, Oregon is 36.69 inches! Our next (small) chance of rain comes on Saturday and then another chance next Wednesday. There late-season rains are good for the summer flowers, but it will also be nice to dry out for a while!
Locality: small) chance of rain comes on Saturday and then another chance next Wednesday.  There late-season rains are good for the summer flowers

File.Name: j321b_j321-001.jpg
Notes: 47385483052 I thought these might be slime molds when I first saw them, but they seem to be tiny cup fungi instead - at least they are cup-like fungi, but maybe not genus Peziza. They are growing on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale, and the ball is actually a little larger than the fungi. They look like tiny goblets. I have no idea what they are, but they seem magical to me! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2019)

File.Name: j321c_j317-031.jpg
Notes: 46522902545 This Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) is resting its abdomen on a somewhat tattered leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). A moment later it was gone. These wasps are just starting to become active for the new year as we move into spring. This photo has a good view of the petiolate (stalked) abdomen of these wasps which distinguishes them from the genus Polistes. It's also good for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2019)
CommonNames: Western Paper Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Polistes
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Polistes

File.Name: j321d_j315-011.jpg
Notes: 46522846255 I found this little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hanging it's abdomen off a leaf of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in the chaparral near Lake Cachuma. I believe it's another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen, and I had the distinct impression at the time that it's a male, though it got away from me before I could make sure. I almost forgot about this photo from last Friday, but I've been saving it all week - for HBBBT! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 15 March 2019)

File.Name: j322a_j322-031.jpg
Notes:
47445293951 Here is a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea, Reduviidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head and the prominent spines and bumps on its front legs and head. Its wings look like gold foil! They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are shy and retiring in my experience. I've been seeing more of them than usual lately. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2019)

Today started sunny and calm after the morning clouds cleared out, but then a breeze kicked up and new clouds appeared in the afternoon. We do have a slight chance of showers tonight and another chance next Wednesday. There late-season rains are good for the summer flowers, but it will also be nice to dry out for a while!
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j322b_j322-011.jpg
Notes: 33569188738 Here is the tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) crawling along a narrow leaf of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It seems to have an injury on its right-side antenna, and I wonder if it's regenerating? (Can they do that??) The nymph is not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last summer, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. It might be the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum), see this photo. This is the first nymph of any kind of Katydid that I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2019)
CommonNames: Katydid; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; California Angle-winged Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Microcentrum californicum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: j322c_j322-003.jpg
Notes: 46529893645 This is a small Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. There are still a few drops on the leaf from our rain yesterday. I don't believe the fly is a proper Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family because it doesn't have the long palps that those flies have - compare this photo for an example. I think it might be a Limoniid Crane Fly in the family Limoniidae. I suppose it might also be a Winter Crane Fly (Trichoceridae), though I don't see the ocelli in any of my photos which distinguish that family. I've been finding a few of these gangly flies lately, and I finally saw my first proper Large Crane Fly today. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2019)
CommonNames: Crane Fly; Wild Sweetpea; Large Crane Fly; Limoniid Crane Fly; Winter Crane Fly
ScientificName: Tipulomorpha; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Tipulidae; Limoniidae; Trichoceridae
associatedTaxa: Tipulomorpha; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Tipulidae; Limoniidae; Trichoceridae

File.Name: j322d_j321-013.jpg
Notes: 32503376217 This is an odd little March Fly in the Bibionidae family - actually in the month of March! It's on new leaves of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek. I believe it's a lady with those widely spaced eyes, compare this photo of a male of possibly the same species. This one might be the species Bibio xanthopus, compare the images at BugGuide. It's face is strange - it seems to have palps like the little crane fly in my [Next] photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2019)
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Bibio xanthopus
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Bibio xanthopus

File.Name: j323a_j323-013.jpg
Notes:
47399503962 Here is a small critter on a new leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) - with raindrops from our small storm today. I believe this is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera), one those wasps that have no sign of a "wasp waist" - see this photo from last year. You can see the woods reflected in some of those drops! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2019)

We did indeed get some more rain today - but only a few hundredths of an inch. That was just enough to decorate this sawfly with raindrops and coax me back inside to curl up with a good book. It's still cloudy now in the evening, and the wind is starting to kick up - we're under a NWS Wind Advisory again tonight.

File.Name: j323b_j323-008.jpg
Notes: 40486293443 I found this interesting spider in leaf mulch under a small Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree. It's a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with cryptic colors. It might be a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus, but I think it's more likely in the genus Ozyptila. For one thing, it has stubby front legs with a length-to-width ratio of about 3:1, while the other genus has a ratio more like 4:1. The genus Ozyptila is also said to have just two spines on the first tibia, and maybe this one has more - but maybe that's just the way its front legs are stacked up. Whatever kind it is, it's an interesting spider and a surprise in the leaf mulch. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2019)
CommonNames: Madrone; Crab Spider; Ground Crab Spider
ScientificName: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Thomisidae; Xysticus; Ozyptila; Ozyptila
associatedTaxa: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Thomisidae; Xysticus; Ozyptila; Ozyptila

File.Name: j323c_j323-010.jpg
Notes: 46729095534 This is a mushroom with a funnel-shaped cap and decurrent gills that grow down the stalk - maybe the Common Funnel (Clitocybe gibba aka Infundibulicybe gibba). I found it growing in the mulch under California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) trees, though there was also Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) nearby. The green leaves in my photo belong to Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis, Caprifoliaceae) which is just starting to leaf out for the new year. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2019)
CommonNames: Common Funnel; California Bay; Coast Live Oak; Nuttall's Snowberry
ScientificName: Clitocybe gibba; Infundibulicybe gibba; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae
