File.Name: j323d_j319-059.jpg
Notes: 46536969185 Here is a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda), probably a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae. Julie found a pair of these in a garden pot and saved them for me in a glass jar with plant cuttings in the bottom. The sun was gone from the ground when I got home, so I got this photo of the hood of my white truck. It's interesting to see the snail so fully extended. This is a native snail and NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails that are so common in the lowlands. I often find their empty shells (like this), but I rarely find them alive and crawling (like this). I believe they are mostly nocturnal and stay out of sight. HSS! (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2019)

File.Name: j324a_j324-009.jpg
Notes:
33584450018 Here is another handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it's genus Platypedia? I found a different cicada a couple of weeks ago that appears to be the same kind, see this photo. Both have green colors that suggest that they are teneral - recently emerged from its final molt from nymph to adult - but maybe that's just their color? I don't believe these early cicadas sing at all, they might just make the clicking noise that I've been hearing up in the oaks - see this page for sound samples. I left this one alone as I continued on my walk today, and it was gone when I came back. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2019)

Today was pleasant with sun and temps in the mid-60s. There was a breeze, but it got less rather than more as the day went on. Our next chance of rain comes on Wednesday with "rain likely" - probably not a big storm, but maybe 1/2 inch. These late-season rains are good for the summer flowers and to keep the creeks flowing, but it would also be nice to dry out for a while!
ScientificName: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Platypedia; teneral
associatedTaxa: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Platypedia; teneral

File.Name: j324b_j324-020.jpg
Notes: 46737434814 I'm pretty sure this is the gnarly larva of an Antlion (genus Myrmeleon, Neuroptera). This is just as I found it today, and it beats me what it's doing out in the open instead of hiding in a funnel-shaped pit waiting for prey. I found this one on the same decomposing sandstone embankment in our canyon as the liverworts I show two photos [Previous], just a few feet away, and it may be that I disturbed its nest when I was getting that other photo. The larva has amazing eyes. I have no doubt these are the inspiration for the giant worm-like creatures in the classic horror movie Tremors! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2019)

File.Name: j324c_j324-012.jpg
Notes: 46737324854 This is a tiny leaf-miner larva that's come to the end of its mine on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It pulled itself free from its tunnel a moment after I got this photo and fell away to the ground. I suspect this is a moth caterpillar, but I believe there are leaf-mining flies and sawflies as well (at least), and I'm really not sure what this one is. Wow, what are the odds of being in just the right place at just the right time to notice and document this moment! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2019)

File.Name: j324d_j324-027.jpg
Notes: 47407382182 Here are two different Liverworts (Marchantiophyta) growing on a decomposing sandstone embankment in the canyon. The larger flat leaves (or thallus) belong to the California Liverwort (Asterella californica) which has a couple of sporophytes on the right side of my photo. These are the ones that remind me of little palm trees, see this photo for lots more. The ruffled moss-like leaves are a different genus Fossombronia liverwort, and you can also see a few tiny lollypop-like sporophytes on the right between those of the other one. See this photo for lots more. I didn't realize that we had this one in our canyon, but here it is. I like these secretive little plants that hide in plain sight! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2019)

File.Name: j325a_j325-021.jpg
Notes:
33591885908 Here is another California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly "kissing" my finger - no doubt it's after minerals like salt. It's really "puddling" on my hand! This one particular butterfly was pestering me - landing on my hat, my shirt, my camera. I finally got a photo on my hand, though it's a literal stretch with my 105 mm macro lens on a tripod. The difference between their upper and lower wings is remarkable and allows them to virtually disappear simply by closing their wings. These butterflies overwinter as adults and are active on warm days like today. We seem to have more of them right now than I've ever seen before. I have another photo (not on Flickr) with six of them on a sandstone outcrop at one time. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2019)

Today was a near-perfect spring day with temps approaching 70∞ and not much breeze. We still have "showers likely" on Wednesday, but now it's sounding like we'll get more wind than rain.

File.Name: j325b_j325-035.jpg
Notes: 47468369931 This is another remarkable and unusual Tmarus spider, probably Tmarus angulatus. It's a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that is less than 1/4 inch long - not counting its long front legs. If you look close, you can see that it has the enlarged palps of a male. I had to break my usual rule and use my on-camera flash for this photo - the spider seemed to disappear in the contrasty light today. I found this one on a potted plant on the upstairs deck after I got home from my walk today. It stands out on a green leaf like this, but you can imagine how well camouflaged it would be on a gray oak twig. I was confused for a while distinguishing these spiders from a couple of spiders in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers, see this photo and this photo. The differences are obvious when you can compare them directly - not so obvious when you see one in the wild by itself! I've only found these a few times, but that might just be because they are so well camouflaged. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2019)

File.Name: j325c_j325-005.jpg
Notes: 33591733238 This is looking close at another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea, Reduviidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It raised its head when I got close, maybe in an effort to "look big" - works for me! You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are shy and retiring in my experience. I've been seeing more of them than usual lately. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2019)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j325d_j324-032.jpg
Notes: 46744992984 Here are top and side views of a cute little Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). There are many different kinds of these weevils, but maybe this one is species Thricolepis inornata. (Thanks again Alice!) This one is small, only about 1/8 inch long, see this photo for how I figured. Needless to say, these are a tight crops. They will become common as spring moves on, but these are the first ones I've noticed this year.
Locality: Thanks again Alice!)  This one is small

File.Name: j326a_j326-026.jpg
Notes:
32533780737 I have no doubt that this is a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working the flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae) in the garden today. It's the first of any kind of Swallowtail that I've managed to photograph this year, though I've seen a few in passing. I know that Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies sometimes develop cream or yellowish colors later in summer, but they are never as bright as this one. See this photo for both butterflies side-by-side. This one didn't want to pose for me, but hopefully it will be back. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2019)

I was preoccupied with plumbing today. We've had a leak behind a wall, and today a plumber went in to find it. Success, problem solved! It's not like I helped, but I felt obliged to hang around - so today's photo are all from close to home. We did have some breeze and clouds moving in today, and showers are likely tomorrow. It sounds like we'll get just a bit of rain and several days of wind.
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Salvia "Waverly"; Lamiaceae; Papilio eurymedon
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Salvia "Waverly"; Lamiaceae; Papilio eurymedon

File.Name: j326b_j326-020.jpg
Notes: 46560304605 Here is a little Click Beetle (family Elateridae, Coleoptera) on a deer-eaten stem of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) on the edge of our garden. These beetles have their thorax drawn out to points that can *click* against the wing covers - sometimes quite dramatically. I was hoping to get a shot of this beetle taking wing, but it was too fast for me. This plant is unfortunately a favorite of the deer - they seem to munch it as fast as it grows. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2019)

File.Name: j326c_j326-009.jpg
Notes: 46752209034 This is a tiny orb-weaver spider tending a line to its web - like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing. I suspect this one is actually a Long-jawed Orb Weaver in the Tetragnathidae family, probably genus Metellina. It has a humped back, very long front legs, and it spins a small perfect orb web that is sometimes slanted towards horizontal. This long-legged spider kind of looks like the genus Tmarus crab spider I showed yesterday in this photo, but it's different. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2019)

File.Name: j326d_j325-020.jpg
Notes: 46560135395 Here is one more shot of a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly on my hand, see my [Previous] photo. The cryptic underwings are remarkable, like the marbled endpapers of fine old books. The butterflies are almost invisible with wings closed unless you happen to see one land. This specimen is somewhat tattered. These butterflies overwinter as adults, but I've been seeing more of them right now in spring than I've ever seen before. I have another photo (not on Flickr) with six of them on a sandstone outcrop at one time. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2019)

File.Name: j327a_j327-010.jpg
Notes:
40516514983 This is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a stem of Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus, Fabaceae) - with raindrops from our storm today. Maybe it's another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen. The lupine is one of several local species which is distinctive for its very narrow leaf lobes and flowers that are not at all whorled. This is the first one I've seen flowering this year. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2019)

We got almost 2/10 inch of drizzle in our rain gauge today, more than I was expecting. We might even get a bit more tonight. Then we might be in for a few days of wind.

File.Name: j327b_j327-003.jpg
Notes: 47482494781 Here is another shot of the tiny orb-weaver spider that I showed yesterday in this photo - with raindrops from our storm today. I believe it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver in the Tetragnathidae family, probably species Metellina mimetoides. It has a humped back, very long front legs, and great camouflage! It's actually hard to see where the spider ends on these dead stems of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). This long-legged spider kind of looks like the genus Tmarus crab spider I showed recently in this photo, but it's different. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2019)
ScientificName: Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Tmarus
associatedTaxa: Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Tmarus

File.Name: j327c_j322-040.jpg
Notes: 32540412187 This orb-weaver spider may look large in this photo, but it's really quite small - only 1/4 inch or a bit more. It looks just like one of the large bottlecap-size spiders I find in the fall, see this photo, and maybe it's a juvenile of the same kind. I believe these are genus Araneus with those barely visible humps, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) The spider is on its web in this photo so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2019)

File.Name: j327d_j317-038.jpg
Notes: 46758973114 Here is the web of a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae), probably another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. If you look close, you can see not one but two spiders below the top of the dome - probably another soon-to-be-mating pair like I showed in this photo a couple of weeks ago. You can see that this web is a broad dome held up by extensive rigging lines on the right. These lines probably do double duty - holding up the web, but also knocking down flying insects. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2019)

File.Name: j328a_j328-016.jpg
Notes:
47489371711 Here is a handsome male Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched for just a moment on last year's dead stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). It's interesting that I've found them on these dead stems before, see this photo from last year. This is the first male that I've seen this year, though I got a shot of a female last week, see this photo. If you compare these two photos, you can see that the female butterflies are more colorful than the males, having orange patches on both their forewings and hindwings. This reverses the usual pattern with ducks and most other butterflies that the males are more brightly colored than the females. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2019)

Today started with some drizzle. Then it cleared up, and the winds kicked up with 20-35 mph gusts. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, but it looks like wind is in the forecast until Sunday at least.

File.Name: j328b_j328-013.jpg
Notes: 40523327253 I believe these odd mushrooms are the Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum) growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see the ribs on the outside of the cap on the right, which extend almost to the cap margin. These mushrooms look very similar to the Peziza cup fungi in this photo, but the ribs of this elfin cup are distinctive. Hah, I didn't notice until I got the photo up on my computer screen, but there's a snail shell in the upper-left, right above the large open mushroom on the left. It's another native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda), probably a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae, see this photo. It looks like it's got a hole in the shell that might be the work of a Snail Eater Beetle (Scaphinotus, Carabidae, Coleoptera), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2019)
CommonNames: Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup; Coast Live Oak; snail shell; Land Snail; Shoulderband Snail; Snail Eater Beetle
ScientificName: Helvella acetabulum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peziza; Gastropoda; Helminthoglypta; Helminthoglytidae; Scaphinotus; Carabidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Helvella acetabulum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peziza; Gastropoda; Helminthoglypta; Helminthoglytidae; Scaphinotus; Carabidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: j328c_j326-038.jpg
Notes: 46765923724 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) on a new leaf in the in the mint patch of the garden. I can't identify the fly, but maybe it's some kind of Muscid fly in the family Muscidae. This is a good shot of the fly's lumpy rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2019)

File.Name: j328d_j325-010.jpg
Notes: 40523237323 Here is another California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly perched on my left hand - while I got the photo with my right hand working a 105 mm macro lens on a tripod, which is a literal stretch! This one particular butterfly was pestering me - landing on my hat, my shirt, my camera, no doubt after minerals. They seem to be unusually common right now. I have another photo of this butterfly here, but this one is a good shot of its rear end - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2019)

File.Name: j329a_j329-019.jpg
Notes:
33619501388 Here are pretty new flowers of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The flowers are just starting to open up here in the mountains, though they've been going for some time at lower elevations. The new flowers are intricate, like little pentagonal origami boxes with secrets inside - probably thrips and tiny beetles! This species is usually rather pale. Other species are deeper blue, but we still have to wait for them - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2019)

It was windy again today with 20-35 mph gusts. It's hard to think about getting photos when it's this windy, but I tried today - with flowers rather than insects. Our NWS Wind Advisory has been extended until midday tomorrow, and I'm hoping it won't have to be extended again.

File.Name: j329b_j329-001.jpg
Notes: 32554076947 Here are striking new leaves and catkins of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I found the first early flowers back in early February, see this photo, though that was just a tease of what is still to come. Most trees are still waiting, but I think they are finally starting to flower for real now. They are beautiful - but I expect some hay fever coming up. It's interesting that the new leaves aren't always red like this. Sometimes they are pale yellow, and the combination of red and yellow trees is wonderful. Now it's time to worry about the Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) larvae which devour the buds, see this photo. I'll be watching for them on the oaks at home - not like there's much I can do about them. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2019)

File.Name: j329c_j326-037.jpg
Notes: 40530071683 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) on a new leaf in the in the mint patch of the garden. I can't identify the fly, but maybe it's some kind of Muscid fly in the family Muscidae. I posted another photo of the same fly's rear end for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group yesterday, and here is the other end of the fly for Fly Day Friday group - so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2019)

File.Name: j329d_j322-006.jpg
Notes: 46772421694 This is a rather attractive fly (Diptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It has unusually large paddle-like antennae and a bristly abdomen. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it's not "just a fly" like so many are. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2019)

File.Name: j330a_j330-025.jpg
Notes:
33626392208 Here is another Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on a leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). I guess it's pausing from its migration flight for a moment. The migration is still going on as great numbers of these butterflies head north towards Oregon, though it's slowed down some, maybe because of the recent wind and rain. This butterfly may have another issue as well. There is some yellow goo at the end of its abdomen, and I wonder if some bird didn't just grab the butterfly's hindwings and a piece of its abdomen as well? See this photo for more about that yellow goo. This butterfly is a trooper - it kept flying. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2019)

Today was just about a perfect spring day - it got up to 70∞, and yesterday's big winds mostly died down. Tomorrow might be more of the same, but then it will start cooling down again with a (slight) chance of rain late next week.

File.Name: j330b_j330-007.jpg
Notes: 47450230792 I believe this is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) that I found on a new leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). I've seen these little flies early in the season before, see this photo from earlier this month. I believe this fly is tribe Phthiriini and maybe genus Acreophthiria - compare this photo at BugGuide. Now why do I so often find interesting flies on Saturdays - the day after Fly Day Friday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2019)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Phthiriini; Acreophthiria
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Phthiriini; Acreophthiria

File.Name: j330c_j330-033.jpg
Notes: 33626184768 This is a tiny orb-weaver spider on what's left of its web after some clumsy photographer ran into it with his tripod. (*Mea culpa*) I suspect that it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver in the Tetragnathidae family, probably species Metellina mimetoides. It has a humped back, very long front legs, and it spins a small perfect orb web that is sometimes slanted towards horizontal. I believe that this one is male with those dark palps. What I can't explain is why its webbing looks so frizzy - as though its cribellate, see this photo. There is the Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers which also makes a mostly horizontal web, but that one is furrier and has different feet - see this photo. I don't think this spider is one of those. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2019)
CommonNames: Long-jawed Orb Weaver; Feather-legged Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers
ScientificName: Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Uloborus diversus; Uloboridae
associatedTaxa: Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Uloborus diversus; Uloboridae

File.Name: j330d_j330-004.jpg
Notes: 33626120928 Here are small "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) with depressed caps and decurrent gills that go down the stalk growing in sandy soil above our retaining wall. I believe these mushrooms are Omphalina pyxidata or kin. I have another photo of the same mushrooms when they are just starting and don't yet have the depressed caps - see this photo. I got this one above our retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2019)

File.Name: j331a_j331-043.jpg
Notes:
46595789625 Here is a tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider on an old seed head of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It's web is off to the right - and if you look close, you can see it is tending a line to its web with the leg on the screen-right, like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing. I'll make a note. I believe the spider is in the genus Araneus, though it's tiny compared to the the large bottlecap-size spiders I find in the fall, see this photo. I actually found two of these tiny spiders today that look quite different, see my [Previous] photo. This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. Given that in my spider the "abdomen dips between the two humps," then maybe it is Araneus montereyensis. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2019)

Today was another near-perfect spring day, though there was a breeze this morning - from the south?? The temperature actually got up into the high 70s, not quite 80∞ but close! It was probably the warmest day of 2019 so far. Now it's expected to cool down a few degrees tomorrow, and high clouds already are moving in. We do have a (slight) chance of rain late next week.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Araneus; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus montereyensis
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Araneus; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus montereyensis

File.Name: j331b_j331-015.jpg
Notes: 47511006621 I found two different-looking Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spiders today that I suspect are the same kind. I believe they are both in the genus Araneus, though they are tiny compared to the the large bottlecap-size spiders I find in the fall, see this photo. The spider on the left is in a shrub of Bush Monkey Flower, and the one on the right is in a shrub of Black Sage. The spider on the right is about 1000 pixels high in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.9 mm, about 5/32 inch or a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The spiders are probably Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. Given that in both of my spiders the "abdomen dips between the two humps," then maybe they are both Araneus montereyensis - but they sure look different! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus montereyensis
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus montereyensis

File.Name: j331c_j331-031.jpg
Notes: 32568749487 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). I believe it's another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen. Now look at the throat of the flower in the upper-left and compare my [Previous] photo of the same kind of spider but much larger. This spider could probably fit between the eyes of that one! I happen to have another recent photo of a similar tiny crab spider on the same kind of flower, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2019)

File.Name: j331d_j331-046.jpg
Notes: 40544965003 This is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). I believe it's another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen. Now look at the throat of the flower in the lower right and compare my [Next] photo of the same kind of spider but much smaller. That spider could probably fit between the eyes of this one! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2019)

File.Name: j401a_j401-011.jpg
Notes:
46795156374 I found this little Snakefly (family Raphidiidae, order Raphidioptera) insect on a branch of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) today. I believe it is genus Agulla with that "kite-shaped" head and female with that long ovipositor that disappears behind a leaf. (See this photo for a male.) What I can't explain is that bit of fluff on the back of the critter's neck - some horrible parasite? a hitchhiker? part of a plant that it picked up? I don't know, but it's gone in another photo taken a few minutes later. Little mysteries - and not an April Fools' joke! (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2019)

Today was another fine spring day, though there was a pretty good breeze this morning from the south. It was a few degrees cooler than yesterday, but still in 70s. There were high clouds moving in. We still have a chance of rain later in the week.
ScientificName: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Agulla
associatedTaxa: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Agulla

File.Name: j401b_j401-027.jpg
Notes: 46795005054 Here are the first flowers I've seen this year of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This shrub is in the same family as the Ceanothus shrubs I've been showing, but those flowers have five showy petals while these are 4-merous - compare this photo. It's also in the same family as California Coffeeberry, but those flowers also have have five petals - compare this photo. I guess this is why Coffeeberry (Frangula californica) and Redberry are no longer placed in the same genus. The plain flowers are followed by red berries, see this photo. I was looking for tiny Psyllid insects on these flowers, see this photo from last year. I didn't find any, but the season is still young. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2019)

File.Name: j401c_j401-010.jpg
Notes: 33641896968 Here are brand new flowers of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. The variety inezianus isn't recognized in the Jepson manual, but the plants do seem distinct - larger, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. Very beautiful. They are a fire-follower, though these are growing in a place that was cleared - not burned - a few years ago. Sometimes that's enough. They are one of my favorite spring flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2019)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus

File.Name: j401d_j331-029.jpg
Notes: 47465945872 This is a female Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched for just a moment on her host plant, a succulent Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. You can't really see what she's doing here, but I'm sure she's ovipositing - laying eggs. Actually she may be laying just one egg. I watched her for some time hoping she'd come back to this plant. She skipped from plant to plant maybe depositing an egg on each one and never came back to the one I was waiting by. This is a different reproductive strategy from certain other butterflies like the Varaible Checkerspot which lay all their eggs at once, see this photo and this photo. This isn't the best photo, but it's a special moment. See this photo for a better shot from a couple of years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2019)

File.Name: j402a_j402-003.jpg
Notes:
47473456402 My first thought was that this is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, though it's not as furry as those flies often are, compare this photo. My second thought is that it's a Snipe Fly in the family Rhagionidae because of the filiform antenna. I have a history of confusing these two groups, but I'll go with my second thought! I found it today on a new leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I reckon it's a male because of those holoptic eyes that meet along the midline. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2019)

Just two photos today because I had something to do in town late this afternoon. It was a crummy day for new photos anyway. Clouds moved in from the ocean-side of the mountains and the wind kicked up. This might have been the first proper marine layer of the year, at least up here in the mountains. I don't mind the clouds, but I don't like the "fog winds" that come with it.

File.Name: j402b_j402-020.jpg
Notes: 40560071683 This is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with cryptic colors that I found on the bed liner of my truck today. I don't know what it was doing in the bed of my truck, and it quickly got away from me when I lowered the tailgate. I guess this spider is a male, though it seems to be missing a palp. I reckon it's either genus Xysticus or Ozyptila or Coriarachne or Bassaniana - one of those groups of similar cryptically colored spiders. I have another recent photo of a similar spider in here, though I think that one has stubbier front legs. Whatever kind this one is, it's an interesting spider and a surprise to find in my truck! (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2019)

File.Name: j403a_j403-035.jpg
Notes:
32590378367 Here is another shot of the Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly that I show in my [Previous] photo. It's on a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It was tilted towards the sun like a living solar collector, trying to catch some rays on a chilly day, but then it straightened up and spread it's wings - and I got this photo. I guess I pestered it enough to consider flying away, but then it decided against it and went back to being a solar collector. I believe this one is female - the males are darker blue, and the color goes right to the margin. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2019)

It was cloudy and cooler today. It never got out of the 50s, which is why I found this butterfly. Unlike yesterday, there wasn't much wind. These might be real clouds unlike the (I think) marine layer clouds yesterday. There is still a chance of rain on Friday.

File.Name: j403b_j403-039.jpg
Notes: 32590290317 This looking down on a Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly on native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The butterfly is actually tilted towards the sun like a living solar collector, trying to catch some rays on a chilly day. A moment later, it straightened up and spread it's wings, see my [Next] photo, and then it leaned over again. These butterflies have large black spots surrounded by white on their forewings. They have smaller spots on their hindwings and sometimes none at all. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2019)

File.Name: j403c_j403-009.jpg
Notes:
40566586593 Spiders aren't the only ones . . .

This webbing is the work of a little caterpillar on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I can actually see just a bit of the rear end of the caterpillar in its nest, but mostly it is hidden underneath a layer of frass (caterpillar poop) that seems to be suspended from the ceiling. The caterpillar has been nibbling on the top layer of the leaf, and in time it will completely skeletonize it. I've seen these before, see this photo and this photo. I don't know what kind of caterpillar it is. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2019)

File.Name: j403d_j330-002.jpg
Notes: 46808902784 This is looking up at spider webbing on a leaf cluster of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). I'm not sure what kind of spider is responsible, but it hardly matters. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2019)

File.Name: j404a_j403-041.jpg
Notes:
40572867753 This is another small Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) trying to hide under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - and exposing its rear end in the process. I've been finding a lot of these tiny crickets lately, usually in a similar pose. I guess they are active at night and hide in daytime. They are perfect for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but I've been showing too many of them lately. At least I waited for a month before posting this one today! This one actually looks slightly different from the one in this photo - is that little horn on he bottom of its rear end an ovipositor? (I'll make a note.) I suspect that that damaged oak leaf is the work of the little caterpillar I showed in this photo yesterday. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2019)

It was chilly today, and we did get a bit of rain - just a few hundredths of an inch, but it's something. We might get showers through Saturday. I've been feeling sick today. I tried to go out for a few photos but gave up. Hopefully this is just short-term bug.
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j404b_j331-005.jpg
Notes: 46623335305 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) about to balloon away on a thread of webbing. One of these times I'll manage to catch one that is just leaving its perch, but not this time. This photo certainly shows the spiders rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2019)

File.Name: j404c_j330-015.jpg
Notes: 47486037252 This is a tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) showing its rear end on a narrow leaf of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). The nymph is not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last summer, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. It might be the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum), see this photo. These tiny nymphs are just starting to appear. This certainly counts as a Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday photo, but I already have my two photos for that group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2019)
CommonNames: Katydid; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; California Angle-winged Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Microcentrum californicum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: j404d_j403-028.jpg
Notes: 46623251825 Here is one more shot of the Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly that I show in my [Previous] two photos. In this photo you can see how it's tilted towards the sun like a living solar collector, trying to catch some rays on a chilly day. A moment later, it straightened up and spread it's wings, see my [Previous] photo, and then it closed up again. These butterflies have large black spots surrounded by white on their forewings, see two photos back, but you can't see its forewings at all in this pose. They have smaller spots on their hindwings like this, and sometimes none at all. Tat makes for a plain butterfly, but they are more interesting on the top side. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2019)

File.Name: j405a_j405-007.jpg
Notes:
33669157788 Here are tiny wasp galls on a staminate catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm still not sure whose galls these are, but they are definitely made by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), see this photo of a different wasp. There's a photo of something similar at BugGuide, but it's in the section of "Unidentified Galls". The galls are tiny, about 1/8 inch long. Is that an insect egg in the middle of the upper catkin? (I'll make a note.) Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but these galled catkins usually stay on the tree long past blooming. Strange and beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2019)

It was cloudy all day, but we didn't get any more rain. The cloud cover made for nice light on these galls which kind of disappear in bright contrasty light. I'm feeling better now, at least not so listless. I did get out for a few new photos like this one, but I already knew where they both were. We still have a slight chance for light rain tomorrow, then it might warm up for a couple of days.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps

File.Name: j405b_j405-003.jpg
Notes: 40579762233 Here are strange mushrooms growing in leaf mulch in the garden under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Hah. it looks like the stem of a Elfin Saddle joined with a cup fungus! The ribs don't extend on to the underside of the cap, so I believe this is the Ribbed Elfin Saucer (Helvella solitaria aka Helvella queletii). Despite the species name, I've been finding these mushrooms in small groups. I haven't seen them since 2011 before our long series of drought years. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Ribbed Elfin Saucer
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Helvella solitaria; Helvella queletii
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Helvella solitaria; Helvella queletii

File.Name: j405c_j403-043.jpg
Notes: 33669001918 This is a little gold-flecked fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, and I'm not going to try and figure it out tonight when I'm getting over being sick. It is certainly not "just a fly" - so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2019)

File.Name: j405d_j403-015.jpg
Notes: 47545596781 I believe this is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) that I found on a new leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I've seen these little flies early in the season before, see this photo from earlier this week. I believe this fly is tribe Phthiriini and maybe genus Acreophthiria - compare this photo at BugGuide. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2019)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Phthiriini; Acreophthiria
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Phthiriini; Acreophthiria

File.Name: j406a_j406-047.jpg
Notes:
46829023854 Here are a pair of Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterflies courting in a tree of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) down by the creek. At least I assume they are courting. I suppose they could also be fighting, but it looked more like the female (on the left) was parked on the tree inviting the attention of the male (on the right) which flew around and around her. After a while they noticed me and went off together to be alone. The female butterfly is actually hanging onto a new stem of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) that is growing up through the willow. The host plant of these butterflies is said to be mostly sycamore which grows just upstream from here, but willows, cottonwoods, and alder will do. I've been seeing a fair number of these Tiger Swallowtails this year, but I still haven't been able to get a photo of the usually more common Pale Swallowtail butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2019)

I was expecting at least some clouds today, but it turned out to be sunny and very nice. They are having good rain up in northern California and southern Oregon where they need it more than we do this year. Sunday and Monday are expected to be even warmer, maybe up to 80 degrees.
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Arroyo Willow; California Honeysuckle
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: j406b_j406-020.jpg
Notes: 47499805112 This is looking face-to-face with an odd Planthopper in the Fulgoroidea. I found this tiny insect on a new leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). My first thought was that it's a Cixiid Planthopper in the family Cixiidae which I've found on this shrub before, see this photo. But it looks different when I look at both photos now. Going through the photos at BugGuide, I think it might be an Achilid Planthopper in the family Achilidae. What a remarkable face it has! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2019)
CommonNames: Planthopper; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Cixiid Planthopper; Achilid Planthopper
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Cixiidae; Achilidae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Cixiidae; Achilidae

File.Name: j406c_j406-033.jpg
Notes: 46636969085 Here is a little Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's another pest on the oak trees that eats the new leaf buds, you can see some damage here. There are many different kinds of these weevils, but maybe this one is species Thricolepis inornata. (Thanks again Alice!) This one is small, only about 1/8 inch long, see this photo for how I figured. See my [previous] photo for another pest of our oak trees. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2019)

File.Name: j406d_j406-018.jpg
Notes: 46636905995 This is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) ovipositing - laying eggs - on budding new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The eggs will hatch into caterpillar-like larvae which devour the new leaves and catkins, see this photo. Really, these little devils can defoliate the trees if they get started early enough when the new leaves are still tender. I'm worried about them this year because the trees are late to open their buds and flower. The leaves do grow back eventually, but the trees don't need the stress. I have another photo here that clearly shows a different species of sawfly. I consider these insects to be a major pest. I know this isn't the best photo, but it shows something important. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2019)

File.Name: j407a_j407-038.jpg
Notes:
46644915715 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). I believe it's another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen. If you know the flowers, then you know how small the spider is. I can also count pixels, it's about 500 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Compare this photo and this photo. The spiders look similar, and I still don't know if they are small juveniles or a distinct species. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2019)

Today started out cool and under clouds, and I thought the forecast for heat might be wrong. But it started to clear before noon, and it did feel warm - up to about 78∞ on the upstairs porch. Increasing high clouds helped keep it from being even hotter, but tomorrow may make up for. It was also quite breezy today with 20-30 mph gusts in the afternoon. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and it sounds like it will be breezy all week. The NWS says that "... damaging High Wind Warning level winds are possible across the Santa Ynez Range and adjacent south coast of SBA County below passes and canyons ...." Darn, Julie and I were hoping to finally make it out to the Carrizo Plain this week, but it would be no fun in the wind.

File.Name: j407b_j407-020.jpg
Notes: 33683548388 I found this tiny winged aphid (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) today on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It really is tiny, about 580 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.3 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It has an impressive proboscis for sucking plant juices. It's just an aphid, but it's also quite beautiful, like it's filled with honey - and in a sense I suppose it is, since aphids also make honeydew! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2019)
ScientificName: Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j407c_j407-007.jpg
Notes: 47560248851 This is a tiny Lace Bug (Tingidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It really is tiny, about 840 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) This plant is where I usually find them, though we seem to have a different species on shrubs of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae, Boraginaceae) - see this photo. The remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body always reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor - but it also looks like bubble-wrap! This is the first one I've seen this year, and I missed them completely last year. Hah, there's not enough DoF at f/128 to photograph these bumpy critters! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2019)
CommonNames: Lace Bug; Wild Sweetpea; Trask's Yerba Santa
ScientificName: Tingidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Tingidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: j407d_j406-023.jpg
Notes: 40594118773 I believe this is a small Pacific Gopher Snake (Pituophis catenifer) - though I'm no expert on snakes, and I rarely notice them unless they call attention to themselves by rattling! Actually Julie and a friend noticed this one first, and I got the photo. It was crawling along the base of a sandstone outcrop which is a favorite place for lizards, so maybe it was hunting. Some of these snakes are quite gentle and don't mind being held, but others are quite nasty - see this photo! I never got close to this one. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2019)

File.Name: j408a_j408-004.jpg
Notes:
33691603358 Here is another galled catkin of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - with a different small wasp (Hymenoptera) checking it out. I don't know what kind of wasp it is, but I assume it's a "parasitic wasp" interested in inserting its eggs into the galls to live off the larvae inside. I'm also not sure whose galls these are, but I believe they are definitely made by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae), see this photo for another shot and some discussion. Hah, I just noticed a tiny White Fly (or something similar) behind the wasp, I'll make a note. There's a lot of life in the oaks when they start to flower! (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2019)

It did feel warm today, but there were enough high clouds to cut the heat from the 80s that were forecast to the high 70s. It was quite windy all day with 20-40 mph gusts. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight and a High Wind Warning tomorrow. That means winds of 60+ mph, hang on! It sounds like it will be windy all week, but I hope they're exaggerating.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; wasp; Cynipid Gall Wasps

File.Name: j408b_j408-014.jpg
Notes: 46652871235 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new flowers of non-native Smooth Vetch (Vicia villosa ssp. varia, Fabaceae). I believe it's another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen. It's only about 1/8 inch long. See this photo from yesterday for another tiny crab spider that might be the same kind, though this one has more vivid colors. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2019)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Vicia villosa; varia; Fabaceae; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Vicia villosa; varia; Fabaceae; Mecaphesa

File.Name: j408c_j408-024.jpg
Notes: 46652775465 Here are odd new flowers on native California Ash aka Foothill Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family. The flowers live up to their species name "dipetala" in having just two petals in addition to two large stamens. It usually grows not too far from the Santa Ynez River, but there are also a few odd shrubs up here in the mountains. The shrub or small tree is deciduous, and the new leaves are quite striking - see this photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 April 2019)

File.Name: j408d_j408-038.jpg
Notes: 46652636835 So that's why they call it "needle grass"! These are new florets of native Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua aka Nasella cernua) in the Poaceae plant family in full flower, and the long awns are needle-like and remarkable. It's some kind of native Stipa grass anyway. They were blowing in the wind today, and I had to take quite a few shots to get this keeper. Our native grasses look like any other weedy non-native grasses until they flower, but then they look quite special. The hallmark of this species is that they have twisted awns. This feature is not visible here, but you can see it in this photo from a few years ago. I had my usual errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, and I made a few stops along the way to get a few photos. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 8 April 2019)
ScientificName: Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua; Poaceae; Stipa
associatedTaxa: Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua; Poaceae; Stipa

File.Name: j409a_j409-015.jpg
Notes:
46852046994 Here is another Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly that was poking around in a pile of non-native weeds that Julie has recently pulled along the road in our canyon, mostly Bur Clover (Medicago polymorpha, Fabaceae). The butterfly had its proboscis out, and was sampling the plants - a kind of puddling on weeds? This butterfly was on the ground, but many more of them were in the air. I haven't noticed them for a few days, but I guess the migration is still on. It's odd that they were flying north into great winds . . . from the north. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2019)

It was windy from the start today, with 30-40 mph gusts already at dawn. It was curious that we were also in a cloud with a couple hundredths of an inch of drizzle early this morning - despite the wind! It cleared up, but the wind continued. It was my good luck to find this butterfly on the ground. We're under a NWS High Wind Warning tonight and then a Wind Advisory until Thursday. That means winds of 55+ mph tonight, hang on!

File.Name: j409b_j409-019.jpg
Notes: 47522862922 This is an odd foliose gall surrounded by new flowers on a shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I believe it is the gall of a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera - probably the Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi). I say "probably" because there are similar galls on native Jim Brush (Ceanothus oliganthus var. sorediatus) and Chaparral Whitethorn (Ceanothus leucodermis) that are much larger and leafier, see this photo and this photo. It's hard to believe that these smaller galls are really made by the same critter. It's also interesting that these galls are considered to be galled flower buds, though I don't understand how this works. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2019)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Gall Midge; Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge; Jim Brush; Chaparral Whitethorn
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi; Ceanothus oliganthus; sorediatus; Ceanothus leucodermis
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi; Ceanothus oliganthus; sorediatus; Ceanothus leucodermis

File.Name: j409c_j408-018.jpg
Notes: 46851801714 Here are new flowers of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. This common shrub or vine isn't really poison, but many people are sensitive to it. It really is a beautiful plant and important for wildlife. It's pretty to look at, but don't touch it if you are sensitive! For some reason, I don't get it. Those are famous last words, but I hike through Poison Oak constantly, and it doesn't bother me unless I really ask for it. That little beetle (Coleoptera) is enjoying flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2019)

File.Name: j409d_j408-041.jpg
Notes: 47575645631 I believe these are a mating pair of Blister Beetles in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera - likely the Red-eared Blister Beetle (Lytta auriculata). They are on a new flower of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family. If the colors seem a bit off, it's probably because I used some of my on-camera "slow flash" for this photo - it was breezy and the beetles were not in the sun. I guess that's a bit of frass (poo) where they are joined. Happy Hump Day! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 8 April 2019)
CommonNames: Blister Beetles; Red-eared Blister Beetle; Chaparral Nightshade
ScientificName: Meloidae; Coleoptera; Lytta auriculata; Solanum xanti; Solanaceae
associatedTaxa: Meloidae; Coleoptera; Lytta auriculata; Solanum xanti; Solanaceae

File.Name: j410a_j410-016.jpg
Notes:
33706127778 This is a stunning Bramble Hairstreak aka Lotus Hairstreak aka Coastal Green Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly hanging on a leaf of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's a female, and this is its host plant. I don't think it's ovipositing in this photo, but it was in other shots I got today. This is actually the first one I've managed to photograph since 2014, and it's in our home canyon as well. The name "Bramble Hairstreak" is really a misnomer as these butterflies have nothing to do with brambles except for being green. They are beautiful butterflies, and I hope to find more this year. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2019)

Today was another windy day with 20-30 mph gusts all day. At least that's better than yesterday! We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and it sounds like it will be windy until the weekend.
CommonNames: Bramble Hairstreak; Lotus Hairstreak; Coastal Green Hairstreak; Deerweed
ScientificName: Callophrys dumetorum; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Callophrys dumetorum; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: j410b_j410-013.jpg
Notes: 46858952684 Here are two shots of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) today. These aren't the best photos, but it's the first of these butterflies that I've seen this year, so it's a record shot. I just went through my notes, and I most often find these butterflies in June, but I have one earlier record from 29 March 2015. The butterflies are distinctive because the black on their forewings runs along the wing rather than across it like our other swallowtails. Flawed as these photos are, you can see that the butterfly's proboscis is yellow with pollen in the shot on the right. Thus pollination happens, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2019)

File.Name: j410c_j407-029.jpg
Notes: 33705870338 Here is a web-spinning caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a dead leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's covered the leaf with a layer of webbing, which I believe is a preparation for molting. I went back to this leaf today, and sure enough there was a fresh-looking caterpillar with its cast-off skin in a separate pile. I got a photo today, but I like this one better. Spiders aren't the only ones - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2019)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: j410d_j406-027.jpg
Notes: 46858809474 A week ago, I posted this photo showing the webbing of a little caterpillar on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can't make out the caterpillar in that photo, but here's another shot taken a few days later in which the caterpillar is visible. If you compare the two images, you can see that it's eaten more of the top layer of the leaf, and in time it will completely skeletonize it. It's also turned more of the leaf into frass (poo). I don't know what kind of caterpillar it is, but I do know that spiders aren't the only ones - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2019)

File.Name: j411a_j411-021.jpg
Notes:
47536822532 Here is another Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on a new leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I was surprised to find it here. Thousands of these butterflies have been flying by as they continue their migration to the Pacific Northwest, and I wonder why this one decided to stop here. This isn't really a proper "butt shot", but it does show the butterfly using its rear end to do what it should - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2019)

Wind. wind, wind . . . we had 30-40 mph gusts all day again. Despite the wind, I managed to get a few decent photos by bumping up the ISO and shooting lots of photos close to the ground. Our official NWS Wind Advisory will end in the morning, and it sounds like Friday will be our last windy day for a while. I won't be able to post photos on Friday. Our power will be out all day for scheduled maintenance, and then we have an event in the evening to attend. I'll be back on Saturday - which will hopefully be a nice calm day!

File.Name: j411b_j411-038.jpg
Notes: 47536763492 This is a fly (Diptera) on a flower of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. The fly is going round and round the flower, slurping the green nectar gland which its vacuum-like proboscis. A slightly larger insect couldn't help rubbing against the stamens, see this photo. I'm not sure what kind of fly this is, but I don't think it's "just a fly" on this interesting flower far from any houses. Any help with identification will be appreciated. It's only Thursday local time, but it is Friday by Flickr time. I won't be able to post tomorrow because of a combination of events, so I'll add it to the Fly Day Friday group tonight instead - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2019)

(See my [Next] photo for a whole flower.) I'm sure the fly is less than 1/8 inch long. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, and any help with identification will be appreciated. It's only Thursday local time, but it is Friday by Flickr time. I won't be able to post tomorrow because of a combination of events, so I'll add it to the Fly Day Friday group tonight instead - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2019)

File.Name: j411d_j411-006.jpg
Notes: 46865532794 I'm pretty sure this is the larva of a Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family. Julie found it in the garden this morning, and I posed it on a dish of oak mulch for a photo. It's about an inch long, but it can stretch itself out to at least double that size. It reminds me of a little seal, and it's tough like one too. This photo shows the larva's interesting rear end, and I'm really not sure what's what. I considered adding this to the Fly Day Friday group, but that seems too weird. It's perfect for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group though, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2019)

File.Name: j413a_j413-062.jpg
Notes:
47603066971 Here are female (left) and male (right) Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterflies on last year's dead stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). The lady has orange spots on both forewing and hindwing, but the tattered male only has spots on its forewing. She seems to be leading him on, and a moment later they were mating. I'll save that photo for a few days for the Insect Hump Day group. It's interesting that I've found these butterflies on dead Mugwort stems before, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2019)

It's good to be back at Flickr after missing yesterday because of power maintenance and an evening event. Yesterday was still pretty windy, but today wasn't bad - some breeze from the south, probably an eddy circulation from winds moving around Point Conception. It was pleasantly warm today, up into the 70s. Hah, now there is a chance of a shower on Monday night! One of these little storms will be our last for some time.

File.Name: j413b_j413-057.jpg
Notes: 46878764564 I found this magnificent fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods today. I'm sure it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), possibly genus Peleteria - compare this photo at BugGuide. It has a fringe around its head like an Elizabethan lace ruff! It seems like I usually find simlar flies in the woods in late summer and autumn, like this one from September. The larvae of these flies parasitize other insect larvae like caterpillars, so it makes sense they would be out both early and late in the season when many insects are involved in reproducing. Yeah, I saved another shot for Fly Day Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Tachinidae; Peleteria
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Tachinidae; Peleteria

File.Name: j413c_j413-011.jpg
Notes: 46687029455 This is a proper Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family hiding in a shrub of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) near the still-flowing creek. It's more than an inch long, giant for a fly! This is the first photo I've managed to get this year, though I've been seeing them flying off when I disturb their rest. (I assume they are mostly nocturnal but easily awakened?) These large flies have the terminal segment of their palps longer than the other segments combined - those dangly bits at the end of its head. We also have smaller Crane Flies in the same group of Tipulomorpha that differ in having the terminal segment of their palps short, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2019)

File.Name: j413d_j413-007.jpg
Notes: 46686996605 Here is another little Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's another pest on the oak trees that eats the new leaf buds, though I can't see any fresh damage here. There are many different kinds of these weevils, but maybe this one is species Thricolepis inornata. (Thanks again Alice!) It's small, only about 1/8 inch long, see this photo for how I figured. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2019)

File.Name: j414a_j414-040.jpg
Notes:
46886587304 Here is another Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly on last year's dead stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). These butterflies do have an affinity with the dead Mugwort plants - I'm sure I find them here more often than on their Dudleya host plants. This one is female with orange spots on both forewing and hindwing. I found a soon-to-be-mating pair yesterday, see this photo, but I got this shot today about ten feet away. I have no idea if it's the same female. I'm getting more photos of these butterflies than usual this year. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2019)

Today was another lovely spring day with a high temperature of about 70∞. There was a breeze from the south again, one of those frustrating small breezes that keeps everything moving that you want to be still! We still have a 30% chance of rain tomorrow night, but it's hard to believe right now.

File.Name: j414b_j414-027.jpg
Notes: 33733503188 Look close! Here is a another tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a getting-ready-to-open flower stalk of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). I believe is the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I find later in the spring. Those white hairs coming out of its rear end makes it look jet propelled! I've been finding quite a few of these lately, but they don't always have developed tails, compare this photo. The lupines are starting to flower now, but I'm still working at getting a photo I like. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2019)

File.Name: j414c_j414-001.jpg
Notes: 47557650362 This is a tiny caterpillar on a shiny new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The caterpillar was barely visible, about 1000 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.9 mm - a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm not sure what it will become, but it might be an early instar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae , Lepidoptera) - see this photo and this photo. The mature caterpillars look quite different, but they seem to go through many different stages before they get their clown colors. Like I said, I'm still not sure what it will become. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Western Tussock Moth
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae ; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae ; Lepidoptera

File.Name: j414d_j414-043.jpg
Notes: 47610616841 Here are a pair of caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly starting to munch a new leaf of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. (Unlike most of its host plants in the old plant family, this one never got reclassified.) This is a native perennial that is growing in our garden, and the caterpillars might well defoliate it. This is the first wave of caterpillars that come from eggs laid last year. They will pupate and become butterflies which will lay a new generation of eggs in early summer. Those eggs will become the second wave of caterpillars that will eat until the leaves are gone, and then disappear underground until they emerge next spring - and the cycle repeats. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. This year is being a little different in that I found the first caterpillars very early on January 13, see this photo. I've been finding new caterpillars ever since then, but not in great numbers. I haven't seen any butterflies yet, and I wonder how they will do. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2019)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; California Figwort; California Bee Plant

File.Name: j415a_j415-056.jpg
Notes:
40652136323 Here are two shots of tiny Psyllid (Psylloidea) insects on leaves of different Ceanothus shrubs in the Rhamnaceae plant family that are flowering. That's Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) on the left and Jim Brush (Ceanothus oliganthus var. sorediatus) on the right. These critters are really tiny, about 480 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 1.9 mm - substantially less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Is that an ovipositor and an egg that I see through the diaphanous wings of the one on the right?

Both insects might be the same kind, but maybe not. Psyllids of several different genera are said to occur on Ceanothus species, but maybe these are in the genus Ceanothia, and maybe Ceanothia ceanothi - see Chris Mallory's comment to this photo. I've also found pysllids on Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia, Rhamnaceae), and Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata, Anacardiaceae), and near Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) - see this photo and this photo and this photo. I've been looking for these tiny insects, and I'll keep looking for them. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2019)

It was another pleasant spring day, though the temperature was nearly ten degrees cooler than yesterday. There was a breeze from the south, and there were high clouds in the morning becoming thicker and lower as the day went on. We still have a 30% chance of rain tonight, and we might get some drizzle - and then more wind?

File.Name: j415b_j415-033.jpg
Notes: 33741251978 This is looking close at a single flower of of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. That central column of stamens and styles is fascinating to watch as the flowers mature. They start with all parts up in the air, then he stamens drop down like in this photo, then the stamens lift up again and the style branches drop down with their stigmas - see this photo. I have many photos of his fine plant, try a search. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2019)

File.Name: j415c_j415-041.jpg
Notes: 47618543241 This is the real native California Mustard (Caulanthus lasiophyllus) in the Brassicaceae plant family. In past years, this plant has also been known as Thelypodium lasiophyllum and Guillenia lasiophylla - those darned botanists! It's not one of the showy but non-native "wild mustards" that take over the foothills, but it's a true native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. This one is growing in a place that was cleared but not burned. The seed-pods get long as the mature and bend down to give the stems a shaggy look, see this photo. It's not a common plant in our mountains, even after wildfires. These plants were large and robust when I first found them a few years ago, but they seem to get smaller every year that I find them here. One of these years, they'll disappear until the next clearing. That's how it is with fire-followers. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2019)
ScientificName: Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla
associatedTaxa: Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla

File.Name: j415d_j415-012.jpg
Notes: 40651860953 Here are new flowers of perennial Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. You can see one of the distinctive palmate leaves in the upper-left - recognizable even when out of focus. The flowers come in impressive racemes with sometimes hundreds of individual flowers. They are mostly whorled like these, but sometimes they are not whorled at all. They are just starting to flower in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2019)

File.Name: j416a_j416-024.jpg
Notes:
40659636093 I found this fairly large beetle (Coleoptera) on the ground today. At first I thought it might be injured, but maybe it just hasn't tucked its wings under their covers yet. I reckon it's either a Ground Beetle (Carabidae) or a Darkling Beetle (Tenebrionidae). I'm guessing it's a Carabid with exposed mouth parts and long antenna segments and the tarsi of all three legs that seem to be made up of 5 segments. It's not a Pinacate-type beetle (genus Eleodes or Coelocnemis, Tenebrionidae) because it doesn't have fused wing covers. From its shape, it might be a Snail Eater Beetle (Scaphinotus, Carabidae), but it got away from me before I could get a look at its face. On the other hand, it does look different from the beetle in this photo which I'm pretty sure is a real Snail Eater. Another possibility is a Caterpillar Hunter in the genus Calosoma, and I think this one is more likely. In short, I don't know what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2019)

We did get a splash a rain last night, but only about 1/100 inch - hardly enough to settle the dust. We've been in and out of the clouds since then, and it's been pretty windy with 20-30 mph gusts. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, but it sounds like tomorrow might be a nice day.
CommonNames: beetle; Ground Beetle; Darkling Beetle; Snail Eater Beetle; Caterpillar Hunter
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Carabidae; Tenebrionidae; Eleodes; Coelocnemis; Tenebrionidae; Scaphinotus; Carabidae; Calosoma
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Carabidae; Tenebrionidae; Eleodes; Coelocnemis; Tenebrionidae; Scaphinotus; Carabidae; Calosoma

File.Name: j416b_j416-007.jpg
Notes: 46901624344 Here is another small spider with big humps. It's an Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider with its web nearby in the cactus patch of our garden. Here it is huddled under one of the spines after a bit of rain last night. It's curious that I found a spider that looks just like this one about 20 feet away just over a month ago, see this photo and this photo. That spider disappeared one day, but did it take a walk or hitch a ride on the wind? This one is tiny, the part that you can see is only about 1/8 inch high. It's small size suggests Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, but those over-size humps don't seem right. Maybe it's a juvenile of our large Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides spiders? Whatever it is, it's a cute spider - I'm glad I found it again. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2019)
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Araneus bispinosus; A. montereyensis; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
