File.Name: j416c_j416-037.jpg
Notes: 46710289785 I believe these are new flowers of the Spreading Larkspur (Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum, Ranunculaceae). You can see some of the mostly undivided basal leaves at the base of the stems which are the mark of this species. The plants are growing in a shady place at the bottom of an embankment, just starting to get some afternoon sun. The flowers are beautiful and look deep blue when you see them, though they do an odd color shift when you try to photograph them, and end up looking more magenta. Another plant that does this is Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus, Plantaginaceae), see this photo. These larkspur have been flowering for a week or two, though this is my first photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2019)
ScientificName: Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Ranunculaceae; Penstemon heterophyllus; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Ranunculaceae; Penstemon heterophyllus; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: j416d_j413-069.jpg
Notes: 47625720651 Here are a mating pair of Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterflies on last year's dead stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). That's the male of the left and the female on the right. I watched as these butterflies chased each other and engaged - see this photo taken a few minutes earlier. After getting a few photos, I left them alone. Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2019)

File.Name: j417a_j417-014.jpg
Notes:
47632982261 This is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working new flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It's the first of these butterflies that I've managed to photograph this year, and the first open flowers as well. These are usually our most common butterfly, and one that is relatively easy to photograph. In time, I will have taken enough photos of these butterflies - but not yet! I found the first caterpillars very early this year on January 13, see this photo. I've been finding new caterpillars ever since then, but not in great numbers - see my [Previous] photo. I wonder how they will do this year. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2019)

Today was a gorgeous spring day with moderate breeze from the south and temps in the low 70s. I'll take a day like this anytime!

File.Name: j417b_j417-010.jpg
Notes: 46908696104 Here is a group of web-spinning caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly with their webbing on a shrub of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). I believe that they use their webbing as an aid to molting, see this photo, maybe as something to pull against? Several of the "caterpillars" inside the webbing are actually cast-off skins, and it may be that this is a communal molting station that different caterpillars share. The new generation of caterpillars later in summer will be even more dependent of their webs, see this photo. See my [Next] photo for the first of the adult butterflies that I've managed to photograph this year. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2019)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: j417c_j417-048.jpg
Notes: 47632852271 This is a another orb web of a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider under the eaves on our upstairs porch. You can see the spider in the center if you look close, with its distinctive hump pointing to the 3:00 position. These spiders decorate the stabilimentum of their web with the bodies of their prey - looks like winged ants in this case, we've had quite a few of them in our house lately. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2019)

File.Name: j417d_j417-004.jpg
Notes: 47632771911 Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey by a flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) that still has a ways to go before flowering. The spider could fit comfortably on a dime, but it's still bigger than the usual genus Mecaphesa spiders I've been finding lately. It does have sparse hairs on its abdomen and carapace. I'm not sure what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2019)

File.Name: j418a_j418-015.jpg
Notes:
47639944981 I'm pretty sure this is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the genus Tetragnatha of the family Tetragnathidae. The shape of these spiders is distinctive - long and narrow. They are said to be most common near water, and I found this on on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) about five feet from our creek that has finally stopped flowing. I don't think I've found one of these spiders before, and I wonder if it's significant that the creek has been actually flowing for months - something that hasn't happened for quite a few years. It's an attractive spider. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2019)

Today was another gorgeous spring day with temps approaching 80∞. There was an annoying breeze from the south in the morning, like a "fog wind" without the marine layer fog. It was still a very nice day.
CommonNames: Long-jawed Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j418b_j418-006.jpg
Notes: 32697366827 Here is the debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) insect. You can see some of the actual larva, but you can't really make out its impressive jaws. (See this photo.) This one is on a shiny new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Apparently these debris-carrying larvae cover themselves with bits of litter, perhaps to deter other predators - though I don't know what they find in woods that is so white. It's great camouflage. The next time you see a bit of lint moving along a leaf, look closer! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2019)

File.Name: j418c_j414-005.jpg
Notes: 46724151645 This is a slender Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). There are hundreds of different kinds of these flies, so I won't try to guess what this one is - though the pattern on its rear end looks pretty distinctive. Maybe it's genus Sphaerophoria? Whatever it is, HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2019)

File.Name: j418d_j411-061.jpg
Notes: 33762287078 Here is a tiny Tick (Ixodidae, Arachnida) waiting on the end of a new grass stem. Hah - it's waiting for you or me to walk by so it can fall away and attach itself! Our most common tick is the Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus), see this photo, but this one looks slightly different - for one thing, it doesn't have black legs. Maybe it's the Pacific Coast Tick (Dermacentor occidentalis)? It's tiny, about 1/8 inch long. *Sigh* I've already been ticked a couple of times this year - I still feel it on my arm. What, me worry? This is also a shot of the tick's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2019)

File.Name: j419a_j419-005.jpg
Notes: 33769832358 Here is another Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working new flowers of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). I've been seeing a fair number of these Tiger Swallowtails this year, but I still haven't been able to get a photo of the usually more common Pale Swallowtail butterfly. I'm not complaining, these swallowtails are beautiful - but it is curious. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2019) It was cooler today with temps in the low 70s, still very nice. That annoying breeze from the south was back. It feels like a "fog wind", and I expected the marine layer to spill over the mountains from the ocean side at any moment. I guess these are "eddy winds" wrapping around Point Conception and coming at us from over the ocean to the south. It was still a very nice day.

File.Name: j419b_j419-033.jpg
Notes: 47647224551 Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with a little beetle (Coleoptera) for prey in a cluster of flowers of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I'm sure we miss much more than we see in the masses of flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2019)

File.Name: j419c_j413-059.jpg
Notes: 46923134174 This is an impressive fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods. I have another photo of the same fly here. I'm sure it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), maybe genus Peleteria - compare this photo at BugGuide. It has a fringe around its head like an Elizabethan lace ruff. It seems that I also find similar flies in the woods in late summer and autumn, like this one from September. The larvae of these flies parasitize other insect larvae, so maybe it makes sense they would be out both early and late in the season when many insects are involved in reproducing? In fact, I saw a fly just like this one hanging out by a nearby colony of ground-nesting bees, see this photo from last year. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2019)

File.Name: j419d_j413-017.jpg
Notes: 47594250672 This is looking at the remarkable face of a Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family hiding in a shrub of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) near the creek - see this photo for (nearly) the whole fly. You can clearly see in my photo that these large flies have the terminal segment of their palps longer than the other segments combined - those dangly bits at the end of its head. We also have smaller Crane Flies in the same group of Tipulomorpha that differ in having the terminal segment of their palps short, compare this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2019)
CommonNames: Large Crane Fly; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Crane Flies
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Tipulomorpha
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Tipulomorpha

File.Name: j420a_j420-059.jpg
Notes:
32712594007 Here is another tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider in a flowering shrub of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I had to fight the shrub to get this close, and I never noticed the spider's web, but I can see that it's tending a line to its web with its screen-left foot, like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing. It's tiny, less than 1/4 inch long. This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. Given that in my spider the "abdomen dips between the two humps," then maybe it is Araneus montereyensis. It has rich colors like the tiny spider in this photo - with the addition of that bright line between the humps. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2019)

We started out in a cloud this morning, and we were under clouds for the rest of the day. I guess today was our first real "June Gloom" day under deep marine layer clouds. There was some breeze like there always is with marine layer, but it wasn't as bad as it could be. It never did clear.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus montereyensis
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus montereyensis

File.Name: j420b_j420-046.jpg
Notes: 32712464667 I found this sleek little Snakefly (family Raphidiidae, order Raphidioptera) insect waiting for prey on a new flower whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I believe it is genus Agulla with that "kite-shaped" head and female with that long ovipositor on the other end. Snakefly heads always reminds me of the aliens from the 1953 scifi movie "The War of the Worlds"! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2019)
ScientificName: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Agulla
associatedTaxa: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Agulla

File.Name: j420c_j420-033.jpg
Notes: 47654938321 Here is another Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) clinging to a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). This fly was smaller than some, less than an inch long. It's got the dangly long palps that characterize this family, see this photo for a closer view of a similar fly. My first thought was that this is a female because of its fat abdomen, but maybe it's a female because there's no sign of an ovipositor? (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2019)

File.Name: j420d_j420-049.jpg
Notes: 33777429358 This is our other Live Oak in full flower. It's probably Interior Live Oak (Quercus wislizeni) or maybe Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei), both of which seem odd to find in our mountains on the coast but here they are. These plants occur as both trees and shrubs, and they are fairly common - this one is a tree. One difference with Coast Live Oak that's visible in this photo is that the leaves are flat and not cupped like Q. agrifolia. They are also shiny and glabrous on the underside, and the catkins are airy and lighter colored. There are groups of catkins on the left and right of my photo. If you look in between, maybe you can spot the little Chaparral Camel Cricket hiding under a leaf with its butt sticking out! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2019)
CommonNames: Interior Live Oak; Shreve Oak; Chaparral Camel Cricket
ScientificName: Quercus wislizeni; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Q. agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Quercus wislizeni; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Q. agrifolia

File.Name: j421a_j421-022.jpg
Notes:
47663723081 This is a handsome Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched for a moment on new leaves of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). I'm sure this one is newly emerged - it's the first one I've seen this year, and it's so darn pretty! These butterflies are quite variable, compare this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2019)

Today started under clouds, but it cleared up early and was quite pleasant with temps in the mid-60s. There was little breeze in the morning, but it's been picking up as the day went on. Now there's a NWS Wind Advisory for Sundowner Winds on the coast tonight.

File.Name: j421b_j421-017.jpg
Notes: 46939440094 Here is a little "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaflet of perennial Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family that's in full flower. There are quite a few of these adults and nymphs on the lupine leaves, and they are doing some damage that's visible in my photo. I don't know what they are, possibly Plant Bugs in the family Miridae and maybe genus Irbisia. I've been seeing something similar on other plants as well. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2019)
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Plant Bugs
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Miridae; Irbisia
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Miridae; Irbisia

File.Name: j421c_j421-014.jpg
Notes: 47610530702 This photo shows the pupal husk of an emerging two-winged Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) coming out of its gall on native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I first noticed something similar last year in this photo. Then I did some research and found this on page 61 of Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) describing the ways of Gall Wasps (Cecidomyiidae): ". . . the mature pupae exit only about two-thirds of the way out, using the pressure of the last part of the passage to hold them firmly in place while they complete their transformation into adults." That pretty much perfectly describes what I see in my photo. I saw this same gall a few days ago, and the pupal husk was not there. I also have this photo of the galls just starting to develop last December. The shrubs aren't flowering yet, but they will be soon. I guess the galls are now ripe and the flies are emerging to be ready for the flowers to start the cycle again. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2019)
ScientificName: Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: j421d_j420-002.jpg
Notes: 47663286281 Here is a little wasp (Hymenoptera) on a new frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens, Polypodiaceae) with water drops from our drizzle yesterday morning. I call it a wasp, but I suppose it could also be a wasp-like Cuckoo Bee in the genus Nomada. These bees are cleptoparasites that lay their eggs on the pollen masses made by other bees. I'm really not sure how to tell them apart, but I can't recall any of our wasps that look quite like this. Whatever it is, it's quite beautiful with the drizzle drops. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2019)
CommonNames: wasp; Western Bracken Fern; Cuckoo Bee
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Nomada; cleptoparasites
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Nomada; cleptoparasites

File.Name: j422a_j422-034.jpg
Notes:
40706717323 This is a new flower of the uncommon Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family. There are only a few plants with flowers in this place, but they are hanging on after so many drought years. They are growing in a place where the sun rarely shines, and it was a struggle to get this photo in deep shade with a breeze. I don't like using flash, but taking lots of photos and bumping up the ISO and aperture helps. The flowers seem to be highly variable, compare this photo by Marlin Harms in San Luis Obispo county. This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - "rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere." Last year I didn't find the first flowers until May 5 (here), and the year before I found them on April 2 (here). This year seems right in the middle. I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower on my map, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.) (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2019)

We started out in a cloud today, but then it cleared out with modest breeze and temps in the mid-70s. The breeze did pick up as the day progressed - it seemed to be particularly annoying just when I was trying to photograph this flower! It's curious how often that happens.

File.Name: j422b_j422-018.jpg
Notes: 32730089247 Here are two shots of a little spider on a flower bud of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). I didn't recognize the spider when I first saw it with its legs spread out on the left, but I instantly knew it as an uncommon Tmarus spider when I saw it from the side. It's probably species Tmarus angulatus - a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that is less than 1/4 inch long not counting its long front legs. If you look close, you can see that it has the enlarged palps of a male. I usually find these interesting spiders with their long front legs together, see this photo and this photo. I've only found them a few times, but that might just be because they are so well camouflaged - at least on a neutral background like a tree branch. Their camouflage doesn't work so well on green leaves! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2019)
ScientificName: Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus; Thomisidae

File.Name: j422c_j422-009.jpg
Notes: 46756922585 Here is another Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) clinging to a grass plant, probably non-native Red Brome (Bromus madritensis ssp. rubens). Unlike the crane fly in this photo a few days ago, I have no doubt that this one is female - it's large with eggs and has a pointed ovipositor at the tail end. The fly has green eyes! I like the contrast between the smooth curves of the fly and the sharp edges of the grass. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2019)

File.Name: j422d_j421-009.jpg
Notes: 32729730477 The male pollen-bearing staminate catkins of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) are obvious and even sexy, see this photo from last spring. My photo today shows the female pistillate flowers of this tree which will swell to acorns in time. They even have three petals - a three-lobed petal-like involucre at least. They are minute, and you have to look close to find them. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2019)

File.Name: j423a_j423-023.jpg
Notes:
47682888341 This is an interesting Timema insect (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) on a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) today. I believe this is a male with asymmetric cerci or "claspers" on its tail. It's also quite small, but because these insects don't have wings, I'm not sure if it is immature or just that size. Hah - I actually found this one crawling on the leg of my tripod after making my way through some brush. I coaxed it onto this Chamise stem, and that's probably where it belongs. We have green Timemas and brown ones and green ones with stripes - and a few oddballs like this one. They are highly variable. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects which are endemic to California and neighboring states. I have a bit more info in my comments on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2019)

There was no morning fog today, and it was quite warm - in the low 80s. It suddenly feels like summer is just around the corner, but I don't think that impression will last. There was some breeze today, mostly from the south, calm in the morning becoming windy in the afternoon.
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: j423b_j423-026.jpg
Notes: 46766692085 Here are a couple of tiny beetles (Coleoptera) inside a new flower of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. I believe the beetles are tiny Carpet Beetles (Dermestidae, Coleoptera), probably genus Anthrenus. The larvae of these or similar beetles are notorious for cleaning up dead animal parts or that old butterfly collection you forgot about. The adults are rather pretty and visit flowers, I have another photo of a slightly different one here. I actually found the first flowers of this shrub in early October near Arroyo Burro, and got my
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Dermestidae; Coleoptera; Anthrenus
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Dermestidae; Coleoptera; Anthrenus

File.Name: j423c_j423-007.jpg
Notes: 33805170488 Here is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working new flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. See this photo for the top side of the wings, but the bottoms are beautiful too. This is still only the second of these handsome butterflies that I've managed to photograph this year. These are usually our most common butterfly, and one that is relatively easy to photograph. In time, I will have taken enough photos of them - but not yet! The flowers are interesting too. These are bisexual with both stamens and pistils, but some other shrubs are only pistillate. See my [Previous] photo for a comparison shot. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2019)

File.Name: j423d_j423-010.jpg
Notes: 33805064708 This is looking close at new flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family on two different shrubs. Do you see the difference? The flowers on the left are bisexual - they look staminate at first, but there are female pistils down under the stamens if you look close. The flowers on the right are pistillate only on the whole shrub. I was surprised when I noticed this, but sure enough the The Jepson Manual says that the flowers are either bisexual or pistillate. (That's the print edition; for some reason the Jepson eFlora doesn't mention it.) It's interesting how you can think you know a plant, and then find out you don't know it at all! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2019)
ScientificName: Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; The Jepson Manual
associatedTaxa: Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; The Jepson Manual

File.Name: j424a_j424-007.jpg
Notes:
32748956187 This is probably a Baja California Chorus Frog (Pseudacris hypochondriaca), but I can't really tell from this photo. We seem to be right on the line between this species and the Pacific Chorus Frog (P. sierra), so who knows. (There used to be just the Pacific Treefrog (Pseudacris regilla or Hyla regilla), but the species was split up - those darn herpetologists!) Like the Timema insect I showed yesterday, they come in both green and brown forms, and I believe they can slowly change color from one to the other - it looks like this one is doing just that. I found this one today hiding among the leaves of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) just a hop and a jump away from one of our two "water gardens". This garden is nothing fancy, just an old fiberglass bathtub filled with water and plants, but these tubs are important for the frogs and other critters that need a drink. Seven years of drought has taken a toll. This is the first frog I've actually seen this year, but I've been hearing them every night for weeks. They are chorus frogs, and they really make a racket! There are sound recordings at CaliforniaHerps. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2018)

There was no morning fog again today, but it was a few degrees cooler than yesterday - in the mid-70s. It was breezy all day, mostly from the south.
CommonNames: Baja California Chorus Frog;  Pacific Chorus Frog; Pacific Treefrog; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Pseudacris hypochondriaca; P. sierra; Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Pseudacris hypochondriaca; P. sierra; Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: j424b_j424-004.jpg
Notes: 46775984335 I found this mating pair of Vestal Tiger Moths (Spilosoma vestalis) on a succulent plant in the garden this morning. I think of Tiger Moths as being active in the fall, but this one only flies in springtime. They have a bright red patch on their belly and front legs, but it's not visible here - see this photo from a few years ago. I found these moths like this and got my photos. Then I went off for a few hours, and they were in the same position when I got back. Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2018)

File.Name: j424c_j420-048.jpg
Notes: 47691948291 Here is webbing on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) - but I'm pretty sure it's the webbing of a caterpillar and not a spider. If you look close, you can see an accumulation of frass (aka poop) inside the web that makes a tubular retreat. When I first saw it, the caterpillar was sticking out - but he snapped back inside when I got close. I do have another shot of the caterpillar that lives inside, see this photo from last year. Spiders aren't the only ones, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2018)

File.Name: j424d_j420-006.jpg
Notes: 47691871281 This is the orb web of a small Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider with drizzle drops on a wet morning a few days ago. The is the work of the same spider with the big humps that I showed in this photo and (probably) in this photo. The whole web is less than a foot across, strung between cactus plants in the garden. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2019)

File.Name: j425a_j425-007.jpg
Notes:
32757542237 Here is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly perched for a moment on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I figure all these butterflies are newly emerged, but look how tattered this one already is compared to the one in this photo. Butterflies have a hard life, though we don't always appreciate it with all the flowers and sunshine. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2019)

There was no morning fog again today, and it was in the low-70s. But it was breezy all day again, mostly from the south. I kept looking up at the skyline expecting to see marine layer clouds spilling over the mountains. There were no clouds, but it still felt like "fog winds".

File.Name: j425b_j425-020.jpg
Notes: 46784513655 Hah - I was looking for something to photograph today while sitting in my chair by the creek, and I started to take pictures of this tiny Syrphid Fly aka Hoverfly (Syrphidae, Diptera) in the air. It would hover in one place for a while and then zip away to another place nearby. I couldn't get close enough, but I kept taking photos anyway. That's our wellhead painted blue in the background, and it was sheer dumb luck to get this nice composition. This fly might actually be the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be the common species in central California, see this photo. I usually see them hovering in the air just a little too far away to photograph. We also have non-biting Deer Flies (Stonemyia, Tabanidae) that usually do their hovering a bit closer to me, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2019)

File.Name: j425c_j424-028.jpg
Notes: 40734178623 Here is a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging from its web. What balance and control it has holding onto its web with those tiny claws! These spiders normally make perfect orb webs that they decorate with the bodies of their prey, but this one is repairing its web. It's a good view of the spider's impressive spinnerets, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2019)

File.Name: j425d_j420-061.jpg
Notes: 47647418982 I spotted this little beetle snugguled into a new leaf bud of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) on a cloudy day with just its rear end sticking out. I'm pretty sure its a Buprestid Beetle aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera), probably a Spotted Flower Buprestid in the genus Acmaeodera. There are many different kinds, and this is the first one I've noticed this year. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2019)
CommonNames: beetle; Bigberry Manzanita; Buprestid Beetle; Metallic Wood-boring Beetle; Spotted Flower Buprestid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Acmaeodera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Acmaeodera

File.Name: j426a_j426-003.jpg
Notes:
47656273022 I like to think that these lizards are romantically involved. I found them this morning in the cactus patch of our garden. They are Blue-belly Lizards aka Coast Range Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii) - or more properly Coast Range Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii). I'm sure that's the darker male in the background with blue mating colors that you can see on its belly if you look in the shadows. And I guess that's a lighter female in front standing by her man. She has blue colors too but not so intense. They disappeared into the cactus patch a moment later. I suspect these are a pair, but I've been wrong before, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2019)

It was clear but cooler today, only in the 60s. It was breezy again from the south, and there was a marine layer that didn't come over the mountains but came close. We actually have a 20% chance of showers on Sunday night.
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizards; Coast Range Fence Lizards; Coast Range Fence Lizards
ScientificName: Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii; Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii
associatedTaxa: Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii; Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii

File.Name: j426b_j426-020.jpg
Notes:
47656155722 Here are the first new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family fully open in the mountains today. This deciduous shrub has everything - gorgeous white flowers like these, edible nuts (here), and pretty autumn leaves (here). The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. They are pretty common here on San Marcos Pass - both in woodland and chaparral. They are stunning flowers, worth hunting for. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2019)

The shrubs are unusual, a singleton in their family here in California. The specimen map at the Jepson eFlora shows them all around the central valley in California, but I suspect their distribution is highly localized. They are common here on the pass, but I don't think I've seen them anywhere else in Santa Barbara County. There is just one record from San Luis Obispo county to the north and none from Ventura county to the south.

File.Name: j426c_j425-030.jpg
Notes: 32765760407 Here are two shots of a tiny blue (or "blue dun") fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It really is tiny, only about 545 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.1 mm - substantially less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I don't know what kind of fly it is, and I would appreciate any help with identification. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2019)

File.Name: j426d_j422-003.jpg
Notes: 32765712277 This isn't "just a fly", but I'm not sure what kind of fly (Diptera) it is. My first guess based on its color and the way it holds its wings would be a Root-Maggot Fly in the family Anthomyiidae. Any help with identification will be appreciated. I found it in a non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) shrub on the edge of our garden. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2019)

File.Name: j427a_j427-033.jpg
Notes:
47664979292 Here is a handsome Blue (Polyommatinae) butterfly perched for a moment on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I never did get a look at the bottom of its wings before it took off, but I'm guessing this one is a male Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly with that narrow black margin around both wings. The Pacific Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly is similar above, but I believe the dark margin of that one doesn't go all the way around both wings, compare this photo. Whatever it is, this is a handsome butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2019)

We were in and out of a cloud today as marine layer fog spilled over the mountains - several times. With the fog comes the "fog winds" - those chaotic winds that come with the marine layer. It was quite cool, only in the mid-50s. There was some sunshine and moments when the breeze was not so bad, that's when I got my photos today. There's now a 30% chance of showers Sunday night into Monday.
CommonNames: Blue; Black Sage; Silvery Blue; Pacific Azure
ScientificName: Polyommatinae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Celastrina echo
associatedTaxa: Polyommatinae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Celastrina echo

File.Name: j427b_j427-051.jpg
Notes: 40751448353 This is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly perched on last year's dead stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) down by the creek. This is the first of any kind of skipper that I've seen this year. They seem to have two separate broods, the first one now and again in summer. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2019)

File.Name: j427c_j427-019.jpg
Notes: 40751326113 Here is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) visiting a flower of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It seems to be grasping the pistil of the flower and bringing it to its proboscis, but I'm not sure what it's after - pollen or nectar on the stigma? This fly is quite striking with black and white stripes on its abdomen. It might be the species Scaeva pyrastri, see this photo for another shot and some discussion in the comments. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2019)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Scaeva pyrastri
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Scaeva pyrastri

File.Name: j427d_j427-039.jpg
Notes: 46801315505 This little Bumble Bee (Bombus) is working new flowers of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It was having trouble keeping its pep up as we were in and out of the clouds today. A few minutes after I got this photo, the bee was crawling away on the ground as the temperature suddenly dropped. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2019)

File.Name: j428a_j428-038.jpg
Notes:
47728402481 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) dealing with the cold and drizzle today on a leaf of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum, Anacardiaceae). It was cold enough today that it didn't want to fly away and let me get close. It might be another of the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it seems more brilliant than the other ones I've photographed, like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2019)

We were in a marine-layer cloud almost all day with temps in he 50s and almost 1/10 inch of drizzle. I wouldn't mind the fog, but the fog winds were blowing, and the combination of low-light and breeze makes getting my kind of photos difficult. On the other hand, it was a good day to find flies that were too chilled to fly away. There's nothing to do but bump up the ISO a notch and keep shooting!
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae; Syrphus opinator
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae; Syrphus opinator

File.Name: j428b_j428-044.jpg
Notes: 47728097491 What a strange-looking fly (Diptera) this is! It's perched on a leaf of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum, Anacardiaceae) with some drops from our drizzle today. My first thought was that it's some kind of Bee Fly (Bombyliidae), though the oversize antennae seem wrong. Now I think it might be some sort of Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, possibly genus Nebritus which also has oversize antennae. I'm still not sure about this, as my fly seems hairier that the ones shown at BugGuide - especially on the base of the antennae. I would appreciate any help with identification. I have another nice photo of this fly, but I'll save it the next Fly Day Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae; Bombyliidae; Therevidae; Nebritus
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae; Bombyliidae; Therevidae; Nebritus

File.Name: j428c_j428-041.jpg
Notes: 46811411515 I'm pretty sure this is a Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) on a new leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) - with drizzle drops. It was a gloomy day, and I thought this fly wasn't going to move much, but it took off right after I got my one (and only) photo. These handsome flies are not common, but I find them now and then in the woods. I don't know much more about them. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2019)

File.Name: j428d_j428-029.jpg
Notes: 33850780608 I found this little Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae, Hymenoptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) - with a problem. It had a rather large water drop stuck between its wings from our drizzle today, and it was dancing around trying to shake it off. It finally managed to knock off most of the drop, but its wings were still stuck together. It finally got away from me, and I never did see how it finally solved its problem. These aren't the best photos because the wasp was moving and it was gloomy and breezy today as well as drizzly - but they do show the wasp's predicament. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2019)

File.Name: j429a_j429-001.jpg
Notes:
33860764588 This is another Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) on a new leaf of California Figwort (Scrophularia californica, Scrophulariaceae). It was a cold and drizzly again today, and this fly didn't move much even when I bumped the stem several times trying to get close. I like their rear ends - like those collapsible cups they used to sell for backpacking with nested cups that open up. I saw several of these flies today out in the cold and drizzle, just hanging onto a leaf out in the open. These handsome flies are not really common, but I find them now and then in the woods. I don't know much more about them. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2019)

We got about 2/10 inch of rain last night and today - even some thunder and lightning last night! That's not a lot of rain, but it's enough to change the look and feel of the woods. I had something to do in town this morning, but I got my walk in the woods in the afternoon. It was still dark and drizzly, but there was only a little breeze. "I don't mind the weather so the wind don't blow" - as the old song goes. It's a good time to look for flies and other insects grounded on the leaves.
ScientificName: Rhagio; Rhagionidae; Diptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Rhagio; Rhagionidae; Diptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: j429b_j429-005.jpg
Notes: 47737850021 Here is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) grounded by inclement weather on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It might be the same kind of fly I showed yesterday in this photo - it doesn't seem as brilliant, but maybe that's just the light? Both flies might be the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. Some flies (at least) seem to deal with the cold and drizzle by just hanging onto a leaf out in the open. They are worth looking for on crummy days like today. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2019)

File.Name: j429c_j429-002.jpg
Notes: 46948379594 I found this odd Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a leaf of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum, Anacardiaceae) today. Maybe its an Achilid Planthopper in the family Achilidae, compare this photo of another one of the same kind. It was a crummy day for new photos, and I was happy to get this one. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2019)

File.Name: j429d_j427-057.jpg
Notes: 47684505062 I found this tiny Tick (Ixodidae, Arachnida) on the dashboard of my truck, and I moved it outside for a photo. At first I was confused about what it is, but now I believe this is a male Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus). The female ticks look different with a shield-like structure on their back, see this photo and this photo. According to Wikipedia, the unfed females are also larger at 3.18 mm as opposed to the males at 2.54 mm. I got my photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can measure it by counting pixels, the tick is about 610 pixels across which comes to 2.39 mm, not quite 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) That's pretty close to the size given for a male. The adult male ticks do not become engorged. The adult females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't need this. These ticks are the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us - see this photo! Sound fantastic, but apparently it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2019)

File.Name: j430a_j430-009.jpg
Notes:
47692976982 Here is one of the first new flowers of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus, Liliaceae) in the woodland - with drops from the drizzle we've been having for the last few days. They are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily. They are a "tender trap" for pollinators - easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out past those hairs without carrying a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. They are very elegant, one of my favorite spring flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2019)

We've had a bit more than 1/4 inch of drizzle in the last few days. That's not a lot of rain, but the slow drizzle has been enough to change the look and feel of the woods. It's been chilly and drizzly without much breeze, but it's still a challenge to get my close photos because of the low light and the effect of the breeze is magnified just like the things I try to photograph. We almost had a few moments of sun this afternoon, then the clouds came back in. Tomorrow might bring the sun back for good.

File.Name: j430b_j430-013.jpg
Notes: 46828964875 This is looking close at soggy flowers of (I believe) Spreading Larkspur (Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum, Ranunculaceae). They are soggy from the drizzle we've been having for the last few days. See this photo for the whole plants from a couple of weeks ago. The individual flowers have a nectar-secreting "spur" and make me think of deep-blue witches' hats. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2019)

File.Name: j430c_j430-034.jpg
Notes: 46956131064 I found this little wasp (Hymenoptera) today by a flower bud of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). I guess it's another Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae) like the wasp in my [Previous] photo, but I'm not sure. It has interesting white patches on its antennae, its thorax between the wings, and the tip of its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2019)

File.Name: j430d_j430-028.jpg
Notes: 47692711792 I found this little Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae, Hymenoptera) on the end of a new stem of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It looks like the same kind of wasp I found struggling with a water drop a couple of days ago, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2019)

File.Name: j501a_j501-006.jpg
Notes:
32810361447 This is a little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a cabbage-like flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). The fly is female. I can clearly see her ovipositor in other photos, and I suspect she's about to oviposit on the flower, see this photo and this photo for other similar flies. One possibility for ID is the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), see BugGuide. I wonder if these flies have something to do with the galled flowers I sometimes find on this plant, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2019)

We did finally get some sun today after a cloudy morning, but with the sun came more wind. Low light and slight breeze, or normal light and wind - both pose the same photo problems! Tomorrow is expected to be sunny for a change after a few gloomy days.
CommonNames: Tephritid Fruit Fly; Canyon Sunflower; Sunflower Seed Maggot

File.Name: j501b_j501-026.jpg
Notes: 40787196643 Here is a mating swarm of winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) emerging through cracks on the edge of the chipseal road in our canyon. This isn't a particularly large swarm, compare this photo and this photo. Rain yesterday, termites today - I guess this is how they synchronize their swarms with other termite colonies so that they'll all emerge at the same time, thereby increasing the chance of mating across swarms to form new colonies. Maybe they are Subterranean Termites (family Rhinotermitidae)? I don't believe these damage houses, at least I hope not. I seem to find them in both spring and autumn. I went through my notes, and I have about equal records from October-January and March-May. They seem to have these two distinct broods. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2019)

File.Name: j501c_j501-037.jpg
Notes: 46837103835 I happened to notice this small but well-formed spider web in a shrub of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) today. The web is only about two inches across. It was almost invisible, but I took the photo just to see it it would come out, and it did. I'm sure it's the work of a tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider that's probably visible on the leaf to the left. (I'll make a note.) The web is basically rectangular which seems unusual. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2019)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Orb Web Weaver
ScientificName: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Araneus; Araneidae
associatedTaxa: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Araneus; Araneidae

File.Name: j501d_j427-014.jpg
Notes: 33876403978 This is the retreat of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in a shrub of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). The spider is probably in there. These spiders generally make a composite web with a proper orb web and a little retreat off to the side held up by cobweb-type webbing, see this photo. I didn't notice the orb web this time, and maybe it was a casualty of our recent weather, but the spider's retreat came through just fine. This spider has decorated its house with the bodies of its prey, pirate fashion. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2019)
CommonNames: Labyrinth Spider; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Metepeira; Araneidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Metepeira; Araneidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: j502a_j502-050.jpg
Notes:
47708664002 I found this handsome Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Wild Cucumber aka California Manroot (Marah fabaceus, Cucurbitaceae). There are lots of different kinds, maybe this one is genus Chinavia or Thyanta? (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2019)

It was sunny today for the first time in a few days, but it was also fairly breezy from the south. I was out along West Camino Cielo road, and I could see a layer of haze climbing up into the mountains from the ocean side. It wasn't a proper marine layer, but I reckon it brought some "fog wind" along with it. I believe these are really "eddy winds" wrapping around Point Conception and coming at us from over the ocean to the south. Other than the breeze, it was quite pleasant with high temps near 70∞.
CommonNames: Green Stink Bug; Wild Cucumber; California Manroot
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae; Chinavia; Thyanta
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae; Chinavia; Thyanta

File.Name: j502b_j502-024.jpg
Notes: 33884162078 Here are a mating pair of tiny beetles (Coleoptera) inside a new flower of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. One group of three stamens has started to drop and dusted the top beetle with pollen. The handsome flowers have been going for at least a month now, see this photo from April 1. I also found mating Lady Beetles today, but I'll save them for next Wednesday aka hump day. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2019)
CommonNames: beetles; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: j502c_j501-019.jpg
Notes: 33884114858 This is the rear end of a fly (Diptera) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) down by the creek. It's "just a fly" - but its bristly butt is perfect for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2019)

File.Name: j502d_j427-044.jpg
Notes: 40794683893 Here is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a narrow leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). Its left-side antenna is just out of sight, it's not missing. This nymph is not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last summer, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. It's probably the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum), see this photo. This photo shows the katydid's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2019)
CommonNames: Katydid; Black Sage; California Angle-winged Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Microcentrum californicum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: j503a_j503-034.jpg
Notes:
47715616062 Here is a humble Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly working a new flower of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) in the woods today. The plants are just starting to flower in this place near the native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in my [Previous] photo. The butterfly visited the sage flowers after I got too close to it on the Horkelia, but it always came back to these flowers. This appears to be the darker-colored spring form of the butterfly, rather than the lighter-colored summer form shown in this photo. These butterflies have a weak flight like a moth, but that doesn't mean they are easy to approach! I don't usually find them visiting flowers, so I was happy to get this shot. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2019)

Today started nice, but then marine-layer clouds started to spill over the mountains from the ocean-side and the "fog winds" kicked in. We were on the edge of it. It never actually got foggy here, but it was windy. The clouds finally backed off, the winds calmed down, and it was quite pleasant.
CommonNames: Common Ringlet; Wedge-leaved Horkelia; Black Sage
ScientificName: Coenonympha tullia; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Coenonympha tullia; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae

File.Name: j503b_j503-016.jpg
Notes: 47715521332 This looking down on another Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly on native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The butterfly is actually tilted towards the sun like a living solar collector, trying to catch some rays on a chilly morning after the fog moved in. I've seen these butterflies in this position before, see this photo. If you compare the two shots, you can also see how variable they are with their hindwing spots. I've also captured the upper wings this year of both male and female butterflies. It's curious that all of these photos were taken on Black Sage. I guess they like this shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2019)

File.Name: j503c_j503-012.jpg
Notes: 32824422567 This little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) seems to be ovipositing - laying eggs - on a new flower head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) the the canyon. If you look close, you can even see what might be an egg in about the right place, I'll make a note. The fly was visiting lots of the flower heads that don't have open flowers yet, and I guess she was scattering her eggs around. This fly has a golden thorax like the ones in this photo and this photo taken in the last few days, but she is much smaller. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2019)

File.Name: j503d_j428-049.jpg
Notes: 47715399512 What a strange-looking fly (Diptera) this is! It's perched on a dead stem of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) with some drops from our drizzle when I got the shot a few days ago. My first thought was that it's some kind of furry Bee Fly (Bombyliidae), though the oversize antennae seem wrong. I have another photo of the same fly here, and that shot makes me think it's some sort of Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, possibly genus Nebritus which also has oversize antennae. I'm still not sure about this, as my fly seems hairier that the ones shown at BugGuide - especially on the base of the antennae. I would appreciate any help with identification. It's an odd one, good for HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Bombyliidae; Therevidae; Nebritus
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Bombyliidae; Therevidae; Nebritus

File.Name: j504a_j504-015.jpg
Notes:
46986605334 Here is a flawless California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly perched for a moment on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's the first one I've managed to photograph this year, and I'm sure it's newly emerged with that bright blue on its forewings. They will lose that blue patch with age, and it's an iridescence that only appears when the light is just so. As far as I know, these butterflies never feed, but they do take moisture. I guess they emerge with full bellies and fly until they're empty. I usually only manage to photograph them on the ground when they are "puddling" - taking moisture - so I was happy to get this photo up in a tree. These oaks are also their host plant, so it's fitting to show them together. By chance I also spotted my first Lorquin's Admiral butterfly today in the nearby willow trees, see this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2019)

The marine-layer clouds must have been right at our level today as they would spill over the mountains and then retreat - again and again. With the fog comes the "fog winds" - even when we're only on the edge of it. Today was cooler than yesterday, maybe a sign of the little storm that's approaching tonight and will bring a chance for a shower over the next two days.

File.Name: j504b_j504-020.jpg
Notes: 46986549944 I believe this is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera), but its colors are anything but common. Our usual Sawflies are dark like the one in this photo, but this one is colored more like we expect a wasp to be. This is one those wasps that have no sign of a "wasp waist". It's on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It appears to be struggling with that spider webbing along the edge of the leaf, but it did finally get away. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2019)

File.Name: j504c_j504-004.jpg
Notes: 46859244275 This is a particularly handsome Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging in what I call the inner circle of its web. These spiders always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2019)

File.Name: j504d_j504-027.jpg
Notes: 46859035235 Here are two native plants in the Rose family (Rosaceae) that have just started flowering this week - and that have something in common besides a family resemblance. On the left is a flower of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata), and on the right is a flower of Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa aka Drymocallis glandulosa). What they have in common is that both have interesting stamens. The flowers on the left have flat stamen filaments that remind me of those wide matches that some restaurants used to give out when I was a kid, and the flowers on the right have "Lolita"-type stamens that look like little lollipops. The Horkelia is also the flower that the Common Ringlet butterfly is visiting in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2019)
ScientificName: Rosaceae; Horkelia cuneata; Potentilla glandulosa; Drymocallis glandulosa
associatedTaxa: Rosaceae; Horkelia cuneata; Potentilla glandulosa; Drymocallis glandulosa

File.Name: j505a_j505-025.jpg
Notes:
32840867467 Here is another handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). I believe it is genus Platypedia. I've found two other cicadas this spring that appear to be the same kind, see this photo and this photo. All of these have green colors that suggests they are teneral - recently emerged from the final molt from nymph to adult - but maybe that's just their color? I don't believe these early cicadas sing at all, they might just make the clicking noise that I've been hearing up in the oaks - see this page for sound samples. This was a good find on a gloomy day. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2019)

The marine layer spilled over the mountains again this morning, and this time it stayed all day. It may be that the marine-layer clouds morphed into real clouds, as we have a slight chance more rain tonight and tomorrow, and we did actually get a few drops at one point today. *Sigh* With the marine layer comes the "fog winds", and we had enough of a breeze to keep things moving all day. There's nothing to do but bump up the ISO a notch and take lots of photos to get a few keepers!
ScientificName: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Platypedia; teneral
associatedTaxa: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Platypedia; teneral

File.Name: j505b_j505-005.jpg
Notes: 40818081113 This is the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly starting to pupate on a dead twig of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I'm sure that I'll find a chrysalis in this place tomorrow, like this one from last year. I'll make a point of checking on it. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2019)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; chrysalis

File.Name: j505c_j505-013.jpg
Notes: 33907246888 This is another tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It has a proper orb web nearby, and it's tending a line to it with its screen-right foot, like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing. It's really tiny, about 660 pixels across which comes to 2.6 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. Given that in my spider the "abdomen dips between the two humps," then maybe it is Araneus montereyensis. These tiny spiders seem to be highly variable, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2019)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Araneidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus montereyensis
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus montereyensis

File.Name: j505d_j504-017.jpg
Notes: 33907197628 Here is another shot of the flawless California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly I found yesterday on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), see my [Previous] photo. As I was getting my photos, it suddenly got low - as though it were a limbo butterfly! Maybe it was trying to catch some rays on a cool day? Whatever its reason, it was gone a moment later. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2019)

File.Name: j506a_j506-022.jpg
Notes:
33915825608 Here is a very pale Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched in a shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) - with a ripening berry. The butterfly was really interested in the colony of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) nearby, and it was hoping I would leave. Compare this photo of a fresh specimen from a couple of weeks ago. I'm not sure if today's butterfly is just prematurely worn or a distinct color variant. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2019)

We did get a few sprinkles today but not enough rain to measure. It was cloudy in the morning, then clear before noon, then cloudy again in the afternoon. The wind mostly held off until later in the day, so it was a nice morning.
CommonNames: Gabb's Checkerspot; Eastwood Manzanita; Wedge-leaved Horkelia
ScientificName: Chlosyne gabbii; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae

File.Name: j506b_j506-012.jpg
Notes: 46876298455 I found this fantastically colorful "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. I don't know what kind of moth it will become, but I wonder if that moth will be as colorful as the caterpillar it comes from. Today happens to be Monday, so HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2019)

File.Name: j506c_j506-026.jpg
Notes: 47740324502 This is the nymph of a Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a bristly stem of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). The little hopper has an embryonic quality, and I suppose that's appropriate for a nymph. It seems larger than most leafhoppers, and maybe it will become the hopper in this photo when it grows up? (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2019)
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: j506d_j506-005.jpg
Notes: 47792552361 Here is another photo of almost certainly the same Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) that I showed yesterday. I went back to the same plant of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) this morning and found it again on the same leaf, though it was turned around. I believe it is genus Platypedia. I got this photo as the sun was breaking through the clouds today, but I think I prefer the clarity of my previous photo taken on a gloomy day - see that photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2019)

File.Name: j507a_j507-006.jpg
Notes:
47800406841 I found another little wasp (Hymenoptera) today on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). It was a chilly morning under dark clouds, and the wasp didn't want to leave. I guess it's another Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae), compare this photo of a similar wasp on the same kind of plant a couple of days ago. I wonder if there's something yummy (for wasp larvae) in the new flower buds that are just starting to open? (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2019)

We were mostly in the clouds today, and when I drove into the Santa Ynez Valley on an errand, I could see why. It was mostly sunny in the valley, and a cloud bank was spilling over the mountains from the ocean-side. There was a breeze today but not as bad as the usual fog winds. Tomorrow might be nice, then we have a chance for another small rain later in the week.

File.Name: j507b_j507-007.jpg
Notes: 47748105672 Here are two photos of a little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a cactus plant in the garden. If you look close, you can see that the spider is carrying a passenger - that orange girdle in the space between abdomen and cephalothorax. (I'll make a note.) I believe it's the larva of a mantidfly in the family Mantispidae of the Neuroptera, compare this photo at BugGuide. I've found something similar at home and in Oregon. I have seen an adult mantidfly at home, so I know they're around but not common - see this photo. I believe the spider is a male of the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus, see this photo of the female. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2019)
ScientificName: Salticidae; Mantispidae; Neuroptera; Thiodina hespera; Colonus hesperus
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Mantispidae; Neuroptera; Thiodina hespera; Colonus hesperus

File.Name: j507c_j507-026.jpg
Notes: 46884014825 This is a another small Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider on a leaf of native Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata, Caprifoliaceae) that's just starting to flower. I believe the spider is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira). These spiders generally make a composite web with a proper orb web and a little retreat off to the side held up by cobweb-type webbing, see this photo. This spider seems to have its shelter on a leaf instead, and it doesn't look very protective. It's orb web is off to the right. The spider seems to have a hint of a cat face on it's abdomen. Maybe it's a male with enlarged palps? (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 8 April 2019)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Chaparral Honeysuckle; Labyrinth Spider
ScientificName: Araneidae; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Metepeira
