File.Name: j507d_j502-038.jpg
Notes:
47010994564 One of these ladybugs is not behaving like a lady!

This is a mating pair of Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on a leaf of native native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. That's a new flower bud in the background. These plants are just starting to flower, see this photo. The beetles can have any number of spots, or no spots at all, but they always have two white diagonal lines that converge behind the head. Here they are making more ladybugs. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2019)
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae

File.Name: j508a_j508-029.jpg
Notes:
47807789591 Here is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly standing by its host plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. The butterfly has been ovipositing - laying eggs - on these plants, moving from plant to plant and quickly leaving her egg before moving on to the next plant. I did get another photo of the butterfly actually ovipositing, but this is the better photo. They are said to use plants in the snapdragon family (Scrophulariaceae) and the plantain family (Plantaginaceae), and the plant in this photo was formerly considered a "scroph". That's interesting because Penstemon plants that used to be considered scrophs are now considered to be in the plaintain family! I've seen them on this plant before, see this photo and this photo. This is the first photo I've managed to get of a Buckeye butterfly this year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2019)

We've been in a cloud for most of the day, though the sun did come out for an hour or two in the afternoon. There was one of those annoying breezes all morning that keeps everything in motion and makes getting new photos difficult. Despite the problems, it was a good day for butterflies.
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Penstemon
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Penstemon

File.Name: j508b_j508-018.jpg
Notes: 47755688222 This is another Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on the edge of the chipseal road in our canyon. I haven't been seeing any more migrating butterflies, and I suspect from its pristine appearance that this one is newly emerged. These butterflies do pause to make new butterflies while they are migrating, see this photo of one ovipositing - laying eggs - a month ago when the migration was still happening. I got this photo shortly after the sun finally broke through this afternoon, and the butterfly seemed a little clumsy, maybe from the chill that was still in the air. It did manage to fly off after I got my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2019)

File.Name: j508c_j508-019.jpg
Notes: 47807689841 Here is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on the chipseal road in our canyon. I got this photo shortly after the sun finally broke through this afternoon, and the butterfly seemed a little clumsy, maybe from the chill that was in the air. I'm still not seeing a lot of these butterflies, and I'm still finding caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2019)

File.Name: j508d_j503-038.jpg
Notes: 47755595052 This is spider webbing on new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, but you can see a bit of it under the flat leaf on the left. I'll make a note. The spider is tiny, probably less than 1/10 inch. I'll keep trying to get a decent photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2019)

File.Name: j509a_j509-018.jpg
Notes:
46898523605 Here are two shots of a brand-new chrysalis of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a dead twig of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I found a caterpillar curled up on this branch a few days ago, see this photo. I thought I'd find the chrysalis the next day, but I've been checking everyday since including yesterday, and it didn't change until last night or this morning. You can see the outlines of the butterfly's future antennae and proboscis on the pupal shell. It's beautiful, and something magical is starting to happen inside. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2019)

It was gloomy all day, with one of those annoying breezes that keeps everything in motion and makes getting new photos difficult. The was some light drizzle. There's a chance of real rain through Sunday night with showers becoming likely on Saturday. I like the comment in the NWS Forecast Discussion yesterday that it's been "oddly troffier than normal month of May" - "troffier" referring to the small low-pressure troughs that we've been having lately.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: chrysalis; Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: chrysalis; Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: j509b_j509-002.jpg
Notes: 46898429585 This is the tiny nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). My first thought was that it's an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) because of its long proboscis, but its shape seems wrong. I don't know what it is, but it's rather pretty - I wonder what it will look like as an adult. I got this photo this morning when it was dark and gloomy with a slight breeze and a few sprinkles, and it came out better than expected. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2019)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Reduviidae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Reduviidae

File.Name: j509c_j505-022.jpg
Notes: 40848301093 Here is another Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) sprawled across a new leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I believe this one is a male, compare the pointed rear end of the female in this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2019)
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: j509d_j505-009.jpg
Notes: 40848205493 I guess this is the rear end of a cricket tucked in between two oak leaves - some sort of Long-horned Orthopteran (Ensifera, Orthoptera) anyway. I know it's not one of the Chaparral Camel Crickets (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) that like to hide in a similar way. I've seen lots of those crickets' behinds, and they look quite different from this one, see this photo for example. I don't know whose rear end this is, but it's a spectacular "butt shot" - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2019)
CommonNames: cricket; Long-horned Orthopteran; Chaparral Camel Crickets

File.Name: j510a_j510-011.jpg
Notes:
47032130674 I found this little wasp (Hymenoptera) today on a leaf of California Figwort (Scrophularia californica, Scrophulariaceae). It was a chilly day under dark clouds with thunder and some drizzle, and the wasp didn't want to leave. I guess it's another Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae). We seem to have several different kinds active in the woods right now, compare this photo, this photo, this photo, and this photo - all taken within the last two weeks and all different. These are all "parasitic wasps" - parasitoids really - that lay their eggs in the bodies of other insects for the larvae to consume - like the movie Alien. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2019)

I had something to do in town this morning, so I only got out for a short time with my camera. There was thunder but not much rain. We ended up with less than 1/10 inch, but I hope that's not the end of it. It actually cleared up for a spell this afternoon, but then clouds started moving in again, and we still have a 50% chance of rain on Saturday. Hah, we have another chance of rain next Thursday or Friday.
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Ichneumonidae; Alien
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Ichneumonidae; Alien

File.Name: j510b_j510-004.jpg
Notes: 46905359835 This is another Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) on a leaf of California Figwort (Scrophularia californica, Scrophulariaceae). It was a cold and drizzly again today, and this fly wasn't inclined to move. I saw several of them today out in the cold and drizzle, just hanging onto a leaf out in the open. It's interesting that they seem to prefer certain kinds of leaf - this California Figwort and Canyon Sunflower seem to be favorites. These handsome flies are not really common, but I find them now and then in the woods. I don't know much more about them. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2019)
ScientificName: Rhagio; Rhagionidae; Diptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Rhagio; Rhagionidae; Diptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: j510c_j509-011.jpg
Notes: 47769400182 I found this odd little fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) yesterday. It was a chilly day with some drizzle, and the fly was not inclined to move, so I managed to get close. My first thought was that it is some sort of Long-legged Fly (Dolichopodidae) because it does have long legs and the way the top of its head dips between the eyes, but nothing matches at BugGuide. I can't identify this fly, and I would appreciate any help with identification. It's another odd one, good for HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2019)

File.Name: j510d_j509-009.jpg
Notes: 47769326612 Here is a little caterpillar on a deformed leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) which has been invaded by a rust fungus (Pucciniales, Basidiomycotina). I don't know what kind of caterpillar it is, but it sure seems to be eating the orange fungus spores. I assume it's some sort of moth. This rust fungus sure looks like photos of Gymnoconia nitens or Arthuriomyces peckianus. The plants completely change when they are infected - the leaflets are thin and widely spaced, and the stems have no thorns, see this photo. I find this every year on a few plants in the canyon - maybe the same plants? It doesn't seem to be getting worse over the years. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2019)

File.Name: j511a_j511-047.jpg
Notes:
40862416783 This isn't the "gold bug" from Edgar Allan Poe's story, but it will do until the real thing comes along! I believe it's a Buprestid Beetle aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera). It's probably a Spotted Flower Buprestid in the genus Acmaeodera, and just maybe species Acmaeodera hepburnii. There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. This one is perched on an old stem of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2019)

The NWS forecast called for a 50% chance of rain today, but it was mostly sunny all morning. I figured that if rain was heads, then it was coming up tails. But then in the middle of the afternoon dark clouds built up, we had lots of thunder, I unplugged my computer, and it rained hard with big drops . . . for about ten minutes! There was just a few hundredths of an inch of rain, but it was a nice moment. Our next chance of rain comes on Thursday as our "troffy" month of May continues.
CommonNames: Buprestid Beetle; Metallic Wood-boring Beetle; Spotted Flower Buprestid; Bird's Beak
ScientificName: Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Acmaeodera; Acmaeodera hepburnii; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae
associatedTaxa: Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Acmaeodera; Acmaeodera hepburnii; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: j511b_j511-034.jpg
Notes: 47776739212 I found this remarkable fly (Diptera) working new flowers of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) in the woods today. After consulting BugGuide, I'm pretty sure it's species Dioprosopa clavata, a Syrphid Fly in the family Syrphidae. If you know what to look for, you can its halteres and the "spurious veins" on its wings. It's remarkable how much it looks like a wasp with that petiole-like abdomen. See this photo for another fly that looks like a wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Dioprosopa clavata; Syrphidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Dioprosopa clavata; Syrphidae

File.Name: j511c_j511-006.jpg
Notes: 40862154143 Here is a tiny spider in the throat of a new flower of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica, Scrophulariaceae). The flower is only about 5 mm or 3/16 inch high, which gives an idea of scale. At first I though the spider was an ant, but count the legs and body parts. (It may actually be an ant-mimic?) I've been trying to get a photo of the little wild bees that are working the flowers, but I haven't got any photo good enough to show yet. Actually, I'm pretty happy just to get an acceptable photo of the flower. These might be the hardest flowers in the woods to photograph, with widely spaced flowers on long stems that seem to sway in the slightest breeze. Along with the usual stamens and pistil, they have a sterile "staminode" - a special stamen that does not contain pollen - that's visible at the top of the flower tube. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2019)
CommonNames: spider; California Figwort; California Bee Plant

File.Name: j511d_j510-033.jpg
Notes: 32884775277 This is a little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a cactus plant in the garden which I believe is a male of the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus, see this photo of the female. But the spider isn't the star of this photo, only the host. If you look close, you can see that the spider is carrying a passenger - that orange girdle in the space between abdomen and cephalothorax. (I'll make a note.) I believe it's the larva of a mantidfly in the family Mantispidae of the Neuroptera. I have seen an adult mantidfly at home, so I know they're around but not common - see this photo. I have another shot of probably the same spider showing the passenger in this photo taken a few days ago. Looking at the original photo, I think I can see three pairs of legs and antennae. It looks pretty gruesome, and I wonder if the spider is suffering. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2019)
ScientificName: Salticidae; Thiodina hespera; Colonus hesperus; Mantispidae; Neuroptera
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Thiodina hespera; Colonus hesperus; Mantispidae; Neuroptera

File.Name: j512a_j512-021.jpg
Notes:
47785513782 Here is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly kissing my hand! I'm sure its actually "puddling" - getting moisture and minerals. This butterfly was definitely attracted to me - landing on my shirt, my tripod, my camera lens, and finally on me. I held my left hand out with the butterfly, and got the photo with my 105 mm macro lens on my tripod. It's a bit of a stretch! I only use manual focus, and I was too close at first, resulting in this photo which is almost full-frame. I adjusted my focus and got a more distant shot, as far as I could reach, but I like the immediacy of this first photo. I have another recent shot of a different butterfly kissing my hand, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2019)

A breeze picked up as soon as I got outside with my camera this morning. It felt like "fog winds" - and sure enough, marine-layer fog started to spill over the mountains about an hour later. We were always on the edge of the fog, and the winds were not as bad as they sometimes are. The temperature got to about 70∞. All in all, a nice day.

File.Name: j512b_j512-059.jpg
Notes: 33960105738 I finally got a photo today of one of the little wild bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) that have been working the new flowers of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica, Scrophulariaceae). The bee is about the same size as the flowers, about 3/8 inch. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, but I've seen these bees in years past, and Bernhard Jacobi has suggested genus Lasioglossum, see this photo and Bernhard's comment. The wild bees sure do love this Bee Plant! This particular specimen is growing near where we park our cars, and it's become huge this year, seven or eight feet tall with dozens of flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2019)
CommonNames: wild bees; California Figwort; California Bee Plant
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Lasioglossum
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Lasioglossum

File.Name: j512c_j512-050.jpg
Notes: 47047849754 Here are two shots of a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a non-native plant of Windmill Pink (Silene gallica), (Caryophyllaceae). It was stumbling around like it was drunk. I'm not sure what it is, but I would guess it's a male of the genus Osmia. It has the right metallic sheen, it seems to have two sub-marginal cells on its forewings, and a male wouldn't be collecting pollen - but don't take my word for it! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2019)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Silene gallica; Caryophyllaceae; Osmia
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Silene gallica; Caryophyllaceae; Osmia

File.Name: j512d_j512-005.jpg
Notes: 33959908598 I found this tiny Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a flower stalk of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) this morning. It's teneral - recently emerged from the final molt from nymph to adult. I know that because I first found it by its old skin, but my first photos aren't so good because it was in shade with a breeze. It eventually crawled into a patch of sunlight, and I got my photo. It's tiny, about 1/8 inch long. I believe it is genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae, see this photo of another mature adult. I've been finding nymphs like this one, but this is the first adult I've seen this year. They have skinny wings that can't be much help for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2019)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; teneral; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; teneral; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae

File.Name: j513a_j513-008.jpg
Notes:
32901305067 I found this "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick in a tangle of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) this morning. That's its tail on top with two pairs of prolegs and its head down below with three pairs of true legs that are not visible here. It would be perfect camouflage if the plant stems were brown rather than green. I've found similar caterpillars on this plant before, see this photo and this photo. This caterpillar is not small, maybe an inch or two long. I don't know what kind of moth it will become. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2019)

Today started nice, but then marine-layer fog started spilling over the mountains propelled by "fog winds". I tried to stay ahead of the fog by going down the hill on the valley side, which worked for a while until it got to me. It stayed gloomy at home until late in the afternoon when it finally cleared. This may be our pattern for some time as "May gray" segues into "June gloom".

File.Name: j513b_j513-022.jpg
Notes: 46928876075 This is a little caterpillar on a soon-to-be-flowering stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I'm pretty sure this is the critter that goes with the webbing in this photo, though the webbing is not so conspicuous here. The caterpillar seems to be munching the flower buds. You can still see a bit of its retreat behind the flowers on the right, and it almost instantly disappears inside if disturbed. I believe this is a real caterpillar that will become some kind of moth - but I don't know what kind. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2019)

File.Name: j513c_j513-032.jpg
Notes: 47845110401 I found this little beetle (Coleoptera) working new flowers of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). Hah, it's got a snoot full of pollen! I don't know what kind of beetle it is, though the long antennae and swollen femurs seem distinctive. Maybe Lepturinae or Cantharidae? Any help with identification will be appreciated. These shrubs will become literally covered with small white flowers in time. I may have a shot of the same kind of beetle on a different shrub with small white flowers, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2019)
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Lepturinae; Cantharidae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Lepturinae; Cantharidae

File.Name: j513d_j512-023.jpg
Notes: 33967645528 Here is one more shot of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on my hand, see my [Previous] photo for the whole story. This butterfly was definitely attracted to me - landing on my shirt, my tripod, my camera lens, and finally on me. I held my left hand out with the butterfly, and got the photo with my 105 mm macro lens on my tripod. Here I adjusted my focus and stretched out my arm a bit further. I got the whole butterfly, but I also like the immediacy of my [Previous] photo. The blue color on the butterfly's forewings is a sign that it is newly emerged, as they will lose it with age. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2019)

File.Name: j514a_j514-025.jpg
Notes:
40885644603 I found another well-camouflaged "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick today! It's on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), and I'm sure I only noticed it because I got this photo of a similar caterpillar yesterday. That's its tail on the left with two pairs of prolegs and its head on top with three pairs of true legs that are barely visible. It's interesting that it has little protuberances coming out from its body that are like the "pegs" that the needle-like leaves of chamise come from. This caterpillar is not small, it's at least an inch long. I don't believe I've seen one of these before, and I don't know what kind of moth it will become. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2019)

We were right on the edge of the marine layer today. The fog would spill over the mountains propelled by "fog winds" - and then back off for a moment of calm. We now have "rain likely" Wednesday night and Thursday, and it sounds like this might be a real storm with 1-2 inches in the mountains. There's more info in the new post at the California Weather Blog with the title "Highly unusual sequence of winter-like storms headed for California (in late May!)

File.Name: j514b_j514-015.jpg
Notes: 33974916488 I found this small green caterpillar on a flower head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) today. I believe it's the caterpillar of a Gossamer-Wing Butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group of Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks. I won't try to guess which one. I don't notice these very often, so this was a nice find. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2019)

File.Name: j514c_j514-032.jpg
Notes: 47062692654 Here is a remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I usually find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana), but I guess any sage will do. I believe this is the nymph of Closterocoris amoenus in the Miridae family, see this photo of the adult. This one has the beginning of "training wings". These are remarkable mimics - they look like ants, move like ants, and even have a faux "nodal spine" like some ants have for real. You can see its long proboscis folded under its body, which real ants don't have. This one didn't want to pose, but I did the best I could to get a photo. I've just started noticing these this year. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2019)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Salvia apiana; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Salvia apiana; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae

File.Name: j514d_j514-028.jpg
Notes: 47852096811 This is a Pinacate Beetle (genus Coelocnemis, Tenebrionidae, Coleoptera) on the ground in the canyon today. The genus Eleodes beetles are similar, but this one has fine golden-brown hairs on the inside margin of the hind tibiae. (These hairs are not visible in my photo, but I actually turned the beetle over to see them. It didn't complain - I guess it was playing dead on a chilly morning.) They are also known as a Stink Beetles or Stinkbugs or Acrobat Beetles or Clown Beetles, but I prefer the old name. Wikipedia says that "the name pinacate is Mexican Spanish, derived from the Nahuatl (Aztec) name for the insect, pinacatl, which translates as 'black beetle'." Some kids like to torment these large beetles - don't let them! The beetles put their heads to the earth, think of them as wise. These beetles are said to release a brew of noxious chemicals from their rear ends, but I can't vouch for that. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2019)
CommonNames: Pinacate Beetle; Stink Beetles; Stinkbugs; Acrobat Beetles; Clown Beetles

File.Name: j515a_j515-014.jpg
Notes:
33982201958 This is a late-stage caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on a soon-to-be-flowering stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in the drizzle today. It doesn't seem to have the good sense to get out of the drizzle, but at least it's gone under the stem. The caterpillars without raindrops look remarkable and fanciful, see this photo and this photo. This is the first mature caterpillar I've managed to photograph this year, though see this photo of probably an early instar from a month ago. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2019)

Today was gloomy and drizzly, with one of those annoying breezes that keeps everything moving and makes getting new photos difficult. There's nothing to do but reduce the aperture and bump up the ISO a notch and take lots of photos to get a few keepers! We have a 100% chance of rain tonight, and it sounds like this might be a real storm with an inch or more in the mountains. These late-season rains are good for the summer flowers, but they also keep the weeds going.
associatedTaxa: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: j515b_j515-016.jpg
Notes: 33982133138 Here is another shot of the chrysalis of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the drizzle today. I found the chrysalis last week, see this photo for more info. It's beautiful, and something magical is happening inside. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2019)

File.Name: j515c_j515-022.jpg
Notes: 46943153775 This is part of the web of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the drizzle today. I like the drops on the webbing and that out-of-focus green plant in the background. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2019)

File.Name: j515d_j514-005.jpg
Notes: 47807025282 Here is a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider making its web. The spider goes around and around, tacking its web to each ray as it goes. This is the same handsome spider that I showed in this photo last week. These spiders always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2019)

File.Name: j516a_j516-012.jpg
Notes:
46949701435 Here is the tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a narrow leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) - but it's more ornate than the nymph in my [Previous] photo. This one is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. This is the first of these little nymphs I've noticed this year. These are a favorite of mine that always remind me of little ponies decked out with jeweled finery. The adults are not so pretty, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2019)

We got 1.6 inches of rain last night! That's a lot for this time of year in our winter wet, summer dry Mediterranean climate. After the rain comes the wind, and we've had 30+ mph gusts all day once the rain cleared out. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and I hope that's the end of it.
CommonNames: Katydid; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: j516b_j516-016.jpg
Notes: 33988535218 Here is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a narrow leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). This nymph is not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in my [Next] photo, though I found them both of the same shrub today. This one is maybe the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) or a Chaparral Katydid (Platylyra californica), see this photo from a few years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2019)
CommonNames: Katydid; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; California Angle-winged Katydid; Chaparral Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica

File.Name: j516c_j516-029.jpg
Notes: 33988535218 This is looking at the rear end of a late-stage caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). One way to tell which end is which is that the head-end has two dark "horns" while the rear-end only has one, see this photo. Hah, another way is to notice that the caterpillar has just made frass - caterpillar poo! I've just started seeing these mature caterpillars, see my shot two photos [Previous] to this one. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2019)
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j516d_j511-037.jpg
Notes: 32921640367 This photo shows the rear end of a remarkable fly (Diptera) working new flowers of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) in the woods. I have another shot of the same fly here. I'm pretty sure it's the species Dioprosopa clavata, a Syrphid Fly in the family Syrphidae. If you know what to look for, you can the "spurious veins" on its wings. It's a good wasp-mimic with that petiole-like abdomen. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Dioprosopa clavata; Syrphidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Dioprosopa clavata; Syrphidae

File.Name: j517a_j517-032.jpg
Notes:
46955810105 This remarkable insect looks like a chimera - like someone stuck the front of a mantid onto the body of a paper wasp - but it's not either. I believe it's a Wasp Mantidfly - species Climaciella brunnea in the family Mantispidae of the order Neuroptera. I found it today in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and I thought it was ovipositing at first. It really does mimic a paper wasp in the genus Mischocyttarus or Polistes. I'm surprised by how large this one is, about an inch long. BugGuide says that the larvae are parasitoids of spiders, but the only larvae I've found are on Jumping Spiders and too small to become such a large insect, see this photo. The only other adult mantidfly I've found is this one which is quite small. I must admit that I broke my usual rule and used my on-camera flash for this photo to lighten up the shadows. This is the first one I've ever seen. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2019)

It was sunny today after the morning fog cleared out, but the winds continued - mostly 20+ mph which is an improvement over the 30+ mph winds we had yesterday. Our NWS Wind Advisory continues tonight. Then we have rain in the forecast for Saturday night - and more rain next Tuesday??
ScientificName: Climaciella brunnea; Mantispidae; Neuroptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mischocyttarus; Polistes
associatedTaxa: Climaciella brunnea; Mantispidae; Neuroptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mischocyttarus; Polistes

File.Name: j517b_j517-022.jpg
Notes: 46955741225 This sleek "true bug" is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera). It's the adult that comes from the ant-mimic nymphs that I showed in this photo a couple of days ago. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. I found this adult (and more nymphs) on a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I usually find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana), but I guess any sage will do. I've also found them inside the flowers of Bush Monkey Flower shrubs, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2019)
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Salvia apiana
associatedTaxa: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Salvia apiana

File.Name: j517c_j517-010.jpg
Notes: 46955684955 Here are a mating pair of little Syrphid Flies (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. If you look close at the color pattern of their abdomens, you can see that they are different from the fly in my [Previous] photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2019)

File.Name: j517d_j516-001.jpg
Notes: 47871807751 I found this little golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) floundering in a pan full of water after our rain yesterday morning. I let it crawl onto my finger, and it moved off to this fallen oak leaf. Here it is grooming itself. It flew off a moment later ending a successful rescue. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2019)

File.Name: j518a_j518-008.jpg
Notes:
32933596027 This is some kind of cricket - a Long-horned Orthopteran (Ensifera, Orthoptera) anyway. I was sure that it was not one of the Chaparral Camel Crickets (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) that have been so common this winter, but after spending some time at BugGuide, I believe that is indeed what it is. It's a female, while all the other ones I've found earlier in the year (except this one) were male. On the other hand, the cricket in this photo looks female, and it's different, so there's something here I don't understand. I found this one minding its own business on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) on the edge of the garden this morning. It jumped away from me, but I found it again on the ground and moved it to a place where I could get this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2019)

Clouds have been moving in today. We still had a breeze this morning when I was out getting my photos, but then it calmed down. We've already had some light rain as I write this at 7 pm and maybe 1/2 inch is expected tonight and tomorrow - not to mention more next week. These late-season rains are good for the summer flowers, but they also keep the weeds growing.
CommonNames: cricket; Long-horned Orthopteran; Chaparral Camel Crickets; California Coffeeberry
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: j518b_j518-016.jpg
Notes: 40911314113 I believe this is a two-winged Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) hanging from last year's galls on native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). There's is a pupal husk stuck in an exit hole on the left that's not quite in focus, but it may well be that the fly just emerged from this gall leaving its old pupal skin behind and is now hardening up. I have another shot of a pupal husk and more info at this photo. I don't know what that drop of white fluid is on the fly's behind. I was excited to get this photo today, and I suspect this is something rarely seen. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2019)

File.Name: j518c_j518-033.jpg
Notes: 47877653661 Here are a pair of tiny spiders on the carcass of a small Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). The fly is only about 1/2 inch long, and the spiders are minute - about 560 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The spiders are actually a male and female couple - the brown-colored one has male palps and the gray one has female palps, though it may not be obvious in this photo. I don't think I've seen spiders together like this before. I don't know what kind of spiders they are, and any help with identification will be appreciated. I also don't know if the spiders brought down the fly. Their nearby webbing seemed undisturbed and too small for the job anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2019)

File.Name: j518d_j517-034.jpg
Notes: 47825358052 Here is one more shot of the remarkable Wasp Mantidfly (Climaciella brunnea,Mantispidae, Neuroptera) that I show in my [Previous] photo. I found it yesterday in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This photo shows its face and mantis-like front legs that are neatly folded up. This critter is a Neuopteran not a Mantid, and you can see it here - its "neck" is like the Snakefly in this photo, and it has remarkable eyes like the Green Lacewing in this photo. This was the first one of these I've found. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2019)
ScientificName: Climaciella brunnea; Mantispidae; Neuroptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Climaciella brunnea; Mantispidae; Neuroptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j519a_j519-008.jpg
Notes:
46969651135 I actually found this little Weevil (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) beetle on a White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) shrub this morning huddled up under a leaf after the rain last night. I couldn't see its head, so I moved it to a nearby table with some oak leaves. I returned it to the sage after I got my photos, and it was still there a couple of hours later when I came back. Its snout and antennae are remarkable. This one might be a Nut and Acorn Weevil in the genus Curculio, but BugGuide reports that weevils are "the largest animal family with more than 50,000 species in ~4600 genera worldwide" - so it might also be something else. It's cute and furry. I can imagine Beanie Baby weevils! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2019)

We had just over one inch in the rain gauge this morning, more than I was expecting. I thought it would clear up and be windy after the rain cleared out, but it was cloudy and calm instead with some light showers. Then late in the day it started to rain hard again adding another 1/3 inch to the rain gauge. What an odd month of May-uary it's been!
CommonNames: Weevil; White Sage; Nut and Acorn Weevil
ScientificName: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Curculio
associatedTaxa: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Curculio

File.Name: j519b_j519-028.jpg
Notes: 40919248543 Here is a particularly handsome Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It wasn't moving much even when I got close, and I suspect it was trying to warm up in the sun - with wings open rather than closed like the Silvery Blue butterfly two photos back. It's interesting to compare this one with the pale specimen in my [Previous] photo. That one almost looks like a different kind of butterfly, but if you compare the outline of the checkered spots, you can see that they mostly match. I'm not sure if the previous butterfly is just seriously worn or a distinct color variant. If you compare this photo of a different fresh specimen, you can see that the butterflies just are highly variable. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2019)

File.Name: j519c_j519-032.jpg
Notes: 40919177583 Here is another very pale Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched on (and obscuring) a flowering plant of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). I'm really posting this to compare with the fresh specimen in my [Next] photo. That one almost looks like a different kind of butterfly, but if you compare the outline of the checkered spots, you can see that they mostly match. I'm not sure if today's butterfly is just seriously worn or a distinct color variant. I've found these pale specimens before, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2019)

File.Name: j519d_j519-024.jpg
Notes: 47885550261 This looking down on another Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly on native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The butterfly is actually tilted towards the sun like a living solar collector, trying to catch some rays on a chilly day after the rain stopped this morning. I've seen these butterflies in this position before, see this photo. You can see a sliver of the pretty upper wing in this photo, but I've also captured the upper wings of both male and female butterflies this year. I'm guessing this one is female with a wider dark margin. It's curious that all of these photos were taken on Black Sage. I guess they like this shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2019)

File.Name: j520a_j520-005.jpg
Notes:
47843508272 Here is a mating group of little Tephritid Fruit Flies (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a cabbage-like flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). These flies might be the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), see BugGuide. I've seen several groups of these mating flies, often in threes like this, though I reckon only two of them are actually mating. Those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2019)

We ended up with three inches of rain over the weekend, remarkable for the month of May in our summer-dry climate. Today we had clearing skies and increasing winds of 20-30 mph, and it sounds like the winds will be worse tomorrow. This time I don't care, because Julie and I are heading up to Portland, Oregon for a couple of weeks to spend some time with our grandkids. We'll be boarding the train on Wednesday, and I'll need Tuesday to get organized. I will find time to post a few more photos tomorrow.

File.Name: j520b_j520-022.jpg
Notes: 40929096783 Here is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) taking pollen from a stamen of a flower of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). Look how the fly is grasping the stamen - like a crooner holding a microphone. The plants have just started flowering in the last week. They get their name from the bristly flower heads that look a bit like "woolly bear" caterpillars, see this photo from a couple of years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2019)

File.Name: j520c_j520-015.jpg
Notes: 46979329655 This is a still-forming chrysalis of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. It's different from the one I showed in this photo, but it has a similar story. Last Thursday I found the caterpillar curled up on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) down by the creek just like the one in this photo. I learned from that other caterpillar that it takes a few days to form a chrysalis, and here it is four days later. The pupa is still on the same Mugwort plant, but it's leaning over now after our good rains this weekend. It was still kicking around when I first found it, and I saw it kick off what was left of its old caterpillar skin. You can see the outlines of the butterfly's future antennae and proboscis on the pupal shell - looking green because they are so new after a final molt. It's beautiful, and something magical is starting to happen inside. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2019)

File.Name: j520d_j520-002.jpg
Notes: 40928882793 Our late-season rains have brought up late-season mushrooms! I believe these are some kind of Agrocybe mushroom, probably the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox). They are growing on old wood chips in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. These have brownish spores and an evanescent veil that forms a ring on the stem. By chance, I also found them on the same May 20 date last year, see this photo. The mushrooms in that photo have cracked caps which is typical for these late-season mushrooms that fruit during dry weather. This years crop are smooth because we continue to have good rain. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2019)

File.Name: j521a_j518-023.jpg
Notes:
46988160745 Here is another mating group of little Tephritid Fruit Flies (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a cabbage-like flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). These flies might be the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), see BugGuide. I've seen several groups of these mating flies, often in threes like this, though I reckon only two of them are actually mating. See this photo from yesterday for another group shot. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2019)

The winds have been terrible today with 30-40+ mph gusts. This time I don't care, because Julie and I are heading up to Portland, Oregon to spend some time with our grandkids, and today was my day for errands and packing and getting organized. We'll be boarding the train on Wednesday and return in a couple of weeks. This isn't really a photo trip, but maybe I'll get a few photos on Mt. Tabor.

File.Name: j521b_j518-037.jpg
Notes: 47852101272 This is a little Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a bristly leaf of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). I suspect it's the adult that goes with the nymph in this photo taken on the same plants two weeks ago. This one seems larger than most leafhoppers. I like those black speckles on its face. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2019)
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: j521c_j515-007.jpg
Notes: 47904120031 This is a native grass with raindrops on a drizzly morning last week. I'm pretty sure the grass is California Brome (Bromus carinatus, Poaceae). This native grass is a perennial with flattened spikelets and short awns, which distinguishes it from all the weedy annual bromes like Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) and Red Brome (Bromus rubens) and the rest of their kin that are trying to take over the woodland and grassland. You have to know something about grasses to recognize this one, but it's surprisingly common in the woodland when you look for it. It seems to be strictly a woodland grass, growing under the oaks. We've been encouraging it for a few years in the canyon, and it's doing well. I have this photo of the grass without water drops that's easier to see. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2019)
CommonNames: California Brome; Ripgut Grass; Red Brome
ScientificName: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens
associatedTaxa: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens

File.Name: j521d_j519-015.jpg
Notes: 40937487373 Here are more late-season mushrooms growing on wood chips - different from the ones I showed yesterday in this photo. I've seen these before. They appear to be brown-spored and hygrophanous. Maybe they are Tubaria furfuracea which my mushroom book describes as the "quintessential LBM" or "Little Brown Mushroom". These mushrooms make up in number what they lack in interest. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2019)

File.Name: j605a_j605-039.jpg
Notes:
48010893461 This little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) has captured a different kind of spider as prey. Hah, I found them on my yellow nalgene water bottle today. I don't know what the prey is, but the spider might be species Phlegra hentzi - compare this photo at BugGuide. It's small. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2019)

Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best! We just got back from our Oregon trip last night, and it was a pleasure to get out in the woods again today. I did find time to get a walk with my camera every day while we were in Portland, Oregon. It was a little frustrating because I didn't bring my tripod, but I managed with a monopod - using higher ISO and aperture than usual. It's also frustrating that "edit-details" is not working tonight, and I only get a "404 error" when I try. The main problem is that I can't edit my tags, and I like to get them right. Hopefully all will be back to normal tomorrow.

File.Name: j605b_j604-003.jpg
Notes: 48010764001 I found this handsome Hairstreak (subfamily Theclinae) butterfly on a flowering shrub near the park on Mt. Tabor in Portland, Oregon. I'm from California, and don't feel like I have to know all the Oregon butterflies, but this one might be some subspecies of the Juniper Hairstreak (Callophrys gryneus) - possibly the Cedar Hairstreak (subspecies plicataria), compare this photo at BugGuide. It has wings like the Brown Elfin butterfly in this photo. At first it looks plain brown, but when you look closer you see that it really has a rich palette of many colors like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, combine to make the eye-catching brown. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 26 May 2019)

File.Name: j605c_j604-052.jpg
Notes: 48010782812 This is well-traveled Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on a flowering shrub in the park on Mt. Tabor in Portland, Oregon. There was a mass migration of these butterflies that passed through our mountains in Santa Barbara back in March, see this photo. I guess this one made it all the way to Portland. It looks threadbare, but it was flying just fine and soon got away from me. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 31 May 2019)

File.Name: j605d_j604-013.jpg
Notes: 48010615753 I believe this is a little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on its web in a garden shrub of Azalea (Rhododendron, Ericaceae) which has just about finished flowering. There is a funnel-like opening to a retreat just behind the spider, and the spider zips inside when disturbed. We just got back from our Oregon trip last night. This photo does show the spider's web, so Happy Web Webnesday! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 27 May 2019)

File.Name: j606a_j605-019.jpg
Notes:
48016292338 This is looking at the rear end of the nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a bristly plant of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). Hah, it's got a face on its butt - like a turtle face! This might be the same nymph that I show in this photo, probably the nymph of a Chaparral Katydid (Platylyra californica) or a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). See Alice Abelia's comment on my other photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2019)

Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best! We just got back from our Oregon trip the night before last, and I seem to have brought back a virus as well as new photos. I felt pretty crummy today, and two photos will be enough. I notice that "edit-details" and the map are still not working right.
CommonNames: Katydid; Caterpillar Phacelia; Chaparral Katydid; California Angle-winged Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Platylyra californica; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: j606b_j604-038.jpg
Notes: 48016304767 These photos show the top and rear end of a striking fly on a flower in a Portland, Oregon garden in the neighborhood near Mount Tabor. I believe the fly is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera), possibly a Narcissus Bulb Fly Merodon equestris or kin in the same Eristalini tribe that includes many more bee-mimic flies in the genus Eristalis. It looks like a furry Bumble Bee, and even has flattened hind leg segments like a bee. I figure this counts as half of a "butt shot" so HBBBT! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 30 May 2019)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Merodon equestris; Eristalini; Eristalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Merodon equestris; Eristalini; Eristalis

File.Name: j607a_j607-028.jpg
Notes:
48021920433 Here is a handsome little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). Apparently the butterflies are named for their blue color above and not for any affinity with the ocean - though this female is quite brown on top as you can see in that little tear on its forewing. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2019)

Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best! We're back home after two weeks in Oregon. I'm feeling better now after feeling pretty crummy yesterday, and I did my usual walk today. I notice that "edit-details" and the map are still not working right. C'mon, we're paying for this!

File.Name: j607b_j604-035.jpg
Notes: 48021845306 Here is another striking fly (Diptera) that looks for all the world like a bee - compare my [Previous] two photos. This fly is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera), possibly a Narcissus Bulb Fly (Merodon equestris) or kin in the same Eristalini tribe that includes many more bee-mimic flies in the genus Eristalis. It's furry like a bee and even has flattened leg segments like a bee. This one is less colorful than the fly in my [Previous] photo, and it may be that this one is female with eyes that are separated in front whereas the other one is male with eyes that meet in front - but compare this photo at BugGuide. I guess all we can say is that the flies are highly variable. It's my understanding that this fly has been introduced from Europe and is now widespread in North America. HFDF! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 30 May 2019)
CommonNames: fly; Syrphid Fly; Narcissus Bulb Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Syrphidae; Diptera; Merodon equestris; Eristalini; Eristalis
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Syrphidae; Diptera; Merodon equestris; Eristalini; Eristalis

File.Name: j607c_j604-067.jpg
Notes: 48021740123 I found this little Bumble Bee dead on the ground and posed it on a mossy rock in a Portland, Oregon neighborhood near Mount Tabor. I believe it is genus Bombus, subgenus Pyrobombus with those orange colors, possibly a Black-tailed Bumble Bee (Bombus melanopygus). You can see its long mouthparts. I saw quite a few of these bees working flowers in the park, but I couldn't get an acceptable photo until I found this deceased one. The Syrphid Fly in my [Next] photo is a pretty good mimic of this bee. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 3 June 2019)

File.Name: j608a_j608-039.jpg
Notes:
48027490201 I found this remarkable caterpillar devouring a leaf of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) today - a prickly caterpillar on a prickly plant! I had to do some research on this guy, and I now believe it is the caterpillar of a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly. It's remarkable that the caterpillars are rather scarce locally when the butterflies are so common. They have been migrating north from the deserts to the south, though I did get a photo of one ovipositing close to where I found this caterpillar today during the height of the migration, see this photo from last April. The caterpillar is defoliating the plant, and I hope it still manages to open its flower buds. I'm sorry it hasn't gone after the weedy thistles as well. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2019)

Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best! We're back home after two weeks in Oregon. The weather has been pleasant, though it was breezy today. I notice that "edit-details" and the map are still not working right. I especially miss the "edit-details" window. Is there another way to edit and copy tags from photo to photo? I take some pride in my tags, and I like to get them right.
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Vanessa cardui
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Vanessa cardui

File.Name: j608b_j608-006.jpg
Notes: 48027549022 Here is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) ovipositing - laying eggs - on a flower bud of White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). This native shrub is just starting to flower on the edge of our garden. The larvae of these flies are predators of aphids and other tiny insects, and I wonder what these will devour as they mature? (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2019)

File.Name: j608c_j604-024.jpg
Notes: 48027505762 I believe this is a Rose Curculio (Merhynchites bicolor) beetle on rose plants in the park on Mount Tabor in Portland, Oregon. It's a weevil, and that hole drilled into the flower bud on top might be part of its work. It's cute but destructive. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 29 May 2019)

File.Name: j608d_j604-061.jpg
Notes: 48027351628 This is a Western Boxelder Bug (Boisea rubrolineata) posed on a willow leaf near the Sandy River in Oregon a week ago. We have the same bugs at home, see this photo. It's curious the we don't have Boxelder trees (Acer negundo) at home, but we do have Bigleaf Maple (Acer grandifolium), and I guess that's close enough. (Sandy River, Clackamas County, Oregon, 1 June 2019)
ScientificName: Boisea rubrolineata; Acer negundo; Acer grandifolium
associatedTaxa: Boisea rubrolineata; Acer negundo; Acer grandifolium

File.Name: j609a_j609-020.jpg
Notes:
48033512731 This is a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working new flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This is the first one I've seen this year. It is somewhat similar to the Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly in this photo, though that one appears later in the summer when these are all gone. I also saw an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly today, but I couldn't get an acceptable photo. At least they're out here! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2019)

Today was certainly the hottest day in this (so far) rather cool year - up to about 90∞ on our upstairs deck, with calm winds, at least in the morning. I've been posting photos from our recent Oregon trip, though they're getting pushed out by more recent photos from home. I've got more in the docket, but maybe this is enough. Maps and "edit-details" are still not working right. I especially miss the "edit-details" window. Is there another way to edit and copy tags from photo to photo? I take some pride in my tags, and I like to get them right.
Locality: so far) rather cool year - up to about 90∞ on our upstairs deck

File.Name: j609b_j609-005.jpg
Notes: 48033487628 Here are new flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). The plant is a woody vine that sometimes grows high up into the oak trees, but sometimes also hugs the ground. They are just starting to flower - late this year like most of our wildflowers. The plants always have that that umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf that the flowers grow from. They are a favorite of Pale Swallowtail butterflies, which I'm still waiting to photograph this year. Did you notice the little wild bee on the left-most open flower? (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2019)

File.Name: j609c_j604-063.jpg
Notes: 48033397683 I found this native Iris (Iridaceae) near the Sandy River in Oregon last week. I'm not not what kind it is, but it sure is pretty. This iris was growing in the conifer woods with no standing water nearby. Maybe it is the Clackamas Iris (Iris tenuis or the Oregon Iris (I. tenax)? Note the little wild bee on the left-most petal. (Sandy River, Clackamas County, Oregon, 1 June 2019)
CommonNames: Iris; Clackamas Iris; Oregon Iris; wild bee

File.Name: j609d_j604-055.jpg
Notes: 48033410402 This is the fasciated stem of a non-native weed growing in the park on Mount Tabor in Portland, Oregon. The weed isn't one we have at home, but I believe it is Nipplewort (Lapsana communis) in the Asteraceae family. The main stem is flat like a ribbon, but the plant is still producing flowers on normal stems. Many plants get fasciated, some often and others rarely. There's some info at Wikipedia. They are unusual and worth collecting - in photos at least. There is even a low-traffic Flickr group on Fascinating Fasciations to which I'll add this one. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 31 May 2019)

File.Name: j610a_j610-026.jpg
Notes:
48040631251 Here is another little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). Compare this photo taken a few days ago. This photo isn't quite sharp, but you can actually see an egg at the tip of its abdomen if look close! Hah, I was sitting in my portable chair by the plant trying to get a shot, when this butterfly came right up between my legs - I was happy to get this photo. Apparently these butterflies are named for their blue color above and not for any affinity with the ocean - though this female is quite brown on top. They are usually uncommon here, but I've seen more of them this year that the Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterflies that are usually more common. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2019)

It was downright HOT today, up to 94∞ on our upstairs deck without much wind. I have more Oregon photos from our recent trip, but it's time to move on. I notice that the map and "edit-details" are still not working right. C'mon, we're paying for this!

associatedTaxa: Leptotes marina; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Plebejus acmon

File.Name: j610b_j610-003.jpg
Notes: 48040688042 This is a cute little Treehopper (family Membracidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a furry leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I guess this is a Buffalo Treehopper in the genus Ceresa or Stictocephala. I have more photos of these critters on the same plant here and here. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2019)

File.Name: j610c_j610-004.jpg
Notes: 48040548543 This is a tiny Lace Bug (Tingidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a furry leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae, Boraginaceae). I believe this one is species Corythucha eriodictyonae, and it's probably host-specific. They really are small, about 750 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) We have different lace bugs on Wild Sweetpea that are bumpier and less spiky, compare this photo. These bugs both have remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor - but it also looks like bubble-wrap! You can see the eyes and antennae of the skinny bug inside the armor. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2019)
