File.Name: j610d_j610-012.jpg
Notes: 48040432556 Here are new flowers of new flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The old name for this plant is Rhamnus californica which is similar to the related Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in this photo. Both plants have obvious sepals, but Redberry is 4-merous while Coffeeberry is 5-merous. Another structural difference is that Coffeeberry has true petals in addition to sepals - those little brown flaps that surround each stamen. I guess this is why Coffeeberry and Redberry are no longer placed in the same genus. Ceanothus shrubs are also in the Rhamnaceae family, but they have five showy petals which is different from both. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2019)
ScientificName: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: j611a_j611-016.jpg
Notes:
48046648406 I finally got a photo today of a new Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly. It's ovipositing on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) along our chipseal road. You can actually see the egg that it's pushing out if you look close! Compare this photo of a Marine Blue butterfly that is also pushing out an egg that I just posted yesterday. This isn't the best photo, but it's one I've been trying to get all week. These butterflies are usually pretty common in the mountains, but they seem pretty scarce this year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2019)

I had an appointment in town this morning, and it was already uncomfortably hot by the time I got home. It got up to 96∞ on our upstairs deck without much wind. We also had 40-50% humidity. Yipes, it was 106∞ down by the river today! We've been under a NWS Heat Advisory all day. The good news is that tomorrow is expected to be cooler - and much cooler by the weekend.

File.Name: j611b_j611-004.jpg
Notes: 48046695842 I found this sleek little Snakefly (family Raphidiidae, order Raphidioptera) insect on a leaf of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum, Anacardiaceae) today. I believe it is genus Agulla with that "kite-shaped" head and male without a long ovipositor on the other end, compare this photo. Snakefly heads always reminds me of the aliens from the 1953 scifi movie "The War of the Worlds"! They look like they're made of metal. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2019)
ScientificName: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae; Agulla
associatedTaxa: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae; Agulla

File.Name: j611c_j611-019.jpg
Notes: 48046627842 This isn't a caterpillar, though it is definitely caterpillar-like. It's a larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar - but isn't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. First, they have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of multiple eyespots - see this photo. Second, sawfly larvae always have more prolegs - six or more pairs - while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. This one is on a stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae), where I've found them before - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2019)
associatedTaxa: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; eruciform; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: j611d_j607-015.jpg
Notes: 48046559347 Here are almost-mating beetles (Coleoptera) inside a new flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I don't know what kind of beetles they are other than little ones. They are in a position to be mating, but there is no actual physical connection between them. Maybe this is male "mate guarding" in which males ride on the backs of females for some time to keep other males away. Timemas are another insect that regularly does this, see this photo. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2019)

File.Name: j612a_j612-035.jpg
Notes:
48052553643 Here are a mating pair of Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterflies on flowers of non-native English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. The butterflies don't look all that different from the Acmon Blue in my [Previous] photo, but they are quite different on their upper wings, see this photo. This was a lucky find for a Wednesday, Happy Hump Day! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 June 2019)

It was a few degrees cooler today, but still up to 90∞ on our upstairs deck without much wind. We're supposed to cool down another 20 degrees by the weekend. And thank you Flickr! The maps seem to be working again, and I think I like the "mysterious place with no name" description! Now it's time to get "/edit-details/" working again.

File.Name: j612b_j612-007.jpg
Notes: 48052527383 This is another female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). It's not ovipositing like the one I showed yesterday, but I'm sure she was a few minutes before. The male butterflies are deep blue above, but these ladies only have a sprinkling of blue scales. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 June 2019)

File.Name: j612c_j612-026.jpg
Notes: 48052505482 Here is a goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera on a leaf of non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra, Brassicaceae) that is in flower. The grasshopper is less than 1/2 inch long. I'm not sure how to distinguish nymphs and adults as both are wingless, but this one seems small enough to be a nymph. They are remarkably variable as you can see if you search all my photos for "Morsea californica". This is the first one I've seen this year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 June 2019)
ScientificName: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Brassica nigra; Brassicaceae
associatedTaxa: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Brassica nigra; Brassicaceae

File.Name: j612d_j612-017.jpg
Notes: 48052384966 I believe this is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). These orb-weaving spiders make a proper orb web (right) and then build a little retreat off to the side held up by cobweb-type webbing (left). I have another shot of both webs here and a photo of the spider in its retreat here. One interesting feature of these webs is that there is always a strand of webbing connecting the retreat to the orb web that the spider uses to sense activity. In this shot, you can see it running across the center of my photo. The web appears to be under some tension. Happy Web Webnesday! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 June 2019)

File.Name: j613a_j613-011.jpg
Notes:
48058787493 This is the rear end of a little fly on my leg today. I believe it's a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family. It's related to our common Snipe Flies in this photo, and has a similar abdomen - like those collapsible cups they used to sell for backpacking with nested cups that open up. They also have annoying biting habits. I'll have another photo tomorrow of the rest of the fly for Fly Day Friday, but this one is perfect for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2019)

It really did cool down 20∞ on our upstairs deck - from 90∞ yesterday to 70∞ today. Nice, but here was also an annoying breeze from the south - no fog, but it felt like the usual "fog winds" that come with the marine layer. The cooler weather is appreciated after the heat of the last few days.
CommonNames: Rocky Mountain Bite Fly; Snipe Fly

File.Name: j613b_j612-005.jpg
Notes: 48058680858 This is a remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a flower bud of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). I believe this is the nymph of Closterocoris amoenus in the Miridae family, see this photo of the less ant-like adult. The nymphs are remarkable mimics - they look like ants, move like ants, and even have a faux "nodal spine" like some of our ants have for real. You can see its long proboscis stuck into the flower bud - something that real ants don't have. I also find these nymphs on Black Sage (Salvia mellifera), see this photo. I guess any sage will do, though I've also found the nymphs on Bush Monkey Flower, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2019)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Salvia mellifera
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Salvia mellifera

File.Name: j613c_j611-001.jpg
Notes: 48058662697 Here are "closed" and "open" flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The flowers start out in a closed position, like the flower on the left. It takes a heavy bee like a Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa) or Bumble Bee (Bombus) to lever them open. Once opened, they stay open, and other bees like the one on the right can get in. Even the Honey Bees can't get in until the larger bees get there first. That little bee in my photo is probably the same kind of family Megachilidae bee as my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2019)
CommonNames: White Sage; Carpenter Bee; Bumble Bee
ScientificName: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa; Bombus; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa; Bombus; Megachilidae

File.Name: j613d_j609-013.jpg
Notes: 48058507363 Here is a little wild bee getting both nectar and pollen from a new flower of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). I don't know what kind of wild bee it is, but I'm sure it's in the family Megachilidae. These bees are distinctive because they collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees. The blue pollen of these flowers make this easy to see. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2019)

File.Name: j614a_j614-007.jpg
Notes:
48064285111 I found this odd-looking fly (Diptera) on a faded flower of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). It's small, about 890 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm - a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it has remarkable eyes and interesting antennae, and it looks like its full of eggs. The "double antennae" are actually ordinary antennae with a thickened arista - that usually hair-like bristle that arises from the base of the antennae of many flies. I have found a Syrphid Fly with antennae like this, see this photo, but this fly is something else. Any help with identification will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2019)

Today started clear, but then a marine layer cloud spilled over the mountains and didn't clear out until the afternoon. For some reason the "fog winds" weren't too bad. It stayed cool today in the 60s, even when the sun came out.

File.Name: j614b_j614-019.jpg
Notes: 48064281248 Here is a little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). It's a lady, I can see her ovipositor in another photo. She has amazing eyes and an interesting pattern on her wings that reminds me of a Japanese Rising Sun flag. There are many different kinds of these flies, so I'll leave it at that. I was going to post this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2019)

File.Name: j614c_j613-012.jpg
Notes: 48064296162 Here is an atypical Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). I believe it is genus Thevenetimyia, though I won't try to guess which species. I was going to post this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2019)

File.Name: j614d_j613-008.jpg
Notes: 48064195323 This is the other end of the little fly that I showed on my leg yesterday in my Previous photo. I believe it's a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family. It's related to our common Snipe Flies in this photo, but they have annoying biting habits. We actually have (at least) two biting flies in this genus living in our mountains. The other one is orange in color, see this photo from last year. That one is a real bloodsucker that flies in and out faster than you can swat, but the one in this photo is slower. It has impressive mouthparts that are also visible in this photo from two years ago. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2019)
CommonNames: Rocky Mountain Bite Fly; Snipe Fly

File.Name: j615a_j615-039.jpg
Notes:
48070187651 Here are fantastic new flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. They're just starting in the canyon at home this year, though I've been seeing them higher in the mountains. They look like one of those "Mexican Sages" in the garden, but these are native Californians. I've always wondered what large insect the long pistils and stamens were adapted for, and I show one candidate in this photo. But maybe the real pollinators are tiny like that little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on the stamens on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2019)

Today started gloomy, but the marine layer did finally clear out in the afternoon. We seemed to be right at the level of the fog - it passed in and out over the mountains several times, bringing fresh bouts of "fog winds" every time. It did finally warm up into the 70s.

File.Name: j615b_j615-026.jpg
Notes: 48070192873 Here is another female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). The males have much more blue on their upper wings, compare this photo. I've only been able to photograph females lately, probably because they're ovipositing so I know where they are. This one was just perched here with its wings open, probably trying to warm up after a chilly morning. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2019)

File.Name: j615c_j615-036.jpg
Notes: 48070170952 This is an odd little Plume Moth (Pterophoridae, Lepidoptera) hanging on a new flower of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). Their wings aren't really that narrow, but they fold them up like a fan at rest. There are many different kinds, both diurnal and nocturnal, so I hesitate to guess what this one is - but one possibility is the Geranium Plume Moth (Amblyptilia pica), compare this photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2019)
CommonNames: Plume Moth; Wedge-leaved Horkelia; Geranium Plume Moth
ScientificName: Pterophoridae; Lepidoptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Amblyptilia pica
associatedTaxa: Pterophoridae; Lepidoptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Amblyptilia pica

File.Name: j615d_j615-041.jpg
Notes: 48070090537 I believe this is the caterpillar of a white-fringed Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly on a stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae), see this photo of the butterfly it will become. What an odd capsule it has for a head! Our Duskywing (genus Erynnis) butterflies fall into two groups, those that feed on legumes like this Deerweed, and those that feed on oaks. Another legume-feeding possibililty for this one is the Afranius Duskywing (Erynnis afranius), but I don't think I've ever seen one on those in our mountains - smaller and dark, with no white fringe. This isn't the best photo, but I don't believe I've seen one of these before. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2019)
ScientificName: Erynnis funeralis; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Erynnis; Erynnis afranius
associatedTaxa: Erynnis funeralis; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Erynnis; Erynnis afranius

File.Name: j616a_j616-018.jpg
Notes:
48076655723 Here is another handsome Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on the ground. Hah, we played a waiting game - the butterfly was sitting with its wings closed trying to look invisible, but it finally opened its wings for just a moment, and I got the photo. This one looks in better shape than the last one I got a photo of, so maybe it's a new brood. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2019)

The marine-layer fog was in and out again today, as we seemed to be right at the level of the fog again. It was quite cool today, only in the 60s. Our power will be out tomorrow for another "scheduled maintenance". I don't know if I'll be able to post. And darn, we have yet another "scheduled maintenance" on Friday. [Expletive deleted]

File.Name: j616b_j616-004.jpg
Notes: 48076573383 I'm pretty sure these are new eggs of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly tucked under a leaf of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). Compare this photo and this photo (in the shadows). It's interesting that this butterfly lays its eggs in a mass while the Acmon and Marine Blue butterflies spread their eggs around one by one. This is no doubt because the new caterpillars are communal, see this photo. This clutch of eggs was a lucky find today. I'll keep an eye on it. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2019)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: j616c_j615-006.jpg
Notes: 48076509548 Here is another "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick in a shrub of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). That's its rear end on the left with two pairs of prolegs, and its head on top with three pairs of true legs. It was motionless and quite rigid. Pretty good camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2019)

File.Name: j616d_j614-031.jpg
Notes: 48076375736 This is the early "bird turd" stage of a caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. At least it looks something like a bird turd with that white splotch in the middle. It's small, only about 1/4 inch long. This one is on non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) which is their usual host plant in our mountains. The caterpillars go through a number of different instars before they make a chrysalis and become butterflies, see this photo and this photo. My photo today shows an early instar but probably not the first. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2019)
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; instars
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; instars

File.Name: j617a_j617-010.jpg
Notes:
48087373326 Here is a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). It was chilly and drizzly when I got this photo looking down, and the butterfly was leaning over like a living solar collector, trying to catch some rays. I've seen other butterflies doing he same thing, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2019)

Our power was indeed off all day yesterday. It came back on at 7:00 pm. I figured it was too late to do anything with the day's photos, so here are two shots just to show that I was busy yesterday. It was a gloomy day. The sun only broke through as it was going down, about the same time as our power came back on. Darn, the power will be out again for "scheduled maintenance" on Friday.

File.Name: j617b_j617-017.jpg
Notes: 48087333166 Here is another shot of the little caterpillar that I believe will become a Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly, see this photo from a couple of days ago. It's on a stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). What an odd capsule-like head it has! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2019)

File.Name: j618a_j618-023.jpg
Notes:
48089706721 Here is another female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly - just because they are so pretty and will soon disappear. This one was actually working the native Spanish Clover (Acmispon americanus, Fabaceae) instead of the usual Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae), see this photo. It was perched with wings open, probably trying to warm up after another chilly morning in the fog. The males have much more blue on their upper wings, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2019)

The marine layer cleared up before noon today, but the fog bank sat just over the mountains on the ocean side, coming in and out like it was breathing, with steady fog winds. It wasn't the best day for new photos, but I got a few keepers.
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Acmispon americanus; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Acmispon americanus; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: j618b_j618-038.jpg
Notes: 48089630353 I managed to get photos of all these butterflies today for the first time this year, but none of the photos is the best, so I decided to make a collage. I've been seeing all these butterflies for a while, except for the hairstreak in the top-left , but I haven't been able to get any photos - and not for lack of trying! I'll go clockwise from the upper-left, which is a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium). They are rusty-orange on top, visible when they fly and through that tear in its wing. This is the first one I've seen his year. Next is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola). If you look close, you can see little translucent spots near the tips of its forewings. Then comes a Duskywing butterfly, though I'm not sure if its a Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) or a Funereal Duskywing (E. funeralis). The latter butterflies are said to be more black, so maybe this is the former. And I finally got a photo of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly, my first for the year. Usually they are common in the woods by now, but this year they've been rather scarce. Now I can try to get better photos of each of these. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2019)
CommonNames: Hedgerow Hairstreak; Rural Skipper; Duskywing; Mournful Duskywing; Funereal Duskywing; Pale Swallowtail
ScientificName: Satyrium saepium; Ochlodes agricola; Erynnis tristis; E. funeralis; Papilio eurymedon
associatedTaxa: Satyrium saepium; Ochlodes agricola; Erynnis tristis; E. funeralis; Papilio eurymedon

File.Name: j618c_j618-003.jpg
Notes: 48089580608 I found this Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family today. This one isn't small or large but somewhere in the middle, maybe 1/2 inch long. These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing and don't visit flowers, so there's no easy way to find them. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2019)

File.Name: j618d_j618-036.jpg
Notes: 48089485596 I'm pretty sure these are male (left) and female (right) Western Lynx Spiders (Oxyopes scalaris) in the family Oxyopidae. I found them both on different parts of the same plant of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae), and it was curious that the one on the right was sitting right on a flower. They are much smaller than than their Green Lynx cousins, compare this photo, but the spiny legs show a connection. These spiders don't seem to be very common in our mountains, though they are well-camouflaged and keep to themselves. It was interesting that the one on the right hopped away like a Jumping Spider (Salticidae) while I was trying to photograph it. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2019)
ScientificName: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Salticidae

File.Name: j619a_j619-041.jpg
Notes:
48095385892 Here is a handsome little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched for a moment on a dead plant stem. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year, but I've been seeing them. These feisty butterflies seem to spend their time darting forth from a perch to defend its territory from all other passing butterflies - and anything else that moves! This makes them hard to approach, but I think this one was caught off-guard when the chilly fog rolled back in today. It was taking a moment to catch some rays to warm up, and I got my photo. See this photo for a look under their wings. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2019)

We were in the marine layer again today. I had my usual errand in the Santa Ynez Valley, and I discovered that the fog ends just a mile or two down the road! It was clear in the valley, but we were still in the fog when I got home in the afternoon.

File.Name: j619b_j619-017.jpg
Notes: 48095332437 Here is a brilliant new flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae), the first I've seen this year. I found this one growing along Stagecoach Road today. These plants along the road are vulnerable to drive-by pickers, and I hope they get some respect this year - see this photo from last year along a different road. We have many plants in our canyon at home, but I don't think we'll have any flowers again this year as deer and a mystery grub that eats the flower buds have taken them down. They are beautiful flowers, almost too pretty to be native, but they are. There should be butterflies here, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2019)

File.Name: j619c_j619-009.jpg
Notes: 48095236828 These are stunning new flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. I found this plant growing by Stagecoach Road, not far from Cold Springs Tavern - a place where I've never seen them before. I usually find them higher in the mountains along East Camino Cielo, but they were not flowering yet as of a few days ago. It's a summer wildflower that is not common in our mountains, but it's well worth looking for. It's another plant where you have to pinch yourself and remember that it is native. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2019)

File.Name: j619d_j613-007.jpg
Notes: 48095139406 This photo shows spider webbing on whorls of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae) that is already going to seed. I didn't notice at the time, but I also seem to have captured the spider by the second whorl on the right - I'll make a note. I find similar webbing on other plants that have flowers or leaves in whorls, e.g. this photo of Black Sage and this photo of Prickly Phlox. Maybe this is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae? Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2019)

File.Name: j620a_j620-009.jpg
Notes:
48100439498 Here is another little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly leaning over like a living solar collector trying to catch some rays on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). It was chilly and drizzly again today, and it was really too cold for this butterfly to be active. I've seen other butterflies doing the same thing, see this photo and this photo. I don't imagine it did any good today. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2019)

Tomorrow may be the solstice and the first day of summer, but it was gloomy and drizzly today - and the "fog winds" were especially bad. The sun never did come out.

File.Name: j620b_j619-037.jpg
Notes:
48100350203 Here is a mass of new Farewell-to-Spring flowers in the Onagraceae plant family - at the peak of their bloom even though today is the last day of spring. I believe they are the Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica), though these flowers are not at all speckled. They have erect stems and nodding flower buds. The species Clarkia bottae is very similar. These plants have had a good year with some rain for a change. They'll keep flowering for a while. There's also native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) on the right, and two different native grasses just to the left of it. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2019)

Today is the last official day of spring, so farewell to spring. The summer solstice is officially tomorrow on June 21 at 8:54 a.m. PDT. It's the first day of summer when the sun is highest above the ecliptic and the path of the sun is highest in the sky. Happy solstice!
ScientificName: Onagraceae; Clarkia cylindrica; Clarkia bottae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Onagraceae; Clarkia cylindrica; Clarkia bottae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: j620c_j616-026.jpg
Notes: 48100234301 I believe this is a real House Fly in the genus Musca of the family Muscidae. It might even be a Face Fly (Musca autumnalis), see the comments by Thijs de Graaf on this photo of a similar fly from a couple of years ago. I'm happy to say that House Flies are actually pretty uncommon in our woods. This one in perched on a flower bud of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). This is a nice shot of the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2019)
CommonNames: House Fly; Face Fly; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Musca; Muscidae; Musca autumnalis; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Musca; Muscidae; Musca autumnalis; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: j620d_j615-008.jpg
Notes: 48100155731 This is looking at the rear end of the nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) next to a flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). This one is more ornate than the katydid nymph in this photo. It's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, probably Scudderia mexicana. The nymphs are gorgeous, see this photo of maybe the same one. This photo certainly shows the nymph's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2019)
CommonNames: Katydid; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: j621a_j621-022.jpg
Notes:
48106094397 It was overcast and drizzly today - too chilly and wet for insects, but just right for these new flowers of native Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. The succulent plants are just starting to flower here, but I've been seeing them elsewhere in the mountains for some time. They are one of my favorites. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2019)

Yep, it was overcast and drizzly today, but it was nearly dead-calm. After a few days of constant fog winds, this was a treat. We even ended up with nearly 1/10 inch of drizzle in the rain gauge - a wet start to summer.

File.Name: j621b_j618-006.jpg
Notes: 48106053237 Here is a little gold-flecked Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on new flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe this fly is genus Thevenetimyia, maybe the species Thevenetimyia luctifera. Apparently the species name means "light-bearing" which is certainly appropriate. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2019)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Thevenetimyia; Thevenetimyia luctifera
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Thevenetimyia; Thevenetimyia luctifera

File.Name: j621c_j618-010.jpg
Notes: 48105938013 Here is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) visiting a flower of non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra, Brassicaceae) growing down by the creek. Sure, the plant is a weed, but it's been naturalized in California since mission days, and it's now part of the flora. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2019)

File.Name: j621d_j616-033.jpg
Notes: 48105884978 This is an interesting fly (Diptera) that's ovipositing - laying eggs - in sandy soil down by the creek. I was sitting nearby in my chair, when I noticed this fly walking along. It would stop every foot or so and stick its abdomen into the ground, and then walk on and do it again. It finally took off when I got too close. I believe that its a uncommon Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, possibly Thereva comata or Thereva hirticeps. Compare this photo of one also ovipositing in soil at BugGuide. I should add this shot to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Therevidae; Thereva comata; Thereva hirticeps
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Therevidae; Thereva comata; Thereva hirticeps

File.Name: j622a_j622-007.jpg
Notes:
48111247323 This is the caterpillar of an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly by its nest in an Everlasting (Gnaphalium or Pseudognaphalium) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm pretty sure this plant is Wright's Cudweed (Pseudognaphalium microcephalum), but the butterflies seem to use all different everlasting plants. Apparently the caterpillars make their nests from the leaves of the plant, using excreted silk to hold it together. I don't usually find them outside in the daytime, but sometimes I get lucky. See this photo for a later stage of the same kind of caterpillar. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2019)

It did clear up today after noon, but then the marine-layer fog bank sat just over the mountains on the ocean side, with steady fog winds. I'd rather have the fog than the wind.
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium; Pseudognaphalium; Asteraceae; Pseudognaphalium microcephalum
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium; Pseudognaphalium; Asteraceae; Pseudognaphalium microcephalum

File.Name: j622b_j622-026.jpg
Notes: 48111258612 Here is another handsome Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). The plant is a perennial that is still just starting to come up, and this one looks pretty ratty right now growing from last year's dead stems. I've seen them show interest in this plant before, see this photo from last month. It's interesting how striking the butterflies are above, see this photo, and how plain they are below. That's camouflage - they can fold their wings up and virtually disappear. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2019)

File.Name: j622c_j622-045.jpg
Notes: 48111184087 Here is an adult Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that is visiting flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). These butterflies should be common at this time of year, but they've actually been rather scarce. Instead of having one big hatch of them, we seem to have a few at a time - see my [Previous] photo. I found the first caterpillars very early on January 13 and the first adult on April 17, see this photo and this photo. I wonder if our above-normal rainfall might have interferred with their schedule. I've had some concern about them for a while, see the end of my comment on this photo from April 14. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2019)

File.Name: j622d_j622-001.jpg
Notes: 48111102567 This is another caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that is just starting to pupate under a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). These caterpillars typically feed on "scrophs" - plants that used to be in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. Mugwort is something else, but I guess the caterpillar traveled about 20 feet from its host plant before settling down. It seems to take them a few days to form a chrysalis like this one. It's interesting that the adult butterflies have been on the wing for some time and others are still developing. Their timing seems off this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2019)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Scrophulariaceae; chrysalis
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Scrophulariaceae; chrysalis

File.Name: j623a_j623-008.jpg
Notes:
48117546592 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly hanging from a flower cluster of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). The butterfly doesn't look so pale with the sun behind its wings like this - and note the shadows of the flowers on its wings. These butterflies have been scarce this year, and this is the first decent photo I've gotten this year. It's just in time for the Humboldt Lilies, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2019)

The marine layer was lower today, and the fog stayed on the ocean side of the mountains. Without the fog, it was quite warm, into the 80s! I drove down to town in the afternoon, and it was still overcast.

File.Name: j623b_j623-050.jpg
Notes: 48117490252 Here are an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly and a Spotted Flower Buprestid (Acmaeodera) beetle sharing a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is the "violet thistle" as opposed to the "white thistle" - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. The flowers have been open for a few days, but the insects have been slow to find them since it's been so foggy and drizzly lately. Today was sunny, and the flowers are open for business. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2019)
CommonNames: Umber Skipper; Spotted Flower Buprestid; California Thistle
ScientificName: Poanes melane; Acmaeodera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Poanes melane; Acmaeodera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: j623c_j623-053.jpg
Notes: 48117341918 I guess they didn't read the manual! Here are a pair of Buprestid Beetles aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetles (Buprestidae, Coleoptera), probably Spotted Flower Buprestids in the genus Acmaeodera. There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. I believe they were trying to get into a mating position - and after a while, they did, though then they abandoned the effort. They are on a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is the "violet thistle" as opposed to the "white thistle" - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. We only have two flowers so far this year, and that might be all we get after the plants were defoliated by a Painted Lady caterpillar a while back, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2019)
associatedTaxa: Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Acmaeodera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: j623d_j623-029.jpg
Notes: 48117235221 I found this odd little Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. This is an adult with fully developed wings, though I've mostly been finding the nymphs, like this one. I believe it is genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. It's tiny, about 1070 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.2 mm - a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Those skinny wings can't be much help for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops? (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2019)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae

File.Name: j624a_j624-008.jpg
Notes:
48123726761 I was pretty sure I had a Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly in this photo, but I now believe it's another Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta). Compare this photo to appreciate how similar they are. This one is especially confusing because it has several shades of orange and heavier than usual dark markings like a checkerspot, but the markings seem right for it to be a crescent. At least I think that's right! It's perched on a flower cluster of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2019)

Today started clear, but the marine layer showed up later in the morning, lurking just over the mountains on the ocean side, with the usual fog winds. It never really did get foggy today, but the marine layer was close enough to cool things down. That did quiet the butterflies, good for photos.
CommonNames: Gabb's Checkerspot; Mylitta Crescent; Golden Yarrow
ScientificName: Chlosyne gabbii; Phyciodes mylitta; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Chlosyne gabbii; Phyciodes mylitta; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: j624b_j624-014.jpg
Notes: 48123655891 This is a West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly perched for a moment on our chipseal road today. It's the smallest of our Lady butterflies and the least common - at least up here in the mountains. The distinguishing characteristic is the first orange bar in the middle of the front edge of the upper wing which is white in other Ladies, though the American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterflies in our mountains sometimes have a nearly orange bar in that place, compare this photo. There are other differences as well - It has squared-off wing tips, it has four (or five) eyespots below its hindwings, and each of the eyespots has a blue center above. I think size and the shape of the wings are usually enough to tell that it's different. This is the first one I've seen this year, and I never did find one of them last year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2019)

File.Name: j624c_j624-036.jpg
Notes: 48123703107 Here is a handsome Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly working flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). These butterflies are small and quite plain below, but they make up for it by being soft and furry - they look adorable! This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2019)

File.Name: j624d_j624-017.jpg
Notes: 48123599793 This is a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly on new flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's leaning over like a living solar collector trying to catch some rays to stay warm as the marine-layer fog moved in today - though it's leaning away from the camera so it's not so obvious. I've seen other butterflies doing the same thing on chilly days, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2019)

File.Name: j625a_j625-025.jpg
Notes:
48129468421 This is a twofer of Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies working a new flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I was focused on the one on the right when the other one appeared. Lucky shot, but I've been lucky before - see this photo. These swallowtails have been scarce this year, but maybe that's about to change. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2019)

We were in a cloud at home this morning, so I took a drive up East Camino Cielo to see If I could get above the marine-layer fog. I did, but only near the top of what I call FAA Peak - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro at 3600 feet that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. That's where I got all my photos today. Even up there, the marine layer was encroaching. It never did clear out at home.
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae

File.Name: j625b_j625-011.jpg
Notes: 48129428708 Here are a pair of different lady Blue (Polyommatinae) butterflies perched on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). On the left is an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon), and on the right is a Marine Blue (Leptotes marina). The males of each are truly blue on top, but these ladies only have a hint of color. Like ducks, the females are less colorful than the males - but still quite handsome. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2019)
CommonNames: Blue; Deerweed; Acmon Blue; Marine Blue
ScientificName: Polyommatinae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Plebejus acmon; Leptotes marina

Notes: 48129363966 I believe this is a Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera - likely the Red-eared Blister Beetle (Lytta auriculata). It's munching a flower petal of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. I like its shadow down below. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2019)

File.Name: j625d_j623-017.jpg
Notes: 48129352448 Here is another pair of mating Tephritid Fruit Flies (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a cabbage-like flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). Those eyes! These flies might be the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), see BugGuide. I've been seeing mating groups of these flies for some time, sometimes in threes - see this photo. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2019)

File.Name: j626a_j626-016.jpg
Notes:
48135510453 Here is the nymph of a tiny Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) waiting for a meal on old flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). It's the first one I've seen this year, either nymph or adult. It's small, about 1250 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.9 mm - about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's interesting that the adults become common later in summer, but the nymphs are rather scarce - see this photo and this photo. The nymphs usually have these green colors that they lose as adults, which may give them some camouflage. Hah, this one looks like a little Godzilla monster - and maybe it is to the insects that become it's prey! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2019)

Today started with pea soup fog, drizzle, and a breeze that made getting new photos difficult. It did mostly clear up in the afternoon.
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: j626b_j626-018.jpg
Notes: 48135414278 This sleek "true bug" is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) . . . inside a flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae)! It's the adult that comes from the ant-mimic nymphs that I showed in this photo a couple of weeks ago. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. I actually found several of them today - both adults and nymphs - inside different flowers. Well, it was a drizzly morning, and the flowers make good shelter. I usually find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana), but I guess these flowers are another host. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2019)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; White Sage

File.Name: j626c_j624-026.jpg
Notes: 48135403877 This is another tiny Barklouse (Psocoptera aka Psocodea) on a bristly leaf of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). The insect really is tiny, only about 1/8 inch long. Compare this photo and this photo of other ones I've found this year. I don't notice these barklice very often, though I suspect the woods are full of them. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2019)
ScientificName: Psocoptera; Psocodea; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: j626d_j623-002.jpg
Notes: 48135310118 I believe this is a little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the funnel opening of its web. This one is on the windowsill in our bathroom. It's actually a challenge to photograph because it scoots away at the slightest disturbance. I was quiet and lucky with this shot looking down on the web. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2019)

File.Name: j627a_j627-002.jpg
Notes:
48140768102 Here is another West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden today. It's the smallest of our Lady butterflies and the least common - at least up here in the mountains. The distinguishing characteristics are the squared-off forewing tips and the first orange bar in the middle of the front edge of the upper wing which is white in other Ladies. I think size and the shape of the wings are usually enough to tell that it's different. I've seen a few of them already this year, though I never did find one last year. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2019)

Today started clear, but the marine layer showed up before noon and stayed just over the mountains on the ocean side. It never really did get foggy today, but the marine layer was close enough to kick up some fog winds. Darn, our power will be out again "scheduled maintenance" on Friday - and again next Tuesday. This is starting to be ridiculous. I'll post if I can, otherwise my photos for BBBT may have to carry over to FDF.

File.Name: j627b_j627-031.jpg
Notes: 48140631971 This is another goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera on an aging flower cluster of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). It's small, about 2000 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 8 mm - about 5/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm not sure how to distinguish nymphs and adults as both are wingless, but this one seems small enough to be a nymph. They are remarkably variable as you can see if you search all my photos for "Morsea californica". They are not common. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2019)
ScientificName: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: j627c_j627-025.jpg
Notes: 48140669517 This photo shows the furry rear end of a Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on aging flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). The fly is bee-sized, maybe genus Villa or Paravilla or something else in the tribe Villini. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2019)

File.Name: j627d_j627-018.jpg
Notes: 48140589188 Here is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) visiting a flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is the "violet thistle" as opposed to the "white thistle" - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. It's sticking its tail up in the air, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2019)

File.Name: j628a_j628-009.jpg
Notes:
48148759942 I was getting a shot of a little wasp on this native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) when this Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly landed about a foot away! I got the photo almost full-frame. Compare this photo of a different specimen a few days ago that looks more like a Checkerspot. This one looks more typical with upper wings that are mostly one shade of orange. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2019)

Our power was supposed to be out today from 9 to 5. Hah, it dropped out at 8:15 and didn't come back until 6:30. Well that's better then the 10 pm time that the automated phone number gave! I haven't even looked at my new photos today except for these two from this morning. Our next "scheduled maintenance" will happen next Tuesday.

File.Name: j628b_j628-005.jpg
Notes: 48148583906 This is the ant-like nymph of the sleek "true bug" I showed two days ago in this photo. The bug is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera), and this one is hiding among old seed pods of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It looks and moves like an ant, but no ant has a proboscis like that! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2019)

File.Name: j628c_j627-026.jpg
Notes: 48148557861 This a Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on aging flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). The fly is bee-sized, maybe genus Villa or Paravilla or something else in the tribe Villini. I have another shot yesterday of the rear end of this fly in this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2019)

File.Name: j628d_j625-060.jpg
Notes: 48148493536 I believe this dusty fly is a non-biting Deer Fly in the family Tabanidae, probably Stonemyia californica. It's dusty because I found it belly-up on the floor of my truck. I thought it was dead, but it started to climb up my hand and acted quite alive. I placed it on the side of my truck for a photo, and then it flew away. I have a good shot of a less dusty specimen at this photo. I usually see these flies hovering in the air, see this photo. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2019)

File.Name: j629a_j629-024.jpg
Notes:
48155960436 This is a cute little Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris) in the family Oxyopidae that is definitely looking at me. It has a monkey face, like Curious George with little round ears! I found this one on a plant of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae), the same plant where I found both male and female spiders at the beginning of the month, see "https://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/48089485596/">this photo. This one is female. These spiders are much smaller than than their Green Lynx cousins, compare this photo, but the spiny legs show a connection. These spiders don't seem to be very common in our mountains, though they are well-camouflaged and keep to themselves. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2019)

Today was a nice day, though there was an annoying breeze mostly from the south. The temps got up into the low 80s.
ScientificName: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae

File.Name: j629b_j629-010.jpg
Notes: 48155815821 I found this mating pair of little Bee Flies (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. It seems like an odd position, but I actually found them hanging vertically and tilted my camera to make better use of my screen space. If you tilt your head or monitor, maybe it's not such a strange position. The flies might be genus Thevenetimyia, compare this photo. The flies look different enough that it never would occur to me that they are the same kind if I saw them separately. Photos of insects "doing it" are important because it's the one time you can clearly see the difference between male and female. In this case, I guess that's the larger female on the right/top and the smaller male on the left/bottom. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2019)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thevenetimyia
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thevenetimyia

File.Name: j629c_j629-034.jpg
Notes: 48155857437 Here is a little wild bee collecting pollen from a new flower of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). I don't know what kind of wild bee it is, but I'm sure it's in the family Megachilidae. These bees are distinctive because they collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees. The blue pollen of these flowers make this easy to see. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2019)

File.Name: j629d_j629-003.jpg
Notes: 48155700141 It's hard to see the markings on this butterfly's wings - which is the point with this butterfly that is has no strong markings below. It's a Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly. These butterflies were formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that name is now restricted to butterflies from back east. I found the first one this year back in March, see this photo. I believe this is the first one of the second summer brood. It's working flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2019)
CommonNames: Pacific Azure; Echo Azure; Spring Azure; California Coffeeberry
ScientificName: Celastrina echo; Celastrina ladon; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Celastrina echo; Celastrina ladon; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: j630a_j630-018.jpg
Notes:
48165349092 Here is a mama Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on a leaf of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). These butterflies should be common now, but they've actually been rather scarce this year, see the discussion in my comments to this photo. I'm glad that this one is doing her part to ensure a population for next year! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2019)

Today was another nice day, with temps in the mid-80s. It was not as breezy as yesterday.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: j630b_j630-011.jpg
Notes: 48165162881 Here are two different stages or instars of caterpillars of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. I found these both on leaves of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plant in the canyon today. The plants are invasive in our mountains, but we always leave a few for the caterpillars and try to remember to cut the flowers before they make seeds. The butterflies favor this non-native brood plant, but they must have found other Apiacea plants before fennel was introduced, maybe Sanicles or Tauschia, though I've never found a caterpillar on those plants. Yep, the two stages look completely different, and the mature caterpillars look different again, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2019)
ScientificName: instars; Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiacea
associatedTaxa: instars; Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiacea

File.Name: j630c_j630-026.jpg
Notes: 48165103872 This cute little spider is a female of the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus on an oak leaf in the woods. The males have considerably more black on the head, see this photo. This is one Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that you can recognize even without seeing its face with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes. Many jumping spiders look alike, but this one is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2019)

File.Name: j630d_j630-030.jpg
Notes: 48164983877 This tiny spider has just captured a tiny insect. I know, because I watched it happen. I believe the insect is a Cixiid Planthopper in the family Cixiidae, Fulgoroidea. I found it on a nearby leaf and was trying to get a photo when it hopped away to this leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae) . . . and right into the spider's grasp. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, but both spider and prey are tiny, about 900 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm - just over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The spider does have a layer of webbing over the leaf but nothing distinctive. There's also a mystery orb in the photo right below the tip of the hopper's tail, an egg of some sort? (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2019)

File.Name: j701a_j701-023.jpg
Notes:
48175671457 Here is another goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's small, probably less than 3/8 inch long. This is the third one I've found this year, all on different plants. Over the last couple of years, I've found them on Golden Yarrow, Black Mustard, Greenbark Ceanothus, Deerweed, Coast Live Oak, Bush Monkey Flower, and more. I don't like the focus of this photo, but at least its fantastic eyes and antennae are reasonably sharp. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2019)

Today was another nice day, with temps in the 80s. A wind from the nothwest kicked up in the afternoon, and it may be a proper Sundowner Wind as it blows down towards the coast. We are under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight. As it happens, our power will be out again for "scheduled maintenance" all day on Tuesday. I'll post if I can.

*Update* Good news, the power outage has now been canceled. I reckon the NWS Wind Advisory had something to do with that, though it seems quieter now. Scheduling is tough!
CommonNames: Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine
associatedTaxa: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: j701b_j701-002.jpg
Notes: 48175471126 I found this well-camouflaged "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) today on a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). The orange caterpillar blends right in with the yellow/orange flower, and once I spotted this one, I saw several more on the other flowers. That yellow thing that looks like a banana below the caterpillar is just a deformed petal. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2019)

File.Name: j701c_j630-041.jpg
Notes: 48175376991 I had a photo yesterday that showed a little spider with its planthopper prey. Here's another photo of a similar planthopper without the spider, though it's not the same one. I believe it's a Cixiid Planthopper in the family Cixiidae, Fulgoroidea. I found this one after the other one met its fate on a flower petal of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's tiny, about 900 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm - just over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm pretty sure it's the same kind, but I can't explain why the other one looks so blue while this one doesn't. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2019)
CommonNames: Cixiid Planthopper; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: j701d_j628-014.jpg
Notes: 48175388812 This is a Flower Longhorn Beetle (Lepturinae, Cerambycidae, Coleoptera) on a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) - maybe the species Judolia sexspilota. These beetles are pretty common right now on many different kinds of flowers. The sage still has flowers, but they are fading by the day and will soon be done. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2019)
ScientificName: Lepturinae; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Judolia sexspilota

File.Name: j702a_j702-004.jpg
Notes:
48184802491 Here is a brilliant new flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) growing along West Camino Cielo road in Kinevan Canyon today. The plants are just starting to flower, and this particular plant has nineteen buds, so it will be going for some time - see this photo of the same plant last year. These plants along the road are vulnerable to drive-by pickers, and I hope they get some respect this year - see this photo from last year. We have many plants in our canyon at home, though deer and a mystery grub that eats the flower buds have taken most of them down. They are beautiful flowers, almost too pretty to be native, but they are. There should be butterflies here, see this photo from a few years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2019)

Today was another nice day, with temps in the 80s. Winds kicked up again in the afternoon much like yesterday, and we're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight. We've had a house full of people tonight, and I hope these comments make sense!

File.Name: j702b_j702-048.jpg
Notes: 48184784407 Here is a colony of native Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii aka Castilleja martinii) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. The brilliant red color is mostly from petal-like bracts, but the actual flower tubes are also quite red. Flowers and bracts are also quite glandular, even below the flowers - the leaves feel sticky, almost slimy. It's curious that the plants are still flowering in summer, even though I've found them as early as mid-February, see this photo from last year. I expect this of our other Paintbrush species (C. affinis and C. foliolosa), but this one is usually done by now. They are getting a little "long in the tooth" as they keep adding new flowers to their stems. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2019)
ScientificName: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; C. affinis; C. foliolosa
associatedTaxa: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; C. affinis; C. foliolosa

File.Name: j702c_j701-015.jpg
Notes: 48184533487 This is a new flower of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica) in the Rosaceae plant family today in the canyon at home. It's not the first one I've seen this year, but it is the first one I could reach - growing up a steep embankment. The last one of these flowers I managed to photograph was in 2017, and before that is 2015. I guess the drought years have been hard on them. They are doing better this year, look at the buds along with the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2019)

File.Name: j702d_j701-013.jpg
Notes: 48184397542 I believe this native grass is Tall Brome (Bromus grandis) in the Poaceae plant family. I've called this grass California Brome (Bromus carinatus) before, but I'm pretty sure that's wrong. Both grasses have short awns which distinguish them from weedy annuals like Ripgut Grass (B. diandrus) and Red Brome (B. rubens) and the rest of their kin that are trying to take over the woodland and grassland. One further difference is that B. carinatus has flattened spikelets which B. grandis lacks, compare this photo. It is indeed as tall as I am. I have mislabeled this in a few older photos, e.g. here and here. I'll have to get around to correcting this. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2019)
ScientificName: Bromus grandis; Poaceae; Bromus carinatus; B. diandrus; B. rubens; B. carinatus; B. grandis
associatedTaxa: Bromus grandis; Poaceae; Bromus carinatus; B. diandrus; B. rubens; B. carinatus; B. grandis

File.Name: j703a_j703-020.jpg
Notes:
48193142582 This is a mating pair of grasshoppers (Acrididae, Orthoptera) on a leaf of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) that will be flowering any day now. If you look close, you can see that both grasshoppers have very short wings - and they are obviously adults! I believe they are Oedaleonotus phryneicus (Melanoplinae) grasshoppers, and those little stubs are all the wings they will ever get. The colors of these grasshoppers sure is confusing. These are dark, but others are tan - compare this photo. They just are variable, but they all have little stubby wings. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2019)

Today was another nice day, with temps up to about 80∞. I went up East Camino Cielo road looking for red, white, and blue flowers for an Independence Day triptych tomorrow. It was curious that marine-layer clouds seemed to grow in size as I drove along. They never got up to my level in the mountains, though the "fog wind" did.

File.Name: j703b_j703-062.jpg
Notes: 48193046507 I found this Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - almost certainly the species Largus californicus - working new flowers of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae) today. The shrubs are just starting to flower as the first step in producing the red berries which appear in winter and give them their other common name of Christmas Berry. If you look close, you can see pollen on its mouthparts which it will carry to other flowers and other shrubs - thus pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2019)

File.Name: j703c_j703-052.jpg
Notes: 48192938852 Here is a nice combination of native Chia Sage (Salvia columbariae, Lamiaceae) and locally uncommon Wide-throated Yellow Monkeyflower (Mimulus brevipesaka Diplacus brevipes, Phrymaceae). I only know the monkeyflower from this one place in our mountains, but I seem to find it here most years. We're at the northern edge of this species, and it's more common further south. Both flowers are surprising this late in the season. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2019)

File.Name: j703d_j623-023.jpg
Notes: 48192804091 This is the base of a flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I usually call it the "violet thistle" to distinguish it from the "white thistle" that grows higher in the mountains - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. The cobwebby hairs are part of the plant, and give the plant its other common name of Cobweb Thistle - but this one also has real spider webs on it, as well as a couple of tiny spiders! I don't know what kind of spiders they are, but the one you can see looks like the spider with prey in this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2019)

File.Name: j704a_j703-025.jpg
Notes:
48201100612 Here are red, white, and blue flowers for Independence Day. I went for a drive up East Camino Cielo road yesterday and got all of today's photos. These are all natives that are flowering right now.

* Red - Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. The plants in the higher mountains like this one are just starting to flower. These are one of my favorite summer flowers.

* White - Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei) in the Agavaceae plant family aka Yucca whipplei, Liliaceae. Usually they are fading by July, but there are still new stalks that haven't flowered yet.

* Blue - Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The stems are covered in maroon fuzz, but the flowers are true blue. They even look like fireworks going off!

Happy birthday USA! It's a good day to remember our revolutionary heritage. Flowers, not tanks. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2019)

File.Name: j704b_j703-029.jpg
Notes:
48201019427 Today I'm posting red, white, and blue flowers (and berries) for Independence Day. Here are three native red ones that are flowering right now. They really stand out!

* Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. These are the host plants for the Variable Checkerspot buterflies I've been showing.

* Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii aka Castilleja martinii) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. These plants aren't usually flowering this late in summer, but here they are.

* Mexican Pink aka Indian Pink (Silene laciniata ssp. laciniata) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family. The species name "laciniata" sounds like "lacerate" - irregularly slashed and jagged as if torn. Freddy Krueger comes to mind, but I'd rather think of Henri Matisse in his later years.

Happy birthday USA! It's a good day to remember our revolutionary heritage. Flowers, not tanks. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 July 2019)
CommonNames: Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush; Mexican Pink; Indian Pink
ScientificName: Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; Silene laciniata; laciniata; Caryophyllaceae

File.Name: j704c_j703-008.jpg
Notes:
48200909702 Today I'm posting red, white, and blue flowers (and berries) for Independence Day. Here are three native white ones that are flowering right now.

* California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's just starting to flower and will keep going all summer long.

* Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They don't look like flowers unless you notice the yellow centers. They have a sweet maple syrup smell, and they make nice arrangements when dry, but they always end up dusty and full of spider webs.

* Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) in the Convolvulaceae plant family. This one has bracts well below the flower with distinguishes it from C. macrostegia which has bracts by the flower.

Happy birthday USA! It's a good day to remember our revolutionary heritage. Flowers, not tanks. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 July 2019)
CommonNames: California Buckwheat; Green Everlasting; Western Morning-Glory
ScientificName: Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Gnaphalium californicum; Asteraceae; Calystegia purpurata; Convolvulaceae; C. macrostegia
associatedTaxa: Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Gnaphalium californicum; Asteraceae; Calystegia purpurata; Convolvulaceae; C. macrostegia

File.Name: j704d_j703-031.jpg
Notes:
48200801417 Today I'm posting red, white, and blue flowers (and berries) for Independence Day. Here are three native blue ones that are flowering right now. True blue flowers are the hardest to find at this time of year, and even these have some magenta in them. Violet is more common.

* Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. These are not common in our mountains, but there are colonies here and there.

* Ripe berries of Blue Elderberry (Sambucus nigra ssp. caerulea) in the Adoxaceae plant family. These are the first ripe ones I've found this year in the mountains.

* Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's a good-sized shrub, 3-6 feet tall. The flowers should be done by now, but these plants were mowed down by a road crew two years ago, and it's taken them some time to recover.

Happy birthday USA! It's a good day to remember our revolutionary heritage. Flowers, not tanks. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 July 2019)
CommonNames: Chaparral Penstemon; Blue Elderberry; Bush Lupine
ScientificName: Penstemon heterophyllus; Plantaginaceae; Sambucus nigra; caerulea; Adoxaceae; Lupinus longifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Penstemon heterophyllus; Plantaginaceae; Sambucus nigra; caerulea; Adoxaceae; Lupinus longifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: j705a_j705-015.jpg
Notes:
48208983967 I found this little Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (+Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. This fly is a small one, I guess about 1/2 inch long or less. It disappeared a moment after I got the photo. My photo is a deep crop, but it shows a handsome fly. These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing and don't visit flowers, so there's no easy way to find them. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2019)

The marine layer is back. Fog spilled over the mountains from the ocean side this morning, propelled by pretty strong "fog winds". I gave up trying to get photos at home, and went into the valley instead. It paid off.

File.Name: j705b_j702-023.jpg
Notes: 48208875331 This is a non-biting Deer Fly in the family Tabanidae, probably Stonemyia californica, visiting new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It's about the size of a Honey Bee. Hah, this is a less dusty version of the fly in this photo from last week that I found on the floor of my truck. I often see these flies hovering in the air along our road, see this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2019)
ScientificName: Tabanidae; Stonemyia californica; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Tabanidae; Stonemyia californica; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: j705c_j701-011.jpg
Notes: 48208846617 This is a really tiny fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. I believe it's a Frit Fly aka Grass Fly in the family Chloropidae - genus Thaumatomyia in particular, and maybe species Thaumatomyia rubida or Thaumatomyia glabra. I've briefly seen these flies before, but I only have a few photos, most recently this one from December. This fly is really tiny, only about 740 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's probably the smallest fly I know that's genuinely pretty. I should add this shot to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2019)
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Frit Fly; Grass Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Chloropidae; Thaumatomyia; Thaumatomyia rubida; Thaumatomyia glabra
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Chloropidae; Thaumatomyia; Thaumatomyia rubida; Thaumatomyia glabra

File.Name: j705d_j628-022.jpg
Notes: 48208726726 I found this little fly (Diptera) on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It's only about 760 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but one possibility is another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae). I should add this shot to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2019)

File.Name: j706a_j706-021.jpg
Notes:
48217207586 Here is a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I think the butterfly was really interested in the California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) growing next to it, but I probably drove it away from that plant with my attentions. I did get a photo of it ovipositing on the Coffeeberry, but this is the better photo. It's curious that neither of these plants is listed as a host plant in my usual butterfly book An Introduction to Southern California Butterflies. This is the first of these butterflies that I've seen his year, and it seems late - but better late than never! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2019)

The wind from the ocean side of the mountains suddenly kicked up this morning, and I was sure the marine-layer fog was about to spill over . . . but it never did, just the wind. We had an oak tree drop a large branch across our road last night. We managed to trim and drag it to the side this morning. I'm worried about our trees - too many years of drought followed my too much heavy new growth this year.
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: j706b_j706-001.jpg
Notes: 48217147672 Here is a mature caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly, the first I've seen this year. It seems to be crawling out of it's old skin! These caterpillars go through a number of different stages or instars as they mature, see this photo and this photo. The earlier stages all seem to have little spines on their backs, which this mature stage lacks. I found this one on leaves of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plant in the canyon today. The plants are invasive in our mountains, but we always leave a few for the caterpillars and try to remember to cut the flowers before they make seeds. It was most peculiar - after I got this photo, the caterpillar turned around and seemed to be eating its old skin. I'll post a photo tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2019)
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; instars; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; instars; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: j706c_j706-030.jpg
Notes: 48217035262 This is some kind of Long-horned Orthopteran (Ensifera, Orthoptera) in the throat of a flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's tiny, about 1000 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.9 mm - a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I believe it's the nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae), compare this photo of a larger one from last year. Sometimes they have a stripe, sometimes not - maybe different kinds? Once I noticed this one, I noticed a few more in other flowers nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2019)
CommonNames: Long-horned Orthopteran; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Tree Cricket
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Oecanthus; Gryllidae
