File.Name: j706d_j705-014.jpg
Notes: 48216969162 I found this odd Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) yesterday. It's tiny, only about 850 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This one looks subtly different from the ones I've been finding like this one, so I won't try to guess what it will become. It seems to have jet exhaust coming out of its rear end! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2019)

File.Name: j707a_j707-023.jpg
Notes:
48227138806 I'm looking down on this little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly that's leaning over like a living solar collector trying to catch some rays on an old composite stem. We were in a cloud today, and it was really too chilly for this butterfly to be active. I've seen other butterflies doing the same thing, see my photos here, here and here. When I came back along the same path an hour later, the butterfly was gone - so maybe it did some good. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2019)

The marine-layer fog moved in early this morning and stayed most of the day. With the fog comes the "fog winds". It was not a great day for getting photos, but I had pretty good luck finding things to photograph. Nothing to do but kick up the ISO, increase the aperture, and take lots of photos!

File.Name: j707b_j707-034.jpg
Notes: 48227006656 Here is the caterpillar of a Lycaenid butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group that includes Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks. It's on flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I don't know what kind of butterfly it will become, though this happens to be the same shrub where I found a Brown Elfin butterfly ovipositing yesterday, see this photo. It's my impression that Brown Elfin caterpillars are typically more patterned than this one, but see this photo by Alice Abelia at BugGuide that looks very similar. Who knows? (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2019)

File.Name: j707c_j707-006.jpg
Notes: 48226908646 This is a Soldier Beetle aka Leather-winged Beetle (Cantharidae, Coleoptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) this morning. We were deep in marine-layer fog this morning, and it was chilly enough that this beetle wasn't moving. There are over 100 species in California, so I'll leave it at that. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2019)
CommonNames: Soldier Beetle; Leather-winged Beetle; Wild Sweetpea

File.Name: j707d_j707-016.jpg
Notes: 48226814817 This is a tiny parasitic wasp inserting its slender ovipositor into a ripening fruit of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I'm pretty sure the wasp is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea, Hymenoptera) - maybe subfamily Eurytominae, see bleu.geo's comment to this photo of the same kind of wasp from two years ago. It seems odd that the wasp's ovipositor comes from the front of its abdomen, but I believe they fold it under their belly to the middle like this. I call it a "parasitic wasp", but I'm not sure if the wasp is laying its eggs in the fruit or into a grub inside the fruit. Some of these Chalcid wasps are plant feeders, some are gall inducers, and some appear to be hyperparasites that parasitize the larvae inside of galls - see this photo and this photo. It was a pretty crummy day to try to get a photo like this, but I tried. I'll try again on a day that's not so breezy. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2019)
CommonNames: parasitic wasp; Greenbark Ceanothus; Chalcid Wasp
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Chalcidoidea; Hymenoptera; Eurytominae
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Chalcidoidea; Hymenoptera; Eurytominae

File.Name: j708a_j708-011.jpg
Notes:
48236786396 Here are front and side views of a cute little Treehopper (family Membracidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a furry leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I guess it's a Buffalo Treehopper in the genus Ceresa or Stictocephala. I have more photos of these critters on the same plant here, here, and here. In fact, I only seem to find these green ones on this Yerba Santa shrub, which suggests a relationship. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2019)

We were in a cloud again today, with "fog winds" and chilly temps (for July). It was less breezy than it was yesterday, but it was still a challenge to get photos. Each of my photos today has issues, but at least I'm out there trying.
Locality: for July).  It was less breezy than it was yesterday

File.Name: j708b_j708-029.jpg
Notes: 48236677281 Here are two shots of a mature caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly - showing its osmeterium! That's the brilliant orange "thingie" on it's forehead that looks like gummy bear antlers. There's some info at Wikipedia. It's an old naturalist trick to tickle the caterpillar's forehead with a grass stem to bring it out. It doesn't always work, but this time it did. (It's a great trick with kids!) It disappears in a few seconds. The first time I saw this I almost jumped out of my skin - it appears that fast! I've also seen this on less-mature stages of the caterpillars like in my [Previous] photo, but it disappears even faster. This caterpillar is on a stem of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2019)

File.Name: j708c_j708-022.jpg
Notes: 48236675402 This is another caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly crawling out of it's old skin. This caterpillar is less mature than the one I showed in this photo a couple of days ago. These caterpillars go through a number of different stages or instars as they mature, and this is probably the stage I show on the bottom of this photo. It's probably crawling out of the skin of the other caterpillar in that photo. These earlier stages all seem to have little spines on their backs, which the mature stage lacks, compare my [Next] photo. I found this one on leaves of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plant today. The plants are invasive in our mountains, but they have become important for the butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2019)
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; instars; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; instars; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: j708d_j708-034.jpg
Notes: 48236500551 Here are a pair of little beetles (Coleoptera) doing *something* on a flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I don't know what they're doing - acrobatics? fighting? getting in a position to mate? Whatever it is, they separated in a few minutes and went their separate ways. I believe these are Soft-winged Flower Beetles in the beetle family Melyridae, maybe genus Collops but maybe not. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2019)
CommonNames: beetles; California Buckwheat; Soft-winged Flower Beetles
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Melyridae; Collops
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Melyridae; Collops

File.Name: j709a_j709-013.jpg
Notes:
48245779772 Look close . . . this is not an ant but a remarkable ant mimic! It's a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that looks and moves like a real ant, but no ant has a proboscis like that. It even has a faux "nodal spine" like some of our ants have for real. This is actually an adult that took wing while I was taking my photos. That distinguishes it from the more common ant-mimic nymphs of the species Closterocoris amoenus that I've been showing, e.g. in this photo. This one is on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm not sure what kind of true bug it is, maybe a Broad-headed Bug (Alydidae)? I have found these before, see this photo. Any help with identification will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2019)

The marine layer spilled over the mountains from the coast again this morning. Then it retreated but stayed close as evidenced by the annoying "fog winds". It did finally warm up in the afternoon. For the rest of the week, the marine layer is expected to recede and the temps to increase. That's fine by me.
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Closterocoris amoenus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Alydidae

File.Name: j709b_j709-021.jpg
Notes: 48245575121 I found this mating pair of non-biting Deer Flies in the family Tabanidae, probably Stonemyia californica, on a flower of of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) today. It seems like an odd position, but compare this photo of mating Bee Flies from last week. These flies don't seek a blood meal like most other tabanid species but visit flowers instead, see this photo. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2019)

File.Name: j709c_j709-029.jpg
Notes: 48245496406 Here is another Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on a leaf of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). I believe the butterfly is female because of its mostly brown upper wings, and I like this photo because it shows both the top and bottom of its wings at once. The Cordylanthus plant was coming up in a place where there's lots of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) - the butterfly's real interest. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2019)
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: j709d_j709-035.jpg
Notes: 48245455477 This is the nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on the calyx of a flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, probably Scudderia mexicana. The nymphs are particularly striking, though the adults they will become are less so, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2019)

File.Name: j710a_j710-028.jpg
Notes:
48253684111 Here is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a plant of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, probably Scudderia mexicana, but it's growing up to become an adolescent with little wing stubs. It's different from the one I showed yesterday in this photo, and very different from the tiny nymph in this photo from May. These nymphs are particularly striking, though they become less cute as they mature, see this photo of an adult. *Sigh* It happens to all of us. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2019)

It did warm up to 80∞ today, and the marine layer stayed on the other side of the mountains. There was still an annoying breeze from that direction. For the rest of the week, the marine layer is expected to recede and the temps to increase.

File.Name: j710b_j710-023.jpg
Notes: 48253620151 Here is a handsome little Beetle (Coleoptera) with red epaulettes on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I'm pretty sure it's a Red-shouldered Leaf Beetle (Saxinis saucia) in the Chrysomelidae group of beetles, at least that is the genus. It's about the size of a ladybug. The beetle at first looks black, but I can see a hint of very deep blue as well - compare this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2019)

File.Name: j710c_j710-035.jpg
Notes: 48253566636 This is the retreat of another Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae), though it looks nothing like the retreat in my [Previous] photo. I didn't notice any proper orb web associated with this spider web, so maybe it's a new effort? It looks like . . . a thimble made of gauze? Hah, you can see the spider curled up at the top of the webbing - along with the bodies of past victims. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2019)

File.Name: j710d_j705-025.jpg
Notes: 48253518752 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with its retreat on the left and its proper orb web on the right. The retreat of this spider is remarkable - like a silk sack that's maybe three inches long. The spider is actually sticking its legs out at the bottom, holding the special strand of webbing that connects to its orb web, see this photo. The orb web itself seems damaged, and I hope I didn't do it. The spider web is in a plant of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's an unusual perennial composite with an interesting common name that has heads of pretty two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. They deserve their own photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 5 July 2019)

File.Name: j711a_j711-013.jpg
Notes:
48261435511 It would be easy to take this as a plain moth, but those clubbed antennae give it away as a butterfly. This one has no strong markings below even when it is fresh. It's a Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly. It's part of the summer brood as opposed to the spring brood. I found the first one this year way back in March, see this photo. I found this one on a flowering shrub of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae) today. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2019)

I had an errand in town this morning and didn't get home until after noon. There was no marine layer this morning that I could see. It was in the upper 80s today, so we are starting to warm up. The wind was pretty calm today, but we're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight. It's pretty calm so far.

File.Name: j711b_j711-003.jpg
Notes:
48261309451 This is a little wild bee collecting pollen from a new flower of native native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I don't know what kind of wild bee it is, but I'm sure it's in the family Megachilidae. These bees are distinctive because they collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees - you can see the special hairs for that purpose. I have another shot of a similar bee on a different Phacelia, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2019)

If you look close, you can see a difference between the anthers of the flower the bee is visiting and that other flower in the background. The bee's flower has just opened, and this is the first bee to visit it. The flower in the background has been visited again and again, and it's pollen is all gone - leaving these empty anthers behind.

File.Name: j711c_j710-019.jpg
Notes: 48261294412 Here are two shots of different female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterflies in the woods. The photo on the left shows the buterfly's upper wings - brown not blue. She's actually on a flowering plant of native Spanish Clover (Acmispon americanus, Fabaceae), but its growing up through a plant of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae. I watched her oviposit of the clover before I got this photo. The shot on the right shows the other side of the wings of a different lady butterfly that is ovipositing on native Deerweed. Both shots show the butterfly's rear ends, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2019)

File.Name: j711d_j709-011.jpg
Notes: 48261239922 This is the rear end of the remarkable ant-mimic "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that I showed in this photo a couple of days ago. It's on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is an adult that took wing while I was taking my photos, and you can make out its wings neatly folded around its rear end. I'm not sure what kind of true bug it is, maybe a Broad-headed Bug (Alydidae). My photo certainly shows the bug's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2019)

File.Name: j712a_j712-027.jpg
Notes:
48268874321 Here is another handsome Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly showing off its beautiful upper wings on a plant of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon. They can sit quietly for a long time with their wings closed, looking quite plain, and then open their wings in a quick flash! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2019)

It was downright HOT today, into the 90s, and it will stay hot for a few days - we're under a NWS Heat Advisory for the weekend. There was little breeze today, and I hope it stays that way. The combination of heat and wind makes me nervous.

File.Name: j712b_j712-009.jpg
Notes: 48268838007 I found both of these cute little Treehoppers (family Membracidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on different plants in the woods today. They are clearly different - compare their shape and their eyes. The one on the left in on non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) - the same plant we keep around for Anise Swallowtail caterpillars, see this photo. It looks different from this one that I found on Trask's Yerba Santa that is also green, though it may be another Buffalo Treehopper in the genus Ceresa or Stictocephala. The brown one on the right might be the Coast Live Oak Treehopper (Telamona vestita), see BugGuide for more info. It is on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's interesting that the color of each treehopper matches their plant - are they color shifters? The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2019)
CommonNames: Treehoppers; Fennel; Buffalo Treehopper; Coast Live Oak Treehopper; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Membracidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Ceresa; Stictocephala; Telamona vestita; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j712c_j709-002.jpg
Notes: 48268694271 This is just a fly (order Diptera) perched for a moment on last year's fruit of a Prickly Pear cactus in the garden. I suspect it's a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae. It's one those flies you probably don't want to see walking across your potato salad. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2019)

File.Name: j712d_j708-008.jpg
Notes: 48268643331 Here is another odd-looking fly (Diptera) on a faded flower of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). It's small, only about 1/8 inch long. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it has remarkable eyes and interesting antennae. The "double antennae" are actually ordinary antennae with an exaggerated arista - that usually hair-like bristle that arises from the base of the antennae of many flies. I have found a Syrphid Fly with antennae like this, see this photo, but this fly is something else. I've seen one of these before, see this photo from a month ago, also on Coffeeberry. Any help with identification will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2019)

File.Name: j713a_j713-006.jpg
Notes:
48276982072 Here are freshly hatched eggs of some "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in the woods. I found these on a leaf in a flowering shrub of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae) today. I count 14 eggs and 14 nymphs, so it was a good hatch. I have no idea what they will become. There was a breeze when I got this photo, and I took many shots to get this keeper. I'll check back on them tomorrow morning to see how they're doing. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2019)

Today was another HOT one, but it was actually a few degrees cooler than yesterday. It was pretty breezy in the morning - one of those annoying breezes from the south that keeps everything in motion. Hah, my truck has been in the shop for a few days because the "Check Engine" light came on and it was running rough. It turns out I have mice or a wood rat in the engine. This is one of the unexpected drawbacks of living in the woods.

File.Name: j713b_j713-012.jpg
Notes: 48276884567 This is another shot (taken today) of one of the little Treehoppers (family Membracidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) that I showed yesterday in this photo. I believe it is the Coast Live Oak Treehopper (Telamona vestita), and it is indeed on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I found this one just a few inches away from where I found it yesterday, so I guess they don't wander much. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2019)

File.Name: j713c_j713-024.jpg
Notes: 48276788912 This is a tiny Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). These slender bugs are small but alert - almost always when I go to get a photo they immediately amble off, usually disappearing under the flower or leaf they are on. It's remarkable how many different insects and spiders I've found on or in these flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2019)

File.Name: j713d_j712-006.jpg
Notes: 48276665162 Here is another mature caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on a stem of of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae). If you look below the caterpillar in the center near the bottom of my photo, you can see a tiny Parasitic Wasp - maybe a Braconid Wasp (Braconidae, Hymenoptera), though are other possibilities. It's a stretch to think that such a tiny wasp might bring down a relatively huge caterpillar like this but see this photo and this photo. There's also a tiny egg further up the same leaf-filament. I don't know what that is. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2019)

File.Name: j714a_j714-033.jpg
Notes:
48285912457 Here is a little Blue (Polyommatinae) butterfly working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The problem is that we might have both Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterflies that typically feed on legumes like Deerweed and Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupini) butterflies that feed on buckwheat. They are said to be indistinguishable, and it gets tricky with both plants in the area, see this photo for some discussion of these very similar butterflies. I guess I don't really care what this one is - it's a handsome butterfly working handsome flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2019)

Yipes, it was downright HOT today, up to 96∞ according to the thermometer on the upstairs deck - in the shade of course. Our NWS Heat Advisory expires tonight, but it sounds like we're in for one more hot day tomorrow. Then it will start to cool down on Tuesday and Wednesday.
CommonNames: Blue; California Buckwheat; Acmon Blue; Lupine Blue

File.Name: j714b_j714-007.jpg
Notes: 48285827972 This sleek "true bug" is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera). It's the adult that comes from the ant-mimic nymphs that I've been showing e.g. in this photo. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. This one is by a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) - which still has a few fresh flowers on it, though they're fading fast. You can see the bug's impressive proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2019)

File.Name: j714c_j714-011.jpg
Notes: 48285733062 This is another tiny Barklouse (Psocoptera aka Psocodea) on a leaf of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae). The insect really is tiny, only 560 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) That's less than I thought before because I can see in this photo that the insect isn't as long as its wings. Compare this photo, this photo, and this photo of other ones I've found this year. I don't notice these barklice very often, though I suspect the woods are full of them. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2019)

File.Name: j714d_j712-036.jpg
Notes: 48285583777 I found this medium-size Beetle (Coleoptera) a couple of days ago on a large branch that recently fell from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree along our driveway. When I first noticed the beetles, there were about a dozen of them, and mating was going on. The beetles are very fast - running around and also taking wing. I went back the next day, and just just found this one for a photo. My first thought was that it's a Tiger Beetle (genus Cicindelidae), but that doesn't seem right - for habitat and other reasons. Now I think it might be a Long-horned Beetle in the family Cerambycidae, maybe species Rusticoclytus annosus. This one does have rather short antennae for that family, and it's missing one to boot. Any confirmation or help with identification will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2019)
CommonNames: Beetle; Coast Live Oak; Tiger Beetle; Long-horned Beetle
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cicindelidae; Cerambycidae; Rusticoclytus annosus
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cicindelidae; Cerambycidae; Rusticoclytus annosus

File.Name: j715a_j715-008.jpg
Notes:
48294847352 This is a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden today. This is the first one I've seen this year, and I hope there are many more. It's also good timing to get a photo of this moth for Moth Monday - HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2019)

Today was another hot one, up to the mid-90s according to the thermometer on the upstairs deck - in the shade of course. Now we're under a NWS Wind Advisory with 30+ mph gusts so far from the north. This feels like a classic Sundowner Wind which picks up heat and speed as it blows down towards the coast. I'm trying not to think too much about that F-word that rhymes with "ire".

File.Name: j715b_j715-018.jpg
Notes: 48294643796 I found this mating pair of Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterflies on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) today. I actually got to watch them flutter around each other and finally connect. It's interesting that the butterflies have slightly different colors, but I don't know if that means anything. I like the sparkles on the black eyes of their wings. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2019)

File.Name: j715c_j714-042.jpg
Notes: 48294654117 Here are two shots of a little Beetle (Coleoptera) on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I suspect it is a Soft-winged Flower Beetle in the beetle family Melyridae, though it is different from the beetles in this photo. This little beetle was moving around pretty quickly, and my focus is off in both shots - but maybe it works if you put them together. What strange mouthparts the beetle has in the photo on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2019)
CommonNames: Beetle; California Buckwheat; Soft-winged Flower Beetle

File.Name: j715d_j706-020.jpg
Notes: 48294458356 Here are two shots of a Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Hymenoptera) on sandy soil - probably its nest is nearby. I usually find these wasps on flowers like the California Thistle in this photo, but I've had no luck finding them this year. I got these two photos from some distance away over a week ago - I'm not sure I'll get a better photo this year, so here they are. You can see the wasp's remarkable "third eye" between its goggle-like real eyes on the right - see this photo for a closer shot. These wasps are so fast and alert that it's hard to get decent photos of them. I was trying to get a better shot today, and I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2019)

File.Name: j716a_j716-045.jpg
Notes:
48303605981 This is a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's coming up through a plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae, and the flower is just starting to open. This plant is rated 1B.3 by the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants which I think means: uncommon, endemic to California, and not very endangered. The flowers are highly variable, and I want to photograph every one I see because they are all so different! I got my photos today near what I call FAA Peak - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro at 3600 feet that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2019)

It was definitely cooler today - in the mid-80s instead of the mid-90s. Tomorrow is expected to drop another 10∞ into the mid-70s and then into the 60s by the weekend. Today was very nice, but here was a good breeze from the south when I was trying to get my photos. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory again tonight for winds from the north, but not much is happening yet.
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: j716b_j716-010.jpg
Notes: 48303459406 Here is a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. This plant is rated 1B.3 by the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants which I think means: uncommon, endemic to California, and not very endangered. They are just starting to flower higher in our mountains, and it will be a while before they flower in the canyon at home. The flowers are highly variable, and I want to photograph every one I see because they are so different! Did you notice the tiny wild bee flying in from the left? See my [Previous] photo for a closer look and some info. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2019)

File.Name: j716c_j716-038.jpg
Notes: 48303340536 Here are two shots of tiny "Calochortus Bees" (my name) on petals of a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. These bees are quite shy, and I never did get an acceptable photo of a whole bee today, but it was interesting how often I caught them looking right at me like in these shots. I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae - maybe Perdita californica or Perdita calochorti. I don't find these bees anywhere else than these flowers, and the flowers don't last very long - see this photo from last year of a bee waiting for an unopened flower bud to open. I was looking for these bees today, but the flowers are just starting and it was breezy. I'll go back to this place in the next day or two and try again. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2019)

File.Name: j716d_j716-019.jpg
Notes: 48303384607 This is another diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). This interesting summer-flowering plant is just starting to bloom higher in the mountains, but it still needs more time in the canyon at home. This isn't as sharp a photo as my [Previous] shot (here), but I wanted to show the fantastic moth on a native plant where it belongs. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2019)

File.Name: j717a_j717-005.jpg
Notes:
48311675382 Here are two shots of a handsome Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly sitting on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). These butterflies are small and quite plain below, but they make up for it by being soft and furry - they look adorable! That dark streak on the forewings is the butterfly's stigma - a glandular patch that releases pheromones during courtship that only males have. I don't think this one is feeding from the flowers - it's hanging on as the wind picked up! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2019)

Today started sunny but then marine-layer clouds started to rise up over the mountains and the "fog winds" kicked up. It was definitely cooler than yesterday - mid-70s instead of mid-80s. By Friday, it's expected to drop into the mid-60s.

File.Name: j717b_j717-010.jpg
Notes: 48311584827 I believe this is an Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper, and those little stubs are all the wings it will ever get. It seems to be feeding on the foliage of this native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The colors of these grasshoppers is confusing. This one is tan, but others are quite dark - compare this photo. (Hah - and compare this photo at BugGuide by Alice Abelia of a completely black individual!) They just are variable, but they all have those little stubby wings. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2019)

File.Name: j717c_j716-020.jpg
Notes:
48311294046 Here is webbing covering a plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae), but it's the work of caterpillars not spiders. If you look close, you can see a couple of caterpillars towards the top of my photo, and lots more inside the "tent" - along with a lot of frass (poop). The caterpillars will become Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies, but not until next year. They spin their creepy webs on their host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now mean butterflies next year. Spiders aren't the only ones, so Happy Web Webnesday! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2019)

It's curious . . . I usually find these caterpillars on Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) like in this photo, but I haven't found any this year. I did find one butterfly ovipositing last month, see this photo, but the eggs didn't develop. I have found them on Bird's Beak before, but its unusual - see this photo. I got my photo today in the higher mountains at about 3600 feet. These are typically one of our most common butterflies at home at a lower elevation, but something is happening to them this year.
CommonNames: Bird's Beak; Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella
ScientificName: Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; frass; Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; frass; Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: j717d_j714-022.jpg
Notes: 48311336667 I believe this is cribellate spider webbing on a seed-bearing stem of a native grass, probably Small-flowered Melic (Melica imperfecta, Poaceae). The webbing might be the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae. The webbing is frizzy and comprised of very fine threads that are spun into a strand like hand-spun wool or chenille yarn, different from ordinary spider webbing. I have another photo of similar webbing here. There's more info at Wikipedia and a nice article in The Atlantic. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2019)
ScientificName: cribellate spider webbing; Melica imperfecta; Poaceae; Dictynidae
associatedTaxa: cribellate spider webbing; Melica imperfecta; Poaceae; Dictynidae

File.Name: j718a_j718-013.jpg
Notes:
48318903436 Here is the nymph of a tiny Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a bristly seed head of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). This nymph will become the brown adult in this photo, which was taken a few feet away on a different plant. I've found these nymphs on this plant before, see this photo and more if you search. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2019)

It was a challenge to get decent photos today as it was both gloomy and breezy all morning. The marine-layer gloom finally backed up to the level of the mountains after noon, but the fog winds hung around for a while. It stayed cool today, only up to about 70 degrees.

File.Name: j718b_j718-003.jpg
Notes: 48318826931 This is another "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. It's about an inch long, so it's a real inchworm. That's its rear end on the left with two pairs of prolegs, and its head on the right with three pairs of true legs. It was motionless and quite rigid, supported by a line of webbing. That brown blur behind it is another caterpillar. Pretty good camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2019)

File.Name: j718c_j714-015.jpg
Notes: 48318898227 Here is a small dark Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae) in the woods. I believe it's a Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa, Villini), at least it's similar to that species. They've been slow to appear this year, and this is the first one I've managed to photograph. My photo does show the fly's furry behind, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2019)

The bug is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera), and it's by a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). It has an attractive rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2019)

File.Name: j719a_j719-013.jpg
Notes:
48326452676 This is another Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These butterflies have no strong markings below even when fresh, though the males at least are quite blue on top, see this photo. You can see a hint of blue in today's shot. This one is part of the summer brood as opposed to the spring brood. They are one of our first new butterflies. I found the first one this year way back in March, see this photo - and here they are again. This one is tilted towards the sun, trying to warm up on a chilly morning. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2019)

Today was much like yesterday, though the marine-layer fog moved in a little later and moved out a little earlier, and it was not so breezy. It sounds like tomorrow will be more of the same, then it will start warming up on Sunday
CommonNames: Pacific Azure; Echo Azure; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j719b_j719-001.jpg
Notes: 48326498557 Here is an update on the fresh hatch of some "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that I showed a few days ago in this photo. The nymphs are (probably) less than a week old now. They are on a leaf of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae) in the woods. There are 14 empty eggs and 14 nymphs, so all are still accounted for. All have recently molted and changed their appearance - except for that one in the center that looks smaller and still has a red head. I don't know what kind of true bugs they will become, but it's fun to watch them grow up. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2019)

File.Name: j719c_j717-018.jpg
Notes: 48326424012 I found this impressive fly (Diptera) on a blade of grass in the garden. I'm pretty sure it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), maybe genus Peleteria - compare the photos at BugGuide. If I'm right, the larvae of these flies parasitize other insect larvae. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2019)

File.Name: j719d_j716-040.jpg
Notes: 48326351717 This is a nasty biting fly in our mountains - on my leg. I was wearing shorts of course, sitting on the folding chair I bring along on my excursions. These flies hurt! I believe it is in the family Tabanidae of Horse and Deer Flies, but it is not much larger than a house fly and its eyes are quite plain unlike many other Tabanids. I believe it is genus Apatolestes in the subfamily Pangoniinae, maybe the species Apatolestes hinei. I reckon it's OK to call it a Deer Fly even though it is not in the subfamily Chrysopsinae. Usually the Rocky Mountain Bite Flies (Symphoromyia, Rhagionidae) - see this photo and this photo - are the worst biters in our mountains, but lately I've been bothered more by these. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2019)
CommonNames: Deer Fly; Rocky Mountain Bite Flies

File.Name: j720a_j720-005.jpg
Notes:
48333935902 Here is another little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly that's leaning over like a living solar collector trying to catch some rays on a chilly morning. It's on a dead stem in a clump of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that's still flowering, which is the butterfly's host plant. I've shown a number of similar butterflies lately because they are one of the few insects I can count on finding on these foggy mornings. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2019)

The fog came in, the fog went out. Then the fog came back in and stayed, driven by strong fog winds. Finally in the afternoon it cleared up, but it remained cool and breezy. *Sigh* It was that kind of day, but I still managed to get a couple of decent photos.

File.Name: j720b_j720-028.jpg
Notes: 48333637086 This isn't the best photo, but it's an interesting moth, and this happens to be the first day of this year's National Moth Week! This moth only has about a 1/2 inch wingspan, and this is a very tight crop because it flew away just after I got my one and only photo. I believe it is Pyrausta subsequalis aka Pyrausta insequalis, a Crambid Snout Moth in the Crambidae moth family. I suspect it's a diurnal (daytime) moth because that's when I found it. They are said to favor weedy places that contain thistles and plantain - and that's where I found it. They are handsome moths despite their taste for weeds. It's an interesting family of butterfly-like moths, see this photo of a different one. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2019)

File.Name: j720c_j718-016.jpg
Notes: 48333723487 This is the nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a bristly seed head of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). I don't know what kind of true bug it will become, but the nymph is quite attractive. The seed heads of the plant are attractive too, see this photo for a closer look. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2019)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: j720d_j720-002.jpg
Notes: 48333493531 Here are new flowers on a plant of native Coast Tarweed (Madia sativa) in the Asteraceae plant family. I don't usually get to photograph these very glandular flowers because they close up early in the morning, though they get tricked on gloomy days like today and stay open longer. We also have its cousin Slender Tarweed (Madia gracilis) with similar but smaller flowers, see this photo. That one flowers more in the spring and is just about done, while this one is just starting to flower in mid-summer. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2019)

File.Name: j721a_j721-033.jpg
Notes:
48342602796 Here is a handsome Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on a new flower cluster of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) growing in the canyon at home. This butterfly is a male with those deep blue upper wings - and this photo really shows the difference between blue and violet colors! This is one of my favorite summer flowers - also a favorite of some unusual butterflies like the Callippe Fritillary in this photo from last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2019)

We didn't have any marine-layer fog today, but it was still quite breezy from the south this morning. It got up into the 80s in the afternoon, very nice except for that breeze.
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: j721b_j721-006.jpg
Notes: 48342509341 This is a not-so-pale Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly resting for a moment on foliage of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. These butterflies look less pale as we move into summer - they get a cream color, and sometimes I confuse them with Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus). This one was interested in the flowers of the hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden nearby, but I like this photo better. These fine butterflies have been uncommonly scarce this year for some reason. I was happy to find this one today, and I hope here will be many more. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2019)
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; P. rutulus; Salvia x 'Waverly'; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; P. rutulus; Salvia x 'Waverly'; Lamiaceae

File.Name: j721c_j721-034.jpg
Notes: 48342372701 This is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a new flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I did find one of these butterflies in March this year, see this photo. I believe these butterflies overwinter as adults, and this is probably the first of the new brood. My photo is overexposed, but I like it anyway - as a record shot, and also because the butterfly fits the flower so well. It's always been a mystery to me what insects these flowers are adapted for, and this photo shows a possibility. Another possibility is the California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis) in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2019)
ScientificName: Colias eurydice; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Hemaris thetis
associatedTaxa: Colias eurydice; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Hemaris thetis

File.Name: j721d_j721-018.jpg
Notes: 48342436317 Here is another adolescent nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, probably Scudderia mexicana, but it's growing up. It's different from the tiny nymph in this photo from May. These nymphs are particularly striking, though they become less cute as they mature, see this photo of an adult. This one happens to be missing its right-side hind leg. I've always wondered whether they can get it back after their a future molt. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2019)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: j722a_j722-036.jpg
Notes:
48352355381 This is another diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). Sure, it's a challenge to photograph these moths that fly so fast among the summer flowers and (almost) never stop. Happy National Moth Week and Moth Monday! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2019)

I went back to the place that I call FAA Peak today - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro at 3600 feet that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. It's a good place for these interesting summer flowers. I could look down on the marine-layer fog which was hugging the coast instead of spilling over the mountains. It was a nice day, temps in the mid-80s, not much breeze.
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: j722b_j722-042.jpg
Notes: 48352387527 Here are two shots of an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on a new flower of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). It's the first adult I've seen this year, though I have been seeing their caterpillars on different Everlasting plants, see this photo. This butterfly is unique among our Ladies in having just two eyespots under its hindwing. It also almost always has a white dot in the first orange rectangle along the forewing. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2019)
CommonNames: American Lady; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: j722c_j722-041.jpg
Notes: 48352184896 Here is a tiny Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) waiting for a meal among the new flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). This is the first adult I've seen this year, though I did find a nymph last month, see this photo. These are small, but they are effective predators that can bring down prey that is much larger than they are - like the butterflies in this photo and this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2019)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: j722d_j722-033.jpg
Notes: 48352221587 I found this striking green Crab Spider (Thomisidae) under a summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family today. We do have the green crab spider Diaea livens that I show in this photo, but that one is more bristly, has a differently colored abdomen, and is associated with oak trees. This one is something else. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2019)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Diaea livens
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Diaea livens

File.Name: j723a_j723-006.jpg
Notes:
48360908027 This is a white-fringed Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly working some kind of Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) flowers in the garden. This butterfly is larger and browner than the similar Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly that is more black than brown, compare this photo. We also have the Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly which lacks the white fringe, see this photo. This Mournful Duskywing uses Coast Live Oak as its host plant, while the similar Funereal Duskywing uses Deerweed - compare this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2019)

I had things to do in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I didn't get home until after noon. By then it was downright HOT - in the 90s - and it felt more like nap time than photo time. I did get a few photos close to home, including this butterfly.
ScientificName: Erynnis tristis; Lavandula; Lamiaceae; Erynnis funeralis; Erynnis propertius
associatedTaxa: Erynnis tristis; Lavandula; Lamiaceae; Erynnis funeralis; Erynnis propertius

File.Name: j723b_j722-015.jpg
Notes: 48360716086 Here is another Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). This one is more "black and white" than the butterfly in this photo, but they just are variable that way. These handsome butterflies have been uncommonly scarce this year for some reason. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2019)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: j723c_j721-018.jpg
Notes: 48360784237 This is another "true bug" lounging inside a flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). The bug is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera), and it's the adult that comes from the ant-mimic nymphs that I showed in this photo a month ago. It's interesting that the adults don't look anything like ants, just the nymphs. I usually find them on White Sage (Salvia apiana), but I guess these roomy flowers are another host. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2019)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; White Sage
ScientificName: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana
associatedTaxa: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana

File.Name: j723d_j719-010.jpg
Notes: 48360718262 Here are two shots of mating Beetles (Coleoptera) on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). They are going at it on the left. After they separated, the larger one above paused for a moment, and I got the shot on the right. I'm pretty sure they are Red-shouldered Leaf Beetles (Saxinis saucia) in the Chrysomelidae group of beetles, at least that is the genus. They are about the size of a ladybug. At first they look black, but I there's a hint of very deep blue as well - compare this photo from last year. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2019)

File.Name: j724a_j724-020.jpg
Notes:
48369290206 Here are white and orange forms of the Orange Sulfur (Colias eurytheme) butterfly on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). I got both photos today. I believe the white form on the left is female, and the orange form on the right is male. You can see a hint of their dark-clouded upper wings - more obvious when they fly. These butterflies are said to be common in alfalfa fields, but they are uncommon - and wary - up here in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2019)

It was downright HOT again today, up to about 96∞ in the shade on our upstairs deck. Yipes, it got up to 106∞ down by the Santa Ynez River today! We've been under a NWS Heat Advisory today. Hopefully it will be a few degrees cooler tomorrow.

File.Name: j724b_j724-027.jpg
Notes: 48369238031 This is a Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Hymenoptera) working a new flower cluster of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the canyon at home. You can see the wasp's remarkable "third eye" between its goggle-like real eyes - see this photo for a closer shot. These wasps are so fast and alert that it's hard to get decent photos of them. I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2019)
CommonNames: Bembecine Sand Wasp; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: j724c_j724-011.jpg
Notes: 48369194377 I believe this white "thing" is the odd larva of a Dusky Lady Beetle (Scymnini, Coccinellidae), a kind of lady beetle in the genus Scymnus. Those are white waxy outgrowths on its back. I first thought it was a Mealybug Destroyer (Cryptolaemus montrouzieri) which has similar outgrowths, but then I saw the discussion that goes with this page at BugGuide. Cryptolaemus larvae appear to have more ragged wax filaments on their back, and they prefer mealybugs. My larva is definitely munching an aphid while its tending ant looks on. (You can see the aphid - I'll make a note.) I found this odd critter today on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). Once I noticed this one, I saw a few more. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2019)
CommonNames: Dusky Lady Beetle; Mealybug Destroyer; Deerweed
ScientificName: Scymnini; Coccinellidae; Scymnus; Cryptolaemus montrouzieri; Cryptolaemus; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Scymnini; Coccinellidae; Scymnus; Cryptolaemus montrouzieri; Cryptolaemus; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: j724d_j715-021.jpg
Notes: 48369137542 This is another composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with its proper orb web on the left and its retreat on the right. The spider is actually sticking its legs out at the bottom of its retreat, I'll make a note. It's holding the special strand of webbing that connects to the center of its orb web, see this photo for another example. This one is in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2019)

File.Name: j725a_j725-011.jpg
Notes:
48376752156 Here is another little wild bee taking nectar from a new flower of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. This one was taking nectar and not collecting pollen, but maybe I drove it away by getting too close? I often see these bees taking nectar and then backing up to waggle their abdomens among the stamens. I don't know what kind of wild bee it is, but I'm sure it's in the family Megachilidae. These bees are distinctive because they collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees. I have another shot of a similar bee on a different Phacelia, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2019)

I was waiting for a phone call today, so I had to wait around the garden all morning. It wasn't the best day to be out for photos anyway, as there was cloudy monsoon weather for most of the day. There was no rain, but the clouds did cut the heat a bit.

I had to post my Fly Day Friday photos today on Thursday because our power will be out again for "scheduled maintenance" all day on Friday. I'll post if I can. *Sigh* Our power was out all night a few days ago for some unscheduled problem; and we just got word that we have yet another scheduled maintenance next Wednesday. I guess this is the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities. I'm glad the work is being done, but I'm getting tired of the interruptions!

File.Name: j725b_j725-004.jpg
Notes: 48376601201 This is an attractive little fly (Diptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) this morning. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it might be in the family Lauxaniidae, possibly species Minettia flaveola. Another possibility is the genus Suillia in the family Heleomyzidae. I've seen these flies before, e.g. this photo. I get confused by them every year. I know it's only Thursday local time, but it is Friday by Flickr/GMT/UTC time, and our power will be out tomorrow - so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Lauxaniidae; Minettia flaveola; Suillia; Heleomyzidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Lauxaniidae; Minettia flaveola; Suillia; Heleomyzidae

File.Name: j725c_j722-034.jpg
Notes: 48376680652 Here is another shot of the striking green Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that I found a few days ago under a summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family, see this photo. I was trying to nudge it into the flower when it dropped away into the Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) growing around it. That's when I got this photo. HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2019)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Deerweed
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: j725d_j719-005.jpg
Notes: 48376625682 This is "just a fly" on an oak leaf in the woods. I suspect it's a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae - those golden hairs on its thorax are distinctive, but the flies seem to lose them as they age. This is one those flies you probably don't want to see walking across your potato salad. It's only Thursday local time, but it is Friday by Flickr/GMT/UTC time. Our power will be out tomorrow, so this photo will have to do double duty this week - HBBBT and HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2019)

File.Name: j726a_j726-038.jpg
Notes:
48388810897 This is the remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). They are easier to see when they are rearing up like this. They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, until they finally transform into the green moths on our window screens at night. This one is feeding on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), and has decorated itself with stamens and pistils of the flowers. Buckwheat flowers are about 1/8 inch across, so they make a good ruler. I only found this one because it was moving when nothing else was. I've never found them on any other plant, but they are hard to see even when they are right in front of you. What camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2019)

Our power was supposed to be out for "scheduled maintenance" yesterday at 7:00 pm. It came back around 7:30, so I can't complain. I'm posting just two of yesterday's photos this morning just to prove I did something. I'll post more later on today.
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: j727a_j727-015.jpg
Notes:
48391640641 This is a little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched for a moment of a new plant of Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in our canyon today. There were lots of these butterflies a month ago, but then they disappeared for a while. Now they're back. These feisty butterflies seem to spend their time darting forth from a perch to defend its territory from all other passing butterflies - and anything else that moves! This makes them hard to approach, but I got lucky with this one. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2019)

It was downright HOT again today, almost into triple digits in the shade on the upstairs porch, call it 98.6∞ - too hot for insects or insect photographers. We're under a NWS Heat Advisory all weekend. I'm glad there's no wind because of the increased fire danger, but it would feel pretty good right now.

File.Name: j727b_j727-004.jpg
Notes: 48391640177 Here are two shots of the exuviae of a Cicada (family Cicadidae) hanging onto a grass stem. This is the shed skin after the cicada's final molt. That word "exuviae" sounds plural, but it is the preferred word even in the singular. It occurs to me that the word "remains" as in "someone's remains" works the same way. There's some discussion of this at BugGuide here and here. I don't know is this is the same kind of cicada I found back in May, compare this photo of an adult. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2019)

File.Name: j727c_j726-019.jpg
Notes: 48391425696 This is an Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper in a plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). The grasshopper looks like a nymph at first with those stubby little wings, but those little stubs are all the wings it will ever get. The colors of these grasshoppers is confusing. This one is tan, but others are quite dark - compare this photo. (Hah - and compare this photo at BugGuide by Alice Abelia of a completely black individual!) They just are variable, but they all have those little stubby wings. The deerweed is down to it's last few flowers now, but it still has fresh foliage. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2019)

File.Name: j727d_j726-028.jpg
Notes: 48391337306 Here is a Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) that has become ensnared by sparse webbing on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). You can see the rear end of a little spider sticking out from the other side of the stem, I'll make a note. I suspect that this is cribillate webbing, look at the texture of the web in the upper-left and compare with this photo. This might be the work of a be the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae. The fly is curious because I haven't been seeing these large flies since May, and the fly in the photo doesn't look two months old. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2019)
CommonNames: Large Crane Fly; Deerweed; Mesh Web Weaver
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Diptera; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; cribillate webbing; Dictynidae
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Diptera; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; cribillate webbing; Dictynidae

File.Name: j728a_j728-026.jpg
Notes:
48400519361 I found this little Treehopper (family Membracidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is the Coast Live Oak Treehopper (Telamona vestita). Once I noticed this one, I saw several more in the same tree. I like these little insects that look like thorns or bumps on the tree branch! (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2019)

It was downright HOT again today, and maybe it finally made it to the 100∞ mark. It's definitely too hot for insects or insect photographers, but I did my best and found a few (like myself) who lack the good sense to stay in the shade. Our NWS Heat Advisory expires tonight, and it might finally be a few degrees cooler tomorrow.
Locality: like myself) who lack the good sense to stay in the shade.  Our NWS Heat Advisory expires tonight

File.Name: j728b_j728-016.jpg
Notes: 48400561882 Here are two shots of a tiny Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it's a Cixiid Planthopper in the family Cixiidae, possibly genus Cixius. I wish I could look under its wings to see what that light-colored area is. These critters are tiny, only a bit over 1/8 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2019)

File.Name: j728c_j728-010.jpg
Notes: 48400463007 Here are two shots of a small furry Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I'm not sure what kind of Bee Fly it is, maybe tribe Villini of the Anthracinae? (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2019)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Villini; Anthracinae

File.Name: j728d_j725-017.jpg
Notes: 48400371022 This is the nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a glandular flower head of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I've found them on this plant before, see this photo from last year that shows more of the plant. Sometimes the crickets have a stripe, sometimes not - maybe different kinds? These crickets will become the songsters of our woods on late-summer and autumn nights later in the season. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2019)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Boraginaceae

File.Name: j729a_j729-013.jpg
Notes:
48409899047 Here is a mating pair of tiny "Calochortus Bees" on a summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. In my photo, they are on the outside of the flower, near the hairy lip of the petal. I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae - maybe Perdita californica or P. calochorti. I don't find these bees anywhere else than these flowers, and the flowers don't last very long. These bees may have a short life, but it's intense and beautiful. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2019)

It was cooler today, only in the mid-80s rather than near 100∞ like yesterday. It was a little breezy also, and now we're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight - but it's not too bad yet. There was a 100 acre wildfire last night on the slopes of Figueroa Mountain/Ranger Peak. *Sigh* It's getting to be that time of year.

File.Name: j729b_j729-024.jpg
Notes: 48409658681 This is a tiny "Calochortus Bee" (my name) collecting pollen on the stamen of a summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. These bees are quite shy, but it's interesting how often I catch them looking right at me like here - see this photo. I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae - maybe Perdita californica or P. calochorti. I don't find these bees anywhere else than these flowers, and the flowers don't last very long. I got this photo along West Camno Cielo road today. They're flowering lower in the mountains now, but they are still open not in our home canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2019)

File.Name: j729c_j729-002.jpg
Notes: 48409734132 I found this little fly (Diptera) on a stem of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). It only occurred to me after I got the photo that it's another of the biting Deer Flies that have been getting me lately. Hah, I'm not used to seeing them anywhere except on my arms and legs, see this photo. I believe it is in the family Tabanidae of Horse and Deer Flies, but it is not much larger than a house fly and its eyes are quite plain unlike many other Tabanids. I believe it is genus Apatolestes in the subfamily Pangoniinae, maybe the species Apatolestes hinei. I reckon it's OK to call it a Deer Fly even though it is not in the subfamily Chrysopsinae. This one seemed to have no interest in me, so maybe it's a male? (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2019)
CommonNames: fly; White Sage; Deer Flies; Deer Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Tabanidae; Apatolestes; Pangoniinae; Apatolestes hinei; Chrysopsinae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Tabanidae; Apatolestes; Pangoniinae; Apatolestes hinei; Chrysopsinae

File.Name: j729d_j728-002.jpg
Notes: 48409508546 This is spider webbing wrapped around an old flower head of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The spider is actually visible in the center, I'll make a note. It's had pretty good luck hunting, look especially on that one branch in the bottom-left. I suspect that this is another example of cribillate webbing, look at the texture of the web and compare with the frizzy webbing in this photo and this photo. This might be the work of a be the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2019)
ScientificName: Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; cribillate webbing; Dictynidae

File.Name: j730a_j730-044.jpg
Notes:
48419027027 I found this remarkable fly (Diptera) caught in a large spider web today. It was alive and struggling. It's an uncommon Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the unusual family Acroceridae - probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. I usually find these flies working flowers of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae), and they seem a perfect fit for the tubular flowers - see this photo. They use that remarkably long proboscis to get deep inside the flowers. They are very difficult to photograph as they flit between flowers, seemingly at random, but I keep trying. You can see all my photos if you do a search. Sure, I untangled the fly and moved it to a nearby Bush Monkey Flower shrub after I got my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2019)

Today was another very nice day with temps in the mid-80s rather than than near 100∞ like a couple of days ago. There was a breeze, not too bad. *Sigh* Our power will be out again for "scheduled maintenance" all day on Wednesday. I'll post if I can. I guess this is the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities. I'm glad the work is being done, but I'm getting tired of the interruptions.
CommonNames: fly; Metallic Small-Headed Fly; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Diptera; Eulonchus; Acroceridae; Eulonchus smaragdinus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Eulonchus; Acroceridae; Eulonchus smaragdinus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: j730b_j730-006.jpg
Notes: 48418967742 Here is the small but perfect orb web of a little spider in a flower/fruit head of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). On the right you can see some of the umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf that the flowers always grow from. I'm not sure whose web it is, but no doubt it's the work of an Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider - a smaller one than I show in my [Previous] photo. I know it's only Tuesday local time, but it is Wednesday by Flickr/GMT/UTC time, and our power will be out tomorrow - so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2019)
CommonNames: orb web; California Honeysuckle; Orb Web Weaver

File.Name: j730c_j730-004.jpg
Notes: 48418821082 This is a little Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider on a seed capsule of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). It's small, just about 1080 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.2 mm - not quite 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm pretty sure this is a genus Araneus spider, but I'm not sure if it's one of our small species like A. bispinosus or A. montereyensis or a juvenile form of one of our large species like A. gemma or A. gemmoides. I suspect the latter - for it's markings and because it's web is about one foot in diameter, large for such a small spider. It's screen-left leg that is hanging down is actually touching a line to the spider's web, waiting for prey. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Araneus; A. bispinosus; A. montereyensis; A. gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Araneus; A. bispinosus; A. montereyensis; A. gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: j730d_j727-016.jpg
Notes: 48418788047 Here is a mating pair of little Beetles (Coleoptera) on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that is nearly done flowering. I'm pretty sure they are Red-shouldered Leaf Beetles (Saxinis saucia) in the Chrysomelidae group of beetles, at least that is the genus. They are about the size of a ladybug. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2019)

File.Name: j731a_j731-036.jpg
Notes:
48427237627 This is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly ovipositing (laying eggs) on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I suspect she's actually laying her eggs on the stems below the flowers which are not so ephemeral. I have indeed found a caterpillar of these butterflies on this shrub, see this photo taken a few years ago in the same place. This is the first one of these butterflies that I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2019)

I wasn't expecting to find so many butterflies today, but it was pretty windy, and they were about the only insects I could get in focus! We were due to have our power out today for "scheduled maintenance," but it never happened. We finally got a text message from the power company in the afternoon saying that our power had been restored . . . and it was never out. This is no way to run a business.

File.Name: j731b_j731-005.jpg
Notes: 48427143007 If you look close at this photo, you will see that the butterfly has just two eyespots on the bottom of its hind wings. That makes it an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly - our other Lady butterflies have more spots, see this photo and this photo. This is the first one I've seen this year at home. It's working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2019)

File.Name: j731c_j731-006.jpg
Notes: 48426945256 Here is another white-fringed Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). This butterfly is larger and browner than the similar Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly, but that one is more black than brown, compare this photo. Both duskywings are on the wing right now, and the color difference is noticeable. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2019)
ScientificName: Erynnis tristis; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Erynnis funeralis
associatedTaxa: Erynnis tristis; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Erynnis funeralis

File.Name: j731d_j730-016.jpg
Notes: 48426861956 This is a Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly working a faded flower of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). The recent heat has just about finished off the Deerweed, though there are still a few flowers hanging on. It's also just about finished off the Acmon Blues that have been busy ovipositing and working the flowers, and these handsome Marine Blue butterflies are taking their place. Apparently these butterflies are named for their blue color above and not for any affinity with the ocean. I like the sparkle on its hind wing eyespots, also blue. See this photo of another one ovipositing on this same plant last June. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2019)

File.Name: j801a_j801-037.jpg
Notes:
48435258512 Here is an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly ovipositing (laying eggs) on an Everlasting (Gnaphalium or Pseudognaphalium) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe this plant is Wright's Cudweed (Pseudognaphalium microcephalum), but the butterflies seem to use all different everlasting plants. I just found the first of these butterflies in the canyon yesterday, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2019)

I've had good luck finding ovipositing butterflies this year: Gray Hairstreak, Brown Elfin, Variable Checkerspot, Common Buckeye, Acmon Blue, Marine Blue, Painted Lady, and Sonoran Blue!

Today was another very nice day with temps approaching 90∞ - a bit warmer than expected. There was a breeze, but not as bad as yesterday.
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium; Pseudognaphalium; Asteraceae; Pseudognaphalium microcephalum
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium; Pseudognaphalium; Asteraceae; Pseudognaphalium microcephalum

File.Name: j801b_j801-006.jpg
Notes: 48435042671 This is a much smaller spider than the one I show in my [Previous] photo, but you can see a family resemblance in its bristly legs. I believe this is a Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris) in the same family Oxyopidae. It's interesting that this one appears to be guarding its egg sac - behavior that the much larger Green Lynx Spiders also share. This one is in a spent flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I'll try to keep an eye on it. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2019)
CommonNames: Western Lynx Spider; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: j801c_j801-041.jpg
Notes: 48434966576 This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) in a plant of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). It has its prey, a small wild bee? I guess that this one is Peucetia longipalpis because its abdomen has white hairs and lacks the usual chevron patterns of P. viridans. It's my impression that this one has its babies first while it's still summer, while P. viridans comes later in fall - just in time for Arachtober. I've got lots of photos of these spiders over the years, try a search of my photos. This one shows the spider's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2019)
