File.Name: j802a_j802-012.jpg
Notes:
48442133551 Here is another American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly working new flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). Like my [Previous] photo of a Pale Swallowtail butterfly, this one flew into my photo and caught me by surprise - at least this time I managed to fit it in my frame. This butterfly is unique among our Ladies in having just two eyespots under its hindwing. The swirled patterns remind me of the inside-covers of old books, like marbled endpapers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 August 2019)

Today was another very nice day with temps in the 90s. There was a breeze, not too bad. It's already starting to feel hot, and it might be a few degrees warmer this weekend.
CommonNames: American Lady; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: j802b_j801-033.jpg
Notes: 48442208512 This is a not-so-pale Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly sitting on a flower head of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the canyon at home. Hah, I was sitting close to the flowers waiting for a wild bee when this relatively giant butterfly flew into my view. I got the shot more than full frame, but I like it because it shows how creamy the butterfly's wings are. These butterflies look less pale as we move further into summer - they get a cream color, and sometimes I confuse them with Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus). I've been seeing more of these recently, but they're late - they've missed the Humboldt Lilies. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2019)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; P. rutulus
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; P. rutulus

File.Name: j802c_j730-048.jpg
Notes: 48442009966 Here is one more shot of the remarkable fly (Diptera) I found a few days ago caught in a large spider web, see this photo. Yeah, this photo is similar to that one, but it is slightly different, and it shows how hairy the fly's eyes are. It was alive and struggling, spinning around and around. It's an uncommon Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the unusual family Acroceridae family - probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. I usually find these flies working flowers of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae), and they seem a perfect fit for the tubular flowers. They use that remarkably long proboscis to get to the bottom of the flowers. Sure, I untangled this fly and moved it to a nearby Bush Monkey Flower shrub after I got my photos - sorry, spider. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2019)

File.Name: j802d_j730-022.jpg
Notes: 48441955096 This is just a fly . . . walking across my leg. I'm pretty sure it's a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. It looks like its face is made of metal. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2019)

File.Name: j803a_j803-015.jpg
Notes:
48449818246 I'm pretty sure this is a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus, Reduviidae) on a leafless stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that is nearly done flowering. The bug is making a threatening pose, not getting ready to pounce. These predators have a row of four pointed spurs across the back of its thorax, but they are hard to make out in this photo. This is the first adult I've seen this year. The Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) is a cousin that is usually more common, though I haven't seen them yet this year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2019)

It was up to about 90∞ today. There was a breeze from the south/ocean side of the mountains that actually cooled things off a bit in the morning, but it got hot in the afternoon. It might be a few degrees warmer tomorrow.
ScientificName: Zelus tetracanthus; Reduviidae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Zelus renardii
associatedTaxa: Zelus tetracanthus; Reduviidae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Zelus renardii

File.Name: j803b_j803-001.jpg
Notes: 48449704341 Here is a golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) working a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. Maybe this is the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni) - see this photo from last year of what is probably the same bee. It was moving quickly between the flowers, hard to photograph. Note that this bee is heavy enough to hold the flower open - see this photo for an explanation. This sage plant is like the Energizer Bunny - it keeps going and going with new flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2019)
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; White Sage; Morrison Bumble Bee

File.Name: j803c_j803-035.jpg
Notes: 48449816587 This is another Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the canyon at home. You can see some of its remarkable proboscis and the little thorn-like "dingus" in the middle of its abdomen - what is that? I like these wasps. They are fast and alert, hard to capture with a camera. I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2019)
CommonNames: Bembecine Sand Wasp; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: j803d_j803-019.jpg
Notes: 48449745307 I found this impressive Long-horned Orthopteran (Ensifera, Orthoptera) in a clump of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that is going to seed today. It's probably a couple of inches long. It looks like a katydid with those long antennae, but I'm confused that its gray rather than green. I'm also confused that it has a very long ovipositor (which looks adult), but it doesn't have wings (which looks juvenile). Any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2019)

File.Name: j804a_j803-003.jpg
Notes:
48458452917 Here is a handsome Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. These butterflies look a lot like the usually more-common California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterflies, but the orange wing-tips go right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border, and the underwings are different - compare this photo and this photo. These butterflies don't seem to visit flowers. They just sit and wait - to chase away any other butterfly that comes close to their willow tree! This one let me get closer than usual. It actually the first photo of one that I've got this year, though I see them most days when I'm near the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2019)

No new photos today. I woke up this morning feeling under the weather, and it got worse rather than better as the day progressed. I never got outside today but maybe I'm feeling better now since I did post my usual four photos today - something I wouldn't have done this morning.
ScientificName: Basilarchia lorquini; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Adelpha bredowii
associatedTaxa: Basilarchia lorquini; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Adelpha bredowii

File.Name: j804b_j803-025.jpg
Notes: 48458211201 This is another "Green Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera, maybe the Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens), though there are other possibilities. If it is are that species, they don't always have that red line across the thorax - in fact, sometimes they look completely brown. This one is on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that is nearly done flowering and going to seed. It's a legume plant, and I reckon those seed pods are highly nutritious. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2019)

File.Name: j804c_j801-022.jpg
Notes: 48458143116 Here is a tiny Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) waiting for a meal among the new flowers of an Everlasting (Gnaphalium or Pseudognaphalium) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe this plant is Wright's Cudweed (Pseudognaphalium microcephalum), but I'm not sure - it's a confusing group. I'm impressed that the little bug can recognize the flowers as what they are! (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2019)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Everlasting; Wright's Cudweed

File.Name: j804d_j731-025.jpg
Notes: 48458266182 Look close! This is the remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, until they finally transform into the green moths on our window screens at night. This one is feeding on the red-tipped stamens of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), and has decorated itself with stamens and pistils of the same flowers. Buckwheat flowers are about 1/8 inch across, so they make a good ruler. I only found this one because it was moving when nothing else was. I've never found them on any other plant, but they are hard to see even when they are right in front of you. What camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2019)
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: j805a_j805-038.jpg
Notes:
48468202662 I believe this is a female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). At first, I thought it was a Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterfly, and I had one of those "D'oh!" moments that sure, it's a skipper. I don't find these butterflies very often up here in the mountains. Look at that sticky pollen on its proboscis - I guess these flowers are deeper than they seem to be. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2019)

I was feeling under the weather yesterday, but I'm feeling better today. It's warm, in the low-90s and it's no fun feeling sick when it's this hot. I got as far as the buckwheat by the mailbox today (for this photo) and even further for a beer run to town - a sure sign that I'm feeling better!
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Pontia protodice
associatedTaxa: Heliopetes ericetorum; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Pontia protodice

File.Name: j805b_j805-024.jpg
Notes: 48468105647 Here is another Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly in a supine position under a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden today. These swallowtail butterflies were off to a slow start this year, but I've been seeing more and more of them lately. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2019)

File.Name: j805c_j805-035.jpg
Notes: 48467899806 This is another remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, until they finally transform into the green moths on our window screens at night. This one is feeding on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), and has decorated itself with the flower bits of the same flowers. I only found this one because it was moving when nothing else was - but I've been looking for them. I've never found them on any other plant, but they are hard to see even when they are right in front of you. What camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2019)

File.Name: j805d_j805-009.jpg
Notes: 48467849661 Here are two shots of a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). This one is not decorated like the one in my [Next] photo, and I assume it's a different kind. I found it crawling around on a seed head of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. It's only a bit over 1/4 inch long. Like all of these caterpillars, it stands on a platform of two pairs of hind prolegs and three pairs of true legs in front - and walks by looping along. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2019)

File.Name: j806a_j806-008.jpg
Notes:
48476668306 Here is another golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) working a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. I'm thinking this is the same kind that I showed in this photo a couple of days ago, maybe the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni) - but maybe not. I'm not sure because this one is not so golden as the other one, and I also saw one today with a black-tipped abdomen, so there is some variablity. Whatever they are, they move quickly between the flowers, hard to photograph. This is my best shot from two mornings of trying. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2019)

It was quite breezy this morning from the south, hard to get photos when both the bees and the flowers are moving! We expected a service person to come by today between 9 and 12, but they never showed. *Sigh* We called and now they might be by tomorrow between 9 and 3 . . . like we're supposed to wait around all day? This is no way to run a business. Oh well, I was still feeling a little under the weather today anyway, and I intend to take my walk tomorrow.
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; White Sage; Morrison Bumble Bee
ScientificName: Bombus; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Bombus morrisoni
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Bombus morrisoni

File.Name: j806b_j805-011.jpg
Notes: 48476723662 This is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) working a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). It has some pollen in its baskets, but this one is mainly interested in sweet nectar. The combination of black bee and white flowers creates a contrast problem, which I dealt with by exposing for the bee and letting the flowers go over-exposed. These bees travel from flower to flower so quickly that they are very hard to photograph. This is my best shot from two mornings of trying. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2019)

File.Name: j806c_j805-006.jpg
Notes: 48476670182 This is a shy little fly (Diptera) looking at me through a gap in the leaves of this White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). I don't know what kind of fly it is. I usually have trouble focusing on insect faces that are looking right at me, but this shot looks pretty good. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2019)

File.Name: j806d_j803-031.jpg
Notes: 48476446811 Here are a pair of fantastic summer flowers of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in the canyon at home. These flowers are highly variable, but flowers on the same plant - like these - are usually similar. They have a way of coming up through other plants - Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) in this case, which makes a nice radial background. These plants start flowering higher in the mountains and work their way down to us, and they're just about done up higher. I want to photograph every one I see! (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2019)
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: j807a_j807-009.jpg
Notes:
48484683061 I believe this is a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) by the summer-dry creek today. These butterflies are small and quite plain below, but they have their charms. That dark streak on the forewing is the butterfly's stigma - a glandular patch that releases pheromones during courtship that only males have. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2019)

It was breezy again this morning from the south, but at least the breeze from the ocean side of the mountains kept things relatively cool - it was only in the low-80s today. In episode 2, we expected a service person to come by today between 9 and 3, but they never showed or called again. Julie called and was polite but firm, and managed to get through to a supervisor. We'll try again tomorrow.
ScientificName: Ochlodes sylvanoides; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; stigma
associatedTaxa: Ochlodes sylvanoides; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; stigma

File.Name: j807b_j807-023.jpg
Notes: 48484784427 Here is another golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) working a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure this is the same kind of bee I've been finding on the White Sage at home, see this photo and this photo. Maybe it is the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni) but maybe not. These bees move quickly between the flowers, hard to photograph. I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2019)
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Morrison Bumble Bee

File.Name: j807c_j807-003.jpg
Notes: 48484718207 Here is another little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a seed head of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. This one is small, less than 1/2 inch long. It appears to be different from the one I showed in this photo on the same plant. Like all of these caterpillars, it stands on a platform of two pairs of hind prolegs and three pairs of true legs in front - and walks by looping along. There is webbing visible behind the caterpillar's head, but I think that's accidental. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2019)

File.Name: j807d_j807-013.jpg
Notes: 48484632297 This is the web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae). As I understand it, the web consists of three main layers. The main part in the middle is "the bowl" of non-sticky webbing. The spider hangs below this and grabs its prey from underneath. I can see the spider here in the original photo, but it's hard to make out here - I'll make a note. Hanging below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey. Above the bowl is a fine network of random webbing that goes beyond the bounds of my photo that knocks flying insects into the bowl. I found this web in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2019)
CommonNames: Bowl and Doily Spider; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Frontinella communis; Frontinella pyramitela; Linyphiidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Frontinella communis; Frontinella pyramitela; Linyphiidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j808a_j808-009.jpg
Notes:
48492386187 Here are two shots of a handsome Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly showing its wings above and below. It has a good trick to be able to disappear just by closing its wings! This one was nectaring on flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). That's interesting because I don't often see them feeding at all. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2019)

It was breezy again this morning from the south, and it got into the upper-80s in the afternoon. Episode 3 did the trick as our service person did come by as expected today, and everything's cool. I reckon Julie talking with the supervisor (in India??) made the difference by being polite but firm.

File.Name: j808b_j808-011.jpg
Notes: 48492339537 Look close! This is another remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, until they finally transform into the green moths on our window screens at night. This one is feeding on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), and has decorated itself with the flower bits of the same flowers. I only found it because it was moving when nothing else was - but I've been looking for them. I've been finding quite a few of them this year, but I don't believe I ever found one last year. What camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2019)
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: j808c_j807-036.jpg
Notes: 48492250922 Here is a pollen-dusted Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) working a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's much larger than the golden bees I've been photographing lately, compare this photo from yesterday which was taken right next to this one. The bee does have a yellow face, so maybe it's the Yellow-faced Bumble Bee (Bombus vosnesenskii)? I mostly have shots of its rear end just like this, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2019)
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Yellow-faced Bumble Bee

File.Name: j808d_j803-007.jpg
Notes: 48492001301 This is a late-stage nymph of a little cricket (Gryllidae, Orthoptera) hiding in a dry flower cluster of Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum, Asteraceae). At least it's the rear end of a cricket! I never did get a good look at the rest of it, but I'm pretty sure it's the nymph of a Tree Cricket in the genus Oecanthus. It's a good shot of this cricket's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2019)
ScientificName: Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Gnaphalium californicum; Asteraceae; Oecanthus

File.Name: j809a_j809-033.jpg
Notes:
48499954787 This is another golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) working a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. This is an unusually white flower for this species, but that variability is part of their charm. I've been finding these bees on these flowers and on the White Sage at home, see this photo. Maybe they are the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni) but maybe not. This one has its proboscis in a gland at the base of the petal - you can see another gland on the petal to the right. The bee goes 'round and 'round the flower, hitting all the bases, then its time to hurry to another flower, which makes them hard to photograph. I'll keep trying as long as they're around. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2019)

It was breezy again this morning from the south, and it was cooler that it's been, only up to about 80∞. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight for wind from the north, but it's still pretty calm right now. I hope it stays that way.
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Morrison Bumble Bee

File.Name: j809b_j809-031.jpg
Notes: 48499675121 Here are two shots of a small wild bee collecting pollen from stamens of a native flower of Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, but I don't think it is one of the "Calochortus Bees" that I've been showing, e.g. in this photo. It does have pollen baskets on its hind legs, but its has as much pollen on its abdomen. I won't try to guess what kind of bee it is. It's interesting to compare the bee in my [Next] photo using the stamens as a ruler. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2019)

File.Name: j809c_j809-052.jpg
Notes: 48499578131 Here is a little Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that is done flowering and developing its legume-like fruit now. The bug is helping itself to the fruit, and this photo shows its remarkable mouthparts which are articulated almost like another leg. My photo shows the labrum, stylets, and labium which comprise the beak or rostrum of the bug, see this photo for an explanation of the parts. I don't know what kind of true bug it is - there seem to be many kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2019)

File.Name: j809d_j808-016.jpg
Notes: 48499705747 This is a small atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the Diptera - probably genus Cylindromyia and maybe species Cylindromyia intermedia. It's working new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It does have a bristly abdomen like many other tachinids, and it likes to stick it up in the air and shake it around while feeding at the flowers. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2019)

File.Name: j810a_j810-031.jpg
Notes:
48507611776 Here is a mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). There are also orange "milkweed aphids" and an incoming wild bee in the upper-right of my photo - good for scale. These impressive wasps are almost 2 inches long - and they seem larger when you actually see them. They are large and a bit ungainly as they clamber over the flowers that are too small for them. We seem to have several different kinds of these wasps - larger and smaller, antennae coiled and straight, antennae black or orange, wings orange or pale. Male and female differences account for some of this variability but not all. It was quite windy when I got this photo with wasp and flowers whipping about. I'll try to get back to this place when it's calm for a better photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 August 2019)

We didn't have our usual wind from the south this morning, but the wind from the north has been making up for it - with gusts to 35+ mph so far?? Fortunately it's also been cooler today, barely into the 80s. I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today and made my usual stops along the way.
ScientificName: Pepsis; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae
associatedTaxa: Pepsis; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae

File.Name: j810b_j810-026.jpg
Notes: 48507491006 This is a cluster of fuzzy galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) growing under leaves of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The gall masses are actually composed of small individual galls that arise from separate points of attachment, see this photo. Sometimes the shrubs are covered with the galls, see this photo from a few years ago. They've been rather scarce so far this year. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 August 2019)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j810c_j810-014.jpg
Notes: 48507542462 Here are ants tending galls on a stem of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). That out-of-focus brown lump to the side is a developing acorn that just happened to be there. The ants are protecting their meal, as the galls exude a sweet honeydew from the surface. In return for their honey, the ants keep parasitic wasps away - like the Chalcid Wasp in this photo. There are several different galls on the oaks that look like little muffins, but I believe these are galls of the Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis mellifica) in the family Cynipidae because they arise from little ruptures on the oak stem. It was quite windy when I got this photo. I'll try to get back to this place when it's calm for a better photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 August 2019)
CommonNames: ants; galls; Scrub Oak; Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis mellifica; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis mellifica; Cynipidae

File.Name: j810d_j809-011.jpg
Notes: 48507479167 This is the nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on dead seed capsules of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). I found the first tiny nymphs of these crickets a month ago, see this photo taken inside a Bush Monkey Flower. They've been growing, but they still have a ways to go. These crickets will become the songsters of our woods on late-summer and autumn nights later in the season. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2019)

File.Name: j811a_j811-003.jpg
Notes:
48516108132 This is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) pulling out of a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). This flower was originally folded up in a "closed" position until the heavy bee mounted it, like this photo on the left. It's a service they provide for other smaller bees, because once they open a flower, it tends to stay open. I'm using the word "mount" deliberately - that's what it looks like. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2019)

We had a breeze from south this morning, and a breeze from the north in the afternoon - but it's calmer than yesterday, and there are no advisories. It was refreshingly cool today, mostly in the mid-70s. Now it's expected to warm up again for the new week.

File.Name: j811b_j811-052.jpg
Notes: 48515844091 Here is another golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) doing "nectar theft" on a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen, see this photo. It caught me by surprise that the bee is actually standing behind the flower, but I guess there's no other way to approach the calyx. I don't believe I've ever seen this behavior before. I don't think I've ever seen these pretty bees on these flowers either, though this shrub is growing near a colony of the Late-flowered Mariposa Lilies that the bees favor. Maybe these bees are the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni) but maybe not. They are important pollinators of our summer flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2019)
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; Woolly Blue Curls; Morrison Bumble Bee
ScientificName: Bombus; Hymenoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Bombus morrisoni
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Hymenoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Bombus morrisoni

File.Name: j811c_j811-046.jpg
Notes: 48515779861 This is another golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the canyon at home. I've been finding these bees on White Sage and Late-flowered Mariposa Lily, see my photos here and here, but these aren't the only summer flowers that they visit. Maybe these bees are the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni) but maybe not. They are important pollinators of our summer flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2019)
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Morrison Bumble Bee

File.Name: j811d_j811-055.jpg
Notes: 48515666456 Look close! This is another remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). It's hard to see the actual caterpillar, but try looking at the top end of the gold part to spot its "tail" of prolegs and follow it along. This one has been feeding on flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) instead of the California Buckwheat where I've been finding them, e.g. in this photo. They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, and this one has decorated itself with the orange flower bits of the Golden Yarrow. I don't know if its the same kind on a different host or a different kind. I only found it because it was moving when nothing else was. I've been saying that I've never found them on any other plant than the buckwheat . . . and now I have. What camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2019)
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; Golden Yarrow
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: j812a_j812-028.jpg
Notes:
48524830627 Here is a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) lurking on a new flower cluster of native Longstem Buckwheat aka Wand Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. These plants are just starting to flower, and they will go for some time. The pink flowers are rather pretty, but you have to get close and low to see them. They are the host plants for Gorgon Copper butterflies, see this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 August 2019)

It was warmer today than yesterday, but still pleasant, in the low-80s. There was a breeze, not too bad - and not windy in the evening like it's been. It's still expected to warm up this week and then cool down again for the weekend.
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Longstem Buckwheat; Wand Buckwheat

associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Eriogonum elongatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: j812b_j812-044.jpg
Notes: 48524563851 This is a mating pair of tiny Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) on new flowers of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. They remind me of shogun-era samurai warriors with their armor! Their color is interesting. I've always assumed they are color-shifters that can change to match their flowers, but I usually find what I guess are yellowish males riding white females. But see this photo in which the colors are reversed. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 August 2019)
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Solidago californica; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Solidago californica; Asteraceae

File.Name: j812c_j812-020.jpg
Notes: 48524468481 This is looking close at a summer flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. The wonderful large pollen grains seem to explode from the stamens like popcorn. These fine natives are just starting to flower, and they will really come into their own with autumn. I reckon they are important for keeping the hummingbirds alive during this harsh time of year - at least until the manzanita kicks in next winter, see this photo from last December. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 August 2019)

File.Name: j812d_j812-041.jpg
Notes: 48524392116 Here is a well-named Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus tinctorius aka P. arhizus) "mushroom" coming up in bare ground along the short road to the FAA radio on the high place between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro. It looks new, and I reckon it's not left over from last year. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" - the fungus gets its scientific name from the pea-shaped peridioles or spore capsules which are visible in my photo e.g. in the upper-left. They very slowly break down to release the spores. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. There are rumors of it being used as a seasoning and a medicine, though I'm not tempted to try it. These fungi are highly variable in shape, compare this recent photo from home. It's not really fleshy, but it is a fungus at an unlikely time of year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 August 2019)
ScientificName: Pisolithus tinctorius; P. arhizus; peridioles; tinctorus
associatedTaxa: Pisolithus tinctorius; P. arhizus; peridioles; tinctorus

File.Name: j813a_j813-020.jpg
Notes:
48532674782 Look close! This is another remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) that's feeding on a flower bud of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's actually eating a hole in the side of the flower bud. I can tell that this one has also been feeding on flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae), see this photo. They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, and this one has decorated itself with the orange flower bits of the Golden Yarrow and the fuzzy blue bits of the Woolly Blue Curls. Hah, the blue and gold look like school colors! I've been saying that I've never found these on any other plant than buckwheat . . . and now I have. What camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2019)

It was warmer today than yesterday, but still pleasant, close to 90 degrees. There was a breeze, not too bad. It's expected to warm up again tomorrow and then start to cool down again for the weekend.
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; Woolly Blue Curls; Golden Yarrow

File.Name: j813b_j813-016.jpg
Notes: 48532615172 Here is another little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) lurking in new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). They are small but effective predators that can bring down prey that is much larger than they are - like the butterflies in this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2019)

File.Name: j813c_j813-011.jpg
Notes: 48532410821 This is a mama Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris) in the family Oxyopidae guarding its egg sac in a spent flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). If you look close between its two front legs, you can see one of the baby spiderlings! (I'll make a note.) The spiderlings all disappeared back into the nest when I tried to get close, but I'll keep trying to get better photos. This spider is much smaller than the large Green Lynx Spiders in this photo, but you can see a family resemblance in their bristly legs. Both spiders also tend their nests. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2019)

associatedTaxa: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: j813d_j813-014.jpg
Notes: 48532496642 Here is a handsome gall on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) - it's actually a pair of galls with another one behind the main one. I believe they are the galls of a Dried Peach Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis simulata) in the family Cynipidae. They really do look like little peaches, complete with peach fuzz! Galls are just starting to appear for the most part, and I will be getting more and more photos of them as summer slides into autumn. It's convenient timing that galls appear just when insects and flowers are starting to get scarce. There's always something to photograph in our woods, though sometimes you have to hunt for it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2019)
CommonNames: gall; Scrub Oak; Dried Peach Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis simulata; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis simulata; Cynipidae

File.Name: j814a_j814-004.jpg
Notes:
48540301356 Here is a gorgeous Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) on dead seed capsules of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) this morning. They have remarkable eyes, see this photo for a closer look. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2019)

It was warm today, in the low 90s. There was an annoying breeze from the ocean side of the mountains which helped cool things down this morning, though it kept everything in motion and made it hard to get my photos. We're under a NWS Heat Advisory through Thursday night, then it's expected to cool down for the weekend.

File.Name: j814b_j814-015.jpg
Notes: 48540356712 Look close! Here is another remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) that's feeding on flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It actually let the little beetle (Coleoptera) go first. These caterpillars have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, and this one has decorated itself with the white and brown petals of the buckwheat. I've been on a roll finding these little caterpillars on different plants lately including Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) and Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae), see my recent photos here and here. In each case, they decorate themselves with the differently colored petals of their flowers. Either there are more of hem than usual this year, or I'm getting better at spotting them. What camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2019)
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat; beetle
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Coleoptera; Lamiaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Coleoptera; Lamiaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: j814c_j814-001.jpg
Notes: 48540291917 This is a sheet of spider webbing that's built around a dead leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods this morning. It reminds me of wind, like the inside of Dorothy's tornado, though it was just waving a bit in the breeze. It might be the work of a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider, though it could also be the work of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider - they are more common in the woods right now and have similar sheet-like sections on their webs. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2019)

File.Name: j814d_j811-062.jpg
Notes: 48540240442 This is a common Blue Bottle Fly aka Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae of the order Diptera caught up in a spider web in the woods. It's too late to help this one get away, unlike this other unfortunate fly that I helped escape. I reckon this is the whole point of spider webs, so happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2019)

File.Name: j815a_j815-009.jpg
Notes:
48547963646 This is a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) hanging from the bottom of the "bowl" of its web, see this photo and this photo for the whole web. The actual spider is quite small, only about 1/8 inch long. It's interesting that they don't sit in the bowl, but hang under it to grab their prey from below. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2019)

It was hot today, in the upper 90s on our upstairs porch. Our NWS Heat Advisory expires later tonight, and it will hopefully start to cool down into the 70s for the weekend.
ScientificName: Frontinella communis; Frontinella pyramitela; Linyphiidae; in
associatedTaxa: Frontinella communis; Frontinella pyramitela; Linyphiidae; in

File.Name: j815b_j815-019.jpg
Notes: 48548031192 Here is another remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a stem of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. This might be the same caterpillar I showed in this photo a couple of days ago, it's in the same shrub. You can see the caterpillar's head on the left and tail on the right with a loop in between. They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, and this one has decorated itself with the orange flower bits of the Golden Yarrow and the fuzzy blue bits of the Woolly Blue Curls. Hah, the blue and gold look like my old school colors! I've been showing a lot of these lately, and why not. I'll probably keep showing them as long as I find them. What camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2019)
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: j815c_j811-034.jpg
Notes: 48547914832 Here is a little wild bee gathering pollen from the stamens of Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. This might be the same kind of wild bee I show in this photo - and like in that photo, I won't try to guess what it is. Note that it has a slit at the end of its abdomen. I'm not sure what that means, but it does make for an interesting rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2019)

File.Name: j815d_j810-004.jpg
Notes: 48547861847 This is another golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) mounting a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. I'm using the word "mount" deliberately because that's what it looks like. Maybe these bees are the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni) but maybe not. They are important pollinators of our summer flowers. This photo shows the bee's rear end so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2019)
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; White Sage; Morrison Bumble Bee
ScientificName: Bombus; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Bombus morrisoni
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Bombus morrisoni

File.Name: j816a_j816-008.jpg
Notes:
48555446652 Here is another little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) lurking among new flowers of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). Hah, I'm impressed that the bug can recognize that there are flowers inside the green bracts, but indeed there are, and they attract pollinator insects that could become the hunter's prey. Sometimes the flowers stick out of their bracts, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2019)

It was hot again this morning, but it started to cool down in the afternoon to the low-80s by the time I got home. It will hopefully cool down into the 70s for the weekend before it warms up again next week.
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: j816b_j816-023.jpg
Notes:
48555190931 I don't know why so many plant galls are bright red, but they are. It seems like the opposite of camouflage. I found all these today on leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). All of these galls are caused by tiny wasps in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. From the top-left going clockwise, here are:

* Gall of a Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii aka Cynips douglasii);
* Mass of fuzzy galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus;
* Naked forms of the same fuzzy galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus which have mostly failed to develop their distinctive red hairs; and
* Gall of the pinched form of the Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp, which is apparently an undescribed species.

Note that along with being bright red, all of these galls are on the underside of the leaves. The season for oak galls is really just beginning, but they're out there. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2019)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Crystalline Gall Wasp; Crystalline Gall Wasp; Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Antron douglasii; Cynips douglasii; Andricus crystallinus; Andricus crystallinus
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Antron douglasii; Cynips douglasii; Andricus crystallinus; Andricus crystallinus

File.Name: j816c_j814-006.jpg
Notes: 48555137766 This is a tiny humpbacked fly (Diptera) working the flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The fly is only about 1/8 inch long or less. I believe it's an atypical Bee Fly in the genus Geron, there's more info at BugGuide. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane instead of a proboscis. Julie calls it a "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2019)

File.Name: j816d_j811-020.jpg
Notes: 48555221372 This is an attractive little fly (Diptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it might be in the family Lauxaniidae, possibly species Minettia flaveola. Another possibility is the genus Suillia in the family Heleomyzidae, though the larvae of those flies are said to develop in fungi, and this seems the wrong time of year for that. I've seen these flies before, e.g. this photo a month ago. I get confused by them every year. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lauxaniidae; Minettia flaveola; Suillia; Heleomyzidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lauxaniidae; Minettia flaveola; Suillia; Heleomyzidae

File.Name: j817a_j817-011.jpg
Notes:
48562561161 Here is another golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) snuggled into a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. We were deep in a gloomy marine-layer fog this morning, and this bee was being dormant. The sun did start to break through the clouds after I got this photo, and the bee started to move around and finally took off. I found another bee doing the same in a different flower, and I wonder if that's where they spent the night. After all, it was foggy and chilly right at dawn this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2019)

Like I said, we were deep in a gloomy marine-layer fog this morning - with those annoying fog winds. It did finally start to clear up after noon, but the temperature never got beyond the low-70s. It sounds like tomorrow will be much the same.

File.Name: j817b_j817-026.jpg
Notes: 48562506431 Here is another Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper on a plant of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) that's going to seed. You can see a few of the ripening seeds in the calyces where the flowers used to be, always in groups of four. The grasshopper looks like a nymph at first with those stubby little wings, but those little stubs are all the wings it will ever get. The colors of these grasshoppers is confusing, varying from tan to almost black, but they always have those little stubby wings. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2019)

File.Name: j817c_j817-028.jpg
Notes: 48562403971 This looks like the work of a terrestrial caddisfly, but I believe it is the stone nest of a Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae, probably genus Dianthidium. The whole nest is about 1/2 inch across, and the stones are tiny. It's located about waist high on a dead stem of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae), and made entirely out of small stones that are glued together. I see some asphalt on the right, which suggests that the stones were gathered from our nearby chipseal road. The real glue that's holding it all together is visible in the cracks, and I guess its some sort of wax or resin. I don't find these interesting nests very often, and I don't know that I've seen the bee that's responsible. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2019)

File.Name: j817d_j817-003.jpg
Notes: 48562492337 This is just a fly - but what a fly! I'm pretty sure this is another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. It's perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It was reluctant to move on this foggy morning, but It took off just after I got my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2019)

File.Name: j818a_j818-002.jpg
Notes:
48571607617 This is the nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) "hiding" in the flower scar of a cactus fruit in the garden this morning. I don't know what it is about the cactus fruit, but the crickets love to hide there, try a search to find my 23 photos so far! This one is smaller than some of the nymphs I've been finding, but they all still have a ways to go. They will become the songsters of our woods on late-summer and autumn nights later in the season. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2019)

We were deep in a gloomy marine-layer fog again this morning, but at least those annoying fog winds weren't so bad. It did finally start to clear up after noon, but it stayed cool and never got beyond the low-70s. It sounds like tomorrow will start the same and then begin warming up, becoming hot by midweek.

File.Name: j818b_j818-003.jpg
Notes: 48571527557 Here is a cute little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched on a dry grass stem this morning. We were still deep in a gloomy marine-layer fog, and this fly didn't feel like moving. It really is little, just about 1660 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 6.5 mm - about 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figure.) (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2019)

File.Name: j818c_j818-008.jpg
Notes: 48571449447 Here is another "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) munching a flower bud of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). This caterpillar is not decorated like the others I've been showing lately, for example in this photo, and I assume it's a different kind. Like all of these caterpillars, it stands on a platform of two pairs of hind prolegs and three pairs of true legs in front, and walks by looping along. The flowers in this photo are still wet from the morning dew on this foggy morning. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2019)

File.Name: j818d_j817-013.jpg
Notes: 48571228046 This is the other golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) that I found yesterday snuggled into different summer flowers of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae - see my [Previous] photo (here). We were deep in a gloomy marine-layer fog that morning, and this bee was being dormant inside the flower. I got this photo just as the sun was starting to break through the clouds and the bee was waking up - which is why it looks so much brighter than my previous shot. I still wonder if this where the bee spent the night. After all, it was foggy and chilly right at dawn that morning. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2019)

File.Name: j819a_j819-024.jpg
Notes:
48580616796 This is an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly ovipositing (laying eggs) on an Everlasting (Gnaphalium or Pseudognaphalium) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe this plant is Wright's Cudweed (Pseudognaphalium microcephalum), but the butterflies seem to use all different everlasting plants. I have another shot of this kind of butterfly ovipositing on the same kind of plant in this photo from a few weeks ago. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2019)

Today was a lovely day for getting photos and just for being in the woods! The marine-layer fog stayed on the other side of the mountains, the breeze wasn't too bad, and it warmed up to the low-80s . . . but no hotter. It's expected to get hot again now. I won't be able to post tomorrow and maybe the next day, but I'll be back.
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium; Pseudognaphalium; Asteraceae; Pseudognaphalium microcephalum
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium; Pseudognaphalium; Asteraceae; Pseudognaphalium microcephalum

File.Name: j819b_j819-002.jpg
Notes: 48580553816 Here are two shots of a handsome Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly showing its wings above and below. It's perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Usually they are in the willows nearby, but this one was chasing a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly, and it settled here for a moment. Hah, the Sisters seem to taunt the Admirals! They look much alike, but the orange wing-tips of the Admirals go right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border, and the underwings are different - compare this photo and this photo. These butterflies don't seem to visit flowers. I see them most days when I'm near the creek, but I rarely get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2019)
ScientificName: Basilarchia lorquini; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Adelpha bredowii
associatedTaxa: Basilarchia lorquini; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Adelpha bredowii

File.Name: j819c_j819-019.jpg
Notes: 48580631722 Here is a mating pair of little "true bugs" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a plant of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) that's going to seed. I suspect that's the larger female on the left, and that they are Plant Bugs in the family Miridae. I won't be able to post this on Wednesday, but happy hump day anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2019)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Miridae

File.Name: j819d_j819-007.jpg
Notes: 48580576022 This is the nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a stem of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) that is just getting ready to flower. It's probably a Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae. It's interesting how different the nymphs look from the adults they will become. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2019)
CommonNames: Sawtooth Goldenbush; Stink Bug; Shield Bug
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Pentatomidae

File.Name: j821a_j820-050.jpg
Notes:
48596136266 Here are fantastic new seeds on a shrub of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae). They look like little fancy candies in their own plush baskets. The seeds come in fours like most plants in the Mint Family. There are still flower buds at the bottom of my photo, and the seeds get older and ripen as you go up. Hah - did you notice the little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) waiting for prey on the seed cluster at the top-left! Nah, I didn't notice either until I got my photo up on he computer screen. See my [Previous] photo for a closer shot of a different individual. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2019)

I'm back. I didn't have a chance for new photos today, but I had some nice shots from yesterday that I didn't have a chance to post last night. It's been warm, almost 90∞ today. We've been under a NWS Wind Advisory both nights, though the wind is mostly west of us, towards Point Conception.
CommonNames: Woolly Blue Curls; Pacific Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae
associatedTaxa: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae

File.Name: j821b_j820-040.jpg
Notes: 48596079291 This is another little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) waiting for something on the tip of a stem of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae). They always remind me of shogun-era samurai warriors with their armor! They are small but effective predators that can bring down prey that is much larger than they are - like the butterflies in this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2019)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: j821c_j820-024.jpg
Notes: 48596157192 Here are two shots of a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting a flower of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). They aren't the best photos, but these bees are hard to photograph - fast and easy to lose among the multitude of flowers. I'm sure the bee is family Megachilidae, maybe genus Megachile. These bees collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees, and it's interesting how the shape of the flower perfectly matches the shape of the bee's abdomen in the photo on the left. I'll keep trying to get better photos that aren't so overexposed so I can actually see what is going on. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2019)

File.Name: j821d_j820-057.jpg
Notes: 48595933301 This spider makes a dome web rather than a bowl web like the Bowl and Doily Spider spider is this photo. I believe it's either the Filmy Dome Spider (Neriene radiata) or the Sierra Dome Spider (Neriene litigiosa), probably the latter based on range. The spider is actually in this photo near the top of the dome - I'll make a note, though it's probably not visible at this scale. similar webs seem to be more common in the Oregon woods, see this photo. All of these spiders are in the Linyphiidae family of Sheet Web Weavers. I got today's photo above the retaining wall on the wild edge of our garden. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2019)
CommonNames: Filmy Dome Spider; Sierra Dome Spider

File.Name: j822a_j822-039.jpg
Notes:
48603867107 Here is one of the last flowers of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae) in our canyon at home - and the wild bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) that depend on it. The large golden Bumble Bee (Bombus) is sucking one of the nectar glands of the flower - follow its proboscis down to the bump on the outside of the flower which is the back of the gland, see this photo for a close look. The smaller bees might be Sweat Bees in the family Halictidae, see this photo and John Kehoe's comments. There are just a few more of these flowers still coming, and then they will be done for the year. What will the bees do then? (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2019)

Today was a lovely day - more lovely because I was outside to enjoy it after missing yesterday! Highs were in the 80s and there was some breeze, not too bad. Tomorrow is expected to be in the 70s.
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; wild bees; Bumble Bee; Sweat Bees
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Bombus; Halictidae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Bombus; Halictidae

File.Name: j822b_j822-036.jpg
Notes: 48603632606 Here are two shots of a tiny wild bee by a flower bud of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The flower bud has been hollowed out by a remarkable little looper caterpillar, see this photo where I caught it in the act. I believe the bee is a Masked Bee aka Yellow-faced Bee in the genus Hylaeus. These bees are unique in that they lack pollen-collecting hairs. Instead, they carry pollen and nectar in their gut and regurgitate it when they get back to their nests. I've been seeing quite a few of these lately, especially on the California Buckwheat flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2019)
CommonNames: wild bee; Woolly Blue Curls; Masked Bee; Yellow-faced Bee

File.Name: j822c_j819-015.jpg
Notes: 48603684117 These are the calyces of done flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) - with a little beetle (Coleoptera) hanging out of the one on the right. I believe that the beetle is a Soft-winged Flower Beetle in the beetle family Melyridae, maybe genus Collops but maybe not. See this photo from July of what may be the same beetle. Its wings seem too short for its abdomen, which is good for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2019)
CommonNames: Woolly Blue Curls; beetle; Soft-winged Flower Beetle
ScientificName: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Coleoptera; Melyridae; Collops
associatedTaxa: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Coleoptera; Melyridae; Collops

File.Name: j822d_j817-015.jpg
Notes: 48603606357 This is the rear end (at least) of a golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) leaving a summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). In fact it's the same bee I showed in this photo a few days ago with the bee still waking up inside the flower. This is a beautiful bee's behind in a beautiful wildflower, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2019)

File.Name: j823a_j823-030.jpg
Notes:
48609514441 So that's why they're called GRAY Hairstreaks! This is looking down on a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly on a plant of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. These butterflies usually keep their wings folded up while at rest, so I was happy to get this shot today. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 August 2019)

We were deep in marine-layer fog this morning, so I went back to the higher mountains to find some sun. I ended up at the place I call FAA Peak - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro at 3600 feet that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. Even there, I was on the upper edge of the marine layer, and the "fog winds" were substantial. It was finally starting to clear up when I got home around 2:00. It was cool today, only in the 60s for most of the day. It's expected to warm up for the weekend,

File.Name: j823b_j823-039.jpg
Notes: 48609063478 Here are two shots of a remarkable Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) that I found today on flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I'm sure the fly is Geminaria canalis - nothing else is like it. You can see its long proboscis and true eyes in the photo on the right. What's most remarkable are the those dark "eye spots" on its back that reflect light just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes. This fly digs into flowers with its long proboscis, and sticks its butt in the air, and holds its wings out - and it sure looks like a long-nosed creature in the photo on the left! See this photo from last year for the whole fly. These flies aren't common in our mountains, though I found them last year on the same flowers. I thought they had missed the boat when the flowers stopped this year, but they started producing a few new flowers, and here they are - one of them at least. It was quite photo shy, and I took a lot of photos to get these keepers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 August 2019)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Jumping Spider
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Geminaria canalis; Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Geminaria canalis; Salticidae

File.Name: j823c_j820-017.jpg
Notes: 48609366306 Here is a tiny fly (Diptera) about to stick it's needle-like proboscis into a flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). I believe the fly is an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae. Maybe it's in the tribe Phthiriini, but BugGuide seems to be down right now, so I can't confirm that. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2019)

File.Name: j823d_j817-031.jpg
Notes: 48608982758 This is "just a fly" on an oak leaf in the woods. I believe it's a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae - those golden hairs on its thorax are distinctive, but the flies seem to lose them as they age. This is one those flies you probably don't want to see walking across your potato salad. I should add this shot to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2019)

File.Name: j824a_j824-002.jpg
Notes:
48614719152 Here is a lovely California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly perched for a moment on a leaf of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) that is climbing up into an oak tree. These butterflies should be common, but they have actually been rather scarce this year, and I've had few photos of them. The Lorquin's Admiral butterfly in this photo looks similar but lacks the dark border around the orange patches on its forewings. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2019)

What a difference a day makes! Today was pleasant, though there was an annoying breeze from the south all morning. The marine-layer fog we had yesterday disappeared, and it got into the low-80s - warm but not too hot, nice.

File.Name: j824b_j824-009.jpg
Notes: 48614497186 Here are two shots of a goofy-looking net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it is genus Neaethus of the family Tropiduchidae, and this is the first of its kind that I've found since January (here). I guess that one was the last of last year's brood, and this one is the first of this year's brood. I like their faces - like the grille of a big truck! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2019)

File.Name: j824c_j824-014.jpg
Notes: 48614612317 I found this handsome Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera hiding among leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. I find this a confusing group, but this one seem to be different from the green bug in my [Previous] photo - compare the antennae. Maybe this one is the Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens), though there are other possibilities. If it is that species, they don't always have that red line across the thorax - in fact, sometimes they look completely brown. Like I said, it's a confusing group. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2019)
CommonNames: Green Stink Bug; Coast Live Oak; Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug

File.Name: j824d_j824-001.jpg
Notes: 48614420271 I found this handsome Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods this morning. I find this a confusing group, but this one seem to be different from the green bug in my [Next] photo - compare the antennae. There are lots of different kinds, maybe this one is species Chinavia hilaris? (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2019)

File.Name: j825a_j825-020.jpg
Notes:
48620850141 This is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly ovipositing (laying eggs) on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Is that an egg on the tip of her abdomen or just an internal ovipositor?? I have found a caterpillar of these butterflies on this shrub, see this photo taken a few years ago in the same place. And see this photo from a couple of days ago that shows the surprising upper wings of these butterflies - and give them their name. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2019)

It was into the upper 80s today, on the edge of being hot, with that same annoying breeze from the south all morning again. Tomorrow is actually expected to be a few degrees cooler than today, how nice!

File.Name: j825b_j825-032a.jpg
Notes: 48620755671 Look close . . . this is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly ovipositing (laying eggs) on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by our mailbox today. She was doing her best to hide from me, but I got my photo! See my [Previous] photo for a better shot of the same butterfly, but I like these "action shots" that show the butterfly's life. I've had good luck finding ovipositing butterflies this year, see my ongoing list in my comments to this photo. This one is probably Behr's Metalmark (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti), see the species account at BugGuide for more info. (The "splitters" are in control!) This is the first of these butterflies that I've seen in the mountains this year. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2019)

File.Name: j825c_j825-035.jpg
Notes: 48620832677 I found this handsome Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly on the native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by our mailbox today. It's probably Behr's Metalmark (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti), see the species account at BugGuide for more info. (The "splitters" are in control!) It was ovipositing - see my [Next] photo. This is the first of these butterflies that I've seen in the mountains this year. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2019)

File.Name: j825d_j825-004.jpg
Notes: 48620297213 Here is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working new flowers in the mint patch of the garden this morning. I believe these butterflies have two broods - a spring brood early in the year and a summer brood just starting now. Follow its proboscis down to the flower to appreciate what good aim it has! (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2019)

File.Name: j826a_j826-037.jpg
Notes:
48627785696 Here are two shots of a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new composite flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) - one of the first new flowers I've found on this plant. I'm not sure about this, but the bee might be genus Ashmeadiella which my book describes as "small, burly bees, many with red-and-white striped abdomens" - a perfect description. Maybe this is the one? (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2019)

It was hotter rather than cooler today, almost 90∞. There were mid-level clouds in the morning, the remnant of Tropical Storm Ivo. The clouds disappeared, but the humidity persisted.

File.Name: j826b_j826-019.jpg
Notes: 48627873167 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) on a new flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) - one of the first new flowers I've found on this plant. I believe the humpbacked fly is an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. Yeah, the fly's not quite sharp in my photo but the flower is, go figure. I still like the photo - being sharp isn't everything. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Geron

File.Name: j826c_j826-029.jpg
Notes: 48627630986 Here is a clutch of insect eggs on a leaf and surrounding stem of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae). I'm pretty sure they are eggs of some "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera), see this photo and this photo of another recent hatch. These eggs are tiny, the opening is just 180 pixels across in another 1:1 photo, which comes to 0.7 mm. (See here for how I figure.) These plants have just started to flower, see my [Next] two photos. It's a nice thing about plants starting to flower that I look at them closer - and notice things like these eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2019)

File.Name: j826d_j825-048.jpg
Notes: 48627241258 This is a large golden Bumble Bee (Bombus) sharing mint flowers with a Spotted Flower Buprestid (Acmaeodera) beetle. These bees have been working the nearby White Sage flowers for weeks now, but the flowers are finally just about done, and the bees are diversifying. I got this photo in the mint patch of the garden which is just starting to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2019)

File.Name: j827c_j824-032.jpg
Notes:
48633238071 Here is a mating pair of tiny Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) on still-new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the canyon at home. The plants are just about done by now. I've always assumed that these bugs are color-shifters that can change to match their flowers, but I usually find what I guess are yellowish males riding white females. But see this photo in which the colors are reversed. It's interesting that the texture of the bugs is different too - the larger female is bumpier than the male. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2019)

Just two photos today, as I'm not feeling well. I did get outside today to get some new photos, and I got a few despite an annoying breeze that kept everything in motion. But I think I'm better off taking it easy with my tummy right now.
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bugs; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: j827d_j823-013.jpg
Notes: 48633314702 This is a new flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family, with a mating pair of tiny beetles visible inside between the pistils and stamens. I don't know what kind of beetles they are, except they are tiny ones. These summer flowers are important in our summer-dry climate because they keep the insects alive during the hardest time of year. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 August 2019)

File.Name: j828a_j828-003.jpg
Notes:
48639034333 Here is another lovely California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly puddling - taking moisture - on a damp path in our garden this morning. The blue colors on its forewings are particularly handsome - they are iridescent and only show up when the butterfly is fresh and holds its wings just so. I've been thinking that these butterflies are uncommon this year, but there were four of them on this garden path today, so maybe they're just late to appear. I wanted to get a photo of "three sisters" close together, but they wouldn't cooperate. I'll try again tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2019)

My tummy is feeling better today, so I took my usual walk. It was a pleasantly warm day, temps about 80 degrees, with the same annoying breeze we've had for the last few days.

File.Name: j828b_j828-032.jpg
Notes: 48638954618 This is a Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) nibbling new seeds of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The seeds come in fours like most plants in the Mint Family. I'm not sure what kind of grasshopper it is, maybe an unusually pale Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes, Melanoplinae) with those blue legs? (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2019)
CommonNames: Grasshopper; Woolly Blue Curls; Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae
