File.Name: j828c_j828-027.jpg
Notes: 48638902498 Here is another net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it is genus Neaethus of the family Tropiduchidae. The adults have just started to appear for the year. Usually they hold their wings out flat (like this) whereas this one has them folded down. I had the distinct impression at the time that this one is teneral - recently emerged from a molt - and that might have something to do with it. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2019)

File.Name: j828d_j826-003.jpg
Notes: 48638879758 This is a little spider bungalow on a mint leaf in the garden. I don't know what kind of spider it is, but I like the front porch. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2019)

File.Name: j829a_j829-001.jpg
Notes:
48645129371 It's a Godzilla Bee! Nah, I was trying to get a close photo of the tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) in the foreground on the mint flowers in the garden, when this relatively giant Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) photo-bombed me. I only saw it through my viewfinder, but I had the presence of mind to keep shooting photos as fast as I could, and this one came out almost full-frame. The bee walked right over the little spider. I saw the spider afterwards on a leaf below the flowers, and I watched it balloon away into a nearby shrub. I guess it had enough excitement for one morning! (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2019)

Today was warmer than its been, almost 90∞ on the upstairs porch, and less-breezy than it's been at least in the mornings. They are connected, as the normal breeze has been from the south, the ocean side of the mountains, so it's had a cooling influence. It was less breezy this morning, but we're under a NWS Wind Advisory this evening, and there might be a Sundowner Wind event in the works. Fortunately, the wind seems to be diminishing rather than increasing as I write this.

File.Name: j829b_j829-028.jpg
Notes: 48644706003 This is a tiny black wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) doing "nectar theft" on a non-native flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden today. It's robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen, see this photo. The bee does have pollen-collecting hairs on its hind legs, though they are hard to see in this photo, and it must have a very long tongue to reach to the base of that flower. (Is its proboscis really that long??) For these reasons, I believe it's a Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina. Hah, look how the base of the flowers have been shredded by other larger bees doing the same trick! (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2019)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia x 'Waverly'; Lamiaceae; Ceratina
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia x 'Waverly'; Lamiaceae; Ceratina

File.Name: j829c_j829-025.jpg
Notes: 48644667603 Here is a little Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly working new flowers in the mint patch of the garden this morning. I'm not sure what kind of skipper it is, but it's a nice shot of the butterfly's rear end - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2019)

File.Name: j829d_j824-035.jpg
Notes: 48644598838 This is the rear end of a tiny Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) clutching the central pistil of a flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). I can't explain that bump on the bug's butt. My other photos of these predator bugs like this one don't seem to show it, though I've never gotten a photo from quite this angle before. The pistil of the flower is part of the central pedestal which is visible in many of my recent photos, e.g. this one. This photo certainly shows a bug's beatiful butt, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2019)

File.Name: j830a_j830-004.jpg
Notes:
48650562941 This is a furry little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working a flower in the mint patch of the garden today. We seem to have bigger and smaller ones of these, and this one is in-between. Maybe it is genus Villa in the tribe Villini - and maybe even species Villa lateralis which is said to have clear wings unlike most members of the group. It's a cute little fly! Note that it has its proboscis in the flower as well as its foot. I assume it's after nectar - unlike the Syrphid Fly I show two photos back which is going for pollen. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2019)

Note that all my photos today are from the mint patch of the garden. It's a productive spot now that the mints have started to flower. It was another nice day, with temps in the upper-80s and not much wind - even at sundown. I'm getting pretty distracted as we're getting ready to head off to Oregon for a few weeks, leaving on Sunday. Fortunately my innards seem to have settled down by now. I'll be busy getting ready tomorrow, but I reckon I can find a few photos to post.
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Villa; Villini; Villa lateralis
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Villa; Villini; Villa lateralis

File.Name: j830b_j827-027.jpg
Notes: 48650128083 Here is an impressive Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden, maybe species Stenopogon californiae which is said to be quite variable. It's close to an inch long and has impressive whiskers. These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing between their bristly front legs, like the dragonflies they resemble. They don't visit flowers, so there's no easy way to find them, and I was surprised to find this one in the garden - and more surprised when it landed on my tripod and then on my hand! (Photo tomorrow.) I've seen this kind before, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2019)

File.Name: j830c_j827-015.jpg
Notes: 48650442671 This is an interesting little Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on new flowers in the mint patch of the garden. It's lapping pollen from the stamens and is giving one of them a kind of kiss with its proboscis. This fly has Popeye-like enlarged legs! It also has a tiny red Mite hanging off its rear end, unfortunately its hard to see in this photo. I believe the fly is genus Syritta in the subfamily Eristalinae, probably species Syritta pipiens. (The genus Tropidia looks somewhat similar.) I seem to see these little flies every year, see this photo from last year. It's interesting that the Bee Fly two photos ahead seems to be getting nectar instead of pollen. I was going to post this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2019)
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Syritta; Eristalinae; Syritta pipiens; Tropidia

File.Name: j830d_j825-001.jpg
Notes: 48650059383 Here is a cute little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched on the end of a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. There are many different kinds of these flies, so I'll leave it at that. I was going to post this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2019)

File.Name: j831a_j830-001.jpg
Notes:
48656048831 Here is another handsome Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. These butterflies are small and quite plain below, but they make up for it by being soft and furry. See my [Previous] photo to appreciate how different they are from the Umber Skipper butterflies. That dark streak on the forewings is the butterfly's stigma - a glandular patch that releases pheromones during courtship that only males have. It's interesting that I've been seeing lots of these but maybe no Rural Skippers (Ochlodes agricola) that should be common about now. It's been that kind of year, with odd patterns of scarcity and abundance that I can't explain. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2019)

I had no time for new photos today. We're heading off to Oregon for a few weeks starting tomorrow, and there's lots to do before we leave. We'll be pretty much off the grid along the North Umpqua River, just the way I like it. We have some work to do up there, but I'll try to come back with a few photos.

File.Name: j831b_j830-012.jpg
Notes: 48656148927 I was getting a photo of the Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly on the left working new flowers in the mint patch of the garden, when the other skipper showed up on the same flower cluster. You can tell that it's something different even out of focus like this - look at the color, especially the color of the antennae. The second skipper is a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly, see my [Next] photo. Skippers are not the easiest butterflies to distinguish, but there are differences - it gets easier with time and practice. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2019)

File.Name: j831c_j830-021.jpg
Notes: 48656117942 These are nymphs of a Large Milkweed Bug (Oncopeltus fasciatus, Lygaeidae) on a seedpod of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). I have a shot of both nymphs and adults in this photo from last year. I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley yesterday, and I got this photo in ranch country along the way. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2019)

File.Name: j831d_j827-028.jpg
Notes: 48655925991 Here's another shot of the impressive Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) that I showed in this photo yesterday - now it's on the index finger of my left hand! I noticed this fly on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden, then it landed on my tripod and ended up on my hand. It's likely the species Stenopogon californiae which is said to be quite variable. If you compare my previous photo, it's interesting how the fly looks smaller on something familiar like my finger. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2019)

File.Name: j920a_j920-009.jpg
Notes:
48766993963 Here is a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), probably the species Spilomyia interrupta, though there are other similar species in the same genus. This one is squatting on new staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). These flies have remarkable eyes and a curious habit of flicking their wings while at rest. They have an annoying whine as they fly - they sound furious. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2019)

Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best! We got back from our Oregon trip two days ago. Hah, I took 1200 photos on that trip, and I'm still sorting them out and deleting many. I've also been taking my usual daily walks and getting lots of new photos. Today's photos are all flies for Fly Day Friday. Tomorrow I'll have some Oregon slugs and snails for Snail Saturday!

File.Name: j920b_j920-025.jpg
Notes: 48767453907 This fly has a remarkable face. It looks a bit like a common Blue Bottle Fly or Blow Fly in the genus Lucilla or Calliphora, Calliphoridae, Diptera, but its face suggests that it is something more interesting. I think it might be the "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria), compare this photo and BugGuide. If I'm right, this fly is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies in that group. The fly is native to North America. Its larvae feed on dead tissue, including human tissue, but it is the "secondary" screwworm that only feeds on already dead tissue, requiring an exit wound to infest. It actually has some medical uses. It has forensic use as the "principal species on which to base postmortem interval estimations". And the larvae have been used as "surgical maggots" to clean only necrotic tissue from wounds. There's more info at Wikipedia. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2019)
CommonNames: Blue Bottle Fly; Blow Fly; Secondary Screwworm
ScientificName: Lucilla; Calliphora; Calliphoridae; Diptera; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae

File.Name: j920c_j920-030.jpg
Notes: 48767194251 Here is a cute little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) flying into a new flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). There are many different kinds of these flies, so I'll leave it at that. I was going to post this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2019)

File.Name: j920d_j919-010.jpg
Notes: 48766816838 This is a cute little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on an unopened flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). I believe it's female, but you have to look close to see her ovipositor in this photo. She has amazing eyes and an interesting pattern on her wings that reminds me of a Japanese Rising Sun flag. There are many different kinds of these flies, so I'll leave it at that. I was going to add this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2019)

File.Name: j921a_j921-007.jpg
Notes:
48772533841 Here is a gorgeous nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see the "training wings" of this late-instar nymph that will soon become an adult. Note that spike-like proboscis folded under it's head. This may well be the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii), in which case they lose some of their charm as they mature - compare this photo taken last November. This is the first of these nymphs that I've found since last year at this time. I'm happy to find it - it seems late. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2019)

Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best! We got back from our Oregon trip a few days ago, and it took a while to sort through my photos. Meanwhile I'm getting new photos every day. *Sigh* There's never enough time to post them all.

File.Name: j921b_j918-114.jpg
Notes: 48772468366 Even in summer, I found banana slugs in the Oregon woods during our recent trip. Well, it was late summer, and we did get a bit of early autumn rain. This is a large greenish Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus). You can see its eye-stalks and feelers on the left. These Oregon slugs look quite different from the equally large California Banana Slugs (Ariolimax californicus) that we have at home, compare this photo from last year. There is some confusion about their names, but the difference seems clear to me. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 7 September 2019)
CommonNames: Pacific Banana Slug; California Banana Slugs

File.Name: j921c_j918-095.jpg
Notes: 48772592387 This is another native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) that I found in the Oregon woods during our recent trip. It's crawling over a frond of Western Sword Fern (Polystichum munitum, Dryopteridaceae). This one is larger and shinier than the one in my [Previous] photo, and I have no doubt that they are different species. It seems unusually relaxed. I'm not sure what kind of snail it is, but HSS anyway - Happy Snail Saturday! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 September 2019)

File.Name: j921d_j918-088.jpg
Notes: 48772011923 Here is a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) that I found in the Oregon woods during our recent trip. It's smaller, flatter, and duller than the usual snails I find in Oregon, compare my [Next] photo. I'm not sure what kind of snail it is, but HSS anyway! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 September 2019)

File.Name: j922a_j922-034.jpg
Notes:
48778827752 This is a tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider on its web in front of a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. The spider is small, only 765 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm pretty sure this is the Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider, and you can see the six (sometimes eight) black spots on its abdomen. These spiders sometimes build their orb webs across the curvature of a single oak leaf. This one has a web that spans two leaves, but the whole thing is still less than two inches across. I'll have a photo of its whole web for the Web Wednesday group in a few days. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2019)

Today's photos are all spiders - this one from home and the rest from our recent trip to Oregon. Spiders are becoming more common as we head into autumn. I figure it's their role to finish off the summer insects before winter does anyway. The Arachtober group starts in just over a week, so I'm keeping track of all the spiders I find. The group goal is to post spider photos every day in October. My personal goal is to get new spider photos every day.

The autumnal equinox will happen tonight at 12:50 am, so tomorrow is the official first day of autumn. It still hot in the daytime here in California, but there were clouds all morning and there's a hint of an autumn chill when you step out of the direct sun. There's even a chance of real rain next weekend! In some ways this is my favorite time of year, but will it be drought or deluge in the coming year?
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak; Six-spotted Orb Weaver
ScientificName: Araneidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araniella displicata
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araniella displicata

File.Name: j922b_j918-193.jpg
Notes: 48778230133 Here is a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from the top of its dome-like web in the Oregon woods. If you look close, you can see that it has a safety line attached to the tip of its abdomen. I believe it is either the Filmy Dome Spider (Neriene radiata) or the Sierra Dome Spider (Neriene litigiosa). See this photo for the whole web of another spider of the same sort from last year. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 12 September 2019)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Filmy Dome Spider; Sierra Dome Spider

File.Name: j922c_j918-184.jpg
Notes: 48778182128 This cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) was on the trunk of our car parked at our cabin in the Oregon woods. I got a few photos before I shooed it away. Judging by its enlarged palps, it's a male. I think this one might be genus Phanias, maybe Phanias harfordi. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 September 2019)

File.Name: j922d_j918-089.jpg
Notes: 48778138573 I found this neat little spider on a leaflet of native Western Sword Fern (Polystichum munitum, Dryopteridaceae) in the Oregon woods during our recent trip. I believe it's a Running Crab Spider in the family Philodromidae. One distinguishing trait of this family is that leg II is longer than leg I which you can clearly see in my photo. This one might be genus Philodromus. These running crab spiders are active hunters, not at all like many of the Crab Spiders (Thomisidae) that wait in flowers. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 September 2019)
CommonNames: Western Sword Fern; Running Crab Spider; Crab Spiders
ScientificName: Polystichum munitum; Dryopteridaceae; Philodromidae; Philodromus; running; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Polystichum munitum; Dryopteridaceae; Philodromidae; Philodromus; running; Thomisidae

File.Name: j923a_j920-045.jpg
Notes:
48785151963 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) waiting for prey on new staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). It looks like the "crane kick" pose in the original Karate Kid movie! This one might be male with those enlarged palps. I'll record this as the "first flower" of this shrub so I can find it again, but it actually started to flower while we were away in Oregon. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants that look quite different. The flowers are staminate, but those sure look like little pollen-dusted pistils coming out of the stamens. I'm confused. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2019)

I did go out for new photos today, but there was a breeze, and I like this one from a few days ago better. It was cooler today than yesterday, and it's expected to be hotter again tomorrow. There's even a chance of light rain next weekend. That is how it is in autumn, a time of weather changes.
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious

File.Name: j923b_j918-198.jpg
Notes:
48785585877 I found this little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) huddling on the flowers of native Common Rabbitbrush (Chrysothamnus nauseosus, Asteraceae) growing near Mono Lake. I'm not sure, but maybe the bee is genus Melissodes with those long antennae and shaggy pollen baskets on her hind legs that remind me of the woolly chaps that some cowboys used to wear. It was cloudy and windy, and this bee just didn't feel like moving. (Navy Beach, Mono Lake, Mono County, California, 15 September 2019)

We didn't just go to Oregon on our recent trip. We drove back along the east side of the mountains, past Klamath Lake and Reno, with stops at Mono Lake and Lone Pine. Rabbitbrush in flower lined the roads most of the way, like a real yellow brick road.

File.Name: j923c_j918-217.jpg
Notes: 48785018718 Here are masses of Alkali Flies (Cirrula hians aka Ephydra hians) clinging to plant stems on the edge of briny Mono Lake. The plant is probably native Salt Grass (Distichlis spicata, Poacea) growing but failing in a place where there is some fresh water incursion. Or did the lake rise with a good snow pack this year? There were billions of the flies along the shore of the lake, but they are rather difficult to photograph, flying off in waves at the slightest disturbance. See my [Previous] photo for more information. (Navy Beach, Mono Lake, Mono County, California, 15 September 2019)
ScientificName: Cirrula hians; Ephydra hians; Distichlis spicata; Poacea
associatedTaxa: Cirrula hians; Ephydra hians; Distichlis spicata; Poacea

File.Name: j923d_j918-231.jpg
Notes: 48785243846 Here are rafts of Alkali Flies (Cirrula hians aka Ephydra hians) floating in a mostly fresh water spring along the edge of briny Mono Lake. There were billions of them along the shore of the lake, but they are rather difficult to photograph, flying off in waves at the slightest disturbance. These flies and little brine shrimp are the only creatures that actually live in the lake water - it's too salty for fish. (Hah, there's more!) These flies spend their larval and pupal life stages underwater, only the adults come out of the water. They are an important source of food for migratory birds, and the pupae were once an important food for local native Americans. I've always wanted to try them. (Navy Beach, Mono Lake, Mono County, California, 15 September 2019)

File.Name: j924a_j924-005.jpg
Notes:
48790565038 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best! This is an extraordinary diurnal (daytime) Lichen Moth (Lithosiini, Arctiidae) - probably Lycomorpha fulgens or Lycomorpha regulus. I don't know anything about them, except here is this one working staminate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's peacefully sharing the flowers with a little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera). These brilliant moths seem quite uncommon in our mountains. The last one I photographed was in October 2015, on the same kind of flowers. They look remarkably like small Tarantula Hawks (Pepsis, Hymenoptera) with those orange wings and so-blue-it-looks-black bodies, compare this photo. I don't know if this counts as mimicry. When you look close in the woods, you never know what you'll find! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2019)

It was downright HOT today, in the 90s - and up to 102∞ down by the Santa Ynez River. The National Weather Service reports that it should be much cooler tomorrow, and there's even a chance of light rain on Friday. That's our autumn weather in a nutshell . . . cha-cha-cha-changes!

I don't know what kind of grasshopper it is, but its colors are striking. I usually don't worry much about IDs away from my mountains at home, but I would appreciate any help identifying this creature. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 17 September 2019)

Notes:
48790410983 Spider love! Just count the legs and divide by two . . .

I'm not sure what kind of spiders these are. Their webbing was between two posts at our Oregon cabin. They are tiny, only a bit more than 1/8 inch long. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 12 September 2019)

File.Name: j925a_j925-019.jpg
Notes:
48795965808 I found this Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. I assumed at the time from the spines on its thorax that it's a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus), but that started looking wrong when I got the photo up on my computer screen - compare this photo of the real thing. This one is the wrong color and too large - almost an inch long - to be that species. After doing some browsing at BugGuide, I suspect it is species Heza similis. There's also a record at iNaturalist. Both sites only record this species from Arizona, so this is an interesting find here in California. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2019)

Today was expected to be much cooler than yesterday, but it turned out to be quite warm - in the mid-80s. It was also quite breezy today, windy enough to keep everything in motion. The forecast still calls for a substantial temperature drop tomorrow with morning clouds and temps in the 60s. There's less talk of light rain this weekend, but there might be some drizzle.

File.Name: j925b_j925-025.jpg
Notes: 48796391367 Here are two shots of a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on its web in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). In the close shot on the right, you can see that this spider actually has eight spots. It's small, only about 760 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) These spiders sometimes build their orb webs across the curvature of a single oak leaf. This one has a web that spans three leaves, but the whole thing is still only about 1.5 inches across. I have another photo of the same spider here. Happy Web Webnesday! File.Name: j925c_j918-127.jpg
Notes: 48796168701 Here are two shots of a little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) staying dry under an open-mesh web in the Oregon woods. The spider is hard to see in the shot on the left which I got while it was raining, but look in the center towards the top. I got the shot on the right on the next day when the rain had stopped. The spider looks quite dry under its webbing, a testament to the power of surface tension. I don't think of these spiders spinning webs, but here it is. This spider is among the needles on a branch of a Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii, Pinaceae) sapling in the Oregon woods. After I noticed this one, I found several more doing the same thing. The spider in my [Previous] photo has more deluxe accommodations. Happy Web Webnesday! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 8 September 2019)

File.Name: j925d_j918-156.jpg
Notes: 48796139426 This is a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) getting out of the rain between two leaves of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum, Ericaceae) near our cabin in the Oregon woods. I don't think of these spiders spinning webs, but this one has made a shelter under the leaves with webbing across the front. See my [Next] photo for another example. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 9 September 2019)

File.Name: j926a_j926-003.jpg
Notes:
48801139793 Here are two photos of interesting flies (Diptera) on two different flowers growing a few feet away from each other - California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) on the left and Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) on the right, both in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe the fly in an atypical Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) in the genus Neacreotrichus, compare the images at BugGuide. It's an attractive fly. It has a remarkably long curved proboscis, and what gives with those divided eyes? (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2019)

The fog came in, the fog went out; the fog came in again . . . and stayed all day. The marine-layer fog was pushed over the mountains by steady fog winds up to 20 mph from the south. There has been some drizzle. With the fog and wind today, I'm glad my photos came out as good as they are.

I believe the spider is a male of the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus. I have more nice shots of the other end of this spider if I ever get a chance to post them - maybe during Arachtober which starts soon. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2019)

Notes: 48801493652 I found this odd Cattail (Typha, Typhaceae) growing as is along Lone Pine Creek in the Alabama Hills near Lone Pine, California on the way home from our recent trip to Oregon. I wonder what happened? (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 17 September 2019)

File.Name: j927a_j927-041.jpg
Notes:
48806498652 Here are two tiny humpbacked flies on new flowers of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). The small composite flowers are actually on the same plant, just inches apart. I believe the flies are both atypical Bee Flies in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. I thought at the time that the two flies are male and female, and maybe they are, with a smaller male on the left and the larger female on the right - compare this photo of a mating pair at BugGuide. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2019)

We were in the clouds again today, but for some reason there was no wind. It was almost chilly, only in the low 60s. Really, a very nice day for being outside and getting a few photos.

File.Name: j927b_j927-023.jpg
Notes: 48805957668 I found this bristly fly (Diptera) today on staminate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group today, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2019)

File.Name: j927c_j925-006.jpg
Notes: 48806247726 This is a large bee-size Fly (Diptera) working new flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's almost the size of a Carpenter Bee! I'm pretty sure it's a Mexican Cactus Fly (Copestylum mexicanum) in the family Syrphidae. I had to overexpose the flowers to bring out some detail in the black/blue fly. It's been a few years since I've seen one of these. Apparently the larvae feed on rotting cactus. We have cacti in our garden some distance away, but I don't know any other source up here in the mountains. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2019)

File.Name: j927d_j918-204.jpg
Notes:
48805783903 The real challenge showing the Alkali Flies (Cirrula hians aka Ephydra hians) at Mono Lake is just showing a few flies and not gazillions of them like this photo and this photo. Here are just a few flies after most of them too off when I got too close. It's only a few flies, but lots of remains of pupal cases of the flies - which the flies seemed to be interested in. Here's the remarkable story of these flies from the Mono Lake Committee website:

"Alkali flies spend two of their three life stages entirely underwater. The larval and pupal life stages develop within the lake. When the adult fly is ready to emerge from the pupa case its head comes apart! The head separates and a small sac inflates and pops the top off the pupa case. The sac then collapses, the fly's head reassembles itself, and the fly emerges from the case to float to the surface where it then begins its adult life cycle. Eventually adult flies return underwater to lay eggs or feed on algae. Tiny hairs trap a thin layer of air which allows the fly to 'scuba dive.' On calm days in the summer you can watch small, silvery teardrop shapes amble along the bottom of the lake in shallow water."

I assume that the pupae in my photo are the cases after the flies emerge. There was a ring of them around the lake, probably washed ashore by the wind. It's sometimes said that these flies and little brine shrimp are the only creatures that actually live in the lake water apart from algae and bacteria - it's too salty for fish. Now there are recent reports of extremophilic nematodes that also live in the lake, read about them here and here. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group today, but HFDF anyway! (Navy Beach, Mono Lake, Mono County, California, 15 September 2019)

File.Name: j928a_j928-027.jpg
Notes:
48811202498 This is an impressive bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) hiding under a leaf in an apple tree on the wild edge of our garden. That's a ripening apple behind the spider on the right. If you look close, you can see that the spider has twin humps on it's abdomen, a sign that it is genus Araneus, maybe Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides or both. (These species are known to cross.) If you look closer, you can see that it's tending a line that goes to its web - look close at its two left front legs. It's a proper Halloween spider - early for Halloween which doesn't happen for a month, but just in time for Arachtober which starts in a few days. I hope the spider sticks around. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2019)

We got 1/2 of 1/10 inch of drizzle last night and this morning - not much, but I reckon it's our first proper storm caused by a low pressure system from the north. It's early for that. It was cloudy again all morning, then it started to clear out in the afternoon. That's when the wind picked up, to 35 mph so far. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight, though it seems to be quieting down now.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: j928b_j928-003.jpg
Notes: 48811494901 Here are bunches of ripening berries of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae), still wet from the drizzle this morning. You can see the distinctive "perfoliate" leaves that the berries come from - like little umbrellas. The berries look tasty, but in my experience they taste bitter, even when dead ripe. Some web sites (e.g. Wikipedia) just repeat the claim that the berries are "edible but bitter" - isn't that an oxymoron? Even the birds seem to ignore them until there's nothing else. Pretty though. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2019)

File.Name: j928c_j927-002.jpg
Notes: 48811423086 This is an adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) "hiding" on the remains of an old flower on a cactus fruit in the garden. This one is a narrow-winged female, different from the broad-winged males in this photo - which is also on a cactus fruit. I don't know what it is about the cactus, but the crickets love to hide there, try a search to find my 20 photos so far! These crickets are the songsters of our woods on late-summer and autumn nights, but they go quiet when it's as chilly as it's been lately. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2019)

File.Name: j928d_j927-039.jpg
Notes: 48811377616 Here is another shot of the tiny humpbacked fly that I show in my [Previous] photo on the left. I believe it's an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane instead of a proboscis. Julie calls it a "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. The flower belongs to native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). The plant is kind of weedy, but it flowers in the fall when not much else is available, and insects and spiders like it. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2019)

File.Name: j929a_j929-035.jpg
Notes:
48817820888 This is another tiny Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) waiting for prey by a cluster of flowers in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. These bugs always remind me of shogun-era samurai warriors with their armor! They are small but effective predators that can bring down prey that is much larger than they are - like the butterflies in this photo and this photo. This is the season of the predators. I figure it's their role to finish off the summer insects before winter does anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2019)

It was sunny today, but chilly and breezy. Hah, chilly as in the 40s and 50s - not like the several feet of unusual September snow in Montana today, see NYT. Yeah, I have many more Oregon photos from our recent trip, but my new photos are crowding them out. It's time to move on.
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: j929b_j929-040.jpg
Notes: 48818205772 I found this handsome granite-colored Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) on the edge of the chipseal road in our canyon today. I'm pretty sure it's a Band-winged Grasshopper (Oedipodinae), probably the Thalassica Grasshopper (Trimerotropis thalassica) - though I'd have to see more of its legs to be sure. It has light green wings in flight. I saw quite a few of these today. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2019)

File.Name: j929c_j929-013.jpg
Notes: 48818162852 This is an adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) "hiding" on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This one is a broad-winged male, different from the narrow-winged female I showed yesterday in this photo. These crickets are the songsters of our woods on late-summer and autumn nights. Only the males "sing" by rubbing their wings together, see this photo of one caught in the act. They work the night shift and try to hide during the day - though this one isn't trying very hard. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2019)

File.Name: j929d_j929-001.jpg
Notes: 48818109712 Here are two shots of little net-winged Planthoppers (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera). I believe they are both genus Neaethus of the family Tropiduchidae. I found the one on the left on a Prickly Pear cactus in the garden this morning. It's surrounded by clumps of those horrible glochid spines - those short hair-like prickles that are barbed and and very difficult to remove. It's probably a pretty safe place to be. I found the one on the right in a shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) later in the day. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2019)

File.Name: j930a_j930-002.jpg
Notes:
48823755948 I found this Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) this morning. It's named for the tiny size of it's prey, not for any personal resemblance. You can see its mighty spike-like proboscis folded under its head - see this photo and this photo of the proboscis in use! Like I said yesterday about a different predator, this is the season of the predators. I figure it's their role to finish off the summer insects before winter does anyway. That goes for spiders too - and tomorrow is the start of Arachtober! (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2019)

It was sunny today, but chilly and breezy again, with gusts up to 40 mph so far. Fortunately there were moments of calm in between the gusts of wind, and I got a few nice photos. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight. This has the makings of a sundowner wind event, though it seems to be quieting down as I write this.

File.Name: j930b_j930-024.jpg
Notes: 48824200047 Here are two shots of a fresh-looking little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly working new flowers of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I was surprised to see this butterfly as the last one I managed to photograph was two months ago at the end of July, see this photo. My book says they have multiple broods, but it seems to me that this is their third distinct brood with gaps in between. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2019)
ScientificName: Phyciodes mylitta; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phyciodes mylitta; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: j930c_j930-037.jpg
Notes: 48823960001 This is a white-fringed Duskywing butterfly working an autumn flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) - at least it shows most of the butterfly which is missing a chunk of its hindwing. I'm pretty sure this is a Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) which is smaller and darker than the very similar Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) in this photo which is more brown in color. This is the butterfly that comes from the caterpillar on Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that I show in this photo. That plant is growing nearby - but so are Coast Live Oak trees that the Mournful Duskywing caterpillars utilize. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2019)
CommonNames: Duskywing; Telegraph Weed; Funereal Duskywing; Mournful Duskywing
ScientificName: Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Erynnis funeralis; Erynnis tristis; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Erynnis funeralis; Erynnis tristis; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: j930d_j929-014.jpg
Notes: 48823560978 Here are two shots of another lovely California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly puddling - taking moisture - from oak leaves still wet from some drizzle the day before. I've never seen these butterflies visit flowers, but they often stop to take moisture. This one looks pristine, like it's freshly emerged. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2019)

File.Name: ja01a_ja01-006.jpg
Notes:
48829493718 This is a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sac in a plant of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). Maybe it's the same spider that I found in the same plant last August, see this photo. I guess this one is Peucetia longipalpis because its abdomen has white hairs and lacks the usual chevron patterns of P. viridans, though it does have some white markings. It's my impression that this one has its babies first while it's still summer, while P. viridans comes later in fall - hopefully in time for Arachtober, but everything is late this year. I've got lots of photos of these spiders over the years, try a search of my photos. Arachtober 1. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2019)

If you don't know about Arachtober, be sure to check it out. The group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it like a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day of the month. I don't know that I can do it again, but I'll try - and I have lots of spider photos in the archive for backup. One problem is the weather. Today was quite windy again, and I only got this photo because I already knew where the spider is. Hopefully the wind will subside tomorrow.
ScientificName: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Peucetia longipalpis; P. viridans
associatedTaxa: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Peucetia longipalpis; P. viridans

File.Name: ja01b_ja01-023.jpg
Notes: 48829910647 I found this cute little net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) in the woods today on a stem of Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae, Asteraceae) which is just starting to flower. This hopper looks like the genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae that I've been showing lately, e.g. in this photo, but it's only about half the size - about 800 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It also has distinctly patterned wings which the other one lacks. I believe this one is the species Neaethus maculatus which is said to be the only member of this genus with spotted wings, see the species page at BugGuide. I've only found it once before, see this photo. This was a good find today, and I even got a decent photo despite the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2019)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae; Neaethus maculatus
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae; Neaethus maculatus

File.Name: ja01c_ja01-033.jpg
Notes: 48829681441 I found this lobster-colored true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) working the fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. I've seen this critter before, always late in the season, see this photo and this photo. I believe this bug is genus Arhyssus or Liorhyssus, a Scentless Plant Bug in the family Rhopalidae. It's almost the same color as the faded flowers of the buckwheat, which is great camouflage. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2019)
CommonNames: true bug; California Buckwheat; Scentless Plant Bug
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Arhyssus; Liorhyssus; Rhopalidae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Arhyssus; Liorhyssus; Rhopalidae

File.Name: ja01d_ja01-030.jpg
Notes: 48829628251 These are similar but different butterflies, even including the difference in their condition. On the left is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) and on the right is a Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini aka Limenitis lorquini). They look much alike, but the orange wing-tips of the Admiral goes right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border like the Sister has. Curiously, I got both photos today, in the same Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), just two minutes apart. Usually the Admirals stay in the willows nearby, but this one was chasing the Sister, and it settled here for a moment. Hah, the Sisters seem to taunt the Admirals! (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2019)
CommonNames: California Sister; Lorquin's Admiral; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Adelpha bredowii; Basilarchia lorquini; Limenitis lorquini; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Adelpha bredowii; Basilarchia lorquini; Limenitis lorquini; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ja02a_ja02-008.jpg
Notes:
48835102852 Here is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Yeah, I'm looking for new spiders to photograph every day for the Arachtober group, but I can always find time for these impressive predators. They even look kind of spider-like. Like I wrote a few days ago, autumn is the season of the predators, both spiders and insects. I figure it's their role to finish off the summer insects before winter does anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2019)

Today was sunny and a few degrees warmer than yesterday. The big winds are done, but there's still a slight breeze from the south just to make things difficult. Telegraph Weed in my [Next] photo is a tall plant that sways back and forth in the slightest breeze - and it was swaying today.
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ja02b_ja02-023.jpg
Notes: 48834450338 This is a striking little Lynx Spider (Oxyopidae), but is it an actual Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia) or just green? It's tiny, less than 1/4 inch long, on a flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). Yeah, I should have measured the flower; I'll do it tomorrow. I suspect that is indeed a juvenile Green Lynx Spider, maybe the same Peucetia longipalpis that I showed yesterday because it has white hairs on its abdomen and those are the early ones . It's curious that I've found near-spiderlings on these flowers before, see this photo from a couple of years ago. And here they are all alone at the top of a five-foot plant that's swaying in the breeze! Arachtober 2. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2019)
CommonNames: Lynx Spider; Green Lynx Spider; Telegraph Weed
ScientificName: Oxyopidae; Peucetia; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Peucetia longipalpis
associatedTaxa: Oxyopidae; Peucetia; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Peucetia longipalpis

File.Name: ja02c_ja02-046.jpg
Notes: 48834395548 I thought I was done getting photos today, but I spotted this spider web in the oak tree that comes close to our upstairs deck - and I got a photo. It's a large web a couple of feet across - and about 25 feet off the ground. For scale, compare the size of the oak leaves in my [Previous] photo! If you look close, you can see the spider in the upper-left - I'll make a note. This is another one of those bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that make large webs for Halloween, no doubt genus Araneus, maybe Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides or both. (These species are known to cross.) I have another recent photo of a similar spider here. Neither of these spiders is easily accessible, but I'm hoping for more so I can get a better close shot. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: ja02d_j930-017.jpg
Notes: 48834895272 Here is another shot of a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on its web in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can't actually see the six (or eight) black spots of its back in this shot, but my photo last week does show the top-side of the same type of spider in a different oak tree. These spiders are small, only about 1/8 inch long. They sometimes build their orb webs across the curvature of a single oak leaf. This one has a web that spans several leaves, but the whole thing is still only about 1.5 inches across. Hah, compare the size of the leaves in my [Next] photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2019)
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ja03a_ja03-013.jpg
Notes:
48839569138 Now why am I thinking of the balcony scene from Romeo and Juliet? Here are a pair of tiny Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) in a flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). They seem to be more interested in each other than their usual prey, but they got away from me before I could see what happens next. Like I said yesterday about a different insect predator, autumn is the season of the predators, both spiders and insects. I figure it's their role to finish off the summer insects before winter does anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2019)

Today was sunny and even warmer than yesterday, in the mid-70s. The big winds are done, but there's still that steady breeze from the south that makes keeps everything in motion and makes getting my photos difficult.
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: ja03b_ja03-033.jpg
Notes: 48839864896 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) about to balloon away from a flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). It appears to have three strands of silk coming from its spinnerets that go on much further than my photo shows. I only had a few moments to get photos after I spotted the spider, and this was the only shot that actually shows the silk. A moment later and *whoosh* the spider was gone! I believe it's another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen. It's tiny. The flower petals/ligules are only about 5-8 mm long, and the spider is less than half of that. Arachtober 3 and HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2019)

File.Name: ja03c_ja03-002.jpg
Notes: 48839802246 I found this Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a Prickly Pear cactus in our garden this morning. I believe it is a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia because of its narrow wings that do not look like leaves - compare this photo of something different. It's curious that this one is not quite as green as I expect them to be. These are the large adults that come from the small nymphs that are so pretty, see this photo. Common as the nymphs are, I don't see the adults very often. I suspect they're mostly nocturnal, so they stay out of sight. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2019)

File.Name: ja03d_j929-009.jpg
Notes: 48839738246 This is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) among foliage and old flowers of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). This is a female with narrow wings, compare this photo of the broad-winged male. I reckon it's trying to hide "Ostrich style" in the belief that if it can't see you then you can't see it. (Of course that doesn't work with nosy photographers!) It's rear end looks like . . . a dolphin face? HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2019)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: ja04a_ja04-017.jpg
Notes:
48844372413 I've been showing predators other than spiders for the last couple of days, here and here, and this is another one - a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are shy and retiring in my experience. It's interesting that I was also finding them last spring, e.g. this photo from March. Hah - it's got bumps and spines like a little dinosaur! I also found a different kind of assassin bug which will keep until tomorrow. Like I've said, autumn is the season of the predators, both spiders and insects. I figure it's their role to finish off the summer insects before winter does anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2019)

Today was sunny and even warmer than yesterday, in the upper-70s. The temperature is creeping up and will soon be in the 80s again if the forecast works out. It was almost calm this morning when I was out with my camera, though a breeze did kick up in the afternoon. Nice day!
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ja04b_ja04-001.jpg
Notes: 48844820502 I found this impressive bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in its hideaway in an apple tree on the wild edge of our garden. If you look close, you can see that it's touching a line to its nearby web, like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing. I believe it is genus Araneus with those paired humps, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) I have another photo of the same spider from a week ago here. It's a proper Halloween spider, and I hope it sticks around. Arachtober 4. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: ja04c_ja02-018.jpg
Notes: 48844535981 I believe this is an uncommon Stiletto Fly in the genus Thereva of the family Therevidae, see BugGuide. I found it on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and it got away from me soon after I got my photo. I've seen these handsome flies before, see this photo from last year and this photo from the year before, both from October. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2019)

File.Name: ja04d_j929-063.jpg
Notes: 48844132853 Here is a small dark Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a new flower of Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in our canyon. I believe it's a Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa), at least it's similar to that species. They've become quite common on the remaining late-season flowers now. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2019)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Hemipenthes sinuosa
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Hemipenthes sinuosa

File.Name: ja05a_ja05-028.jpg
Notes:
48850069026 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae), probably another genus Mecaphesa spider with those hairy legs and abdomen. I believe these little spiders are color-shifters, and this one is a perfect match for the flower it's on. That's good camouflage, but the spiders are also hard to see because they are so darn small. The round center of the composite flower is about 5 mm or 3/16 inch across - and the spider is less than half that size. I haven't been seeing very many of these flower-loving spiders compared to previous years, and I suspect they are just late getting started like so many plants and insects in this odd weather year. Telegraph Weed is a tall weedy-looking plant that sways back and forth in the slightest breeze - and it was swaying today. I had to take many shots to get this keeper. Arachtober 5. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2019)

The temperature did get up into the 80s today, a real "Chamber of Commerce" day. There was still that annoying steady breeze from the south that makes keeps everything in motion and makes getting my photos difficult. I was surprised to see some clouds move in from a disturbance far out in the ocean in the late afternoon - a bit of tropical weather?

File.Name: ja05b_ja05-016.jpg
Notes: 48850209992 I found this cute nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) curled up under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. I suspect that it's the same nymph I found yesterday, see my [Previous] photo, as it was on a a different in the same small tree. I believe it is the nymph of a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus), but I'm open to suggestions. It has remarkable antennae. Hah, it looks like a kitty-cat curled up in a laundry hamper! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2019)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Four-spurred Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Zelus tetracanthus
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Zelus tetracanthus

File.Name: ja05c_ja04-007.jpg
Notes: 48849931676 This is the nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) - maybe the Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus) but maybe not. It's on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. It's different from the other assassins I've been showing lately in photos here and here and here. This one has four little points on it's thorax, though you can only see one of them in my photo - I'll make a note. These points or spurs are more evident in the adults, like in this photo, and the odd antennae with that long first segment seem more distinctive in the nymphs. See my [Next] photo for another shot. Like I've been saying, autumn is the season of the predators, both spiders and insects. I figure it's their role to finish off the summer insects before winter does anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2019)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Four-spurred Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Zelus tetracanthus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Zelus tetracanthus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ja05d_ja05-003.jpg
Notes: 48849888411 This is a small but adult Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) this morning. The little bugs look sort of like Assassin Bugs, but they are strictly herbivores, using their proboscis to stick plants rather than other insects. These slender bugs are quite alert - almost always when I go to get a photo they immediately amble off, usually disappearing under the flower or leaf they are on. I like the shadow in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2019)
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: ja06a_ja06-004.jpg
Notes:
48856143233 This is a Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on its web in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's tiny, only about 800 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I have another shot of the same spider from two weeks ago showing its whole web, see this photo. These handsome spiders sometimes build their orb webs across the curvature of a single oak leaf. This one has a web that spans several leaves, but the whole thing is still only about an inch across. This little spider usually hangs upside-down on its web hiding its gorgeous colors, but today it turned over. Arachtober 6. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2019)

Today was another very nice day with temps in the low-80s like yesterday. Also like yesterday, there was the same steady breeze from the south that makes keeps everything in motion and makes getting my photos difficult.
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ja06b_ja06-002.jpg
Notes: 48856053798 Here are two little net-winged Planthoppers (Fulgoroidea) doing *something* on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe they are both genus Neaethus of the family Tropiduchidae. But what are they doing? They do look like a larger female (left) and a smaller male (right). It seems like they should be mating, and maybe they are - but they don't seem lined up in the right way. So do I add it to the Insect Hump Day group on Wednesday? (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2019)

File.Name: ja06c_ja06-032.jpg
Notes: 48856328036 Here is a clutch of tiny insect eggs on the underside of a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family - with the tiny hatchlings that came out of them. The eggs are tiny, with lids only about 140 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 1/2 mm. The whole clutch is only about 5 mm or 3/16 inch across. (See here for how I figure.) I'm sure these are the eggs of true bugs in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, but I won't try to guess beyond that. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2019)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: ja06d_ja06-016.jpg
Notes: 48856432372 This is another photo of the nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) that I've been showing two and three photos back. I got this photo today in the same oak tree where I found it before. I've been thinking this was the nymph of a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus), but I'm having second thoughts. You can see in this photo that it has two little horns on the front of its head - just like the bug in this photo which I think might be the species Heza similis. The antennae look similar too. It's an interesting species with few records at BugGuide and iNaturalist. Maybe so, maybe not. I don't know what it is, and I welcome suggestions. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2019)

File.Name: ja07a_ja07-001.jpg
Notes:
48862618796 This is a most peculiar Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers aka Hackled Band Orb Weavers. It's hanging upside-down on what's left of its web with its head and long front legs on the left. You can see the bundle of hairs on its front leg in back that is the spider's namesake. These spiders make a proper orb web that is distinct because it's usually horizontal, but I think I clobbered this one's web before I noticed it - see this photo of a different spider's intact web. This one is about 1000 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.9 mm, a bit more than 1/8 inch. Tiny! (See here for how I figured.) It has some interesting anatomy, and I'll keep trying to get a better photo that shows it better. *Gulp* I used my on-camera flash for this photo. I try to avoid flash, but sometimes it's necessary. Arachtober 7. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2019)

It was quite warm today, in the upper 80s. We were expecting a visitor this morning, but it turns out we had the day wrong - he's coming tomorrow. By then a breeze had picked up, but at least I got this photo while waiting before I had to leave for errands in the valley. I'll have distractions tomorrow too, and I hope another interesting spider shows up. This photo marks week one of trying to get a new spider photo each day for the Arachtober group!
CommonNames: Feather-legged Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Hackled Band Orb Weavers

File.Name: ja07b_ja07-015.jpg
Notes: 48862773592 I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I stopped in the usual place along Highway 154 to see what's happening. The Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae) is done flowering, but it's still releasing seeds, and this seedpod is carrying both a nymph and adult of a Large Milkweed Bug (Oncopeltus fasciatus, Lygaeidae). They seem to like the seedpods, I have another photo here. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 October 2019)
ScientificName: Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Oncopeltus fasciatus; Lygaeidae
associatedTaxa: Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Oncopeltus fasciatus; Lygaeidae

File.Name: ja07c_ja06-011.jpg
Notes: 48862016393 I found these remains of (I believe) a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on bare ground yesterday morning. It wasn't here the day before. I don't know what got the butterfly, maybe something as ordinary as a car going by too fast in the canyon. Then the scavengers like ants got to work. Like I've been saying, autumn is the season of the predators, both spiders and insects - and even birds and cars. I figure it's their role to finish off the summer insects before winter does anyway. Even beautiful butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2019)

File.Name: ja07d_ja06-029.jpg
Notes: 48861652473 This is a line of Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) eggs on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae - maybe F. californica ssp. tomentella with that white underside). The eggs look like little beans. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "In-A-Row". I haven't posted to that group in a while, but my photo from yesterday seems perfect for this week's assignment. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2019)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; F. californica; tomentella
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; F. californica; tomentella

File.Name: ja08a_ja08-006.jpg
Notes:
48867873861 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on flower buds of (I think) Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa again with those hairy legs and abdomen. It's tiny, only about 775 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Most of these flower-loving spiders that I've been finding his year are tiny. I suspect they are just late getting started like so many other plants and insects in this odd weather year, and I hope they don't end up missing their flowers. Arachtober 8. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2019)

Our visitor did arrive this morning - an hour late after getting lost. (I'm happy to say that our house does not show up on Google Maps.) I used that time to find this little spider in the garden. By the time our visitor left, the wind had already kicked up. The rest of my photos today are more Crab Spiders - all genus Mecaphesa and all taken on October 4. Arachtober only allows one photo per day for the first two weeks, and good ones have been piling up.
Locality: I'm happy to say that our house does not show up on Google Maps.)  I used that time to find this little spider in the garden.  By the time our visitor left
