associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Salvia leucantha; Lamiaceae; Mecaphesa; Mecaphesa

File.Name: ja08b_ja04-029.jpg
Notes: 48868051737 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. It's really tiny. The round center of the composite flower is about 5 mm or 3/16 inch across - and the spider isn't even half that size. I haven't been seeing very many of these flower-loving spiders compared to previous years, and I suspect they are just late getting started like so many plants and insects in this odd weather year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2019)

File.Name: ja08c_ja04-025.jpg
Notes: 48867294918 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the backside of a flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa again with those hairy legs and abdomen. It's tiny. The flower petals/ligules are only about 5-8 mm long, and the spider is less than half of that. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2019)

File.Name: ja08d_ja04-032.jpg
Notes: 48867776821 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hugging a flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants that look quite different, and this is a female or pistillate flower that will produce seeds. All of my recent photos of this plant have shown the male or staminate flowers which produce pollen - and which flower first. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen, and probably a male with those swollen palps. It's a male spider on a female flower. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2019)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious; Mecaphesa

File.Name: ja09a_ja09-008.jpg
Notes:
48872674098 This spider's web looks pretty chaotic, but the spider is an Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on a stem of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). I believe it is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) which makes a composite web with a proper orb web and a separate retreat off to the side made with cobweb-type webbing. My photo today shows the spider in its rather minimalist retreat. It looks like one of the big orb-weavers, but it's really tiny - only about 600 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I don't know if it's a juvenile or just tiny. I've been trying for a week to get a decent photo of this spider, and I figure this shot will do. It has a small orb-web of to the side, but I haven't been able to get a photo of that yet - it looks quite invisible in sunlight. Arachtober 9. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2019)

It was cooler today, only up to about 70∞ - but it made up for it with very little wind, at least in the morning when I was out with my camera. Winds did kick up in the afternoon to 20+ mph. Santa Ana winds are expected down south tomorrow, but we usually miss those here because there's no direct path to the desert in Santa Barbara County. Here's hoping.
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Telegraph Weed; Labyrinth Spider

File.Name: ja09b_ja09-004.jpg
Notes: 48872612538 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) curled up among seedpods of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen, though I usually find those spiders on flowers. I'm starting to find more and more of these spiders, but they are all tiny. I suspect they are just late getting started like so many other plants and insects in this odd weather year. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2019)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: ja09c_ja09-001.jpg
Notes: 48873107371 Here is a little orb web in a shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that's just starting to flower. The web is only a couple of inches across, compare the web in my [Previous] photo that's a couple of feet across. I don't know what kind of spider made this web, but there are several kinds of small Orb-Weaver Spiders (Araneidae) in our woods. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2019)

File.Name: ja09d_ja07-007.jpg
Notes: 48872504453 This is another one of those large Halloween webs of a bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae), probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. I could see the spider in a tree over to the side in a place that's hard to get to, very similar to the one in this photo. This web is almost invisible when it's not catching backlight like in this photo, when it jumps right out at you. Don't you hate it when you walk into one of these large webs, especially at night - where's the spider?? This web is a couple of feet across, compare the web in my [Next] photo that's only a couple of inches across. That's a small Madrone tree on the right and the dry stalk of a wild Chaparral Orchid, see this photo from last year. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; where's the spider??
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; where's the spider??

File.Name: ja10a_ja10-033.jpg
Notes:
48878382861 I got photo bombed today! I was getting some shots of this fine flower of Rod Wirelettuce aka Tall Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria virgata, Asteraceae) up in the higher mountains today when this Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) flew into the frame - and I got the photo. Maybe the bee is genus Anthophora or Habropoda in the family Apidae, and maybe it's the Urbane Digger (Anthophora urbana) which is said to be one of the most common solitary bees in California. Impressive mouthparts! I found lots of similar bees last year, but they've been scarce this year. This photo was a lucky accident. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 October 2019)

I was expecting wind and heat today, but it was cool and not very windy at all until the afternoon. I drove up into the higher mountains looking for something different to photograph, and I ended up at the place I call FAA Peak - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro at 3600 feet that hosts a FAA radio transmitter.
CommonNames: Rod Wirelettuce; Tall Stephanomeria; Wild Bee; Urbane Digger
ScientificName: Stephanomeria virgata; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Anthophora; Habropoda; Apidae; Anthophora urbana
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria virgata; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Anthophora; Habropoda; Apidae; Anthophora urbana

File.Name: ja10b_ja10-041.jpg
Notes: 48877719638 I found these two impressive spiders in the woods yesterday and today in different places on different plants, but they have several features in common too. Both are fairly large and seriously bristly. Both were motionless when I found them - I actually poked them both to make sure they were alive, and they both dropped away after poking. Their colors are different but similar. If you look close, you can see an interesting difference - the one on the left has slender lady palps, and the one on the right has swollen man palps. (I'll make notes.) I suspect they are male and female of the same species, possibly Araneus andrewsi - see this photo. I can't explain why the female isn't on a web. Whatever they are, they are impressive spiders. Arachtober 10. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2019)

File.Name: ja10c_ja10-015.jpg
Notes: 48878199336 Here is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) "hiding" under a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) . . . with its rear end sticking out. I reckon it's trying to hide "Ostrich style" in the belief that if it can't see you then you can't see it. (Of course that doesn't work with nosy photographers!) This one is a male with broad wings, these are the ones that make a racket at night. The narrow-winged females don't sing, but they also hide in the daytime, see this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2019)

File.Name: ja10d_ja05-001.jpg
Notes: 48878155921 This is the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). I've been finding both nymphs and adults in the woods lately, see this photo and this photo. My photo today shows the bug's cute rear end - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2019)

File.Name: ja11a_ja11-029.jpg
Notes:
48882661978 This is a cute little "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera looking at me from a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's a Stink Bug aka Shield Bug (Pentatomidae). There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2019)

It was warmer today, in the upper-70s. There was an odd breeze that was off and on, but at least there were some calm moments to get a few photos. Santa Ana winds have been blowing down south, and there is a wildfire.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Stink Bug; Shield Bug

File.Name: ja11b_ja11-012.jpg
Notes: 48883066281 Here are two different shots of the same little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider taken today (left) and yesterday (right). It's tiny, only about 1100 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.3 mm - about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The shot on the left shows the spider on its perfect orb web that is about the size of a CD. These spiders decorate their webs with the bodies of their prey and other debris to make up a line along the midline of the web, like a once-living stabilimentum. They always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. This spider actually left the center of its web and started dancing around the top, and I got the shot on the right. This shot shows the carapace of the spider, and it looks like its been lacquered to a high gloss - you can even see a reflection in it. It also looks like this spider might be a male with swollen palps. Arachtober 11. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2019)

File.Name: ja11c_ja06-018.jpg
Notes: 48882506208 This is another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae perched on a edge of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These flies seem to come in both big and small versions, which I assume are different kinds. They are probably the most common flies in the woods right now. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2019)

File.Name: ja11d_ja03-027.jpg
Notes: 48883017766 Here is a particularly gnarly fly (Diptera) wprking staminate flowers of a still-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. Hah, it has two little antennae-like bristles on its long proboscis! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2019)

File.Name: ja12a_ja12-011.jpg
Notes:
48887665983 This is another little green Lynx Spider (Oxyopidae) that I suspect is a juvenile Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia). It might be the same one I showed last week in this photo - it's on the same plant of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae), though I've been checking it every day since and didn't find it until today. The spider is 1350 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.3 mm - still less than 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Arachtober 12. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2019)

Today was much like yesterday, in the upper-70s. There was another odd breeze that was off and on, but at least there were some calm moments to get a few photos. It's a good sign that Red Flag Warnings have now expired down south, and winds seem to be moderate at all stations.
CommonNames: Lynx Spider; Green Lynx Spider; Telegraph Weed

File.Name: ja12b_ja12-024.jpg
Notes: 48888148456 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on the involucre of a new flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). (Look in the upper-right.) The whole involucre is less than half an inch long which gives an idea of scale, and the spider itself is less than 2 mm long. I'm starting to find more and more of these spiders, but they are all tiny like this one. I suspect they are just late getting started like so many other plants and insects in this odd weather year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2019)

File.Name: ja12c_ja12-026.jpg
Notes: 48888093471 This is looking into a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae) - with seeds and tiny nymphs of "true bugs" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. The nymphs are only about 240 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to a bit less than 1 mm. (See here for how I figure.) This is near where I found the eggs and hatchling bugs in this photo, and I suppose they might be related. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2019)

File.Name: ja12d_ja11-020.jpg
Notes: 48888019576 Here are two shots of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a new flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. I don't think I've ever seen any butterfly visiting these flowers before. In the first shot, he's fully into the flower trying to reach the nectary down at the end of the floral tube. Then he's starting to pull out, showing the iridescent "dog face" on his upper wing. It's hard to photograph because these butterflies always perch with their wings closed, and they push off from the flowers so fast that my reflexes just aren't up to it. If you search my photos, you can see more of my attempts to photograph this butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2019)
associatedTaxa: Colias eurydice; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: ja13a_ja13-029.jpg
Notes:
48894659857 Here are lots of tiny spiderlings coming out of their nest on a cactus plant against the outside wall of our house. Not coincidentally, they are probably Common House Spiders (Parasteatoda tepidariorum) in the spider family Theridiidae. Mama spider is still around - she is that brown blur in the background - but I haven't been able to get a decent photo yet. I'll keep trying. Looking at all these progeny, it's sobering to think that every creature - including you and me - needs to produce just one offspring who makes it into adulthood in order to have a stable population. Arachtober 13. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2019)

Yesterday was mostly calm with some windy moments, but today was mostly breezy with some calm moments. It was cooler also, with highs in the low 70s. Tomorrow sounds much the same.

File.Name: ja13b_ja13-002.jpg
Notes: 48893872898 I found this "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick on a stem of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods this morning. That's its tail on the left with two pairs of prolegs and its head on top with three pairs of true legs that are barely visible. It's pretty good camouflage even though the colors are wrong. I've found similar caterpillars before, but usually in the spring, see this photo. This caterpillar is not small, maybe an inch or two long. I don't know what moth it will become. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2019)

File.Name: ja13c_ja12-017.jpg
Notes: 48893799423 I found this tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon. The round center of the composite flower is about 5 mm or 3/16 inch across - and the bee is a bit less. I believe it is a Masked Bee aka Yellow-faced Bee in the genus Hylaeus. These bees are unique in that they lack pollen-collecting hairs. Instead, they carry pollen and nectar in their gut and regurgitate it when they get back to their nests. I've been seeing quite a few of them lately on these flowers and the Coyote Bush. Given their numbers, I find them hard to photograph - they seem quite photo shy. What big eyes they have for their size - I guess they can see me coming. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2019)
CommonNames: wild bee; Telegraph Weed; Masked Bee; Yellow-faced Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Hylaeus
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Hylaeus

File.Name: ja13d_ja12-019.jpg
Notes: 48893719848 This is a Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) on a bare stem of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae), maybe a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes, Melanoplinae) with those bluish legs. This one is missing one of its hopping legs. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2019)
CommonNames: Grasshopper; Bird's Beak; Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae

File.Name: ja14a_ja14-009.jpg
Notes:
48900589903 I believe this is a tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in a still-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). It looks like one of the big orb-weavers like this one, but it's really tiny - only about 560 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) That's even smaller than the similar spider in this photo, though foreshortening might make the difference. I don't know if it's a juvenile or just tiny. These spiders make a composite web with a proper orb web and a separate retreat off to the side, see my [Previous] photo. There is always a line that connects the two, and this spider has its screen-left front legs on the line waiting for a strike. Arachtober 14. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2019)

Today was cooler and breezy. Tomorrow will be a problem as we have another maintenance power outage so that the power will be off from 8:00 to 5:00 . . . they say. If they really finish their work by 5:00 pm, I'll be able to post tomorrow. Otherwise maybe not.

File.Name: ja14b_ja14-008.jpg
Notes: 48901164317 Here is the web of the tiny spider in my [Next] photo in a still-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). You can just barely see the tiny spider on a leaf on the right, I'll make a note. The whole scene is just two or three inches across. I believe it is the work of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) which makes a composite web with a proper orb web (left) and a separate retreat off to the side made with cobweb-type webbing (right). The two are connected by a line that runs from the spider to the center of its web, and you can see that line in this photo - I'll make another note. The spider usually sits in its retreat with his "finger" on the line, waiting for a strike. It's interesting that the connecting line seems to be under tension pulling the orb web towards the spider. I guess it needs to be tight in order to transfer vibrations. Note that this spider doesn't have much of a retreat, but sometimes they are substantial - compare this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2019)

File.Name: ja14c_ja14-026.jpg
Notes: 48901131842 These butterflies are showing their age, but they are not down yet. They are similar but different, and I got both photos today in different small oak trees. On the left is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) and on the right is a Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini aka Limenitis lorquini). They look much alike, but the orange wing-tips of the Admiral goes right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border like the Sister has. See this photo for another recent comparison shot. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2019)
ScientificName: Adelpha bredowii; Basilarchia lorquini; Limenitis lorquini
associatedTaxa: Adelpha bredowii; Basilarchia lorquini; Limenitis lorquini

File.Name: ja14d_ja14-020.jpg
Notes: 48900362243 I noticed this gray moth on a gray stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), but it took a moment to register. I poked it hoping to see some activity, but it refused to play, and I left it alone. I don't know what kind of moth it is, but it has wonderful camouflage. It's also good timing to get a photo of this moth today for Moth Monday - HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2019)

File.Name: ja15a_ja15-006.jpg
Notes:
48906687297 This is the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) hiding under the curl of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This nymph already has its "training wings" and will probably become an adult with its next molt. It's an attractive nymph, the adults are less so - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2019)

We expected our power to be off today for a maintenance power outage . . . and it never happened. We had no notice, no explanation, no nothing. This is no way to run a business. SCE called today to say the power might be shut off on Thursday because of high winds and wildfire danger. Whatever happens on Thursday, it was windy enough today to make getting new photos difficult.
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ja15b_ja15-018.jpg
Notes: 48905904603 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on new composite flowers of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). The involucre of the flowers - that scaly green part below the actual flowers - is about than half an inch long which gives an idea of scale. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. Is it chewing its toenail or picking its chelicerae?? It was quite windy today, and I got a many photos that didn't come out - but this one will do. Arachtober 15. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2019)

File.Name: ja15c_ja15-033.jpg
Notes: 48906578322 This is another little "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera looking at me from old flowers of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). It's a Stink Bug aka Shield Bug (Pentatomidae). There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2019)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Pentatomidae

File.Name: ja15d_ja11-032.jpg
Notes: 48906532882 Here is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a dry flower cluster of Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum, Asteraceae). This one is male with broad wings, compare this photo of the narrow-winged female. Usually they try to hide during the day so they can sing at night, but this one isn't even trying. Do they know that their time is coming to an end? (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2019)

File.Name: ja16a_ja16-013.jpg
Notes:
48911460726 This is an impressive large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on its web. I believe it's genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) It just finished wrapping its prey while I was watching - it appeared to be a winged ant or maybe an ant-like wasp. This photo shows the spider's face with chelicerae and fangs. If you look close, you can spot two tiny flies caught in the web along the top of my photo, and I wonder if the spider came back for them? A few minutes later, the spider zipped up a line and disappeared into its retreat in a nearby shrub. It's a proper Halloween spider, and I hope it sticks around for Halloween. Arachtober 16. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2019)

It was breezy this morning when I was out getting my photos, but the wind backed off in the afternoon. It was also mostly overcast today, a nice change from contrasty sunlight we've been having. I couldn't have got this photo in the shade without flash, but it was no problem under a cloudy sky. I guess these clouds are the first sign of a low-pressure trough that's expected to pass over tomorrow. No rain yet, but wind is in the forecast for a few days. We might lose our power at some point to a SCE "Public Safety Power Shutoff". I hope not.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: ja16b_ja16-019.jpg
Notes: 48910889508 Usually when I find these large Halloween spider webs in the woods, the spider is hiding under a leaf off to the side. I had good luck finding spiders this morning though, and this one is actually different from the one in my [Next] photo. This web is about two feet across. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) It came out on its web to wrap its prey, which appears to be a fly (Diptera), probably a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2019)
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; Diptera; Sarcophagidae
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; Diptera; Sarcophagidae

File.Name: ja16c_ja12-005.jpg
Notes: 48910858363 This is the thimble-like retreat of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). The webbing looks pretty cobwebby, but there is a neat orb web nearby that's attached by a connecting line, see this photo and this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2019)

File.Name: ja16d_ja10-023.jpg
Notes: 48911331011 Here is one more photo of the nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) that is very different from the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) in my [Previous] photo - just compare the antennae for starters. I found this one in the same oak tree where I found it before, e.g. my photos here and here. It's hanging like a bat under an oak leaf. I now think this might be species Heza similis. It's an interesting species with few records at BugGuide and iNaturalist. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2019)

File.Name: ja17a_ja17-026.jpg
Notes:
48916657967 Those eyes . . . This is looking close at a Green Lacewing (family Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) that I found this morning on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. This is not the work of a "star filter" or other such cheats! I guess the 6-sided star in its eye is a diffraction pattern from the many 6-sided "ommatidia" simple-eyes that make up the compound eyes of this insect. I've photographed this before, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2019)

It has been windy from the start today, building up to 45 mph gusts so far. It was windy enough this morning that I got my spider photo for the Arachtober group inside his morning. I don't know what I'll do if the wind keeps up all night as expected. In fact it's expected to be windy through the weekend, but I hope that's exaggerated. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and we might lose our power at some point to a SCE "Public Safety Power Shutoff" - but so far, so good.

File.Name: ja17b_ja17-001.jpg
Notes: 48915871218 I believe this is a Long-bodied Cellar Spider (Pholcus phalangioides) in the family Pholcidae catching a beam of sunlight in the corner of a window inside our house. I usually find these little long-legged spiders on our windows, or walking across the ceiling, or in the sink or shower, or in my empty coffee cup in the morning just before I pour . . . pretty much everywhere inside, there must be 100s of them all told! Their legs look like they're made of capillary glass tubing. Apparently they are introduced from Central Europe - but they sure have made themselves at home here. I got lucky with the dim light on this one - my 1/4 second exposure worked. It was windy today from the start, so I figured I better look for my daily spider indoors. Arachtober 17. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2019)

File.Name: ja17c_ja16-036.jpg
Notes: 48916571427 Here are two photos showing the rear end of the same little net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae. I have many shots of these cute little insects, e.g. here and here. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2019)

File.Name: ja17d_ja16-018.jpg
Notes: 48916503332 This is another photo of the large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in my [Previous] photo. I believe it's genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) It came out onto its web to wrap its prey and is now heading back towards its retreat. This photo shows some advanced web handling - pulling itself up in the upper-left and simultaneously letting out new line in the lower-right. This certainly qualifies as a shot of the spider's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: ja18a_ja18-013.jpg
Notes:
48921030728 Here is another nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) hiding on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Compare this photo of another similar nymph. Both of these nymphs already have their "training wings" and will probably become adults with their next molt. They are attractive, the adults are less so - see this photo. Like I've been saying, autumn is the season of the predators, both spiders and insects. I figure it's their role to finish off the summer insects before winter does anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2019)

It was pretty windy this morning, but then the wind started to die down instead of getting worse, and it wasn't a bad day for getting new photos. We are under a NWS Red Flag Warning through Sunday night - as well as a Wind Advisory and a High Wind Watch. The wind did start picking up in the afternoon with 20+ mph gusts, but it seems pretty calm again as I write this.
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ja18b_ja18-037.jpg
Notes:
48921478051 This is some spider's exuviae - the shed skin after a molt. It's small, only about 1/4 inch across. I'm pretty sure from the leg geometry that it's the remains of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), and I suspect from the green color that it's the spider Diaea livens that I show in this photo and this photo. I did find a different green crab spider this summer, see this photo. I found this spider exuviae in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) today, tangled up in some other spider's webbing. I wonder it it used that webbing to help pull itself free? Arachtober 18. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2019)

Grammar note: That word "exuviae" sounds plural, but it is the preferred word even in the singular. It occurs to me that the word "remains" as in "someone's remains" works the same way. There's some discussion of this at BugGuide here and here.
ScientificName: exuviae; Thomisidae; Diaea livens; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: exuviae; Thomisidae; Diaea livens; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: ja18c_ja18-032.jpg
Notes: 48920898603 I found this "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a leafless stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). Late in the season like this, these plants lose all their leaves, but they continue to metabolize with green photosynthetic stems. The caterpillar was originally lying straight along the stem, and I nudged it to move over a bit and show its legs. It made this nice loop instead. That's its tail on the left with two pairs of prolegs and its head on top with three pairs of true legs that are clearly visible. It's pretty good camouflage even though the colors aren't quite right. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2019)

File.Name: ja18d_ja18-012.jpg
Notes: 48921612652 This is the shadow of a fly (Diptera) on the other side of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's probably another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae since these are the most common flies in the woods these days. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2019)
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Flesh Fly

File.Name: ja19a_ja19-019.jpg
Notes:
48926167393 Here is another clutch of tiny insect eggs on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) - with the tiny hatchlings that came out of them. The eggs are tiny, with lids only about 140 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 1/2 mm. (See here for how I figure.) I'm sure these are the eggs of true bugs in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, but I won't try to guess beyond that. I've had good luck finding eggs and hatchlings lately, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2019)

It was almost calm this morning, but then the wind started picking up in the afternoon with 20-30 mph gusts. We are under a NWS Red Flag Warning through Sunday night - as well as a Wind Advisory and a High Wind Watch. Like last night, it seems pretty calm again as I write this.

File.Name: ja19a_ja19-032.jpg
Notes: 48926626456 I found this little spider hiding under the curl of an oak leaf today as the sunlight was leaving it. I thought the photo was way too dark when I got it, but it lightened up a bit on my computer, the focus is good, and I like the POV. My thought at the time was that it's a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, maybe a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. I'll try to get a better look at it tomorrow if I can find it again. Arachtober 19. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2019)

File.Name: ja19c_ja19-014.jpg
Notes: 48926765267 Here is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) "hiding" under an old seedhead of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) . . . with its rear end sticking out. I reckon it's trying to hide "Ostrich style" in the belief that if it can't see you then you can't see it. (Of course that doesn't work with nosy photographers!) This one is a female with narrow wings. Sure, this one would be perfect for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but I've already added too many similar photos like this one to that group. One more doesn't hurt here though. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2019)

File.Name: ja19d_ja19-002.jpg
Notes: 48926709932 This Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) has just graduated from nymph to adult. It's not the one in my [Previous] photo, but it is the one in this photo taken on a few days ago on the same curled leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I got this photo this morning before the sun was properly on it, but even so I can see that its adult colors are not quite developed yet - compare this photo of a different adult. I'm sorry I missed its coming out. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2019)
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ja20a_ja20-023.jpg
Notes:
48933216152 I found this tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe it is a Masked Bee aka Yellow-faced Bee in the genus Hylaeus. I have another recent photo of one of these bees on a different composite flower here. These bees are unique in that they lack pollen-collecting hairs. Instead, they carry pollen and nectar in their gut and regurgitate it when they get back to their nests. The small flowers are gorgeous when they first open like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2019)

Today was breezy in the morning when I was out getting my photos, then it turned quite calm for the afternoon. It was warm today, in the low 80s. Our NWS warnings and advisories will expire tonight, and I think our wind event is over.
CommonNames: wild bee; California Aster; Masked Bee; Yellow-faced Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Hylaeus
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Hylaeus

File.Name: ja20b_ja20-020.jpg
Notes: 48933149752 Here is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) guarding a new composite flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The involucre of the flowers - that scaly green part below the actual flowers - is about than half an inch long which gives an idea of scale. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. It might be the same spider I showed in this photo a few days ago, at least it's on the same plant. The spider was curled up on the flower when a little bee fly approached - the spider instantly assumed the position! Arachtober 20. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2019)

File.Name: ja20c_ja20-036.jpg
Notes: 48933061982 I went back to the same little oak tree where I got this photo yesterday and got another photo of the same spider which shows it better - but lacks the great POV of that first shot. I believe this spider is indeed a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. My book says they are "best recognized by the tuning fork-shaped mark on the carapace and their serrated dorsal abdominal stripe" - which perfectly describes my spider. This is a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae, and it has a nice web on he other side of the leaf. I got a photo, but I'll save it for the Web Wednesday group. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2019)

File.Name: ja20d_ja20-030.jpg
Notes: 48932816986 Here is a tiny parasitic wasp inserting her slender ovipositor into the base of a composite flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the wasp is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea, Hymenoptera) - maybe subfamily Eurytominae, see bleu.geo's comment to this photo and compare this photo at BugGuide. It may seem odd that the wasp's ovipositor comes from the front of its abdomen, but I believe they fold it under their belly to the middle like this. I call it a "parasitic wasp", but I'm not sure if the wasp is laying its eggs into the developing seeds of the flower or into a grub inside the seeds. Some of these Chalcid wasps are plant feeders, some are gall inducers, and some appear to be hyperparasites that parasitize the larvae inside of galls. Nature is complicated like that! (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2019)
CommonNames: parasitic wasp; California Aster; Chalcid Wasp
ScientificName: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Chalcidoidea; Hymenoptera; Eurytominae
associatedTaxa: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Chalcidoidea; Hymenoptera; Eurytominae

File.Name: ja21a_ja21-001.jpg
Notes:
48939172556 Here is one final shot of the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) that I've been following before and after its final molt to become an adult. The last shot I got was this photo that shows its adult wings, but the colors are off, and I suspect it's still hardening off. Before that, I got this photo of a nymph still with "training wings". Note that all three photos are on the same curled leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Soon this assassin will be ready for the world, I'm sorry I missed its coming out. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2019)

There was little breeze today, and it was quite warm - only in the low 80s on our upstairs porch on the shady side of the canyon, but up to 90 degrees according to the NOAA/RAWS/MesoWest weather station a mile away in a sunnier place. It was a very nice day, our wind event seems to be over, and we never did lose our power.
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ja21b_ja21-007.jpg
Notes: 48939054151 Here are two shots of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with cryptic colors that I found on a dandelion-like seedhead of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon today. It's really tiny, just 580 pixels in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.3 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) This is not one of the flower-loving Mecaphesa spiders that I've been showing lately, but something else. It might be a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus, but I think it's more likely genus Ozyptila. (And that's not to mention genera Coriarachne and Bassaniana!) It has stubby front legs with just two spines on the first tibia. Whatever it is, it's a surprising spider, so tiny! Arachtober 21a. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2019)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Telegraph Weed; Ground Crab Spider
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Xysticus; Ozyptila; Coriarachne; Bassaniana
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Xysticus; Ozyptila; Coriarachne; Bassaniana

File.Name: ja21c_ja21-024.jpg
Notes: 48938446333 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) looks armed and dangerous - but only to the little Bee Flies that seem to be its main prey. It's guarding a new composite flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The involucre of the flowers - that scaly green part below the actual petals - is about than half an inch long which gives an idea of scale. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen, but it looks different from the one in this photo from yesterday. (Maybe it's a male?) The spider has probably been sitting like this for hours, just waiting. Arachtober 21b. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2019)

File.Name: ja21d_ja21-015.jpg
Notes: 48938957521 This is another photo of the "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a leafless stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae), compare this photo from a few days ago. Late in the season like this, these plants lose all their leaves, but they continue to metabolize with green photosynthetic stems, and this caterpillar likes to lay straight along them. That's its tail on the left with two pairs of prolegs and its head on top with three pairs of true legs that are clearly visible. It's pretty good camouflage even though the colors aren't quite right, and I figure it works for Moth Monday - so HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2019)

File.Name: ja22a_ja22-035.jpg
Notes:
48944907471 Here is an odd caterpillar of a Lycaenid butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group that includes Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks - on fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. I think it might be the caterpillar of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly with all those spiky hairs, compare this photo and Alice Abelia's comment. It seems late to be finding this, but see this photo of a butterfly ovipositing on the same shrub taken at the end of August. Maybe they just are late, and they hopefully won't miss all the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2019)

There was little breeze again today, and it was quite warm - in the high 80s on our upstairs porch on the shady side of the canyon, and into the 90s according to the NOAA/RAWS/MesoWest weather station a mile away in a sunnier place. It's expected to stay hot for a few days.
CommonNames: Lycaenid butterfly; California Buckwheat; Gray Hairstreak
ScientificName: Lycaenidae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Strymon melinus
associatedTaxa: Lycaenidae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Strymon melinus

File.Name: ja22b_ja22-009.jpg
Notes: 48944993127 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is casting a line to a nearby shrub, and for some reason I picked it up perfectly in my photo. I don't think the spider is properly ballooning - for that trick they need to cast a line nearly straight up to catch the wind and carry them away, see this photo. This is more like what Spider-man usually does with his webbing, it's a way to get directly from here to there. I believe this spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. It might even be the same spider I showed in this photo yesterday, at least it's in the same plant of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). Arachtober 22a. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2019)

File.Name: ja22c_ja20-045.jpg
Notes: 48944738926 Here are two shots of another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with cryptic colors that I found on a leaf of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by our still-dry creek. I think it's standing on its tiptoes trying to get its butt up in the air so it can balloon away - there was enough breeze to keep everything in motion but not enough to achieve lift off. This might be the same kind of spider I showed yesterday in this photo, but it seems to have longer legs than that one, so it may be a real Ground Crab Spider in the genus rather than genus Ozyptila. Then again, that might just be the pose. I got this photo the day before the one I posted, and here it is. Arachtober 22b. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2019)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Arroyo Willow; Ground Crab Spider

File.Name: ja22d_ja22-003.jpg
Notes: 48944891272 This is a seedhead of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) growing along our road in the canyon. The composite plants are kind of weedy, but they are native, and they do have their charms. It's interesting that they seem to have two different kinds of seeds - something I seem to forget every year until I rediscover it again. I believe they have an outer ring of seeds visible on the bottom of my photo that come from the ray flowers - nearly glabrous and without the dandelion-like hairs that help them fly. The inner seeds come from the disk flowers - quite hairy and with the dandelion-like pappus hairs. Spiders like these seedheads too, see my [Previous] photo here and this one from a couple of years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2019)

File.Name: ja23a_ja23-031.jpg
Notes:
48950184951 Here is a little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera on leafless stems of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). I'm not sure how to distinguish nymphs and adults as both are wingless, but this one seems large enough to be an adult, maybe 3/4 inch long. The last one I found was much smaller and probably a nymph, see this photo from early July. They are remarkably variable as you can see if you search my photos. They are not common. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2019)

Today was another warm day in the 80s with no wind to speak of, but there was an annoying breeze that kept everything in motion. It's expected to start cooling off over the weekend.

File.Name: ja23b_ja23-026.jpg
Notes: 48950124866 This is looking close at the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) that I show in my [Previous] photo. You can see the spider in its retreat on the left, with its legs out, and holding the special strands of webbing that connect the retreat to the center of its orb web on the right and transfer the vibrations of struggling prey. The material of its retreat is interesting, like woven fabric. Sometimes there's lots of it. see this photo and this photo. Some of these spiders are tiny, but this one is larger, and its web is about a foot across. Arachtober 23a. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2019)

File.Name: ja23c_ja23-021.jpg
Notes: 48950255147 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with its retreat on the left and its proper orb web on the right. The spider is actually sticking its legs out at the bottom of its retreat, but it's more visible in my [Next] photo. It's holding the special strands of webbing that connect the retreat to the center of its orb web and transfer the vibrations of struggling prey, see this photo for another example. I've been finding tiny spiders of this kind like this one and this one, but this spider is larger, and its web is about a foot across. It's in a seed-bearing shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae), and the spider has incorporated a couple of the tiny dandelion-like seeds into it's retreat. Arachtober 23b and Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2019)

File.Name: ja23d_ja20-038.jpg
Notes: 48949473293 Here is the sheet-like web of a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae - in fact, you can see the spider under the leaf in the top-right. I believe this spider is a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, see this photo and this photo for more info and a closer view. I got this photo in the same small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree where I got the orther photos. I expect to find these spiders in the spring, so it's interesting to find this one now in autumn. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2019)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Hammock Spider; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ja24a_ja24-011.jpg
Notes:
48955426551 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This is the same flower that the butterfly is working in my [Previous] photo. The spider is really tiny, just 550 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's interesting that I've found similar tiny spiders on this plant before, compare this photo from last year. I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen, and maybe it just is that size. Hah - the spider would creep out onto the petal, notice me, and then hurry back under the petals to hide. A few minutes later it would try again. I got this photo on its third try. Arachtober 24a. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2019)

Today was much like yesterday - warm, with an annoying breeze that kept everything moving. It's expected to start cooling off substantially over the weekend.

File.Name: ja24b_ja23-013.jpg
Notes: 48955559387 I found this female Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterfly working new flowers of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. It looks a bit like the introduced Cabbage White butterfly in this photo, but it's markings are more interesting and it is a native. I don't find these butterflies very often up here in the mountains. See my [Next] photo for another view of the small but attractive autumn flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2019)
ScientificName: Pontia protodice; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Pontia protodice; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: ja24c_ja21-021.jpg
Notes: 48955469332 Here is a rosy-cheeked little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen, probably the same spider I show in this photo and this photo. Hah, I'm getting to know the spiders in our canyon personally. Arachtober 24b and HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2019)

File.Name: ja24d_ja21-013.jpg
Notes: 48954695398 This is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) "hiding" on a leaf of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon . . . with its rear end sticking out. They work the night shift and try to hide during the day. I reckon this one is trying to hide "Ostrich style" in the belief that if it can't see you then you can't see it. This is another female with narrow wings. Sure, I've added too many shots like this to the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but the crickets are so obliging - so HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2019)

File.Name: ja25a_ja25-025.jpg
Notes:
48959861843 Here is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly perched for moment in a shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). They can sit quietly for a long time with their wings closed, looking quite plain, and then open their wings in a quick flash - it's a good trick! Butterflies are getting scarce as we move deeper into autumn, but I have been seeing some of our winter residents like the California Tortoiseshell butterfly. No photos yet, but it's just a matter of time. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2019)

We had some excitement this afternoon. Three people died in a two-car accident on Highway 154 near Cold Springs Bridge that started a fire that burned into the brush. There was a fast fire response that contained the fire before it spread beyond a couple of acres. This could have been a bad one . . . but not yet.

(And just now after I wrote the above, the power went off. I was thinking about Public Safety Power Shutoffs - it was hot today, but there was little wind, so wtf? The power came back on after a few minutes, maybe just a glitch?)
Locality: And just now after I wrote the above

File.Name: ja25b_ja25-003.jpg
Notes: 48959820298 The belly of the beast . . . This is looking at the underside of a large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on its web. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) The orb webs of these spiders are a few feet across, but sometimes they span roads. You don't want to walk into one of these large webs, especially at night - where's the spider?? Arachtober 25. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; where's the spider??
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; where's the spider??

File.Name: ja25c_ja25-030.jpg
Notes: 48960514997 Here is another remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, until they finally transform into the green moths on our window screens at night. This one is feeding on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) and has decorated itself with stamens and pistils of the flowers. I was finding quite a few of these last July, but I haven't seen them since until now. The buckwheat flowers are fading to brown, and it's interesting that the caterpillar is brown to match. Last summer they were white to match the fresh white flowers, compare this photo. It's remarkable camouflage. I only found this one because it was moving when nothing else was. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2019)
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: ja25d_ja25-017.jpg
Notes: 48960303016 This is "just a fly" on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), probably another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. It's grooming itself. These seems to be the most common flies in the woods these days. I've been a bit preoccupied with finding spiders lately, but I've had no luck finding a spider with a fly for prey. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2019)

File.Name: ja26a_ja26-022.jpg
Notes:
48964732138 Here is a little "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera looking up at me from inside a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). I believe it's a Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae. The seedpod is full of seeds - and also full of tiny nymphs of another true bug, see this photo, I could see them scurrying about in the background. Last year, a very similar bug spent all winter inside a different seed capsule, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2019)

Today was still warm but cooler than yesterday, with little wind again. Tomorrow is expected to be 20+ degrees cooler than today if the forecast works out. It's curious that there's a cold upper level trough coming through that might also bring another round of Santa Ana winds down south. I hope the winds spare us again.
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Stink Bug; Shield Bug
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Pentatomidae

File.Name: ja26b_ja26-001.jpg
Notes: 48964683088 This is the other side of the large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I showed yesterday in this photo. (I got a new photo today because I didn't much like my photo of its topside yesterday.) I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) This spider has a large web that's close to our home, and it's the first one I've found this year that's actually easy to get to - just in time for Halloween if it sticks around. Arachtober 26a. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2019)

File.Name: ja26c_ja26-024.jpg
Notes: 48965161141 Here are two shots of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. I'm not sure, but I suspect it's a male of the Ground Crab Spiders in the genus Xysticus or Ozyptila that I show in this photo and/or this photo. The spider is really tiny, just 500 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I was lucky to find it for a few photos before it ballooned away. Those green hind legs are strikingly different from the rest of the spider. Arachtober 26b. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2019)

File.Name: ja26d_ja25-009.jpg
Notes: 48965293632 This is a stunning gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). These galls are monothalamous which means they contain just one wasp larva,and I believe there is just a single generation. The pointed beak is distinctive. I don't think of these galls as plant pests - they are more like decorations! I'll be looking for more galls as we move towards winter. They nicely fill the gap between summer insects (and spiders) and winter mushrooms as subjects to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2019)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: ja27a_ja27-020.jpg
Notes:
48971460876 I had to stare at this scene for several seconds before I realized what it was - a dragonfly! I believe this is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata on a fading leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It was overcast and chilly enough today to ground this dragonfly, but not for long - it started vibrating its wings to warmup for take-off, and a few minutes later it was gone. I usually find these dragonflies grounded in the woods on cold winter days, and this seems early for them. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2019)

It was indeed twenty degrees cooler today than yesterday, in the low 60s instead of the 80s. Marine-layer clouds spilled over the mountains early this morning and pretty much stayed all day. There was some "fog wind" but not too bad. The NWS Forecast Discussion ends on an ominous note today: "Downtown should normally have about an inch of rain by the end of October so the current zero is not a great start to the rain year.
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: ja27b_ja27-014.jpg
Notes: 48970797133 Here is another large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in its retreat to the side of its large orb web. I believe it is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) Its retreat is pretty minimal compared to some, just a folded leaf of California Coffeeberry and a few fallen leaves of Poison Oak. It was overcast today, and I was looking closer than usual trying to find my spiders for the day. This one was in a shady place, but the cloudy skies open up the shadows, and I couldn't have got this shot otherwise. Hah, it's interesting to compare this giant with the tiny spiders in my [Previous] photo! Arachtober 27a. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: ja27c_ja27-030.jpg
Notes: 48971437097 Here are a pair of really tiny spiders that I found in the woods today. The one on the left is just 480 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.9 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I found it in under an oak leaf. The one in the right is even smaller, just 360 pixels in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.4 mm - about 1/16 inch. It's on the tip of a cactus spine in our garden. I don't know what these spiders are, but the one on the left is definitely a male with swollen palps. A good thing about the Arachtober quest for spiders is that it makes you look close! Arachtober 27b. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2019)

File.Name: ja27d_ja24-012.jpg
Notes: 48970636048 This is looking at the whole web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) that I showed closer a few days ago in this photo and this photo. As I said with those photos, these spiders make a composite web with a retreat (left) and a proper orb web (right). If you look close, you can actually see two webs here, with a smaller one of a different spider of the same kind on the right. In my comments on the earlier photos, I said the webs are a foot across - nah, eight inches max. These webs are on dead branches in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). Arachtober 27c. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2019)

File.Name: ja28a_ja28-023.jpg
Notes:
48978115377 Here is another goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). Toothpicks come to mind! They are remarkably variable, compare this photo of another recent find and try a search of all my photos. They are not common. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2019)

It was sunny today but rather cool, mostly in the 60s. There was a breeze from the south, not too bad - and not like the Santa Ana winds they've been having down south. Those winds have been diminishing, but they will ramp up again tomorrow and especially Wednesday. I'm glad we don't get those winds from the desert here in Santa Barbara, but we we have our own Sundowner Winds to devil us.
ScientificName: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae

File.Name: ja28b_ja28-029.jpg
Notes: 48977844601 I believe this is a tiny Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) hanging from the bottom of the "bowl" of its web, see this photo and this photo for the whole web. The actual spider is quite small, only about about 620 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm - almost 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) These spiders don't sit in the bowl, but hang under it to grab their prey from below. This photo is actually looking through the web which explains its lack of clarity, see this photo for a clearer shot taken under the web. The prey is interesting too, I'm pretty sure that's a Barklouse (Psocoptera aka Psocodea) above the spider's head, compare this photo. I'm not sure what the other critter on the left is. Arachtober 28a. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2019)
ScientificName: Frontinella communis; Frontinella pyramitela; Linyphiidae; in; Psocoptera; Psocodea
associatedTaxa: Frontinella communis; Frontinella pyramitela; Linyphiidae; in; Psocoptera; Psocodea

File.Name: ja28c_ja28-020.jpg
Notes: 48977767906 Here is another shot of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This is the same spider as my [Previous] photo, and it might be the same spider I showed in this photo a few days ago - it's on a different flower in the same place. The spider is really tiny, about 550 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I usually figure that the yellow disk flowers in the center are 1/8 inch across, and they are indeed 830 pixels across which comes to 3.3 mm or 1/8 inch. By the same reckoning, the violet petals are about 1/4 inch long. I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen, and I'm not sure if it's a juvenile or maybe it just is that size. Arachtober 28b. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2019)

File.Name: ja28d_ja28-018.jpg
Notes: 48977714716 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This is the same spider as my [Next] photo, and it might be the same spider I showed in this photo a few days ago - it's on a different flower in the same place. The spider is really tiny, about 550 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen, and I'm not sure if it's a juvenile or maybe it just is that size. Arachtober 28c. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2019)

File.Name: ja29a_ja29-005.jpg
Notes:
48983315001 I was surprised to find this nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) today. I believe it's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, but it seems odd to find it now at the end of October. I was mostly finding these nymphs in July, see this photo. And I found Katydid eggs early this month, see this photo. The adults are out there, so what's this middle-stage nymph doing on the fading flowers today? Hah, did you notice that it's missing one of its hopping legs? (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2019)

It was sunny again today but still rather cool, mostly in the 60s. There was a breeze from the south, not too bad - and not like the Santa Ana winds from the east that they've been having down south. Those winds have diminished today, but they will ramp up again tonight into strong Santa Ana winds. It's remarkable that they have ferocious 60-80 mph winds 50 miles south of us, and we have mild 5-10 mph winds in our forecast - all because we have no direct path to the desert here in Santa Barbara. We do have our own Sundowner Winds to devil us, but that's another story.
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Scudderia

File.Name: ja29b_ja29-023.jpg
Notes: 48983465252 I found this small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a spent flower of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) down by the creek today. It's a different one than the spider in my [Previous] photo, though they look similar. My impression was that this one is a bit larger. I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen - and a male with those swollen palps. It was curious that I found these two spiders just a few inches apart on the same plant, and they might both be male. Arachtober 29a. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2019)

File.Name: ja29c_ja29-022.jpg
Notes: 48983416452 I found this small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a spent flower of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) down by the creek today. It seems like an odd plant to find one of these flower-loving spiders on, but they do attract some insects when the flowers are fresh. I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. It was curious that I found two of these spiders just a few inches apart on the same plant, see my [Next] photo. You can't tell from this photo, but I believe this one is a male - and the other one too. I like the light in this photo and the almost-translucent character of the spider's legs and carapace. Arachtober 29b. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2019)

File.Name: ja29d_ja29-002.jpg
Notes: 48982634753 Here's another shot of the large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on its web that I've shown a couple of times already in this photo and this photo. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) This spider has a large web that's close to our home, and it's the first one I've found this year that's actually easy to get to - just in time for Halloween if it sticks around for two more days. It's a handy spider for a quick morning photo just in case I get skunked for new photos the rest of the day. I didn't get skunked today, I just like this photo. Arachtober 29c. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: ja30a_ja30-004.jpg
Notes:
48988271213 This is a Cobweb Weaver aka Comb-Footed Spider in the family Theridiidae guarding her egg sac in a shelter made from fallen leaves suspended in the air by webbing. The leaves belong to a shrub of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) that is dropping them for winter. This photo was a reach, and I really couldn't see much when I got the photo . . . and it came out better than I expected. I found this spider in the woods today, but it looks like a Common House Spider (Parasteatoda tepidariorum), compare this photo from home. It's about the size of a grape. According to my book, there are 93 species of these spiders along the west coast, so there are other possibilities. Arachtober 30a. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2019)

It was sunny and cool again today. There was a breeze from the south but not like the Santa Ana Winds they've been having down south. It's remarkable that they are having ferocious winds 50 miles south of us, and we have mild 5-10 mph winds in our forecast - all because we have no direct path to the desert here in Santa Barbara. We do have our own Sundowner Winds to devil us, but that's another story.

File.Name: ja30b_ja30-014.jpg
Notes: 48988752896 I found this interesting spider webbing on a stem of Prickly Phlox (Linanthus californicus aka Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods today. I believe the frizzy webbing is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae. The webbing is comprised of very fine threads that are spun into strands like hand-spun wool or chenille yarn, different from ordinary spider webbing. I have photos of similar webbing here, here, and here - not to mention my shot two photos [Back]. There's more info at Wikipedia and a nice article in The Atlantic. Note that the plant is starting to green up even though we've had no rain yet for this season. Arachtober 30b and Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2019)
associatedTaxa: Linanthus californicus; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Dictynidae

File.Name: ja30c_ja26-003.jpg
Notes: 48988696666 Here's a proper spider web for Halloween! The spider in the middle is the large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I've shown several times already, most recently in this photo. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) The orb webs of these spiders are a few feet across, but sometimes they span roads. You don't want to walk into one of these large webs, especially at night - where's the spider?? Arachtober 30c and Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; where's the spider??
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; where's the spider??

File.Name: ja30d_ja23-002.jpg
Notes: 48988585431 Start with the photo on the right which shows interesting spider webbing on a stalk of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I believe this frizzy webbing is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae, see this photo for more info. Now look in the top-left of that photo - spot the insect? I didn't see it until I got the photo up on my computer screen. I went back the next day and got the photo on the left. When I tried to nudge the insect into a better position, it fell apart as I touched it - it was just a husk. I'm not sure, but I think it might be a Thread-legged Bug in the subfamily Emesinae of the Assassin Bugs (Reduviidae), possibly genus Empicoris and maybe the species Empicoris rubromaculatus. I have another photo here, but I rarely see them. I know that some of these bugs steal prey from spider webs which could explain where I found it, see this photo. I've already got my two photos for the Web Wednesdays group, but HWW anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2019)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Mesh Web Weaver; Thread-legged Bug; Assassin Bugs
