File.Name: ja31a_ja31-007.jpg
Notes:
48993607866 It's the rear end of this large spider, and it's also the end of the month of Arachtober. This is the same large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I've shown several times already, most recently in this photo yesterday. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) It's tucked under a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). These spiders make large Halloween webs that you don't want to walk into at night - where's the spider?? Arachtober 31a and HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2019)

The Arachtober group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it as a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day of the month. This year I've added the condition to find all my daily spiders in the canyon at home. I've been successful this year! I've also learned a lot from other group photos, mostly that there are more spiders in the world than I find at home in October. Now to get back to photographing all the other creatures in the woods.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; where's the spider??
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; where's the spider??

File.Name: ja31b_ja31-039.jpg
Notes: 48993720647 I've been lamenting that I didn't get any acceptable photos of Jumping Spiders (Salticidae) during the month of Arachtober - I've seen a few, but no photos - and now on the last day I got a photo! I noticed a rolled up leaf on a shrub of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) today, and there was this tiny spider inside. I had to use flash to get some light into the tube, but it's a nice hiding place. I've also spotted these spiders hiding under leaves to stay out of the rain in Oregon, see this photo. I wonder how long this one will stay here? Arachtober 31b. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2019)

File.Name: ja31c_ja20-027.jpg
Notes: 48993473656 Here is another really tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae). This is actually the first of these tiny spiders that I found, though for some reason I never posted the photo. I figure the group of orange disk flowers in the center of the flower is about 1/8 inch - and the spider is much smaller. I've found similar tiny spiders on this plant before, see this photo from a few days ago and this photo from last year. I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen, and maybe it just is that size. If you look close, you can see that the spider is holding an orange bit of a disk flower - bait? Arachtober 31c. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2019)

File.Name: ja31d_ja14-023.jpg
Notes: 48993622792 This is looking at the underside of a Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider hanging upside-down on its web in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's tiny, only about 1/8 inch long. I have another shot of the same spider from a few weeks ago showing its handsome upper side, see this photo. These spiders sometimes build their orb webs across the curvature of a single oak leaf. This one has a web that spans several leaves, but the whole thing is still only about an inch across. It was odd - I got this photo on one day, and the spider was gone the next day. I've been hoping I'd find it again on a different leaf, but no such luck. Now, where do spiders go when they disappear like that?? Arachtober 31d and HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2019)
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: jb01a_jb01-018.jpg
Notes:
48998778747 Hah - just because Arachtober has come to an end doesn't mean that I can't keep posting spider photos! This is the web of a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on our upstairs porch - but there's no line of trash through the web because one of us accidentally knocked the web down the day before yesterday. This web is now brand new, and I'm hoping the spider will decorate it again before Web Wednesday for another photo. The whole web is about the size of a CD disk, and this is just part of it. If you look close, you can see something remarkable - its inner web makes a clockwise spiral, while its outer web makes a (rough) counter-clockwise spiral. It's easier to see in this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2019)

This photo is proof of how calm it was today, as I wouldn't be able to get it on a normal breezy afternoon. I hope they are getting this calm weather down south and east where the wildfires are, but I'm afraid its still windy. It was smoky today from the Maria Fire by Santa Paula. It's a bad one.

File.Name: jb01b_jb01-009.jpg
Notes: 48998528001 "Want to go on a ride?" Here is a tiny winged aphid on dandelion-like seeds of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon today. If the seeds go off in the wind, the aphid will go with it - and I wonder if that's the plan? The aphid is colored deep red, and there are lots of them on the flower stalks of this composite plant. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2019)

File.Name: jb01c_ja31-035.jpg
Notes: 48998489856 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) in a jaunty pose on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), probably another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. They seem to be the most common flies in the woods these day. They seem to come in all different sizes, from big to small, but they all have reddish eyes and red butt like the glowing ember of a cigar. Despite their unpleasant common and scientific names, I like these flies. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2019)

File.Name: jb01d_ja30-032.jpg
Notes: 48997923763 This is an attractive little fly (Diptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it might be species Minettia flaveola in the family Lauxaniidae - compare this photo at BugGuide, especially the hairs on its head. Another possibility is the genus Suillia in the family Heleomyzidae, though the larvae of those flies are said to develop in fungi, and it's the wrong time of year for that. I've seen these flies before, e.g. this photo a couple of months ago. I get confused by them every year. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Minettia flaveola; Lauxaniidae; Suillia; Heleomyzidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Minettia flaveola; Lauxaniidae; Suillia; Heleomyzidae

File.Name: jb02a_jb02-007.jpg
Notes:
49003198568 "Oh that feels sooo good!" Here is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on an oak leaf scratching a hard-to-reach place on its back. I know just how good that can feel! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2019)

It was warmer than it's been today, up to 80∞ - and almost as calm as it was yesterday. I believe the Santa Ana winds down south and east are finally quieting down, and the NWS Red Flag Warning expired at 6 pm. I hope the wildfires are also quieting down.

File.Name: jb02b_jb02-019.jpg
Notes: 49003712781 This is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new composite flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen, and this one appears to be male with those enlarged palps. Hah - just because Arachtober has come to an end doesn't mean that I can't keep posting spider photos! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2019)

File.Name: jb02c_jb02-034.jpg
Notes: 49003822877 Here is today's photo of the same kind of true bug nymph that I show in my [Previous] photo. I found this one on a stem of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae), but I've also been seeing them inside the nearby seed capsules of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae), see this photo of the recently hatched nymphs. (The lily is growing up through the manzanita.) It's striking how the nymphs change appearance as they mature, and the adults will probably look completely different. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2019)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae

File.Name: jb02d_ja28-021.jpg
Notes: 49002970423 I wish I understood better what this photos shows. It's a couple of "true bugs" - one of them an adult and the other a nymph. My first thought was "OMG! That adult is eating the nymph!" Then my second thought was that maybe they're just sharing. I found these on a shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae), but this is the adult that I've been finding inside the nearby seed capsules of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae), see this photo. (The lily is growing up through the manzanita.) The nymphs like to hang out in the seed capsules also, see this photo of recently hatched nymphs. I checked at BugGuide, and there are Predatory Stink Bugs in the group Asopinae. Like I said, I wish I understood better what this photos shows. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2019)

File.Name: jb03a_jb03-019.jpg
Notes:
49009737583 Here is another shot of the little "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that I showed in this photo yesterday, probably a Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae. In this photo, it has its proboscis in a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) - which makes me think it was just a coincidence that it was with a nymph in that other photo. This shot has a good view of the bug's remarkable mouthparts, see this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2019)

I'll be busy for a few days (or more) helping a dear friend recover from hip replacement surgery. I might be able to post photos in the evening, but I probably won't be able to get out with my camera for new photos. No problem, I've got many good photos in my "Good Stuff" folder that I've just been waiting for an excuse to post.
Locality: or more) helping a dear friend recover from hip replacement surgery.  I might be able to post photos in the evening

File.Name: jb03b_jb03-014.jpg
Notes: 49010208266 This is an unusually dark Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly resting on ripe seed heads of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). Flowers for these butterflies are getting scarce in the woods as we move closer to winter, but there still are a few - and soon there will be new ones, if they're not already out there. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2019)

File.Name: jb03c_jb03-007.jpg
Notes: 49010139516 This is "just a fly" (Diptera), but it does look different from the usual flies I see in the canyon. What I like about this photo is that I captured the fly head-on with decent focus. Usually when I face a fly like this, my focus is too close or too far - but this is just right. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2019)

File.Name: jb03d_ja31-028.jpg
Notes: 49009550418 Here is another photo of the true bug nymphs that I show three and four photos back, here and here. These are where I usually find them, inside the seed capsules of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae) which are full of the irregular seeds. Compare this photo of recently hatched nymphs that are probably the same kind. It's striking how the nymphs change appearance as they mature, and the adults will probably look completely different yet again. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2019)

File.Name: jb04a_jb02-023.jpg
Notes:
49015751633 Here are two different wild bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on new autumn flowers of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). From a distance they look very similar - tiny and black - but when you get closer, you see the differences. I believe the bee on the left is a Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina which does have special hairs or scopae on its rear legs for collecting pollen. And I believe the bee on the right is a Masked Bee aka Yellow-faced Bee in the genus Hylaeus which lack pollen-collecting hairs but instead carry pollen and nectar in their gut and regurgitate it when they get back to their nests. They were both working the flowers on this November day. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2019)

I spent the day driving around helping my dear friend get ready for her hip replacement surgery tomorrow morning. Yipes, we have to be at the hospital at 5:00 am! Even with the extra hour gained from the end of Daylight Savings Time, that's pretty extreme. No time for new photos this week, but I'll post what I already have - if I'm still awake in the evening.
CommonNames: wild bees; Sawtooth Goldenbush; Small Carpenter Bee; Masked Bee; Yellow-faced Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Ceratina; scopae; Hylaeus
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Ceratina; scopae; Hylaeus

File.Name: jb04b_ja30-034.jpg
Notes: 49015713623 Here is a tattered California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on foliage of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - which happens to be its host plant. Their season is coming to an end. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2019)

File.Name: jb04c_jb03-005.jpg
Notes: 49015647203 This is the tiny gall of a Pumpkin Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus minusculus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on a fallen leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It does look a bit like a tiny pumpkin or gourd. It's only about 410 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.6 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Small as it is, this wasp-induced gall is home to the tiny wasp larva that lives inside. Note that this gall does cause a color change in the leaf surface. I guess it counts as a "nutrient sink" diverting some nutrients from the leaf - but not much. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2019)
CommonNames: Pumpkin Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Dryocosmus minusculus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Dryocosmus minusculus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: jb04d_ja30-020.jpg
Notes: 49016103181 Usually I show these autumn flowers with tiny spiders or bees like this, but here's one by itself. This is a new flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods a few days ago. The flowers are particularly gorgeous when they first open, less so when they've been around for a while. These plants started flowering in August (photo), and with luck they'll continue until the new year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2019)

File.Name: jb05a_jb05-003.jpg
Notes:
49021861547 Here are three photos closing in on a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider. These aren't successive crops, but closer and closer photos - the one on the right is a 1:1 macro shot, and the spider is 960 pixels high, which comes to 3.8 mm - a bit more than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) After seeing the spider on the right, try to find it on its web on the left! These spiders decorate their webs with a "trash line" mostly made of the bodies of their prey, pirate fashion. (They also incorporate anything that comes along, like dandelion-like composite seeds.) This is the same spider that I showed in this photo during Arachtober last month. It's handy that these spiders stay in one place for weeks at a time. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2019)

My dear friend had her hip replacement surgery early this morning, so I had a chance to get out with my camera for a few shots before I went down to visit. The operation was a success, but it will be another day (or two) before she comes home. Maybe I'll be able to get out again with my camera in the morning.

File.Name: jb05b_jb05-010.jpg
Notes: 49021094188 This is another shot of what I believe is a tiny Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) hanging from the bottom of the "bowl" of its web, see this photo for another shot of the same spider last week. The actual spider is quite small, only about about 1/10 inch across. I'm not sure what its prey is - some kind of tiny midge-like fly. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2019)

File.Name: jb05c_jb05-015.jpg
Notes: 49021045363 Here is another tiny gall on the underside of a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's almost as small as the gall of a Pumpkin Gall Wasp in this photo from yesterday, just 480 pixels across instead of 410 pixels - about 1.9 mm across by 2.5 mm high. I don't know whose gall this is, but I'm sure it's a Cynipid Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). Small as it is, this wasp-induced gall was home to the tiny wasp larva that once lived inside. That exit hole is less than 1 mm across. I don't know of that hole in the leaf is the result of the gall or just a coincidence. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2019)
CommonNames: gall; Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Wasp

File.Name: jb05d_jb05-009.jpg
Notes: 49021723732 This is a drought-stressed plant of native Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) growing on a near-vertical sandstone outcrop in the canyon. These succulent plants look half-dead by the end of our dry summer, but this one is hanging on. We're all waiting for rain this year. I have another photo of the pretty flowers from last spring - with raindrops! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2019)

File.Name: jb06a_jb06-012.jpg
Notes:
49026781246 I suspect this is a brand-new adult Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) that has just graduated from nymph to fully-winged adult. I'd wager it's the same one as the nymph I showed in this photo a few weeks ago - it's on the same branch and nearly the same leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) as that one. This one is in a defensive position with its front legs up in the air. These predators are named for the tiny size of their prey, not for any personal resemblance. You can see this one's mighty spike-like proboscis folded under its head - see this photo and this photo for what it does with that proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2019)

My dear friend had her hip replacement surgery yesterday, but she's not going home from he hospital until tomorrow. I had a chance to get out with my camera for a few shots before I went down to visit today. She's coming home tomorrow, but we still don't know when. I'll get out with my camera in the morning if I can.
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: jb06b_jb06-001.jpg
Notes: 49026726306 This looks like one of the small galls I been showing lately here and here, but it's actually an odd insect - a small Gall-like Scale Insect in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. It feels hard like it has a shell. I found this one on a stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. This one seems to match the species Allokermes essigii in these photos that I found on the web. Unlike some other scale insects, I usually find these alone or in small groups. They don't seem to do any damage to the trees. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2019)
CommonNames: Gall-like Scale Insect; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Allokermes essigii
associatedTaxa: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Allokermes essigii

File.Name: jb06c_jb05-020.jpg
Notes: 49026671661 Here is spider webbing on a dead tendril of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's catching a beam of sunlight in the autumn woods where we are rapidly losing the sun on our north-facing side of the mountains. You realize how fast the sun moves across the sky with photos like this - the light is only perfect for a minute or two as the sun moves on, so get that photo now. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2019)

File.Name: jb06d_ja28-002.jpg
Notes: 49026115983 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) on a dead branch in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). The spider's remarkable retreat is the most visible - like a woven silk sack that's maybe three inches long. If you look close in the background, you can see the spider's proper orb web. This looks very similar to the web I showed in this photo taken last July. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2019)

File.Name: jb07a_jb02-004.jpg
Notes:
49030955858 Here is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on an oak leaf. This is the same little fly I showed a few days ago in this photo scratching a hard-to-reach place on its back. In this photo it's just showing its rear end. I know I'm posting this on Thursday by local time, but that is Friday by Flickr/GMT/UTC time, so I'll go for the daily double - HBBBT and HFDF as well! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2019)

My dear friend came home from the hospital today after her hip replacement surgery two days ago. I'll be a caregiver for a while. I never got out with my camera today, but I hope to tomorrow.

File.Name: jb07b_jb02-020.jpg
Notes: 49030919848 This is looking at the rear end of a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a new autumn flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). This is the same little bee that I show on the left in this photo which I believe is a Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina. It's interesting that the bee has blue/cyan highlight on its abdomen, but maybe that's just a reflection? HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2019)
CommonNames: wild bee; Sawtooth Goldenbush; Small Carpenter Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Ceratina
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Ceratina

File.Name: jb07c_jb01-016.jpg
Notes: 49031333751 Here is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) "hiding" among fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). They work the night shift and try to hide during the day, and they do it "Ostrich style" in the belief that if they can't see you then you can't see them. This is another male with broad wings. Sure, I've added too many shots like this to the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - I won't add this one, but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2019)

File.Name: jb07d_jb02-013.jpg
Notes: 49030786083 This is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on the other side of a composite flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). This is the same spider I showed in this photo a few days ago, probably a male in the genus Mecaphesa. It helps you appreciate how these spiders catch their prey. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2019)

File.Name: jb08a_jb08-019.jpg
Notes:
49036331592 Here are two shots of a nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the outside of a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). You can see its odd proboscis (aka beak or rostrum) with the thin stylets inserted into the side of the seed capsule, no doubt going into the seeds inside. See this photo for more info about their mouthparts. This bug was slowly rocking back and forth, going in and out, reminding me of the oil well pumps that look like giant insects as they rock back and forth to pump oil. (I'm thinking of the San Ardo Oil Field along Highway 101 in the Salinas Valley - as I recall, one of the pumps used to be dressed up as a giant grasshopper!) Compare my [Previous] photo, these nymphs are quite variable. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2019)

I'm still busy as a caregiver for a dear friend who had hip replacement surgery this week and came home from the hospital yesterday. It was nice to get outside with my camera today - a nice day, pleasantly warm with little wind. Things are looking up!
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; rostrum; stylets
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; rostrum; stylets

File.Name: jb08b_jb08-024.jpg
Notes: 49036073691 This is the handsome nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the outside of a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). It's probably the same kind of nymph I've found inside the seed capsules, see this photo, though it's interesting that the colors are different, and both are different from the similar nymph in my [Next] photo. It's striking how the nymphs change appearance as they mature, and the adults will probably look completely different yet again. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2019)

File.Name: jb08c_jb08-012.jpg
Notes: 49035497948 I somehow noticed this tiny caterpillar on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. Hah, that's not a twig but the center vein of an oak leaf that's been half-eaten. The caterpillar is only 1140 pixels long, which comes to 4.5 mm - not quite 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I suspect it is the caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly, compare this photo and this photo. The pose and bumpy skin texture match, though it is much smaller. Caterpillars of Duskywing (Erynnis) butterflies also have a somewhat similar head capsule, but they have smooth bodies, compare this photo. If it is a California Sister, it's an interesting find - I rarely see them as caterpillars, and this is how they overwinter according to my book. I'll try to keep an eye on this one as it grows. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2019)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Adelpha bredowii; Erynnis
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Adelpha bredowii; Erynnis

File.Name: jb08d_jb01-001.jpg
Notes: 49035451428 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods, no doubt another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. They seem to be the most common flies in the woods these day. They seem to come in all different sizes, from big to small, but they all have reddish eyes and red butt like the glowing ember of a cigar. Despite their unpleasant common and scientific names, I like these flies - they are large and husky, like they could take on anything. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2019)

File.Name: jb09a_jb09-001.jpg
Notes:
49040953922 I believe this is a Western Striped Cucumber Beetle (Acalymma trivittatum) in the group Chrysomelidae, Coleoptera - at least it's something very similar. I think of these as a pest species, but I found this one on a leaf of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods this morning. There is also a Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata) which is different and more common, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2019)

It was quite warm today, into the 80s - a little breezy in the morning but nothing extreme. My work as a caregiver continues, but I'm trying to get out for a walk with my camera every morning.
CommonNames: Western Striped Cucumber Beetle; Southern Bush Monkeyflower
ScientificName: Acalymma trivittatum; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Diabrotica undecimpunctata
associatedTaxa: Acalymma trivittatum; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Diabrotica undecimpunctata

File.Name: jb09b_jb09-009.jpg
Notes: 49040855727 This is another shot of the tiny caterpillar that I showed yesterday in this photo. Here it is on the same center vein of an oak leaf that's been half-eaten, in a photo taken today. I believe the white fluff inside the leaf is the work of Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae, see this photo. My current photo shows how tiny the caterpillar is - just 4.5 mm, less than 3/16 inch. I'm becoming more convinced that it is indeed the caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly as it has bumps in all the right places. It must be a baby. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2019)

File.Name: jb09c_jb09-015.jpg
Notes: 49040791417 Here is a tiny dandelion-like composite seed caught up in webbing on the dry stalk of a native grass. The seed belongs to native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) and the grass is Western Rye aka Blue Wildrye (Elymus glaucus, Poaceae). It's that time of year before the rains come when it gets hard to find new subjects to photograph every day. I have to look closer for scenes that catch my eye - like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2019)
CommonNames: Telegraph Weed; Western Rye; Blue Wildrye
ScientificName: Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Elymus glaucus; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Elymus glaucus; Poaceae

File.Name: jb09d_jb08-004.jpg
Notes: 49040524046 I believe this is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, maybe species Metellina mimetoides. It has a humped back, very long front legs, and it spins a small perfect orb web that is sometimes slanted towards horizontal, though the web is quite invisible in this photo. This one might be male with those enlarged palps. It's hanging on its web in front of a cinder block wall that's been painted a light blue. It's curious that it is close to the web of a different Cobweb Weaver spider in the family Theridiidae. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2019)

File.Name: jb10a_jb10-019.jpg
Notes:
49046071608 I believe this is another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging under its web in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods today. It appears to be very male with those swollen palps. It looks different from - but also similar to - the female Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes) in this photo that I found in a different tree a few weeks ago. Maybe those are just gender differences? (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2019)

It was about 10 degrees cooler today than yesterday but still very nice, with a high in the mid-70s. There was little wind again. The days has been much the same for some time now, and I'm ready for a change.
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak; Hammock Spider

File.Name: jb10b_jb10-029.jpg
Notes: 49046704672 Here is a tiny "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) walking across the bristles of a dandelion-like seedhead of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). I'm not sure what it was doing here, unless it was trying to get down to the seeds. The bug really is tiny, just 760 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm - not quite 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I like this landscape. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2019)

File.Name: jb10c_jb10-010.jpg
Notes: 49045934013 This is a handsome "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) hiding between leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. I believe its an adult Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae. The autumn colors are quite attractive, and similar to the nymph in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Stink Bug; Shield Bug

File.Name: jb10d_jb10-009.jpg
Notes: 49046552877 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) on a leaf of non-native "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden today. It's not the best photo, but there are two interesting features - the wasp is missing one of its antennae, and look how it's cradling its abdomen with its wings. I've seen the wasps do this cradling before, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2019)

File.Name: jb11a_jb11-023.jpg
Notes:
49052089533 Here are two shots of a really tiny wasp on a spent flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods. At least I guess it's a wasp and not a bee because it appears quite hairless, compare this photo and this photo of similar bees. Maybe it's a Cuckoo Wasp in the family Chrysididae. It's only about 520 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) If it really is a Cuckoo Wasp, their larvae are said to be either parasitoids that feed directly on the larva of a host or kleptoparasites that "steal" the host's food, and as they say at BugGuide, "Either way the host larva dies." See my [Previous] photo for two more shots of this interesting wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2019)

Today was nice enough, but I had to cut my photo time short to do some shopping for supplies. We have another maintenance power outage tomorrow, so the power will be off from 8:00 to 5:00 . . . they say. If they really finish their work by 5:00 pm, I'll be able to post tomorrow. Otherwise maybe not. We've had many of these outages lately, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities. We also have tree work scheduled for Wednesday and Thursday. I'll get out if I can, but they are working along our road, and I might be house-bound.

File.Name: jb11c_jb11-014jpg.jpg
Notes: 49051873743 Here are seeds and seedheads of four different composite plants in the Asteraceae plant family with dandelion-like seeds that blow in the wind and turn up everywhere. From the top-left going clockwise, here are seeds of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia); native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora); Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis); and Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum). The Coyote Bush seeds are the most widely dispersed - they end up everywhere. In one way, the Telegraph Weed seeds are the most interesting because they seem to have two completely different kinds of seeds - the outer ring of seeds visible on the bottom of my photo come from the ray flowers and are nearly glabrous and without the dandelion-like hairs that help them fly; the inner seeds above come from the disk flowers - quite hairy and with the dandelion-like pappus hairs, see this photo for another look. I wonder if they are both fertile? (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2019)
CommonNames: California Aster; Telegraph Weed; Coyote Bush; Yellow-flowered Hawkweed; Southern Hawkweed
ScientificName: Asteraceae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Heterotheca grandiflora; Baccharis pilularis; Hieracium argutum
associatedTaxa: Asteraceae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Heterotheca grandiflora; Baccharis pilularis; Hieracium argutum

File.Name: jb11d_jb10-002.jpg
Notes: 49052554587 This is looking close at an autumn flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. The wonderful large pollen grains seem to explode from the stamens like popcorn, and they have a depressed center - or are they hollow like beads? These fine natives have been flowering for a while, and they are still going strong. I reckon they are important for keeping the hummingbirds alive during this hard time of year before the rains come. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2019)

File.Name: jb12a_jb12-005.jpg
Notes:
49057194488 This is another large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) carrying its prey along its web today - not quite sharp because it was moving, but I like the POV. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) See my [Previous] photo for more shots. I found this spider in the garden today, catching a beam of sunlight. Its prey looks like a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera). It's autumn, and this is the time for spiders to pick off the pollinators before they're gone for good. It's their time. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2019)

Just two photos today. I was house-bound because power company workers were replacing poles in our canyon, and the power didn't come on until late. I couldn't get out, so I spent some time in the garden and found this spider catching a beam of sunlight in our otherwise shady north-facing location. We were stuck at home today, but tomorrow we will probably bail . . . tree trimmers are coming early, and it will be noisy.

File.Name: jb12b_jb12-014.jpg
Notes: 49057662376 Here are front and back views of another large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on its web today. That gooey mass near the bottom of the photo on the left is what's left of the spider's prey that's visible in my [Next] photo - probably a Syrphid Fly, but there's no telling now. It looks like raw meat, and I suppose it is. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) I found this spider in the garden today, catching a beam of sunlight. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2019)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: jb13a_jb13-006.jpg
Notes:
49062602072 Here is a little spider looking up at me from inside the flower scar of a ripening cactus fruit in the garden this morning. It's cute! I believe its also a male with those swollen palps. This spider didn't have any webbing that I could see, but it looks a lot like the spider in my [Previous] photo - a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, and maybe a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, compare this photo and note the "tuning fork-shaped mark on the carapace". I find the darnedest things inside these cactus fruit, try a search of my photos to see them all. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2019)

We ended up being house-bound again today as a large truck with a cherry picker and a wood chipper was blocking our driveway. It was another nice day, but cooler and breezier - and noisier. I took my usual morning walk to get away from the ruckus. The tree trimmers will be back tomorrow, but hopefully they'll finish early.

File.Name: jb13b_jb13-021.jpg
Notes: 49062546877 I believe this is another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging under its web in a nearly-finished plant of Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum, Asteraceae). It looks similar to the spider in this photo, and like that one, it looks like a male those swollen palps. It also looks similar to the female Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes) in this photo that I found a few weeks ago. The webbing looks more like a sheet when viewed from above, but it looks lumpy when viewed from the side like this. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2019)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Yellow-flowered Hawkweed; Southern Hawkweed; Hammock Spider

File.Name: jb13c_jb13-012.jpg
Notes: 49062276516 This is spider webbing on ripe seed heads of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). I suspect that this is another example of cribillate webbing, it has a frizzy texture like the webbing in this photo and this photo. This might be the work of a be the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae. If you look close in the lower-right corner, you can see that the webbing works. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2019)

File.Name: jb13d_jb13-005.jpg
Notes: 49062420517 Here's another shot of the same large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I showed yesterday in my [Previous] two photos. This time it's in its retreat off to the side of its web under a non-native sage leaf with a different prey - probably a Yellow Jacket-type wasp (Vespidae, Hymenoptera). I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) I was too early to catch that beam of sunlight I found yesterday, so I had to use my on-camera flash for this photo. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes there is no choice. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2019)

File.Name: jb14a_jb14-002.jpg
Notes:
49066681603 This is another photo of an odd Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers aka Hackled Band Orb Weavers hanging upside-down under its web on the side of our house. You can see the long hairs on its front legs that are the spider's namesake. These spiders make a proper orb web that is interesting because it is usually horizontal - I'll keep trying to get a decent photo of this one's web, but see this photo of a different spider's web. The spider is only a little more than 1/8 inch across - easy not to notice! I got another photo of a similar spider in this place a month ago for Arachtober, but then it seemed to disappear. Now it's back. I used my on-camera flash for this photo. I try to avoid flash, but sometimes it's necessary - we lose the sun at our north-facing house as we move towards winter. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2019)

I only got this one keeper photo today. The tree trimmers were back in the morning, and then I had errands in town. It was an interesting day though, with a breeze and lots of clouds. It almost felt like rain, but not yet . . . there is a rumor of rain next week.
CommonNames: Feather-legged Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Hackled Band Orb Weavers

File.Name: jb14b_jb13-009.jpg
Notes: 49066621413 Here is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) "hiding" among the leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. This is another female with narrow wings, compare the male in this photo. The crickets seem to be winding down now - I'm finding fewer of them and nights are starting to get chilly. Sure, I could add this to the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but I've already added lots of cricket rear ends, and I already have my two shots for the group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2019)

File.Name: jb14c_jb12-016.jpg
Notes: 49066588798 Here is one more shot of the same large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I showed yesterday in this photo and [Previous] to that one. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) My photo today shows its impressive rear end with its spinnerets and that mysterious dark patch below them. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2019)

File.Name: jb14d_jb11-032.jpg
Notes: 49067051626 This is a metallic-colored fly (Diptera) showing its rear end on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I reckon the fly is a Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae, but I'm not sure if it's a Common Green Bottle Fly (Lucilia sericata) or something more interesting like the Secondary Screwworm (Cochliomyia macellaria) in this photo. I'll post another photo of it tomorrow for Fly Day Friday, but in the meantime HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2019)

File.Name: jb15a_jb15-003.jpg
Notes:
49071281098 I first found this little spider floating in the air, suspended by an almost invisible strand of webbing. I tried to get a photo, but it was swaying too much, even with no wind to speak of. It scurried up its webbing to this oak leaf, and I got the photo. I guess from its triangular shape and eyes that it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, maybe species Metellina mimetoides. It didn't have any organized web that I could see. I've been finding lots of spiders lately, almost as many spiders as insects. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2019)

It was cool and cloudy today, with some breeze. Now a ridge will replace the trough bringing us a warmer weekend . . . followed by another trough next week that might bring us our first rains of the season next Wednesday or Thursday!

File.Name: jb15b_jb15-018.jpg
Notes: 49071781306 This is a bristly little fly (Diptera) on a bristly leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2019)
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Tachinid Fly

File.Name: jb15c_jb11-028.jpg
Notes: 49071936212 Here is the front end of the same metallic-colored fly (Diptera) that I showed the rear of yesterday in this photo. I found it on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I reckon the fly is a Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae, but I'm not sure if it's a Common Green Bottle Fly (Lucilia sericata) or something more interesting like the Secondary Screwworm (Cochliomyia macellaria) in this photo. I notice that this one is not very bristly on its thorax, so maybe it is something else. Another possibility with big eyes and a smooth thorax is the Oriental Latrine Fly (Chrysomya megacephala) - which sounds yucky, but it may be the one. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2019)

File.Name: jb15d_jb12-021.jpg
Notes: 49071642216 This is the cocoon of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on stem of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family with ripening berries. (The berries will be red and ripe for the holidays, giving the shrub its other common name of Christmas Berry.) The cocoon seems to have two distinct parts - a bristly brown section capped with a soft white section. I believe the brown section is the actual cocoon made of hairs that the caterpillar plucks from its own body, and it may be that the white part is actually what's left of the female moth that emerged. The female moths are born without wings and are really just living sacks of eggs that mate with a winged male, lay their eggs directly on their old cocoon, and die. Compare this photo at BugGuide. There's an excellent photo essay on "Life History of Western Tussock Moths, Orgyia vetusta" by Joyce Gross here, though I still have questions. See this photo for the remarkable caterpillars, and this photo for another shot of a similar cocoon. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2019)
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Toyon; California Holly; Christmas Berry
ScientificName: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae

File.Name: jb16a_jb16-025.jpg
Notes:
49076608141 This is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on ripe seed capsules of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). It's wings look like ripples in clear water (listen). This one is male with broad wings, compare the female in this photo. The males are the ones that make all the noise at night, see this photo of one caught in the act. The crickets seem to be winding down now - I'm finding fewer of them and nights are starting to get chilly. Soon they will be gone. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2019)

It was warmer today as a ridge starts to replace the trough that brought some cooler weather last week. This will hopefully be followed by another trough next week that might bring us our first rains of the season next Wednesday or Thursday!

File.Name: jb16b_jb16-003.jpg
Notes: 49076456756 Here's another shot of the same kind of metallic-colored fly (Diptera) that I've shown lately in this photo and this photo. It's on a leaf of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods this morning. I'm sure the fly is a Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae, but it's not bristly enough to be a Green Bottle Fly (Lucilia), compare this photo, and it has white whiskers instead of orange like the Secondary Screwworm (Cochliomyia macellaria) fly in this photo. Now I'm thinking it might be an Oriental Latrine Fly (Chrysomya megacephala) - which sounds yucky, but compare the images on this page at the Natural History of Orange County, California web site. But what's it doing here in the woods?? I hope it turns out to be something else. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2019)

File.Name: jb16c_jb16-011.jpg
Notes: 49076598987 This is another photo of a little "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera looking up at me from inside a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). I believe it's a Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae, compare this photo from a few weeks ago. The seedpod is full of seeds, and maybe the bug is feeding on them, though it's also feeding on the manzanita shrub that the lily is growing up through. There are also bug nymphs feeding on the seeds, see this photo. I don't know if they are the same kind of bug, but they might be. Last year, a very similar bug spent all winter inside a different seed capsule, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2019)

File.Name: jb16d_jb15-019.jpg
Notes: 49076561532 Here are two more shots of a little spider like the one in my [Previous] photo - not the same one but similar, and in the same Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I guess from its triangular shape and eyes that it's another Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, maybe species Metellina mimetoides. These aren't the best photos, but I like how you can see its fangs in the photo on the right. This spider didn't have any organized web that I could see, but it's been doing fine catching prey - note that black "package" below its fangs. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2019)

File.Name: jb17a_jb17-029.jpg
Notes:
49082164621 Here is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) digging into a flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The bundle of yellow disk flowers in the center is about 1/8 inch across which gives scale. This little bee does have pollen-collecting hairs on its hind legs, so it may be a Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina, compare this photo on the left. It also has a hint of blue in its otherwise black body. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2019)

Today was another warm day into the 80s without much wind. Warm weather is expected again tomorrow, but then there will be a big change, and we might get our first rain of the season on Wednesday or Thursday. There's still only a 40% chance of rain, so I'm trying not to get my hopes up too much.

File.Name: jb17b_jb17-015.jpg
Notes: 49082316442 Here is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on ripe seed capsules of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). This one is female with narrow wings, compare the male in this photo that I found on the same plant yesterday. You have to look twice to notice these crickets on these very buggy-looking plants! The crickets seem to be winding down now - I'm finding fewer of them and nights are starting to get chilly. Soon they will be gone. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2019)

File.Name: jb17c_jb17-006.jpg
Notes: 49082061671 I believe this is another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging under its web in a nearly-finished plant of Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum, Asteraceae). I got this photo today, but it's the same spider I showed in this photo a few days ago that might be a male Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes), note the large palps. That other photo showed the spider under its web whereas I got this photo shooting right through the web. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2019)

File.Name: jb17d_jb17-011.jpg
Notes: 49081467873 This is a Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans) growing with other lichens on a dead stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). It's the only specimen I know of in our canyon, and I get a notion to photograph it again every once in a while because it is so striking. If you compare this photo taken last February, you can see that it's basically the same, though it does seem to have a few more apothecia - those small orange disks which are the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen. This lichen resembles the more common Golden-Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus) in this photo, but it lacks the ciliate apothecia. It really looks like something that belongs in the ocean! I've had a hankering to photograph mushrooms and lichens and slime molds lately, but we're still waiting for our first rain of the season. Maybe this week! (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2019)
CommonNames: Powdered Orange Bush Lichen; Chamise; Golden-Eye Lichen

File.Name: jb18a_jb18-016.jpg
Notes:
49087541388 This is a gorgeous little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Those eyes! It also has a remarkable pattern on its wings that reminds me of a Japanese Rising Sun flag, but it's not visible in his shot - see this photo for something similar. I reckon this one is female with that visible ovipositor. It's curious that I've found similar flies on Coast Live Oak before in November, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2019)

Today was another warm day into the 80s with little wind, but there should be a change tomorrow. We might get our first rain of the season Tuesday night or Wednesday, but now it sounds like most of the rain is going south of us. Much as we need it, I'm trying not to get my hopes up too much.
CommonNames: Tephritid Fruit Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: jb18b_jb18-003.jpg
Notes: 49087986536 This is probably the tiniest spider I've ever noticed that isn't a spiderling. I found it this morning on a shrub of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It's really tiny, just 350 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 1.4 mm - less than 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) That's smaller than the tiny gall in my [Previous] photo! I don't know what kind of spider it is, but it has the kind of webbing that I associate with Mesh Web Weaver spiders in the family Dictynidae - like this. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2019)

File.Name: jb18c_jb18-039.jpg
Notes: 49087945781 Here is another tiny gall of a Pumpkin Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus minusculus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It does look a bit like a tiny pumpkin or gourd. This one is about 460 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.8 mm - a bit over 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Small as it is, this wasp-induced gall is home to the tiny wasp larva that lives inside. Note that this gall causes a color change in the leaf surface. I guess it counts as a "nutrient sink" diverting some nutrients from the leaf - but not much. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2019)
CommonNames: Pumpkin Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: jb18d_jb18-028.jpg
Notes:
49088080092 Here are some different galls that I found on a shrub of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) today. Here they are clockwise from the upper-left. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2019)

* Ron Russo in his Field Guide to Plant Galls (UC Press, 2006) describes two similar galls on different Ceanothus species caused by Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) flies - the Vein Gall Midge (genus Asphondylia) and the Midrib Fold Midge (genus Contarinia). I reckon this is something related.

* This is the integral stem gall of a Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth (Periploca ceanothiella or kin) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the order Lepidoptera. Yep, moths can induce galls.

* This is last spring's rosette gall of the Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. It's dried out by now, but see this photo of a fresh one. I believe the galls actually form on flower buds, but see my next gall today.

* This sure appears to be a fresh gall of the Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge which is just forming. I don't understand this at all, because these galls are supposed to from on flower buds - which are months away.
Locality: Asphondylia ceanothi) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. It's dried out by now
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Gall Midge; Vein Gall Midge; Midrib Fold Midge; Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth; Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge; Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia; Contarinia; Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera; Asphondylia ceanothi; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
