File.Name: jb19a_jb19-008.jpg
Notes:
49093350322 Here's another shot of the same large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I've been showing lately in this photo and its neighbors. Some might say it's obscenely large! This spider lives in our garden, so it's easy to find - even on a day when insects are scarce. Here it is in its retreat off to the side of its web under a non-native sage leaf with a different prey that's already been reduced to black goo. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) It's autumn, and this is the time for spiders to pick off the pollinators before they're gone for good. It's their time. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2019)

One good thing about our lack of weather this autumn is that there's been little wind. Today we did have some weather moving in, and we did have some wind - with 20-40 mph gusts, we're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight. It sounds like most of the rain will go south/east of us, and our chance of rain tomorrow has dropped to 20%. We have another slight chance on Friday. Bah.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: jb19b_jb19-032.jpg
Notes: 49092591463 Here are the first new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family that I've found in the mountains this year - even before our first winter rains. The flowers are actually late this year - I found the first flowers on October 10 two years ago, see this photo. Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) is another early bloomer that I've found as early as the autumnal equinox in September, see this photo, but I haven't seen any open flowers yet this year. Some shrubs just do flower early, though most flowers won't appear until after the rains finally come. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2019)

File.Name: jb19c_jb19-046.jpg
Notes: 49093255282 This is a real autumn photo showing ripe nuts of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. They are proper tree nuts with a husk on the outside and then the hard shell, which you can crack to expose the nut meat inside. I've tasted them, and they seem edible and good. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in both woodland and chaparral both, it has gorgeous white flowers that smell like perfume, the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall, and it has these interesting edible nuts! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2019)

File.Name: jb19d_jb19-004.jpg
Notes: 49093223997 This is another stunning gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). Usually they are red, but sometimes they are green - this one is interesting because it is half red and half green. These galls are monothalamous which means they contain just one wasp larva, and I believe there is just a single generation. The pointed beak is distinctive. I don't think of these galls as plant pests - they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2019)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: jb20a_jb20-025.jpg
Notes:
49098246727 I found this small spider on a Prickly Pear cactus in our garden this afternoon. I believe it's another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, probably a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes - though I didn't see any web. It's definitely a male with those impressive palps. It seems to be crouched over a clump of horrible glochid spines - those short hair-like prickers that are barbed and very difficult to remove. (Yep, I got a few getting this photo.) It's probably a pretty safe place to be. I found another one of these in the cactus patch last week, but that one is smaller and has different palps, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2019)

If you look close, you can spot a rain drop on the spider's screen-right hind leg. Hah, we didn't get much more rain than that from our first storm of the season today - just a couple hundredths of an inch. It was cool and overcast and breezy today, but I like soft light the clouds provide. There is another rumor of rain for Thanksgiving next week.
CommonNames: Prickly Pear; Sheet Web Weaver; Hammock Spider

File.Name: jb20b_jb20-009.jpg
Notes: 49098190647 This is another Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, probably species Metellina mimetoides. It has a humped back, very long front legs, and it spins a small perfect orb web that is slanted towards horizontal, though the web is quite invisible in this photo. I believe this one is male with enlarged palps. It is on a dead stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) - just a couple of feet away from the Labyrinth Spider I show two photos [Previous]. I've been finding quite a few of these spiders lately, see my photos here, here, and here. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2019)
ScientificName: Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: jb20c_jb18-034.jpg
Notes: 49097965781 This is fine spider webbing on last summer's stem and seedpods of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). Webbing like this is quite invisible unless the light is just right - but then it's hard to miss. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2019)

File.Name: jb20d_jb17-044.jpg
Notes: 49098109282 Here is the web of an Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider between stems of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). It's the work of a Labyrinth Spider in the genus Metepeira that has a proper orb web in front and a separate retreat behind. I have another recent shot of a different spider's web that shows the connection between retreat and orb web in this photo and the shot previous to it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2019)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Deerweed; Labyrinth Spider

File.Name: jb21a_jb21-014.jpg
Notes:
49102160993 Here are ants tending aphids on a stem of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today. The aphids are kind of creepy - I don't think I've ever seen them packed so tight. It seems like the wrong time of year to be finding aphids on the willows, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2019)

I had caregiver duties this morning, but I managed to get into the woods in the afternoon. No surprise, but there was afternoon light that illuminated places that are usually hidden in shadow during my usual morning walks - like these aphids. It was a dynamic autumn day - chilly, partly cloudy with new clouds building over the mountains, and still dry.

File.Name: jb21b_jb21-010.jpg
Notes: 49102820142 I found this pair of stunning galls of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae)in the woods today. These galls are usually red like this, but sometimes they are green like the one in this photo growing about a foot away on the same shrub. If you look closer than I did at the time, you can see a hole on the bottom of the gall on the right. I assume that's an exit hole where a mature wasp got out - my book says that adults emerge in November. I don't think of these galls as plant pests - they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2019)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: jb21c_jb19-010.jpg
Notes: 49102741492 Here are two shots of a gorgeous little Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a fading leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe it is genus Scaphytopius and subgenus Cloanthanus. I have another photo of this handsome critter, and it's curious that I got that photo a year ago in mid-October on the same plant - close enough to today's date. I figure the shot on the left establishes that it is indeed a beautiful bug, and the shot on the right shows its interesting behind - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2019)
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Scaphytopius; Cloanthanus
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Scaphytopius; Cloanthanus

File.Name: jb21d_jb16-030.jpg
Notes: 49102517356 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) trying to get away from me in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I often see these wasps fly into the tree, but for some reason they usually take off as soon as they see me. This one let me get a photo of its rear end before it got away, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2019)
CommonNames: Western Paper Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: jb22a_jb22-019.jpg
Notes:
49107869501 This is another Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) working a flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure this is the same fly as my previous photo. The bundle of yellow disk flowers in the center is about 1/8 inch across which gives scale. I went out looking for flies today, and I had good luck finding them. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2019)

I had caregiver duties again this morning, but I managed to get into the woods before noon. It was quite breezy by then, with winds from the southeast. There seems to be growing confidence that we will get decent rain for Thanksgiving next week. There's even a rumor of snow down to 2000 feet - including us.

We have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow, and the power will be off from 8:00 am to 8:00 pm . . . they say. I won't be able to post if that really happens. We've had too many of these outages lately, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; too many
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; too many

File.Name: jb22b_jb22-022.jpg
Notes: 49108002737 Here is another Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) digging into a disk flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure this is the same fly as my next photo. It's interesting to compare this shot with this photo of a small bee in the same pose - the fly is small, but the bee is much smaller. The bundle of yellow disk flowers in the center is about 1/8 inch across which gives scale. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group today, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2019)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: jb22c_jb22-007.jpg
Notes: 49107769256 Here is another Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) resting on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. All my photos today are of golden Syrphid flies, but this one seems larger than the ones in my [Next] and [Previous] photos. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group today, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2019)

File.Name: jb22d_jb20-006.jpg
Notes: 49107222443 This is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) resting on a dandelion-like seedhead of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). I got this photo a few days ago in the morning after we got a trace of rain, and you can see a few drops on the bristles of the seeds. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2019)

File.Name: jb24a_jb24-030.jpg
Notes:
49119732967 Here are two shots of a little wasp (Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I got the photo on the left yesterday, and I really couldn't figure it out until I got the photo up on my computer screen - it has a captive ant which it's holding pinned to its abdomen with its hind leg. (This was about ten feet away from the ants tending aphids that I showed in this photo a few days ago.) The wasp came back to the same leaf today, and I got the shot on the right. I don't know what kind of wasp it is - its curved legs and odd antennae remind me of a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea), but do they ever carry live prey like this? Any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2019)

The power company did indeed cut our power yesterday from 8:15 am to 8:15 pm - twelve hours on a Saturday! Today was nice enough, but there's a forecast for big winds tomorrow night, and the power company is warning that we may be shut down again for a Public Safety Power Shutoff. Like many mountain people, I'm considering alternatives to Southern California Edison . . . but it ain't easy. Rain is still looking good for Thanksgiving!
CommonNames: wasp; Coast Live Oak; ant; Chalcid Wasp

File.Name: jb24b_jb24-007.jpg
Notes: 49119488836 I found this tiny winged aphid on a dandelion-like seedhead of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon today. It looks like the aphid is climbing up onto the bristles of that one seed on the right - hah, if the seed goes off in the wind, will the aphid will go with it? The aphid is colored deep red that almost looks black. I've seen them before in the same unlikely place, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2019)

File.Name: jb24c_jb24-018.jpg
Notes: 49118895218 This is looking close at one of the "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the outside of a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). I believe it's a Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae. The mouthparts are remarkable - jointed, like another leg. Going from right to left, first there is the labrum, a piece of skin that covers the top of the rest; then there is the dark bundle of thin stylets which contain food and salivary channels, but need support; finally there is the segmented labium which is jointed like an extra leg, and supports the stylets. Put them all together, and they become the beak or rostrum of the bug. I don't suppose the bugs have to think about how it all works anymore than we do! I think this bug is just starting to drill - slowly rocking back and forth, going in and out, reminding me of the oil well pumps that look like giant insects as they rock back and forth to pump oil. (I'm thinking of the San Ardo Oil Field along Highway 101 in the upper Salinas Valley - as I recall, one of the pumps used to be dressed up as a giant grasshopper!) I've been finding quite a few of these bugs and nymphs on the Calochortus, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2019)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Stink Bug; Shield Bug; labrum; stylets; labium; beak; rostrum
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Pentatomidae; Calochortus
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Pentatomidae; Calochortus

File.Name: jb24d_jb24-009.jpg
Notes: 49118839898 Here is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on an old flower/seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). This one is female with narrow wings, compare the male in this photo. The crickets seem to be winding down now as the nights turn chilly, but I'm still finding a few. These crickets are active at night and hide during the day, so I was surprised to find this one right out in the open like this. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2019)

File.Name: jb25a_jb25-012.jpg
Notes:
49125354106 Here are two shots of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with cryptic colors that I found on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the canyon today. It's really tiny, just 580 pixels in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.3 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) This is not one of the flower-loving Mecaphesa spiders that I've been showing, but something else. It might be a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus, but I think it's more likely genus Ozyptila. It has stubby front legs with just two spines on the first tibia. I've seen these little spiders before, see this photo and this photo. Whatever it is, it's a surprising spider, so tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2019)

Just two photos today as there's fire on the mountain. It's hard to get accurate information, but its my impression the fire is up near the place I call FAA Peak - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro at 3600 feet that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. It's a place I know well. The winds are pretty nasty tonight, blowing the fire towards Santa Barbara. There's a large evacuation order, but we're just over the line along East Camino Cielo road. Even so, I'm jumpy tonight. I'm doing computer backups as I write this.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coast Live Oak; Ground Crab Spider
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; Xysticus; Ozyptila
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; Xysticus; Ozyptila

File.Name: jb25b_jb25-005.jpg
Notes: 49124821503 This is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a bristly seed cluster of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). This is another female cricket with narrow wings, compare the male in this photo. These crickets are active at night and try to hide during the day. The seed capsules of the phacelia are interesting too - each capsule is enclosed in its own little tent of bristly bracts. This plant is an annual, and these dry stems are all that's left. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2019)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: jb27a_jb26-027.jpg
Notes:
49135189331 This is the Cave Fire burning down from East Camino Cielo (on top) and Highway 154 yesterday. The fire is mostly in the drainage of Maria Ygnacio Creek. The mountain community of Painted Cave is hidden in the smoke to the left, though you can see one of the switchbacks of Painted Cave Road on the left edge of my photo. The community was seriously threatened but saved. The fire started on Monday afternoon and burned 4,367 (they say) acres yesterday before it was effectively doused by an inch of heavy rain late last night. The highway was closed, but I got this photo from half-way down San Marcos Road aka the "old pass road". I'm doubly thankful for that rain - it put out the wildfire, and it was our first real storm of the season. (San Marcos Road, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 November 2019)

Apparently 5,500 people were officially evacuated yesterday, though not all actually left their homes. We were just over the line along East Camino Cielo road. We stayed, but we lost our power on Monday night and didn't get it back until midday today. We spent some time collecting still-dry firewood yesterday and otherwise preparing for the rain, and spent a pleasant evening reading with our Luci lights and staring at the fire in our wood stove. We should do that more often.

File.Name: jb27b_jb26-001.jpg
Notes: 49135140176 I believe this is one of the DC-10 Air Tankers flying low over the treetops after doing a retardant drop on the Cave Fire in our mountains yesterday. These are modified wide body McDonnell Douglas DC-10 passenger jetliners which can carry up to 12,000 gallons of "fire retardant in an exterior belly-mounted tank, the contents of which can be released in eight seconds." (Thanks Wikipedia.) It's pretty wonderful how low and slow these mighty aircraft can fly with that heavy load. I got this photo from our upstairs deck on a mostly clear day. The fire was just a couple of miles way, but the winds were just right to keep it moving away from us. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2019)

File.Name: jb28a_jb28-004.jpg
Notes:
49139886497 Here is snow falling on our upstairs deck and oak trees at about 8:30 this morning. Our house is at about 2,300 feet elevation, and there was a forecast of snow down to 2,000 feet today. I was worried when the snow started to stick. After a few inches of snow builds up in the trees, the branches start to break - it sounds like gunfire! This has happened a few times in our woods over the years, most impressively back in the early 1980s when we got almost a foot of snow that did some serious damage to the woods that's still visible. No problem this time, the snow soon stopped and melted away. That's something to be thankful for - happy Thanksgiving! (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2019)

Drought, fire, rain, and snow - all in the four days since Monday! And it's not over yet, as we just had a heavy dump of hail/graupel that didn't stick to the trees but covered the ground. It's a good thing the highway is still mostly closed from the Cave Fire incident because there would have been accidents with this slippery stuff. My real worry this year was that our rainy season would never come, but we now stand at 2.16 inches for the water year which began in September, almost all from this storm. That's still only about 1/2 of normal for this date, but it's better than nothing - and that's something else to be thankful for.

File.Name: jb28b_jb28-032.jpg
Notes: 49139857472 This is another one of our "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) keeping warm on a cold day inside a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). I believe it's a Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae. It might spend all winter here, see this photo and this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2019)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Stink Bug; Shield Bug
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Pentatomidae

File.Name: jb28c_jb28-037.jpg
Notes: 49139063133 These are ripening berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family, after the rain and snow today - with just a moment of sunshine on an otherwise gloomy day. The berries will be ripe for the year-end holidays, giving the shrub its other common name of Christmas Berry, but I figure they are also appropriate for the Thanksgiving holiday today. The ripe berries are edible, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. Happy Thanksgiving! (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2019)

File.Name: jb28d_jb28-021.jpg
Notes: 49138992808 Here are liverworts and mosses come back from the shadows again! It's amazing how quickly they revive after a rain breaks their dormancy. I don't know what the moss is, but I believe the liverwort is the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). I have before and after shots of the liverwort drying out in this photo. I also found the California Liverwort (Asterella californica) on the same sandstone outcrop today. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2019)

File.Name: jb29a_jb29-027.jpg
Notes:
49143947688 Here is the skeleton of a large shrub of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) that was burned out by the Cave Fire just a couple of days ago, see this photo from Tuesday. Good rain helped firefighters and air crews control this wildfire early. I drove up East Camino Cielo road today to see how far I could get and discovered to my surprise that the road was not closed. I got this photo near the place I call FAA Peak - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro at 3600 feet that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. I'll get back to this place soon for more photos. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 November 2019)

The sun was in and out of the clouds today, but it was cold, only in the low 40s. There's still snow on the ground in places out of the sun - like all around our house on the north-facing side of the mountains. We've had recent fire, rain, and snow. It will be nice to get back to normal, but maybe not yet - according to the NWS, there's at least a chance of rain everyday for the next week!

File.Name: jb29b_jb29-007.jpg
Notes: 49144399266 Here are tiny water droplets on the bristles of a seed cluster of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). It looks almost like ice from a distance, and I suspect the drops formed from melting ice or snow - see my photo two shots back taken nearby. This plant is an annual, and the dead stems are all that's left. I have another recent shot of a seed cluster without water drops in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2019)

File.Name: jb29c_jb29-015.jpg
Notes: 49144349651 This is another one of those "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that I've been finding inside the seed capsules of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily, like this photo from yesterday. I believe the bug is a Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae. This one has found a hideaway between two dead leaves of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) - a different shrub than the one I've found the bugs on before. It looks like a snug place to wait out the rain and snow we've been having. (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2019)

ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Pentatomidae; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae

File.Name: jb29d_jb29-006.jpg
Notes: 49144484822 We had real snow yesterday morning, see my [Previous] photo. Then in the early evening as I was posting that photo, we had a dump of what I first thought was hail that didn't stick to the trees but covered the ground. Patches of it are still there a day later, like this accumulation in a plant of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae). If you look close, you can see that the snow has a granular texture of pea-size pieces that are not solid ice. I believe this is actually graupel aka soft hail or snow pellets. Is corn snow something similar? I like it because it doesn't damage the trees with its weight like real snow does. (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2019)

File.Name: jb30a_jb25-007.jpg
Notes:
49149540017 Here is a sequence showing a tiny spider starting to climb up, up, and away . . . to get away from me! The spider is on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is the same spider I showed more clearly in this photo a few days ago on the day the Cave Fire started. I meant to post this photo along with the other one, but events got in the way - so here it is. I believe it is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the genus Xysticus or Ozyptila. It's less than 1/10 inch long. See my other photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2019)

I usually like to show my most recent photos first, but today it started to rain as I was taking my first photo of the day, so I bailed. I do show that photo in my [Previous] shot, but I figure this one has more pizazz for a lead. It was chilly and rainy today - and I had car troubles. I drove my truck home yesterday with no problem, but when I started it this morning, the clutch didn't work - something with the linkage. So I took Julie's Honda and got a flat tire - and then I discovered there's no spare. Lots of running around.
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thomisidae; Xysticus; Ozyptila
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thomisidae; Xysticus; Ozyptila

File.Name: jb30b_jb30-003.jpg
Notes: 49148795893 These are galled flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). That odd beaked shape is typical. I'm pretty sure these are the galls of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), see this photo of an emerging adult - at least the husk of one. I may have a shot of the actual adult fly in this photo. These shrubs flower in June, so why do I find the galls in mid-winter? I guess the galls stay on the shrub until the next flowering time so that the cycle can repeat, and they're only now starting to develop. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2019)

File.Name: jb30c_jb28-031.jpg
Notes: 49149225336 Here is another gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). The galls usually have red and/or green colors, see this photo and this photo. This one is uniformly brown, and I'm not sure if it's always been that color or turned brown as it matures - or rather as the wasp larva inside matures. The pointed beak is distinctive. I don't think of these galls as plant pests - they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2019)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: jb30d_jb24-020.jpg
Notes: 49149388217 This is a galled stem of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). The stem is abnormally short and chubby because it is holding the caterpillar of a Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth (Periploca ceanothiella or kin) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the order Lepidoptera. Yep, moths can induce galls as well as wasps and flies, and ceanothus shrubs can host them as well as oaks. See this photo for more galls on this plant. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2019)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: jc01a_jc01-004.jpg
Notes:
49155361696 "S" for slug? I found this California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) in the woods today, the first one I've seen this year. It seems like a miracle to find them again after our long dry summer - extra long this year as we only got our first rain last week. Somehow the slugs manage to survive six or more months of hot and dry, and I sure don't know how. There seems to be some confusion about their proper scientific name, but the Oregon Banana Slugs (Ariolimax columbianus) look quite different, with greenish colors and a more pronounced ridge on their back, compare this photo. Wikipedia reports that they are the second-largest species of terrestrial slug in the world, growing up to ten inches long - but this one was smaller, only about six inches long. Mosses have started to green, but seedlings are still weeks away - I don't know what this one will eat, see this photo from last year. I regard these slugs as environmental indicators of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2019)

We got another 7/10 inch of rain yesterday and last night bringing our total to a bit over three inches - almost all in the last few days. That's up to 60% of normal for the date. We got a break today, but more rain is expected Tuesday/Wednesday and again on Friday/Saturday. No rain today, but it was dark and gloomy with a breeze to keep things in motion. Nothing to do but kick up the ISO, increase the aperture, and take lots of photos!

File.Name: jc01b_jc01-009.jpg
Notes: 49155508397 This is the remarkable caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on its Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) host plant. This one is small - not tiny like this one, and not large like this one. (Damn, that tiny one probably perished when the power company crew drove through its branch on their way to replace another power pole.) The butterflies are common in the woods all summer, though they've started to disappear for the year with the recent cold weather. I rarely find the caterpillars, and this is how they overwinter according to my book. I don't know quite how they do it. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2019)

File.Name: jc01c_jc01-027.jpg
Notes: 49154731228 Here is a cute little net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae. I have many shots of these cute little insects, though this is the first one I've seen since this one in mid-October. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2019)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae

File.Name: jc01d_jc01-015.jpg
Notes: 49155377777 This is a spider tucked into a little cave between leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - a pretty nice place to be when the rain starts up again. I believe it's another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, probably a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes - though I didn't see any web. Those large palps indicate it's a boy, compare this photo of a female. You can see its fangs right behind the palps. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Sheet Web Weaver; Hammock Spider

File.Name: jc02a_jc02-002.jpg
Notes:
49160762928 Here is today's shot of the remarkable caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on its Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) host plant that I found yesterday, compare this photo. It's an odd pose, but I've seen it before - see this photo and this photo and this photo. The butterflies are common in the woods all summer, though they've started to disappear for the year with the recent cold weather. I rarely find the caterpillars, and this is how they overwinter according to my book, so maybe this one will become one of next year's butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2019)

It was dry today, but I believe rain is still coming. There was some sun but also gloomy clouds and breeze. It sounds like they're getting heavy rain up north, but it's an "atmospheric river" kind of event and we're off the track. The river of rain will hopefully drop south Tuesday night and bring us some. Gloomy weather goes better with real rain!

File.Name: jc02b_jc02-027.jpg
Notes: 49161356172 Hah - I found this little green spider inside the rain gauge on our upstairs deck today! It's a more complicated rain gauge than the coffee can I used to use, as it has a small inner tube which measures hundredths of an inch and overflows into a larger outer tube which can hold up to ten inches. There's a funnel-like lid on top that directs water into the small tube. The spider seems to want to be inside and made no effort to get away when I took the lid off today. It's actually in no danger unless we get ten inches of rain in one storm - which has happened before but not for many years. It appears to be a juvenile Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae), though I'm confused about species - it has reddish chevron marks on its abdomen like P. viridans, but it also has whitish hairs on its abdomen like P. longipalpis. It's one of those. I wonder how it got into the rain gauge on our upstairs deck?? (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2019)
ScientificName: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; P. viridans; P. longipalpis
associatedTaxa: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; P. viridans; P. longipalpis

File.Name: jc02c_jc02-006.jpg
Notes: 49161039581 This is the gall of the Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on a leaf bud of tree. These galls are almost too perfectly round, like planetary orbs. They're small, about the size of a blueberry. I was finding them in October last year, but this is the first one I've seen this year. They stay on the tree for a while, then turn brown and drop into the leaf litter on the ground, see this photo from last year for more info. I had to use flash for this photo today, it was just too dark and breezy to get the shot otherwise. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's necessary. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2019)

File.Name: jc02d_jc01-006.jpg
Notes: 49161201117 I believe these are some sort of Oak Treehoppers in the family Membracidae, but they lack the bright colors of Platycotis vittata, which is the most widespread species. Maybe they are the related species Platycotis minax? There are only two records at BugGuide, but this one does look similar to mine. These are part of a colony of maybe a dozen individuals on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's interesting that only one has a thorn-like horn on it's head. My first thought was that it's a male/female thing, but the BugGuide info page on P. vittata says that the horn on that species may be entirely absent - so maybe that's true of my treehoppers also. I notice that the branch seems covered with little pits - scars from their feeding? I tried to get a better photo today with no flash, but no luck. I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2019)
ScientificName: Membracidae; Platycotis vittata; Platycotis minax; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; P. vittata
associatedTaxa: Membracidae; Platycotis vittata; Platycotis minax; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; P. vittata

File.Name: jc03a_jc03-014.jpg
Notes:
49165915568 I believe this is another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. It's a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae, though this one didn't have a web. I've found a few of these spiders without webs, maybe because of the rainy weather we've been having lately. It's on a fading leaf of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). This one appears to be female with slender palps, but I've also been finding male spiders with swollen palps recently like this one. (See this photo for mating spiders from last spring!) My book says these spiders are "best recognized by the tuning fork-shaped mark on the carapace and their serrated dorsal abdominal stripe" - which perfectly describes my spider. I had to use flash for this photo today, it was just too dark and breezy to get the shot otherwise. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's necessary. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2019)

We've only had a few sprinkles so far today, but I have to believe there's real rain coming later tonight and tomorrow. The forecast is for 100% heavy rain and breezy! The problem with these "atmospheric river" storms is that they are relatively narrow and hard to predict. A difference of 50 miles north or south can make all the difference.
CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Sheet Web Weaver; Wild Sweetpea

File.Name: jc03b_jc03-010.jpg
Notes: 49165870683 This little Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) is getting ready for our storm tonight. It's tucked in under a canopy of oak leaves that will provide some shelter from the heavy rain that's forecast for later tonight. Oh boy! (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2019)

File.Name: jc03c_jc01-033.jpg
Notes: 49166283266 This is a tiny "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) inside a boat-like seed capsule of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I'm not sure what kind of bug it is, and I'm also not sure what those other things are in the bottom of the boat - seeds or grubs? I've found several different kinds of insects in these old seed capsules, see this photo and this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2019)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: jc03d_jc03-007.jpg
Notes: 49166251966 I found another California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) in the woods today - or maybe it's the same one I showed in this photo a couple of days ago, it was only about ten feet away from that other one. It's crawling on old wood chips in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees on a pretty steep embankment. In fact that's where I usually find them, crawling up and down the hill. I regard these large slugs as environmental indicators of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2019)
CommonNames: California Banana Slug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: jc04a_jc04-005.jpg
Notes:
49171368472 This is the first mushroom of the new season growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I put the car key in the photo for scale - the key is 2-1/2 inches long, and the mushroom is the size of a dinner plate! The first mushroom of the year - and I don't know what it is. A natural guess is genus Cortinarius with those reddish-brown gills, but I'm not convinced. If I can find a fresher specimen, then I can probably figure it out. The mushroom already smelled like a diaper pail - odd because we just got our first rain of the season a week ago. The first mushroom - but soon there will be more! (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2019)

We got another 1.4 inches of rain last night and this morning, followed by gloomy weather with a few showers. This brings our rain total up to 4.44 inches, about 3/4 of normal for the date. We have another good chance of rain on Saturday, and then we have some time to dry out. The rains came late this year, but I'm glad they finally came.

File.Name: jc04b_jc04-009.jpg
Notes: 49171305982 I found this Darkling Beetle (family Tenebrionidae, Coleoptera) on top of the mushroom in my [Next] photo - actually it was under another mushroom that was already overturned, so it was out of sight on the ground. Note that the mushroom cap already has spots of green mold, and it smelled like a diaper pail - odd because we got our first rain of the season just a week ago. This beetle was on the small side for these beetles. This is one of the largest insect families, with about 450 species in California alone. 'Nuff said. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2019)

File.Name: jc04c_jc04-014.jpg
Notes: 49170534628 Here are a pair of what I believe are some sort of Oak Treehoppers in the genus Platycotis, Membracidae on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), see this photo for another shot. I believe that's a nymph on top - it has wings but they are not fully developed. And maybe that color on the adult below is just a reflection of the nymph? That colorful nymph looks more like the widespread species Platycotis vittata, but I'm still thinking the less colorful adults are the related species Platycotis minax. Hah, they're touching noses - being affectionate? Like that other photo, I had to use flash to get this shot on this dark day. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2019)
ScientificName: Platycotis; Membracidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Platycotis vittata; Platycotis minax
associatedTaxa: Platycotis; Membracidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Platycotis vittata; Platycotis minax

File.Name: jc04d_jc02-011.jpg
Notes: 49171203297 This is a little spider sitting on what's left of its orb web. The rain and snow lately have been tough on webs! I was thinking at the time that it's another Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family like the one in this family, but it doesn't seem to have a humped back, so I'm not sure. I was standing on tiptoes to get this photo in a small oak tree, and it came out better than I expected. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2019)

File.Name: jc05a_jc05-032.jpg
Notes:
49176020081 This is looking at the spinnerets and rear end of a juvenile Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on the railing of our upstairs deck. This is the same spider that was living in our rain gauge, see this photo from a few days ago. I had to empty the rain gauge today in preparation for our new storm tomorrow, and the spider finally dropped out. It's still hanging out nearby, and I left the top off so it can go back in if it wants to. I kind of hope it does. In the meantime, this is a nice shot of the spider's rear end - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2019)

It's interesting that I've been finding more spiders than insects lately. The spiders have been doing their job of picking off the pollinators before winter comes, but the insects might be better at hiding out of the rain. It did feel like rain today, it was dark and gloomy enough - but it sounds like we have to wait until tomorrow night. We have "rain likely" from Friday night through Sunday.

File.Name: jc05b_jc05-021.jpg
Notes: 49175950186 Here are two shots of a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on an umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). If you look close, you can see part of its web stretched across the opening of the leaf - only an inch or two. These tiny spiders are only about 1/8 inch long, see this photo for a look at another spider's top side with its six spots. I had to use flash for these photos today, it was just too dark and breezy to get the shots otherwise. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's necessary. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2019)

File.Name: jc05c_jc05-003.jpg
Notes: 49175395668 I found this tiny spider on webbing by spines of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. It's only about 690 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I don't know what kind of spider it is, but it's definitely a male with those enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2019)

File.Name: jc05d_jc03-001.jpg
Notes: 49176052527 Here are a Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) and a non-native European Earwig (Forficula auricularia, Dermaptera) digging into a damaged section of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. They are getting ready for rain. The ladybug looks pretty well covered, and it's still there a couple of days later, but the earwig left for better cover before our good rain last night. I especially like the way the earwig is dug in with just its pinchers showing - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2019)
CommonNames: Lady Beetle; European Earwig; Prickly Pear
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Forficula auricularia; Dermaptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae
associatedTaxa: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Forficula auricularia; Dermaptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae

File.Name: jc06a_jc06-019.jpg
Notes:
49180372868 I found another California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) in the woods today. Actually I found three of them today - two large ones like this, and one smaller. I'm not sure what they are eating, as the seedlings they favor are still weeks away, see this photo from last year. (There are other possibilities for food, see this photo of a different banana slug from Oregon!) I regard these slugs as environmental indicators of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2019)

Today started clear and calm, but winds soon kicked up and clouds started to move in. The trees were shaking, and I was happy to find this slug on the ground for a photo. Light rain has started as I write these comments. The forecast for the next couple of days mentions "long duration light rainfall" that sounds perfect for mushrooms.

File.Name: jc06b_jc05-035.jpg
Notes: 49180322458 Here is a little Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on the outside of our rain gauge yesterday afternoon. This spider has spent the last couple of weeks (at least) living inside the rain gauge, see this photo. I had to empty the rain gauge in preparation for our new storm that is just starting, and the spider finally dropped out. It stayed nearby for a while, and I left the top off overnight so it could get back inside - but I guess it decided to move on. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2019)

File.Name: jc06c_jc03-019.jpg
Notes: 49180933472 I found this interesting green fly (Diptera) on an old flower/seed head of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) near Lake Cachuma last week when I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley. I wasn't even sure it was a fly when I found it, but now I can see that it has two wings and halteres - as well as plumose antennae. It's maybe 1/2 inch long. After I got a few photos, I tried to nudge it's head up - it flew off, and I never did get a look at its face. I guess from its antennae that it's some sort of Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha), but it's a new one for me. There are green Limoniid Crane Flies shown at BugGuide like the Erioptera chlorophylla group, but they seem to be exclusively east coast. Any help with ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2019)
ScientificName: Diptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Tipulomorpha; Erioptera chlorophylla
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Tipulomorpha; Erioptera chlorophylla

File.Name: jc06d_i502-034.jpg
Notes: 49180874297 I was going through some old photos looking for something else when this photo of a little wild bee caught my eye . . . that's no bee, it's a fly! I did post another photo of the same critter, see this photo from 3 May 2018 which has the title "Wild bee hiding in oak leaves". But it is indeed a Soldier Fly in the family Stratiomyidae, species Stratiomys maculosa. It's a remarkable bee-mimic that fooled me! I like to post new photos, but I'll make an exception for this one - this has been a slow week for flies anyway because of rain and snow and chilly weather. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2018)

File.Name: jc07a_jc07-012.jpg
Notes:
49185551791 Here are two more shots of the same tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on an umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) that I showed a few days ago in this photo. These aren't the best shots, but I like them because of what they show. The one on the left (taken today in the drizzle) shows the top-side of the spider, note that it has eight black spots rather than the six in its name. I actually think all these spiders in our canyon have eight spots, see my photos here and here for two more examples. The photo on the right (taken yesterday) shows the spider's web, hard to see because it is so fine. (This photo from September is better.) Comparing the two photos shows the work of last night's rain - the web on the left is now destroyed. I managed to get both these shots without using flash. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2019)

We had almost 3/4 inch of good rain last night, followed by steady drizzle all day, which brings our total to 1.4 inches for the storm and 5.8 inches for the season. That's about 95% of our normal for the date, and it's still raining tonight. The rains were slow in coming this year, but they are now making up for it.

File.Name: jc07b_jc07-010.jpg
Notes: 49185484476 I believe this is some kind of fungus growing on a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I don't know what kind of fungus it is, but the fruiting bodies are kind of pretty - like little red raspberries. They feel hard, not squishy. Are they only on the bark? The individual globules are only about 0.5 mm in size, though the aggregations of them are a mm or two across. It may be that they come from the little orange spots in the upper-left. I'll keep an eye on them. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2019)

File.Name: jc07c_jc07-006.jpg
Notes: 49184934323 Here are tiny new lichens growing on spines of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden today. There seems to be an abundance of new lichens this year, probably the result of good winter rains last year. I don't know what species these are, but they have dark rhizines - those root-like strands that anchor the lichen to the substrate. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2019)

File.Name: jc07d_jc06-012.jpg
Notes: 49185366441 This is another California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) in the woods yesterday. It's a relative babe compared to the one in my [Previous] photo, only about two inches long compared to six or eight inches. You can see this one's breathing hole or pneumostome - that hole in its side - which leads to its single lung. They only have this hole on the slug-right side. We had steady drizzle today, and I thought there would be slugs dancing in the rain - but I didn't find any. I regard these slugs as environmental indicators of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2019)

File.Name: jc08a_jc08-011.jpg
Notes:
49190436468 Here is a Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on an old flower/seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). This one is a Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens) - recognizable by the two diagonal white lines on its pronotum right behind its head. They can have any number of spots or none at all. We used to have huge congregations of these ladybugs overwintering in our canyon, but I haven't seen them for years, see this photo for the last time I saw such a "rave of ladybugs" back in 2012. Maybe coincidentally, 2012 was also the start of our extended drought. I love the brilliant light in this photo. There's nothing like when the sun first comes out after a good rain. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2019)

A good rain indeed! We had another inch of rain last night bringing our total for the season to 6.8 inches, which is 111% of normal for the date, almost all since Thanksgiving. We did see some sun today but also some strong showers, one following the other. Needless to say, I got my photos today in the sun.
CommonNames: Lady Beetle; Black Sage; Convergent Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Hippodamia convergens
associatedTaxa: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Hippodamia convergens

File.Name: jc08b_jc08-015.jpg
Notes: 49190383003 I don't know what the heck these are - seeds or eggs or pupae or alien space pods? They are tiny, less than 1/8 inch long. Once I noticed one of them, I started noticing more - always in the hollows of old flower/seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) - see my [Next] photo. I'll try to keep an eye on these as they mature, but I have the notion that they will effectively disappear as they dry out. Hah, I'm glad I can still find some mysteries in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2019)

File.Name: jc08c_jc08-025.jpg
Notes: 49190347208 I found this furry little beetle on a stem of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) between rain showers today when the sun was actually out. It really is tiny, just 730 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I don't know anything about it, but it's cute! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2019)

File.Name: jc08d_jc08-016.jpg
Notes: 49190309618 This is another one of those "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that I've been finding inside the seed capsules of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily, like this photo shows. This one is on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) - but it's the shrub that the lilies are growing up through. If you squint, maybe you can see a second bug on the leaf behind. I believe the bug is a Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae, but I don't know what kind. I've posted quite a few photos of these bugs recently - I know where they live, so I can find them when other insects are scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2019)
CommonNames: Eastwood Manzanita; Stink Bug; Shield Bug
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Pentatomidae

File.Name: jc09a_jc09-001.jpg
Notes:
49196642341 Here is another Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These ladybugs can have any number of black spots or none at all, but they always have two diagonal white lines on the pronotum right behind its head. There are scattered individuals in the woods right now, see this photo from yesterday, but I haven't seen large congregations of them overwintering in our canyon for many years. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2019)

I was busy this morning, but I made time for a walk later in the day. The sun was out, and it sounds like we'll have the week to dry out after our recent rains. We have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow, and the power will be off from 7:00 am to 6:00 pm . . . they say. I probably won't be able to post if that really happens. We've had too many of these outages lately, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
CommonNames: Convergent Lady Beetle; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; too many
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; too many

File.Name: jc09b_jc09-006.jpg
Notes: 49196550716 Here is a little Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) today. It's about 1,430 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.6 mm - not quite 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Hah - there are about 3,000 species in North America . . . and this is one of them. It seems to have extra-long antennae and a diaphanous quality that is attractive up close like this. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2019)
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: jc09c_jc09-011.jpg
Notes: 49196529071 This is a dying leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that's still on the tree. These oaks are not deciduous, but they are dropping a lot of leaves right now. The failing leaf is quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2019)

File.Name: jc09d_jc08-001.jpg
Notes: 49196699267 I found this oddly curled leaf on the ground under a shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods yesterday. The leaf is anything but typical, but there's no different shrub nearby that it could belong to. Maybe it's infected with a rust fungus or galled? It's quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2019)

File.Name: jc11a_jc10-025.jpg
Notes:
49206107728 This is a tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider on a fading leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). It really is tiny, about 980 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.8 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. It's remarkable that the spider looks so much like a miniature of one of our large Halloween spiders, like this one. This is the first one of these tiny spiders than I've seen this year. It appears to be male with those enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2019)

Today's photos are all spider (and web) shots that I should have posted yesterday. We had another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages yesterday that went on for 12 hours until 7:00 pm - too late to start going through the day's photos. Oh well, I had errands all morning today, so it's just as well that I already had photos to post. The good news is that I got my truck back alive and well with a rebuilt clutch.
Locality: and web) shots that I should have posted yesterday.  We had another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages yesterday that went on for 12 hours until 7:00 pm - too late to start going through the day's photos.  Oh well

File.Name: jc11b_jc10-003.jpg
Notes: 49206062218 Here are two shots of a tiny spider inside the flower scar on the end of a ripening Prickly Pear cactus fruit in the garden. The whole scar is less than an inch across. The spider is about 1020 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.0 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I don't know what kind of spider it is. I find the darnedest things inside these cactus fruit, see this photo of another recent spider and try a search of my photos to see them all. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2019)

File.Name: jc11c_jc10-036.jpg
Notes: 49206676742 This is the web of an Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider strung between old seed heads of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I can't see any spider, but this might be the web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) - see this photo taken last year. I also don't see any obvious retreat like these spiders typically make, but the spider would easily fit into one of the seed openings of the shrub - a pretty good retreat. All spider webs took a beating from the rain and snow last week, and they are just now getting rebuilt. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2019)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Black Sage; Labyrinth Spider

File.Name: jc11d_jc10-012.jpg
Notes: 49205937718 Here is another shot of a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on an umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). Those black things along the bottom are old fruit that never got eaten. If you look close, you can see part of the spider's web stretched across the opening of the leaf - only about an inch. These tiny spiders are only about 1/8 inch long, see this photo for a look at another spider's top side with its six or eight spots. It's hard to get the web to show up in this place in our shady north-facing canyon. I tried both with and without flash on a cloudy day, and I like this shot without flash best. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2019)

File.Name: jc12a_jc12-016.jpg
Notes:
49211375076 Here are two shots of another tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). This one has two rows of three large black spots on its abdomen and two tiny black dots at the end - for a total of eight spots, see this photo for another one. This spider didn't seem to have a proper orb web. Something strange is that the it seems to be holding the ends of a broken strand of webbing, like the Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes, Uloboridae) in this photo. I've found quite a few of these spiders this year, more than usual. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2019)

I seem to have a series going of tiny orb-weaver spiders interrupted by one ladybug rear end. Well, that's what I'm finding these days. Today was warmer than its been, up into the low 60s on our upstairs deck, but it was up to 70∞ in a sunnier place a mile away. It was quite breezy with 20-30 mph gusts all day, but there were also calm moments for photos.
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Southern Bush Monkeyflower
ScientificName: Araniella displicata; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Hyptiotes; Uloboridae

File.Name: jc12b_jc12-023.jpg
Notes: 49211300116 I found another tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider on a dead stem of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. It's only about half the size of the one I found yesterday - just 470 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.8 mm - just a hair more than 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) If you look at its screen-right leg, you can see that it's holding a line that goes down to its nearby web - like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing. I did get a photo of its web, a perfect orb web two or three inches across, but I'll save it for the next Web Wednesday. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2019)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Southern Bush Monkeyflower
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis
