File.Name: jc12c_jc12-028.jpg
Notes: 49211470587 Here is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in a clump of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus, Poaceae). These spiders typically decorate their webs with a "trash line" mostly made of the bodies of their prey, but this one has a clean web with no trash yet. All spider webs took a beating from the rain and snow last week, and they are just now getting rebuilt. These spiders always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd - though this one is a little more abrupt than most. This makes its rear end interesting, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2019)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Giant Rye Grass

File.Name: jc12d_jc08-026.jpg
Notes: 49211439802 This is looking at the rear end of a Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae) on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). As I recall, it's another another Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens), but there's no way of knowing from this photo. These ladybugs can have any number of black spots or none at all, but they always have two diagonal white lines on the pronotum right behind its head - see this photo of the other end. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2019)
CommonNames: Lady Beetle; Black Sage; Convergent Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Hippodamia convergens
associatedTaxa: Coccinellidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Hippodamia convergens

File.Name: jc13a_jc13-019.jpg
Notes:
49216001267 I believe this is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. I found several of these today flying around the patch of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. I was confused at first because these dragonflies usually have more red on their abdomens, but this one does has the typical white and yellow racing stripes on its thorax. I've been looking for these on cold mornings in the canyon, but this is only the second one I've seen this season. (Here is the first one.) I believe they'll stay in the canyon all winter long. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2019)

It was windy today with 30-40 mph gusts. Sometimes there are quiet times between the gusts with these wind events, but today it was just steady wind. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and the winds might continue for a couple of days. I hope not. There's nothing to do but bump up the ISO a notch and take lots of photos to get a few keepers!
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: jc13b_jc13-012.jpg
Notes: 49215935302 This is another in my series of tiny spiders that I've been posting for the last couple of days. It's a Jumping Spider (Salticidae) inside an empty acorn cap of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). It might be a female Pelegrina aeneola, compare this photo. It's got an even tinier fly for prey. I can see in another photo that the fly has just two wings and halteres, I guess that it's some kind of midge or gnat. The acorn cap is about 1/2 inch across. The spider is about 1070 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.2 mm - a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Hah, once I noticed this one, I found two more spiders inside other acorn caps! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2019)
ScientificName: Salticidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Pelegrina aeneola
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Pelegrina aeneola

File.Name: jc13c_jc13-003.jpg
Notes: 49215143458 Here are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. This is an early bloomer that sometimes flowers even before the winter rains start. I've found the flowers as early as the autumnal equinox in September, see this photo from 2013. Last year I found them in early October. In fact, this year is the latest that I've ever found them. It's odd that there are dried up buds on the shrubs that never opened earlier this year when they should have, and I suspect the unusually late start of the rains this year has something to do with it. I'm glad these beautiful flowers are finally blooming. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2019)

File.Name: jc13d_jc12-043.jpg
Notes: 49215602661 This is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) working a flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. The bundle of yellow disk flowers in the center is only about 1/8 inch across which gives scale. Insects and flowers have both been scarce lately now that we've finally had some weather, and I was happy to find several of these flies on the remaining flowers. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2019)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: jc14a_jc14-004.jpg
Notes:
49220256991 This is a Horsehair Fungus (Marasmius androsaceus aka Gymnopus androsaceus aka Setulipes androsaceus) mushroom growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. Or maybe this one is Marasmius quercophilus aka Gymnopus quercophilus that favors oaks? (Those darn mycologists!) Whatever it is, this mushroom is actually growing from an old barely-recognizable acorn, but the fungus has spread to the nearby fallen leaf which is sprouting horsehair-like rhizomorphs that will never produce mushrooms. I got this photo above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods at eye level. This is the first proper mushroom I've found there this season, and I've been waiting for them! (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2019)

Our Thanksgiving rains have finally brought about a regime change in the woods from summer into winter. There are still insects and wildflowers out there, but banana slugs and mushrooms are sharing the stage. We actually had some drizzle last night, but mainly we've had wind wind wind today with gusts to 45 mph. Our NWS Wind Advisory has been extended to Sunday evening. It's expected to be cold tonight, down to 36∞ and maybe colder. There's a chance of rain on Wednesday.
ScientificName: Marasmius androsaceus; Gymnopus androsaceus; Setulipes androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Marasmius quercophilus; Gymnopus quercophilus

File.Name: jc14b_jc14-018.jpg
Notes: 49220166601 Here is the growth of a fungus on fallen leaves and an old acorn of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks electric! It looks just the way I imagine a fungus mycelium to be, but this is all I've ever seen of it. The fungus is growing over the leaf rather than through it like real mycelium would, and I don't think it's leading up to any kind of visible mushroom. I have another shot of something similar in this photo. I got this photo above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods at eye level. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2019)

File.Name: jc14c_jc14-015.jpg
Notes: 49219616123 This is both the cocoon and nursery of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) under a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). The cocoon seems to have two distinct parts, a bristly brown section that feels like excelsior and a soft white section to the side. I believe the brown section is the actual cocoon made of hairs that the caterpillar plucks from her own body, and the white part is what's left of the female moth that emerged. The female moths are born without wings and are really just living sacks of eggs that mate with a winged male, lay their eggs directly on their old cocoon, and die right there. You can see the eggs in my photo which look just like tiny cracked chicken eggs! For more info, see the links in my comment to this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2019)
ScientificName: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: jc14d_jc14-008.jpg
Notes: 49220063466 Here is a Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) growing on a near-vertical sandstone outcrop in the canyon. The sandstone has lost a slab during our recent rains exposing the surprisingly robust roots of the plant. The roots are probably the reason why the sandstone broke - growing in a crack and slowly pushing the rock apart. Compare this photo of another plant nearby taken last month before the rain - they are definitely looking better. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2019)

File.Name: jc15a_jc15-020.jpg
Notes:
49226010932 Hah - we have more spiders in our rain gauge! First we had a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) living in the rain gauge, see this photo from a couple of weeks ago. It finally left, but today I went to empty the rain gauge and found two spiders inside, which I guess are a male and female pair. My photo today shows the larger female spider. Maybe it's a Wolf Spider (Lycosidae) - though the rain gauge in on our upstairs deck, and don't they prefer the ground? It's a more complicated rain gauge than the coffee can I used to use, as it has a small inner tube which measures hundredths of an inch and overflows through an opening into a larger outer tube which can hold up to ten inches. There's a funnel-like lid on top that directs water into the small tube. To get into the gauge, the spiders must have gone through the funnel opening into the inner tube, and then through the small overflow opening into the larger tube. One wonders why. I'll post a photo of both spiders together tomorrow. Now what is it about spiders and rain gauges? (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2019)

Today started windy, then the wind backed off a bit giving me time for a few photos, then it picked up again in the afternoon - and finally it seems to be calming down in the evening. It's been a windy three days reaching a peak of almost 50 mph yesterday. It feels colder tonight but still not freezing. We do have a chance of rain on Wednesday. After so much wind sucking up the moisture, we need it.

File.Name: jc15b_jc15-015.jpg
Notes: 49225730911 This is a little Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It really is tiny, about 1200 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.7 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Hah - there are about 3,000 species in North America . . . and this is one of them. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2019)
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: jc15c_jc15-011.jpg
Notes: 49225653606 Here is an assortment of lichens growing on dead stems of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in the canyon. On the right you can see a brilliant Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans) that I've shown before, most recently in this photo. It's the only specimen I know of in our canyon. Looking at it again today, I noticed what must be a Lace Lichen (Ramalina menziesii) reaching out to it. This lichen is common in the Santa Ynez Valley where it decorates the deciduous Valley Oak trees, see this photo, but I don't think I've ever seen it before up here in the mountains. It's interesting to find these two unusual lichens on the same shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2019)
CommonNames: Chamise; Powdered Orange Bush Lichen; Lace Lichen
ScientificName: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Teloschistes flavicans; Ramalina menziesii
associatedTaxa: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Teloschistes flavicans; Ramalina menziesii

File.Name: jc15d_jc15-002.jpg
Notes: 49225585771 Here is another mushroom growing on a garden path at home - but also under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) which is always nearby. I believe these are some kind of brown-spored Inocybe mushrooms, though the gills are quite pallid at this stage. I expect them to have brown spores, and they do have brown stains on the stems. These have no particular odor. I've had the species Inocybe brunnescens suggested in the past, see this photo from 2016. That seems reasonable looking at the Mykoweb California Fungi website. It's a large genus, many of which are poisonous - but they are not dangerous to handle or photograph! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2019)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Inocybe; Inocybe brunnescens
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Inocybe; Inocybe brunnescens

File.Name: jc16a_jc16-014.jpg
Notes:
49230693073 This is looking closer at the Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in my [Previous] photo. I found it in a small oak tree in total shade this morning, and I guess it was grounded by the chilly weather, though it wasn't actually frosty. Sometimes the abdomens of these dragonflies are red and sometimes orange, but they always have white racing strips tipped with yellow on their thorax below the wings. They will stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out the colder days. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2019)

We still had some residual breeze today but nothing like the strong north winds of the last three days. Days have been chilly - even without the wind chill. We do have chances of rain on Wednesday and Sunday. After so much wind sucking up the moisture, we need it.

File.Name: jc16b_jc16-011.jpg
Notes: 49230618373 I was walking by a small oak tree in total shade on this chilly morning, when this dragonfly seemed to jump out at me! It didn't actually move, but it was staying so still in that dark place that I was surprised to suddenly see it. I guess it was grounded by cold on this chilly day, though it wasn't actually frosty. This is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. If you compare this photo of another dragonfly a few days ago, you can see that their colors are variable, red in this photo and orange in the other. I believe they'll stay in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out the colder days. See my [Next] photo for a closer shot. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2019)

File.Name: jc16c_jc16-004.jpg
Notes: 49230533863 Look at the mysterious seeds or eggs or pupae or alien space pods in this photo from a week ago . . . I'm sure now that they are seeds of the Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) just like the one in this photo. Apparently they swell up with a gelatinous coating after a rain just like Chia Seeds, try a google search on "chia seed frog eggs" to see what I mean! It makes sense - chia seeds come from the Mexican Salvia hispanica which is another plant in the same genus as Black Sage, so it's not surprising that it has seeds with similar properties. I've been looking for seeds ever since that first photo, but they are scarce. I suspect that the seeds are expelled from the seed heads as they swell up, so it's an adaptation. I reckon Benjamin Fabian nailed this one in his comment to my original photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2019)

File.Name: jc16d_jc15-025.jpg
Notes: 49231132332 Here is another shot of the spiders in our rain gauge, see my [Previous] photo for the story. I figure they are a mating pair or at least interested - the skinnier male on the left has enlarged palps, and the larger female on the right has slender lady palps that are more prominent in my other photo. The male is in rough shape, missing two legs and a palp on its right side. The rain gauge is open now, but it has been closed with a tight-fitting lid with a small opening. I don't know how the spiders got in, but they seem to like it here. The female spider got away when I took the top off, but the male doesn't want to leave and is still in there. I'm still not sure what kind of spiders they are. My first thought was Wolf Spiders (Lycosidae). Benjamin Fabian suggests Running Crab Spiders (Philodromidae) in a comment on my other photo, but my spiders do not have leg ll longer than leg l - compare this photo from Oregon. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2019)

File.Name: jc17a_jc17-004.jpg
Notes:
49235567633 Here is another shot of the remarkable caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on its Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) host plant that I first found a couple of weeks ago, see this photo. I got this photo on a cloudy day with a breeze, and white balance is what it is - or isn't. The caterpillar hasn't changed its position much in two weeks, and I suspect it's here for the long haul. The butterflies are common in the woods all summer, though they've disappeared now for the year with the recent cold weather. They overwinter as caterpillars according to my book, and I believe this one will become one of next summer's butterflies. It's not as large as the butterflies, maybe 1/2 inch long or less. Look how its leaf is covered with webbing. The most surprising thing to me is how exposed this one is to the weather - is it really going to sit here all winter? I'll keep checking back. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2019)

We now only have a slight 10% chance of rain tonight and tomorrow, but it did feel like rain today. It was cloudy, and there was a wind up to 20 mph. The chances for rain sound better next week starting on Sunday. A wet Christmas would be a gift for all of us.

File.Name: jc17b_jc17-012.jpg
Notes: 49235484348 This is probably the same little Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae that I show changing color from brown to green over the course of four days in my [Previous] photo sequence. After I got the last of those photos, it moved to this nearby seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae), and here it is today with its still-brown buddies. (The lily is growing up through the manzanita, they are just inches apart.) I suppose it's possible that they are different kinds, but I'm pretty sure it changed color - and it makes a better story, a kind of narrative causality! I encountered something similar last year when a similar true bug I was keeping an eye on suddenly changed from brown to green, see this photo and follow the links back in time. Maybe I didn't just imagine it! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2019)
CommonNames: Stink Bug; Shield Bug; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily

File.Name: jc17c_jc17-046.jpg
Notes: 49235407973 Here are three shots of (probably) the same little Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae changing color from brown to green in the surprisingly quick course of four days. I got these photos on December 8, 10, and 12 of this month on the same leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae), and I say it's probably the same bug because I wasn't there the whole time keeping an eye on it - but I think it is the same. I was hoping to get a final shot of its transformation, but the bug moved to a nearby seedpod of Late-flowered Mariposa Lily, see my [Next] photo. I encountered something similar last year when a similar true bug I was keeping an eye on suddenly changed from brown to green, see this photo and follow the links back in time. Maybe I didn't just imagine it! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2019)
CommonNames: Stink Bug; Shield Bug; Eastwood Manzanita
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; probably
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; probably

File.Name: jc17d_jc15-018.jpg
Notes: 49235840516 The white specks are tiny Non-biting Midge flies, probably in the family Chironomidae of the order Diptera dancing in the sunbeams above the oak trees. I assume this is a mating thing. The flies are nearly invisible unless the sun is at just the right angle - close but not too close. They form strange columns above the trees that slowly move up and down. I'll keep trying to get a better photo, maybe one like this that shows the light trails of the indivisual flies. I may have a shot of one of the tiny flies by itself in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2019)

File.Name: jc18a_jc18-034.jpg
Notes:
49241143807 I was surprised to find this Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) today. I actually found three of them - all narrow-winged females - within a foot of each other in this shrub of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I've actually been hearing frogs at night instead of crickets since the weather turned cold and wet back around Thanksgiving. It's interesting that all the crickets I've seen in the last month (since this one on November 17) are females. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2019)

It felt like rain this morning, but then the sun came out and a breeze picked up. I drove up East Camino Cielo road today to see what I could see, but this photo is from home on the way back.

File.Name: jc18b_jc18-001.jpg
Notes: 49240868566 Here is a mass of (I guess) galls on leaves and last-year's catkins of a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus aka Notholithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know whose galls these are, and they don't seem to be in my book. I can't find any information about this on the web either, which seems peculiar as they are so common on this particular tree. Any help with ID will be appreciated. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 December 2019)

File.Name: jc18c_jc12-026.jpg
Notes:
49240758366 This is the web of the tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider that I showed in this photo a few days ago. You can actually see the spider in the upper-right, but it is tiny - I'll make a note. The web looks like the work of Zygiella x-notata with a missing section like a pie slice, but the spiders looks quite different. This spider also has a line to the center of it's web to sense activity that goes through the missing slice. It's interesting that this sensing line seems to run in the same plane as the web itself, while the one in my [Previous] photo might run behind the web. This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis. The web is between dead stems of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It's small, only a couple of inches across. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2019)

(I was so rattled by the good news tonight as I wrote this, that I moved off the web page without saving it - and then had to write the whole darn thing over again. I hope it makes sense.)

File.Name: jc18d_jc12-044.jpg
Notes: 49240199283 This is a spider's orb web in a shrub of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) that is way past flowering. The web is about twice the size of the one in my [Next] photo - 6-8 inches rather than 2-3 inches. I can't see any spider, but I think this might be the web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) - see this photo taken last year. If you look close, you can see the special strand of webbing that runs from the center of the orb web up to the right that the spider uses to sense activity. That strand seems to run behind the web, which suggests genus Metepeira. I also see some stray webbing in the upper-right that might be the spider's retreat - which also suggests genus Metepeira. Whatever, it's a nice web. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2019)

File.Name: jc19a_jc19-017.jpg
Notes:
49245669122 Here is a tiny Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) waiting at the end of a leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae). The tick's front legs look like antennae, but they are really ready to grab anything that comes by so it can fall away and attach itself - but isn't it facing the wrong way? This one is a female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus). They are the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but see this photo for their remarkable connection with lizards. This one really is small, about 750 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm, not quite 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) *Sigh* I've already been ticked at least once this year - I still feel it on my side. What, me worry? (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2019)

We were expecting another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tonight from 8:00 pm - 6:00 am, but it was cancelled at the last moment this afternoon. This would have been our third power outage in a month. Bah, this is no way to run a business - or a public utility. Today was sunny but still chilly. We have "rain likely" on Sunday and a chance of rain all next week.
CommonNames: Tick; Chaparral Yucca; Western Black-legged Tick

It's probably held in place by nearly-invisible spider webbing, but I prefer to think of it as a perfect balancing act. The seeds are starting to show up everywhere. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2019)

File.Name: jc19d_jc10-032.jpg
Notes: 49244865518 Here are a couple of little Stink Bugs aka Shield Bugs in the family Pentatomidae snuggled into a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). I believe they will spend the winter here like they did last year - see this photo. (Yep, it changed color over the course of winter - see this photo for an explanation.) I had this photo ready to post last week, and never got around to it. It is a nice shot of the bugs' rear ends, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2019)

File.Name: jc19d_jc19-016.jpg
Notes: 49245484402 This is another Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a dead leaf of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). It's another lady cricket as shown by its narrow wings, compare the male in this photo. I'm pretty sure it's different from the one I showed yesterday in my [Previous] photo, because this one has its left antenna shortened and the other one doesn't. You can see its two long cerci with a dark-tipped ovipositor between them at her rear end - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2019)

File.Name: jc20a_jc20-008.jpg
Notes:
49251437627 Here are tiny Non-biting Midge Flies (Chironomidae) lined up on a spider web like starlings on phone lines. These are the tiny flies that dance in the sunbeams above the oak trees, see my [Previous] photo for a close shot of a single fly and this photo for a typical mating swarm. There was a small swarm of flies nearby, but why are they so evenly spaced on the web?? The spider web is interesting by itself, no doubt the work of a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae), see this photo and this photo. I couldn't see the spider today, but at least I know where to look for it. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2019)

It was quite breezy today with winds mostly from the south. I like to think of this as our "rain wind" as our winter storms come from the north but pull clouds and moisture in from the south. It's this south wind hitting the east-west Santa Ynez Mountains that creates the "orographic enhancement" that brings our best rain. There is now "rain likely" for Sunday especially and most of next week. We might get rain for both Thanksgiving and Christmas this year!
CommonNames: Non-biting Midge Flies; Triangle Web Spider

File.Name: jc20b_jc19-023.jpg
Notes: 49251314392 Here are two shots of one of those Non-biting Midge Flies (Chironomidae) that dance in the sunbeams above the oak trees, see this photo from a few days ago. This particular fly was caught in very fine spider webbing on a leaf of leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae). It's dancing days are done, but the swarm was continuing the dance not too many feet away. This one is tiny, only about 600 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's male with those feathery antennae, see this photo of a mating pair for the female. I don't understand their life cycle - BugGuide says their larvae are mostly aquatic, but we have no standing water in the mountains yet. BugGuide goes on to say that "a few [species] occur in decaying matter, under bark or in moist ground. Larvae of some species tolerate seasonal desiccation." I guess ours is one of those. See my [Next] photo for another example of the hazards of spider webs. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2019)

File.Name: jc20c_jc18-038.jpg
Notes: 49251001346 This is a tiny blue (or "blue dun") fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It really is tiny, only about 530 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.1 mm - substantially less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but I've seen them before, e.g. in this photo. I was thinking Frit Fly or Leaf Miner Fly, but then I remembered this photo of what looks like the same kind of fly with aphids. It might be a Silver Fly or Aphid Fly in the genus Leucopis of the family Chamaemyiidae, compare this photo at BugGuide. Whatever it is, it's an attractive little fly, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2019)

File.Name: jc20d_jc17-017.jpg
Notes: 49250504278 This is a tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a sandstone rock that is encrusted with liverworts and moss. I believe the liverwort is the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). The little fly was moving around sucking up something, but I couldn't tell what. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group today, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2019)

File.Name: jc21a_jc21-010.jpg
Notes:
49256213826 I found this gorgeous Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly on sandy soil with fallen oak leaves along our road this morning. I suspect it was just trying to warm up in the sun. This one seems particularly brilliant, and I wonder if it is recently emerged - though that seems unlikely at this time of year. We have a trio of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days in the middle of winter, including the Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) and the California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica). I've also seen the Tortoiseshell lately, but this is the first photo of any of them that I've managed to get. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2019)

The winter solstice happens today at 8:19 pm PST. It's the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting just a bit longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky. It's a time of new hope for the future. Happy solstice everyone!

It's also the official first day of winter - or is it? Here's a little experiment. Try a google search on first day of winter 2019, and you learn that "Winter 2019 in Northern Hemisphere will begin on Saturday, December 21." But try a google search on UK first day of winter 2019, and you learn that "Winter 2019 in Northern Hemisphere will begin on Sunday, December 22." The problem is that the winter solstice happens for us in California on Saturday, December 21, 2019 at 8:19 pm PST, but that's Sunday, December 22, 2019 at 04:19 am GMT/UTC. I reckon winter is for the whole Northern Hemisphere, not just North America, so we should go by GMT/UTC. That makes the first day of winter tomorrow on Sunday. Not that it matters much, but calendars and Google should agree about these things.
ScientificName: Vanessa atalanta; Nymphalis antiopa; Nymphalis californica
associatedTaxa: Vanessa atalanta; Nymphalis antiopa; Nymphalis californica

File.Name: jc21b_jc21-002.jpg
Notes: 49256333532 Here is another pair of what I believe are Oak Treehoppers in the genus Platycotis, Membracidae on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), see this photo and this photo for more shots. Sometimes they have little thorn-like horns on their heads but sometimes not. These lack the bright colors of the widespread species Platycotis vittata, but maybe they are the related species Platycotis minax? They're small and easy not to notice. They often line up like in my photo today and really just look like bumps on the branch unless you get real close. For some reason, their faces remind me of little owls. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2019)

File.Name: jc21c_jc21-006.jpg
Notes: 49256063306 Here is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) trying to get away from me by crawling over foliage of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I suspect from it's green colors that it has recently made the transition from nymph to adult, compare this photo of another recent graduate. It's named for the tiny size of its usual prey, not for any personal resemblance. You can see this one's mighty spike-like proboscis folded under its head - see this photo and this photo for what it does with that proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2019)

File.Name: jc21d_jc14-001.jpg
Notes: 49256192027 This is a not-so-great photo of a Horsehair Fungus mushroom growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak, Fagaceae) trees, but I'm glad I saved it. Look under the green lichen near the bottom of my photo - there is a tiny snail! The mushrooms are tiny, just a few millimeters across, and the snail is only about 1/3 of that. I didn't notice the snail until the photo had been on my computer for a few days. I like these photos where you only discover something interesting after the fact. I don't know what kind of snail it is, or whether it's a juvenile or a just adult. HSS! (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2019)

File.Name: jc22a_jc22-010.jpg
Notes:
49261854816 This is looking into the woods during the surprisingly heavy rain today. The yellow leaves show the fall colors of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in both woodland and chaparral, it has gorgeous white flowers that smell like perfume, it has these interesting edible nuts, and the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall. Also visible in the photo are Coast Live Oak and California Bay trees. Can you spot the Madrone tree in the upper-left? (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2019)

We had more rain than was expected today - 2.4 inches and still falling! It's been raining since about 11:00 am this morning, and it hasn't stopped all day. We've been under an NWS Flood Advisory since before noon which keeps getting extended, now until 9:00 pm tonight. It's rained hard enough at times to cause debris flow problems on the recent wildfire burn, but I haven't heard of any problems so far. This brings our rainfall total to 9.04 inches for the season. We had dropped down to 85% of normal before the rain, but we're back up to 114% on the happy side of normal by my reckoning. I look forward to getting out with my camera tomorrow when there's only a 70% chance of more rain.

File.Name: jc22b_jc22-008.jpg
Notes: 49261314898 Here is the view from our covered front porch today in the surprisingly heavy rain - a vine of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) climbing over a small Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree, with another Madrone to the side and a dark woodland of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees behind. You can see the rain falling. I've shown this scene before when it was too wet to go out into the rain - see my photos here, here, and here. The last of these photos shows the scene at the end of the growing season without rain. The little tree somehow puts up with being smothered. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2019)

File.Name: jc22c_jc19-007.jpg
Notes: 49261735701 Our recent rains have done the native Prickly Phlox (Linanthus californicus aka Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) some good. These plants started to green up in October, but they were looking pretty sad by the time we got our first rains near the end of November. Now they are starting to show new signs of life again after the rains. The new leaves look succulent, but they will become the next crop of sharp spines in time. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2019)

File.Name: jc22d_jc10-007.jpg
Notes: 49261640846 This is another Banana Slug at home in the woods. I got this photo a while ago and never posted it, but it seems appropriate after a day of "banana slug weather" - it rained all day, and I'll bet the slugs are out there right now! I've been calling these the California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus), but ours are probably the Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus), see this notice by a local biologist. Apparently this species occurs near Santa Cruz and Santa Barbara but skips San Luis Obispo in the middle. I regard these slugs as environmental indicators of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. I'll be looking for them tomorrow after the rain lets up. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2019)

File.Name: jc23a_jc23-029.jpg
Notes:
49267060066 I didn't understand when I saw this happening, but now I get it - this is a hand-size Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus) making poop, but its anus isn't where you think it should be. The slug has its breathing hole or pneumostome on the right side only of its mantle - that raised section covering its front end. The slug's anus and genitals share the pneumostome opening, hence this one is making poop through its breathing hole. (Hah, it reminds me of a certain classic South Park episode!) One web site I found puts it like this: "The odd position of these openings so far forward on the body is a result of the slugs evolutionary descent from snails. In a snail these openings must be outside the shell (nobody wants to poop in their own shell after all)." These slugs are still basically snails - though with a fleshy mantle instead of a hard shell. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2019)

We got about 3/10 inch more rain today in one heavy outburst, but that was it. It was cloudy all day, and I did find the Banana Slugs out and active. These are the same slugs I used to call the California Banana Slug (A. californicus), see my comments on this photo. There are several different species along the west coast, which all look similar except for differences in genital structures. I defer to the experts.

File.Name: jc23b_jc23-044.jpg
Notes: 49267004161 The Slug Ouroboros! The ancient myth has the "Worm Ouroboros" biting its own tail and enclosing the world. It's a yin-yang thing. I believe this is a hand-size Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus). I've seen them in this position before, and I thought they were doing something unmentionable involving "coprophagia" - look it up if you don't know the word. (Rabbits do that too.) But that turns out to be wrong because their anus is not where you expect it to be - see my [Next] photo! So what is this one doing? I suspect it's eating its old slime. That doesn't sound very appetizing, but it's better than the unmentionable! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2019)
CommonNames: The Slug Ouroboros; Southern Pacific Banana Slug

File.Name: jc23c_jc23-012.jpg
Notes: 49266944346 This is the California Liverwort (Asterella californica) growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. Most of these have round buds on their "leaves" (more properly a thallus) that will become umbrella-like sporophytes for sexual reproduction as in this photo. But these liverworts have two different reproductive strategies, and the plants in my [Previous] photo have bumpy tongue-like structures on their leaves that will become the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain. A few plants on the left side of this photo have gemmae too. I'll try to get a closer photo showing both gemmae and sporophytes at once, like this photo shows. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2019)

File.Name: jc23d_jc23-010.jpg
Notes: 49266408893 This is the California Liverwort (Asterella californica) growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. Most of these have a bumpy tongue-like structures on their "leaves" (more properly a thallus) that will become the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain. But these liverworts have two different reproductive strategies, and the plants in my [Next] photo have round buds on their leaves that will become umbrella-like sporophytes for sexual reproduction as in this photo. A few plants on the left side of this photo have sporophytes too. I'll try to get a closer photo showing both gemmae and sporophytes at once, like this photo shows. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2019)

File.Name: jc24a_jc24-007.jpg
Notes:
49271491091 These are the first two Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that I've seen in the woods this year. They are coming up through fallen oak leaves in the garden at home, but they are no doubt growing from a buried stump or root. Sometimes these mushrooms come up even before the rains, see this photo from 2017, but I've been waiting for them this year. I like the fallen leaves that make up the oak mulch in my photo, and I cropped the image wide to accent it. Hah, I noticed something on my computer screen that I didn't notice when I got the photo - there are two really tiny snails on the green leaf to the right! I'll make a note, but view the photo large to see them. Mushrooms and snails for the holidays? Why not - they are something that the rain brings with a gift of new life. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2019)

It was very windy today with 30-40 mph gusts. It was no fun trying to get a few photos for the holidays in the wind, but I did get a few with my usual strategy - kick up the ISO, increase the aperture, and take lots of photos! We have more rain expected tomorrow for a real Christmas gift.

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: jc24b_jc23-004.jpg
Notes:
49271444546 Here are raindrops on a cluster of tiny cactus spines in the garden. Actually these aren't proper cactus spines at all but a tuft of horrible glochids - those short hair-like prickles that are barbed and cause irritation and are very difficult to remove, there's more info at Wikipedia. I like the tiny lichens at the base of this glochid, see this photo. I reckon this photo is like the centerpiece for a holiday table spread or the top ornament of a Christmas tree - appropriate for the holidays. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2019)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: jc24c_jc24-014.jpg
Notes:
49271397271 These are early flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family with a few raindrops from our recent storms. The flower buds are really the stand-out feature of this shrub, along with the think folded leaves that look something like taco shells. The pretty buds form in September and then wait fully half a year to open, see this photo from last April. They are beautiful and appropriate for the holidays. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2019)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: jc24d_jc24-023.jpg
Notes:
49271363081 Here are ripe Christmas berries on native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The ripe berries are edible, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. They are beautiful and appropriate for the holidays. The shrubs seem to be having a good year, though the berries always look better from a distance. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2018)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: jc25a_jc25-012.jpg
Notes:
49276656757 This is another dragonfly hanging at an awkward angle from a branch of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. You can see the typical white racing stripes with a yellow tip on its thorax. These dragonflies stay in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and/or stormy days like today by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. It's a fun challenge to look for them. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2019)

There was a threat of rain all day today, and then it really started to come down just before 6:00 pm. We've had over an inch since then with rates over 3/4 inch per hour - including a five minute burst of 0.12 inches of rain at 6:45 pm which comes to 1.44 inch/hour! Fortunately, it didn't last for a whole hour. A NWS Flash Flood Warning has been issued for areas below the recent Cave Fire - that means WATCH OUT for a debris flow! Along with heavy rain all night and a NWS Flood Advisory, we're also under a NWS Winter Storm Warning for possible snow until tomorrow morning. It will be cold tonight into the mid-30s, but hopefully not quite cold enough to snow - it wreaks havoc with our oak trees.

File.Name: jc25b_jc25-034.jpg
Notes: 49276540967 Spiders and their webs have been scarce after heavy rains this week, but I found this one today in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's web is just slightly visible, but I believe it is an orb web. I suspect from its long front legs that the spider is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, but it doesn't have the humped back of the Metellina mimetoides spider in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Long-jawed Orb Weaver

File.Name: jc25c_jc25-008.jpg
Notes: 49276497907 I found this odd little tunnel of webbing on an umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) today. I suspect it's the work of some kind of spider, though I suppose it could belong to an insect larva as well. Hah - I kept hoping it would stick its head out the door while I was getting my photo but no such luck. It's not the best photo, but I was happy to get it today since spider webs have been scarce after our heavy rain this week. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2019)

File.Name: jc25d_jc25-001.jpg
Notes: 49275741883 Here is the first little Mycena mushroom I've seen this season growing in the oak mulch above our retaining wall this morning. I've been expecting these little mushrooms ever since our Thanksgiving rains, but we've had to wait until Christmas. I expect there will be many more after our current rain ends - see this photo from a few years ago. Maybe this one is Mycena californiensis with colored gill edges (here) and the broken stem exuding a red juice from the base (here) - but maybe not. I didn't want to pick the first mushroom I found just to find out. There are also other fungi in this photo - root-like rhizomorphs of a Horsehair Fungus like this photo shows and really tiny white fungi that are maybe the same as this photo shows. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2019)

File.Name: jc26a_jc26-007.jpg
Notes:
49280137277 Here is a tiny spider on a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I was trying to get a photo of the red fungus that looks like raspberries that I show in this photo, and a bit of one clump is visible at the top of this shot. The spider was interested in the fungus or in some critters on the fungus. I thought at first that it is one of those Crab Spiders with cryptic colors like the one in this photo. But after looking closer, I believe it is a Running Crab Spider in the family Philodromidae because leg ll is longer than leg l, and I believe it is genus Philodromus because of the spacing of its upper set of eyes - the middle pair are closer to the outside eyes than each other. (My book says succinctly that the posterior medians are closer to the posterior laterals than to one another.) This spider is tiny, about 700 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Once I started looking for tiny spiders, I keep finding them. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2019)

We had exciting weather last night with a Flash Flood Warning and an actual Tornado Watch! (Apparently a tornado did touch down in Ventura County last night - here's the story.) Neither event happened here, but our storm was intense - and short-lived, it was over by midnight. We ended up with 1.6 inch of rain - nice, but less than the 2-4 inches I was hoping for. We got no more rain today, but there is a chance for more rain on Sunday night and Monday. This storm brings out total up to 10.90 inches of rain for the season since September 1 - that's 122% of normal, not bad!
Locality: Apparently a tornado did touch down in Ventura County last night - here's the story.) Neither event happened here
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Running Crab Spider

File.Name: jc26b_jc26-036.jpg
Notes: 49280096822 This is another tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider on a done flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon today. The flat receptacle of the flower is about 1/4 inch across which gives scale. The spider is also about 850 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) That's about half the size of the flower, so it checks out. This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. I still have trouble telling them apart. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2019)

File.Name: jc26d_jc21-005.jpg
Notes: 49279368783 Here is another shot of the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) trying to get away from me that I first showed in this photo a few days ago. I saved this shot for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday. The assassin is crawling over bristly foliage of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I suspect from its green colors that it has recently made the transition from nymph to adult, compare this photo of another recent graduate. It's named for the tiny size of its usual prey, not for any personal resemblance. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2019)
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: jc27a_jc27-011.jpg
Notes:
49284304613 Here is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched on a dead grass stem on a cold day in the mountains. I noticed this fly while I was looking for fungi and snails above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods at eye level. It was too cold for the fly to take off, though it was crawling around very slowly. I had an appointment in town this morning, and I had to scrape ice off my windshield before I left. I got this photo when I got home, and it was still too cold for this fly. I guess this is what flies do when it gets cold - they hole up under a leaf or in the mulch and wait . . . and hope some nosy photographer doesn't come along and force you to move! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2019)

Like I said, it was cold this morning. The sun was shining, but I got this photo in a place where the sun won't shine direct until the equinox. Hah, most of the area around our house is like that! White balance is what it is. Tonight is expected to be another cold one, and then it will slowly start warming up. We now have "rain likely" for Sunday night and Monday.

File.Name: jc27b_jc27-006.jpg
Notes: 49284915147 This is looking face-to-face with a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a cold day in the mountains. I noticed this fly while I was looking for fungi and snails above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods at eye level. It was too cold for the fly to take off, though it was crawling around very slowly. I had an appointment in town this morning, and I had to scrape ice off my windshield before I left. I got this photo when got home, and it was still too cold for this fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2019)

File.Name: jc27c_jc26-005.jpg
Notes: 49284589441 These are tiny Disk Fungi or Cup Fungi on a pencil-sized fallen twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I like them because they do look like little mushrooms. The individual disks are only about 200 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 0.8 mm, about 1/32 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I'm sure these are ascomycetes rather than basidiomycetes, but that's all I know about them. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2019)
CommonNames: Disk Fungi; Cup Fungi; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: jc27d_jc26-012.jpg
Notes: 49284068653 Here is another shot of what I believe is some kind of fungus growing on a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I don't know what kind of fungus it is, but the fruiting bodies are kind of pretty - like little red raspberries. They feel hard, not squishy. They seem to be only on the bark. The individual balls are only about 0.5 mm in size, though the aggregations of them are a mm or two across. Any help with ID will be appreciated. This is the branch on which I found the spider in my [Previous] photo. I guess this is a fungus - I don't know what else it could be - so happy Fungus Friday! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2019)

File.Name: jc28a_jc28-021.jpg
Notes:
49290720122 Here are two shots of a furry little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It might be another female Pelegrina aeneola spider, compare this photo and this photo. I figure this cute little spider is a lot less threatening than the tick in my [Previous] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2019)

It was another cold day but nice on the sunny side of the canyon. A little breeze but not much. Now why is it that some storms pass by with great winds, but other storms just quietly go away? We now have a 90% chance of rain for Sunday night and Monday, and it's cold enough that we're under a NWS Winter Storm Watch - which means possible snow.
ScientificName: Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pelegrina aeneola
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pelegrina aeneola

File.Name: jc28b_jc28-035.jpg
Notes: 49290651367 This is the same female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) that I show on the left in my [Previous] photo. Here she is questing - waiting on the end of a grass stem for a potential host to grab on to. Hah, that could be you or me! Thank you Jerry Friedman for suggesting that word in a comment to this photo which shows similar behavior. I had to use my on-camera lash for this and my previous photos, it was just too breezy and dark in the shade to get the shots otherwise. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's necessary. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2019)

File.Name: jc28c_jc28-024.jpg
Notes: 49289871858 I found these two different Ticks (Ixodidae, Arachnida) a few inches apart on dead stems of the same perennial grass plant - probably Small-flowered Melic (Melica imperfecta, Poaceae) though it hardly matters. Both images are the same size crop of the original 1:1 macro shots, so they are true to scale. The larger tick is 875 pixels which comes to 3.4 mm, about 1/8 inch, and the smaller one is 625 pixels mm which comes 2.4 mm, about 3/32 or 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I thought they were different kinds at first, but now I believe they are female and male Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus). According to Wikipedia, the unfed females are larger at 3.18 mm as opposed to the males at 2.54 mm - very similar to my dimensions. I have another photo of a male which is also very similar. Apparently the adult male ticks do not suck blood to become engorged. The females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't. This raises the question what this one is doing at the end of the grass stem. Well, it is facing the wrong way, and maybe it's looking for a mate rather than a meal? These ticks are said to the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us - see this photo! Sounds fantastic, but apparently it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2019)
CommonNames: Ticks; Small-flowered Melic; Western Black-legged Ticks
ScientificName: Ixodidae; Arachnida; Melica imperfecta; Poaceae; Ixodes pacificus
associatedTaxa: Ixodidae; Arachnida; Melica imperfecta; Poaceae; Ixodes pacificus

File.Name: jc28d_jc27-022.jpg
Notes: 49290544667 I like the topography of this mossy rock outcrop in the woods. A little rain changes everything. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2019)

File.Name: jc29a_jc29-035.jpg
Notes:
49296751832 This is fairly fresh scat of a Black Bear (Ursus americanus) near the place where we leave our trash can on pickup day, though it's probably been through a rain or two. This bear has become a problem, raiding all the trash cans in our canyon. At least it hasn't forgotten how to live in the wild - this scat seems to be made up entirely of pits and skins of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia, Rosaceae). (Which is odd as I haven't seen any cherries for some time - does the bear have a stash?) Hah, at first I thought they were garbanzo beans from the spilled garbage! I've seen a bear near home before, see this photo from 2016. We'll have to try planting the seeds - passing through an animal is sometimes just the thing to force native seeds and berries to sprout. In the meantime, we need to bear-proof our trash can. Bears and mountain lions should be invisible where people live - or there will be trouble. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2019)

There was some sun this morning, but clouds started moving in and a breeze picked up by the time I got outside with my camera. We have an 80% chance of rain later tonight and tomorrow morning. I hope it doesn't snow again on I-5 near the Grapevine - the local traffic has been terrible from people seeking a detour after the last wintery storm.

File.Name: jc29b_jc29-009.jpg
Notes: 49296000503 Here is another shot of the remarkable caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on its Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) host plant that I first found at the beginning of the month, see this photo. I check on this caterpillar every day or two on my usual walks, and this is the first time I've seen it away from its usual perch - though just a couple of inches away on the same leaf. It seems to be nibbling the oak leaf. I was thinking it would be dormant all winter, but I guess not - though it was back in its usual place when I passed by again a few hours later on my way home. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2019)

File.Name: jc29c_jc29-030.jpg
Notes: 49296576557 I believe this is the mushroom formally known as the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila), now more properly called Gymnopus dryophilus - though I suspect the common name will stick. You can tell by the fallen leaves that oak trees are nearby. This mushroom is growing in a little nook at the base of a sandstone outcrop - with moss and liverworts and seedlings and new shoots of native perennial Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). Some mushrooms are finally starting to appear this year, but they are taking their own sweet time. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2019)
ScientificName: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: jc29d_jc29-002.jpg
Notes: 49296293271 This is a flabby clump of Brown Witch's Butter (Tremella foliacea) growing on a old stump of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This one is larger than the more common yellow Witch's Butter (T. aurantia) in this photo. Both species are said to grow as parasites on Stereum fungi, and that might well be Stereum hirsutum growing as a bracket fungus behind it. I put my pocket knife in the photo for scale - it's not quite four inches long. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2019)
CommonNames: Brown Witch's Butter; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Tremella foliacea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; T. aurantia; Stereum; Stereum hirsutum
associatedTaxa: Tremella foliacea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; T. aurantia; Stereum; Stereum hirsutum

File.Name: jc30a_jc30-004.jpg
Notes:
49302004493 It's always fun to try to find the tiniest mushroom in the woods, and these are a contender - little fairy cups growing from a fallen oak leaf. I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale - the mushroom caps are just a bit larger, about 200 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 0.78 mm. (See here for how I figured.) Hah, I also measured the ball of my pen and got 180 pixels, which comes to 0.705 mm - just what it should be. And look at those black spots on the leaf that are sprouting even tinier new ones! I got this photo above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods at eye level and saves my knees. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2019)

Our expected storm last night fizzled out and dropped less than 1/10 inch rather than the inch-plus of rain I was hoping for. I like this hard-boiled comment in the NWS Forecast Discussion this morning, "With precip chcs fading like a cheap postcard the attention turns to the winds." (Sometimes those discussions have a unique literary merit, abrv and all!) Actually the wind wasn't bad in our mountains, though there was just enough breeze to keep everything in motion. All my photos today are of tiny things.
Locality: Sometimes those discussions have a unique literary merit

File.Name: jc30b_jc30-002.jpg
Notes: 49302433146 It's always fun to try to find the tiniest mushroom in the woods, and this is a contender. I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale - the mushroom cap is just a bit larger. It's just 260 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.0 mm. (See here for how I figured.) I suspect it's actually a Mycena mushroom, but it hardly matters. I got this photo above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods at eye level and saves my knees. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2019)

File.Name: jc30c_jc30-014.jpg
Notes: 49302526157 I was thinking when I got this photo that it's a fancy aphid, and maybe it is - though it doesn't seem to have the typical tube-like projections on its rear end called cornicles. But it just looks too fancy to be an aphid, and I wonder if it isn't a Barklouse (Psocoptera aka Psocodea)? All the barklice I've managed to photograph (like this) have patterned wings, and this one's are clear. It's small, just 750 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's on a leaf of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I'm not sure what it is, and any help with ID will be appreciated. I had to use my on-camera flash for this photo, it was just too breezy and dark in the shade to get it otherwise. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's necessary. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2019)

File.Name: jc30d_jc30-012.jpg
Notes: 49302218571 Here is another shot of what I believe is a male Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) on a dead stem of a perennial grass plant - see this photo for another shot of probably the same tick with a female as well. It's tiny, just 630 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.5 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The female ticks are larger and look different with a shield-like structure on their back, which has something to do with the fact that the males do not suck blood to become engorged. The females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't - mosquitoes and deer flies are similar. This raises the question what this one is doing at the end of the grass stem if not to hook on to a host. Well, it is facing the wrong way, and maybe it's looking for a mate rather than a meal? I had to use my on-camera flash for this, it was just too breezy and dark in the shade to get the shot otherwise. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's necessary. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2019)

File.Name: jc31a_jc31-016.jpg
Notes:
49308012637 Here are the first new leaves I've seen this year of native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. This plant is a perennial that completely dies back after flowering, and it's a treat to see it come back for a new season. It's like noticing that the days are getting slightly longer after the winter solstice - proof that the system works. That seems appropriate for the beginning of another year, so Happy New Year! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2019)

There was some breeze on this last day of the year, but not too bad. It was noticeably warmer today, up into the 60s rather than barely up to 50∞ like its been. I'm not complaining, but it sounds like the next ten days will be dry. Wind is in the forecast.

File.Name: jc31b_jc31-004.jpg
Notes: 49307752526 This is another shot of the remarkable caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on its Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) host plant that I've been keeping an eye on since I first found it at the start of the month, see this photo. Here it is on its usual perch, but what an odd pose - like a sphinx or a seahorse. The webbing on the leaf is also interesting - this caterpillar is exposed to the elements, so maybe it's rigging to hang onto? I still think this caterpillar will be more-or-less dormant all winter long - ready to metamorphosize into a butterfly next spring. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2019)

File.Name: jc31c_jc31-019.jpg
Notes: 49307156773 Here are two shots of those Non-biting Midge Flies (Chironomidae) that dance in the sunbeams above the oak trees, see this photo from a couple of weeks ago. I believe my photo shows female (left) and male (right) flies. There are two differences to note. The male has feathery antennae, the female less so; and the female has a larger abdomen to hold her eggs. I found the female today on on a leaf of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I found the male a few days ago on a fine spider web that's nearly invisible, but I don't think it's caught - a few minutes later it flew off. See this photo for a mating pair. These flies are tiny, about 560 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.2 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) BugGuide says their larvae are mostly aquatic, but we have no standing water in the mountains yet. BugGuide goes on to say that "a few [species] occur in decaying matter, under bark or in moist ground. Larvae of some species tolerate seasonal desiccation." I guess ours is one of those. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2019)
CommonNames: Non-biting Midge Flies; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: jc31d_jc30-015.jpg
Notes: 49307813727 This is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on a dead plant stem in the woods - it looks like a bump on the stem unless you look close. This spider didn't have any web that I could see, but all spider webs took a beating from the rain last week. These spiders always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2019)

File.Name: i101a_i101-003.jpg
Notes:
39441621801 Here is one more photo of a tiny spider which I now believe is a Triangle web spider (genus Hyptiotes) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers. In particular, I believe it is Hyptiotes gertschi, see my [Previous] photos and this photo at BugGuide. I've called this spider several names before, including Uloborus diversus and Tmarus angulatus, but they don't match. (I believe I have a real Tmarus in this photo - it has a similar shape but much longer front legs.) Now I need to go back through all my photos to see which are Hyptiotes and which are Tmarus. (*Manana*) I found this one in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). This spider is small, only about 1/4 inch long to the tip of its toes. I'm just learning about these interesting creatures. Hah - it looks like a furry octopus! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2018)

We had an increasing "mackerel sky" today and some winds from the south, both of which are rain signs for us. There is a small chance of rain for the next few days, but the NWS is pushing back into next week the storm that would bring our first real rain of the rain year - which started in September! I hope they don't push it right off the table.
CommonNames: Triangle web spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: i101b_i101-009.jpg
Notes:
38562855355 This is looking at the furry underside of a tiny spider which I now believe is a Triangle web spider (genus Hyptiotes) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers. In particular, I believe it is Hyptiotes gertschi, see my [Next] and [Previous] photos and this photo at BugGuide. I've called this spider several names before, including Uloborus diversus and Tmarus angulatus, but they don't match. I believe that this photo shows the cribellum of the spider - that brown plate that's visible on the furry belly of the spider. A Wikipedia explains: "The cribellum is a silk spinning organ found in certain spiders. Unlike normal spinnerets, the cribellum consists of one or more plates covered in thousands of tiny spigots. These spigots produce extremely fine fibers which are combed out by the spider's calamistrum, producing silk with a woolly texture. The fibers are so small in diameter that prey insects easily become entangled in them, without any glue needed." The calamistrum that this refers to is a row of specialized leg bristles used to comb out fine silk. It might be visible in my photo on the distant rear leg, I'll make notes. I'm just learning about this. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2018)

By the way, this photo shows that the spider is definitely clutching the broken ends of its web! See this photo for another shot. I guess it moves by producing new webbing and reeling in the old as it goes?
CommonNames: Triangle web spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers
ScientificName: Hyptiotes; Uloboridae; Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloborus diversus; Tmarus angulatus; cribellum; calamistrum
associatedTaxa: Hyptiotes; Uloboridae; Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloborus diversus; Tmarus angulatus; cribellum; calamistrum

File.Name: i101c_hc30-012.jpg
Notes: 27663333679 This is almost the whole web of a tiny spider which I now believe is a Triangle web spider (genus Hyptiotes) in the family Uloboridae of the Cribellate Orb Weavers. In particular, I believe it is Hyptiotes gertschi, see my [Next] two photos and this photo at BugGuide. I've called this spider several names before, including Uloborus diversus and Tmarus angulatus, but they don't match. I found this web (and the spider) in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). The spider makes this vertical web that is shaped like a pie slice of a normal orb web. If you look in the lower-left of my photo, you can see how the web is separating into a very fine mesh - a sign that it is the web of a cribellate spider that produces a different kind of webbing from non-cribellate spiders. I'm just learning about this. I went back today to try to get a better photo of the webbing, but the silk is so fine that it just disappears in my photos. I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2017)
CommonNames: Triangle web spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Southern Bush Monkeyflower; cribellate spider
ScientificName: Hyptiotes; Uloboridae; Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloborus diversus; Tmarus angulatus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
